author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This a vegan recipe - so yes - miso - and no to all the cheese and butter. I’m vegetarian but always am thankful for the vegan recipes - please keep them coming!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
I prefer my original recipe: blanch b. raabe. press out water. chop into 1" long pieces. (This recipe chops too finely for my taste.) fry in generous amount of olive oil with chili flakes, salt and pepper and  big pieces of garlic until cooked through and garlic is browning to get bitter. pour over pasta. toss. add parmesan. remove garlic if you don't want to eat it. 

no need for miso or onions IMHO, though i can see how someone would like this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Chris Right you are. My Italian would add anchovies to mix but otherwise miso, lemon, onion, no way.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Chris I think they are trying to make it vegan?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Chris what you suggest here is a completely different recipe. Perhaps you are looking for this https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1025299-braised-broccoli-pasta?smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share&cgs=c

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This flavorable pureed sauce is fantastic and highly adaptable. I'm planning to make the pasta at some point, but I had a pot of navy beans and some kale--so I sauteed the kale, added the sauce, and piled it all on top of the beans. Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I’ve been making a simpler version for years.  Saute broccoli rabe with garlic, a slab of butter, olive oil, red pepper flakes and spike seasoned salt or rock salt.  I often add cremini or white button mushrooms.  When pasts is al dente stir in together and toss.  I change the pasta up sometimes, thin whole wheat spaghetti, whole wheat or tri-color rotini... it’s all good.  Top off with parmesan or a shake of nutritional yeast for vegans.   So good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Thabks! A flavorful creamy sauce with no cream or high calorie nuts, switch-up from silken tofu— I’m a happy camper. Made the pasta version first, for the pasta water, doubled everything and so far it was great on sheet pan multi veggies and on green and white beans. I do like the crispy panko mixed with grated lemon peel on anything too. So glad I wasn’t discouraged by the person who was so sure these ingredients wouldn’t work that they didn’t try it. Added to my basic sauces now.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This got raves! I used an immersion blender to purée the onions etc. in the skillet, a nice simplification. Will add to my regular rotation, and btw—the broccoli rabe is indeed tender after boiling for a minute or two.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Oh, and any color miso works. I used 2 T red in case it overpowered, but it seems safe to do the whole 3. Miso umami can be a bit flat for some, so the fresh raw lemon note is key.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
And yet another version: cook pasta until a minute of two BEFORE it's al dente.Add chopped broccoli rabe to the boiling pasta and let cook for two minutes. Drain the pasta and broccoli rabe.

in the same pot, heat olive oil and red pepper flakes and as much minced garlic as you like (I don't use very much) for a couple of minute. Add pasta and broccoli rabe to the olive oil mixture, let heat through and serve with parmesan cheese and black pepper.

I've been making this for years and it is easy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Agree with other reviewers - this is a bomb vegan sauce that can be used in lots of other contexts.  Super easy and super flavorful without being heavy.  Will definitely be making it again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made this as is and it was spectacular. Sauce was creamy and flavorful. Passed fresh grated Parmesan at the table.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I chose the recipe because I was curious about the miso aspect. I am not vegan, so it didn’t dawn on me the miso  was a substitute for cheese and dairy. I loved the sauce, it was creamy and tasted a bit cheesy to me because of the miso. I followed the recipe as written, maybe heavy on the  red pepper flakes. I am happy to have leftovers to take for lunch for 2 days. Bonus, I am doing a vegan challenge in May. I now have one great recipe to cook and share with the group. I have some white beans in the fridge. I was too lazy to add them today, but will add to my lunch and the next time I make it to bump up the protein.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I do not know why this got 5 starts. I prepared it exactly according to the recipe, and found the result to be sloppy and frankly, gross. Pasta is good, and so is broccoli rabe. I'm not vegan, so the miso did nothing for me except require me to use every tool in the kitchen. Huge clean up project!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I use black squid ink pasta with this, and add fresh tomatoes and a little parmesan or dairy free Violife. I also use balsamic vinegar and a little Ume plum vinegar on the Rappini. I guarantee this is delicious. Cook the stems separately by the way.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I make this regularly (without onions) and add a grilled sausage (now switching to vegan sausage due to high cholesterol) and Beans. It's my comfort food. I don't blend it but sounds good to me. I wonder about needing 1/3 cup oil- seems like adding excessive fat to it.  I love the idea of adding miso paste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Question: My local grocery store never seems to carry broccoli rabe. What would be a good substitute?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
@Liv mustard greens, broccolini, kale. Or maybe roast some asparagus or try tossing in fresh arugula at the end (it will wilt from the heat if the pasta).

Funny thing—I live in a rural-ish county where I often (especially so when I moved here 25 years ago) can’t find the ingredients I need, but our local grocery store has always had broccoli rabe. I have never been able to figure out who besides me is buying it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Terrific recipe.  Really delicious.  The instructions have you prepare the sauce while the pasta cooks.  Since the pasta takes ten minutes and the sauce 15 plus blending, the pasta will need to reheat in the pan with the sauce.  Alternately, you can delay putting the pasta in the water until the sauce is about half done.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: My recipe - go to an Italian deli.  Buy 1 pound of prepared broccoli rabe.  Ear a little bit as soon as you get home.   Cook.apond of pasta to your desired "doneness."  Drain the cooked pasta.  Throw the rabe in,  stir and eat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Not an expert cook here and very recipe dependent. Not so brilliantly, I began making this without reading all the steps first; I was just happy that there were so few ingredients and one of them was broccoli rabe. When I got to the blender part I thought that didn’t sound appealing at all so I just left the onions and garlic in the skillet and later added the broccoli and then the pasta. Forgot to add the miso (which I purchased specifically for this recipe) and pasta water (does pasta water really add anything anyway?). Couldn’t have been more pleased with how it came out.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: As a weeknight recipe, this is a fail. As a recipe for weekend/company, it is not any better than many of the easier options available. The timing of things is an issue. Need pasta water to make the sauce - means pasta has to be well along before doing the sauce. You either allow the pasta to sit and dry out while you make the sauce and finish with the broccoli rabe in the pan, or extract the pasta water early. Also, two separate pots and a blender mean too many dishes and harder cleanup. Once it was done, my husband and I both liked it, but not enough to make it again. I didn’t have fresh basil so added a bit of pesto, and it worked wonderfully.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The sauce is truly unique and delicious!  Thrilled that it is also vegan.  It pairs so well with linguine but I thought the broccoli rabe was lackluster.  I chopped it, which was fine, but that seemed to be the most boring part but at least I got my veggies in!   I would make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I was excited for this recipe but it turned out really bitter. Followed it step by step. Ending up tossing it all out. Disappointing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Kat Rapini is bitter. And delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: We loved this, but I couldn’t believe no one commented on the length of time to cook the onions and garlic.  I didn’t even leave it on medium heat, but in 5 minutes, they were browning- not the desired outcome!  Maybe the author meant to turn it down to low?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this with frozen broccoli and for protein added around a 1/3 block extra firm tofu to the blender, nutritional yeast and a can of cannellini beans. It was great. Next time I may add more beans and miso.  This was a very comforting veganized dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: What if you omit the pepper flakes?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Should I include all the leaves on my broccoli rabe, or does “trim” mean to remove them?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Eat the leaves, trim the bottom of each stem.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: like liv, i can almost never find broccoli rabe (or broccolini.)  i'm wondering if anyone has tried the recipe with regular broccoli?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @gigi Broccolini might be better, since it’s a smaller diameter, with a long stalk. May have to cut in half to get No.2 pencil size diameter. You won’t get the bitter flavor of rapini, but it should still tasty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Doesn't anyone like to add anchovies too?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The 1 - 2 teaspoons of crushed red pepper is too much unless you want it hot. Next time would use 1/2 teaspoon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I make this regularly though I use anchovies and a little sizzled tomato paste to get the umami. I also find it easier to take an immersion blender to the pot after adding some of the chopped broccoli rabe, which infuses the pasta with more flavor. It goes wonderfully well with a good quality smoked mozzarella.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Superb as is. Give it tray, even if you're not vegan!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made the recipe as written with the exception of boiling the pasta until 2 minutes from al dente and then added in the rabe, as suggested by another. I halved the recipe for 2 diners but messed up a bit by adding a full 1 tsp of crushed pepper, rather than 1/2. It was too spicy and I can do spicy. Overall, this dish was very underwhelming in flavour. My husband didn't like the lemon zest..he got a clump. And, this is NOT week night friendly. Clean up wasn't fun. I will not be making it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Cooked as written, we loved it. Ate the last bit of leftovers (not quite enough for two) for breakfast tossed with one air-fried russet potato, olive oil scrambled eggs, and of course the squeeze of lemon wedge and Romano cheese. Just as great as the first time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Amazing recipe, pretty simple to make when you're tired, tasted professional even for an amateur

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Love this recipe and strongly recommend it. It's amazing with soy sauce in either the sauce or on top. 

(Fyi it did leave the stovetop looking like a war zone, but that's bc I'm not careful😅)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The miso’s essential to this, IMO (and, yes, vegan). To save on cleanup, I skipped the blender step, just mixing the miso and rabe with the sautéed onions and garlic once they’d cooked down. I didn’t miss the sauce, though my spouse thinks it might be better that way.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Needs 2 tablespoons of miso rather than 3 in my opinion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Broccoli Rabe Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Very good! We used an immersion blender in the pan. Still a ton of dishes. Definitely want to keep the broccoli pieces bigger next time and add more. Miso and red pepper are great!

recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: These things are dangerous!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: @John They sure sound delicious! Can’t wait to do a trial run this week before Thanksgiving.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: These look amazing. Adding them to my Christmas menu!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: I made these exactly as written. They were wonderful. Do be sure to pipe them pretty thick so that you get a good helping of the soft, pillowy mixture beneath the crispy edges. The first couple I did were a bit on the small side. These fed far more people than you might expect from three potatoes, they are so pretty and indulgent each person only really needed three or four swirls. A great pay off in terms of taste and presentation! Of course it helps if you have a ricer and the piping tools.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: These look amazing! My granddaughter is allergic to eggs is there anything you could substitute? Thanks.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: Haven’t tried substituting in this particular recipe, but I would start by trying a “chia egg” substitute- for every egg in the recipe, mix 1T ground chia seeds with 3T water in a separate bowl. Let stand for 5-10 minutes until it turns into a gel. Mix well and add to recipe when it calls for eggs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody:
@Mom of kid who has recently outgrown an egg-allergy!
Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: @Catherine the egg is just to bind the potato so you could make a small amount of roux (equal quantities of butter and flour, cooked until thickened) and mix that in instead of

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: Could the Duchess potatoes be prepared thru the eggs addition and then frozen? I have potatoes that need to be processed and I thought it would make day of much easier in the flurry of holiday entertaining.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: I made these for Thanksgiving, refrigerated them and browned at the last minute. I also froze several (not browned) and they have been just as delicious as the others.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: We made this Duchess Potatoes recipe ahead for Thanksgiving dinner, and browned at the last minute. It is beautiful on the plate, wonderfully smooth and delicious! I may make it again for Christmas!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Duchess Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: I’m going to try making these in advance for Christmas by forming them on a sheet pan in rounds and freezing. They should only require some extra time in the oven. I can use only what I need and have the rest for other meals. After freezing they should bag up nicely.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Emulsifying the feta is trivial with an immersion blender. Use a narrow vessel just wider than the blender head (e.g., a Mason jar or the beaker that might have come with the blender), push the head down so that it's completely immersed in the ingredients before you begin, and then move the head up and down a bit as you blend to catch all the cheese. Equally effective with yogurt if, like me, you despise mayo!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Su- the immersion blender is genius! Thanks for this tip. And I finally had a use for the tall beaker it came with! That thing has been rattling around since I got the blender as a wedding gift 20 years ago.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Su or use your emersion blender to make your own mayo. ;-)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Su came to the comments to see if someone had suggested yogurt over mayo and got an excellent tip to boot. Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe 3 times due to an excess of lettuce in my garden - not iceberg, but something very similar called "Krispi".   I agree with others that the dressing was a bit bland.  The second 2 times.......and this might get me kicked out of the forum......I sheepishly admit I used all the same liquid ingredients (mayo, water, etc.)....but subbed the seasonings and herbs with commercial Hidden Valley Ranch powder.  (Insert game-show loser music).   In my defense I was cooking for kids.......

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Janet H. I added capers

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Janet H. Whatever works for kids or picky adult eaters.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: I added a small grated garlic clove to the dressing and about 1/8 cup more water. Served it along side hanger steak and it was the perfect accompaniment!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Think: green flecks not green puree. 

What does that mean exactly? I was going to use a hand blender instead of a normal blender because it seems more practical, but then I read that remark and didn't know what to do with it.

Also, would some thinly sliced red onions be a good addition?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Think of visible tiny pieces of herbs rather than completely imulsified. 
Easy with an immersion blender - which is also easier to clean than,  say a Vitamix in which it's easier to over blend.
Of course you can add anything that tickles your fancy. Including red onion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Thinly sliced red onions are always a good addition!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night and it was well received, both the wedge and the dressing. The lemony cucumbers are a delight. Easy recipe if you have a heavy duty blender and a mandoline. Personally, I think the feta ranch needed a bit more of a punch but that's just me.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Kate I agree it needed a bit more bunch, I swapped out some of the water for lemon juice and then added lots of pepper and that helped!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Adding creme fraiche , mayo and freshly squeezed lemon juice (instead of water) to the feta would make it more tasty

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I have a cheap, small ninja blender, and I found emulsifying the feta to be no problem whatsoever. It was a feta block soaking in brine, so perhaps a drier feta is more difficult. The dressing is good with lots of pepper! 

I also rested some thin slices of radish with the cucumber in the lemon juice before serving.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: The dressing in this is crazy delicious. I don't care much for ranch, but this dressing is clutch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: I assume you could use fresh mint rather than dried? I have both peppermint and spearmint in my garden right now.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Truly 5 star recipe! Easy to make and gobbled up by everyone. I used dried dill and it was still delicious.  Make the dressing ahead of time so the flavors can meld.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight over long full pieces of romain lettuce, added some extra lemon 🍋  to the dressing and Raman spice on the top because i was missing sumac. It was delish along side a Dungeness crab dinner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: I’ve begun slicing rather than wedging iceberg. You don’t get nice even-sized pieces, but it's a lot easier to eat. And the dressing stays put.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Waste not want not. Strip all the dill off its stems, chop by hand or in a food processor, pack lightly into a container and freeze. Scoop out chunks as needed and add to recipes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: It was ok. Full disclosure, I've never had a wedge salad before this so I wasn't 100% sure what the goal should be. I liked that I could plate up custom made salads for spouse and I, though - lots of red onion for them, peperoncini for me. But overall it felt lacking in some richness or umami. Like other reviewers, I replaced some of the water with lemon, and also I used fresh parsley plus dried dill and mint. Still just kinda meh. Breadcrumbs maybe? Some anchovies?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Second the immersion blender. I had an extra head of lettuce, limes, and cotija cheese from taco night and used them!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is so good, and very fun to pull out of the fridge for leftovers.  Next time I'll add some other veggies in with the cucumbers (I'm thinking thinly sliced cherry tomatoes and carrot ribbons made with a veggie peeler).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: How can I make this with charred cabbage?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Want to put away dill? Make a dill pesto and freeze it.

commentBody: @Tom Hayden I make dill shallot butter, rewrapped in the butter papers to freeze

@Laurence

commentBody: I don't see any "cooking" ☺

author: Alexa Weibel

commentBody: Something I do when I get a big bunch of dill is to make ( and freeze) dill/shallot butter. Soften a pound of butter- use quarters so that you can roll the finished butter in the wrappers ( I feel very clever doing that!)- and mix

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: And mix in chopped dill and chopped shallots( or dried chopped shallot ) and flaky salt. Roll into logs and freeze. So easy to slice off some while frozen to add to veg, rice,potatoes,etc.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Is there a good substitute for feta, which I’ve always had difficulty digesting, not the case with other cheeses.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Needed a lot of help to make good, which is fine.  More lemon, pepper, herbs, etc.  and more liquid ( I chose to add milk and lemon juice rather than more water).  It was fine.  Not great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: I agree with other comments that the dressing was slightly bland as written.  But it was delicious after I added 2-3 tablespoons of buttermilk and the juice of half a lemon.  This was slightly healthier than the typical blue cheese - bacon wedge salad, but still went over well with my family.  Served it with a roasted beef tenderloin for a vaguely steakhouse inspired Sunday dinner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: I found it too salty and would use less feta next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Looks delicious.  But here in the US it is hard to find iceberg lettuce!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Emulsion blender is definitely the way to go! Also, through an error at the grocery store where I bought marinated feta cubes, it turned out to be a serendipitous was mistake. I weighed the feta and found that a 9.5oz jar is 6oz of feta. So, buy a jar of marinated feta, strain and save the oil. Use 3tbsp of the oil and the drained feta for the dressing. Voila extra flavor! The lemon-cucumber is tasty. A delicious wedge salad!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: I used a food processor, crumbled feta (it’s what I had), extra dill, lemon juice, a tsp of honey and a bit less olive oil for the dressing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Used leftover dressing from this (which solidified a bit in the fridge) with smoked trout on crackers for breakfast. I would make it again for this purpose alone.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was a beautiful presentation and a special way to eat salad. The teenagers were charmed. But I didn't love the texture of the dressing, the feta cheese creating that dry feta grit in the mouth even after the recommended time in the Vitamix. I would have preferred it with green goddess, following all the other instructions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: Love how easy the salad comes together.  Next time I will try either a different feta based dressing or other creamy dressing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Feta-Ranch Wedge Salad Recipe
commentBody: We gave this some Main Dish Energy by topping it with roasted cannellini beans (cooked in the toaster oven because heatwave). My iceberg didn’t have enough of a core to cut nice wedges, but it was fine as a chopped salad instead.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Oh dear oh dear oh dear, this is upside down. Do the cold stuff first (lettuce/pico/sour cream/guacamole etc), THEN the tostada, then the hot queso/protein. Get your little gap piece of flour tortilla (about the size of a pint glass opening, you can cut it from a flour soft taco tortilla), then fold the edges over. Sear seam-sized down with the hot ingredients closest to the hot pan. This way the cold ingredients stay cold!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Pat M Brilliant technique, thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Pat M Just made these with your technique and they came out great! Thank ya!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Pat M will try that next time.   Think we will hear the tortilla first to get some crunch on the other side then use your technique.  Definitely lose much of the crunch when you follow the recipe as written.   Wish we saw this BEFORE we made it 😊

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: For this recipe or any recipe that calls for making a ground beef taco filling, do yourself a favor and replace the ground beef with finely diced or shredded slow-roasted pork shoulder. Ups your game considerably. Use your favorite slow roasted pork shoulder method for dinner some night and freeze the leftovers in one-pound portions and you'll always have some on hand for a recipe like this. Magical!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
@Chris in Redlands
Thanks so much. Made a big difference.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Chris in Redlands shredded brisket or chicken also work well. Key is to sear the shredded meat first to add to the texture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I've been making these for years.  I like to add a thin layer of crushed black beans to the basic recipe.   I've also made these with nothing but refried pinto beans, cheese and lots of pickled jalapenos.  I'm contemplating making one with sardines which I'm sure no one would eat except me.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Mary from Mississippi I’ll sit next to you for one with sardines!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I’ve not made this specific recipe but I have made crunch wraps at home. If you’re worried about flipping them bake them- seamed side down on an oiled sheet tray and brush the tops with oil. Bake 375° till brownish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Homemade crunchwraps are one of my at home budget cooking staples. Tortilla chips can also be used for crunch in place of the tostada.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: This works, but try to make the finished wrap as thin as possible.  If it's too thick, then the sides won't get enough contact with the pan, during the frying phase, to get crisp.  And that crispness is essential!  The crunch from the fried exterior, plus the gentler crunch from the interior tostada, offsets the softer fillings and is what makes this more exciting than a quesadilla!  PS you can use shredded cheddar instead of queso, though it won't be as Taco Bell-ish, which IMHO is a positive.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: We use a cast iron steak weight to make the wrap thin.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
I almost never post online- but these are worth it. Lots of great recipes here at NYT but someone in my house is usually unimpressed. These were a huge exception- Everyone- even my super picky teen LOVED them. 

Followed the recipe almost exactly and they were a hit (only swap was Heinz chili sauce instead of adobo in the sour cream).

The extra large tortillas I didn’t even know the store carried solved my assembly worries. 

Made my own queso with sharp cheddar, whole milk, butter and a little bit of Colby Jack- made it in the microwave- super easy and the perfect compliment.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Steve - my local cash and carry (US Foods in Oakland) has enormous burrito size tortillas 20”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Don't tear up your tortillas! Get a stack of six-inch tortillas (the same size as your tostadas) and make it your top layer. Then when you fold the big around the whole thing (sort of like a flat dumpling), you pick it up *very carefully* and put the "pinwheel" side down in the pan first to sear it. It will all stick together and then you can flip it easily to sear the other side. (This is not a theoretical solution - this is how I actually do it. We've hosted crunchwrap parties and this works!)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
I have been making something like this for years.  I use a taco meat recipe from cooks illustrated that is great.  I put it together slightly differently, I put the tostada on the large tortilla first, then the meat,then the toppings (the toppings depending on what I have available).  Then I put a 6” tortilla on top and make the fold.   When frying it you have to be really careful,  it is easy to burn them.  I admit to using a bit more oil than most recipes call for so I get a little bit of a deep fried effect.
My kids love them, I can play with the fillings forever, and if you are cooking for a crowd you can keep them warm in oven uncut until you’re ready to serve.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: How do l flip the thing over into the skillet without it falling apart?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Carefully!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Vol it’ll crisp up and hold its shape. That’s why you put it seam side down first. It’ll essentially seal the parts that could fall apart and leak…

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Vol. I make one slit toward the middle place the ingredients in the opposite section on the same side flip that  section on to the next and then flip to the last. Sear in a pan. There are videos online how to do this. The trick is finding large enough tortillas to accomodate the fillings.  I have to break up the tostado.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Easy and tasty. Used ground turkey instead of beef, no onions, tortilla chips in place of tostadas. Rubbed a little olive oil on both sides and grilled in George Foreman grill.  Stayed together perfectly and easy cleanup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: The recipe suggests you assemble the wrap and then heat the pan for grilling it. Definitely have the pan with oil heated and ready so you can immediately grill the wrap before the lettuce wilts and the tortilla gets soggy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: What is a tostada shell

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Arlene I have that question, also. A tostada is something completely different in my lexicon. I am guessing that here it means a small fried corn tortilla, since people are talking about crunch and suggesting substituting tortilla chips. Maybe another commenter who has the answer will chime in.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
@Arlene

It’s a crispy taco shell that is completely flat. Usually they can be found in the same isle as the tortillas

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
@Arlene
I couldn’t find the tostada shells so I just broke taco shells in half.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: If you're concerned about the filling falling out when you try to flip it, you might want to put the tostada shell on top when you're assembling and then pleat/fold the outer tortilla over it. No hole in the middle that way.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: If you have a panini maker that converts to flat plates, this is the time to get it out and dust it off. Lightly brush both sides of the crunch wrap with oil and let 'er rip!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Instead of ground beef, I use a equal volume of rehydrated TVP.  Makes a much more healthful meal, and tastes practically the same!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Addition to step 5: Sprinkle some shredded cheese into a non-stick skillet (I didn't use oil and it was fine) then place the wrap seam side down and cook until the cheese is crispy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I roll it like a burrito. Much easier and tastes the same!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and very fun! Obligatory spoiler alert that I changed something - I actually used freshly sliced cabbage (which I prefer in tacos) and it was delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Clever! I'm a little bit jealous that I didn't think about this myself. Will be trying this next time!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: My kids describe these as fantastic and helped cook and assemble the wraps.   They're tasty, a bit spicy and very adaptable to what you have on hand. We used plain Greek yogurt instead of the sour cream, romaine lettuce instead of iceberg and salsa instead of Pico de Gallo.  The NYT queso was superb and the only thing we added were Hatch green chilis.  We baked the wraps at 375 as was suggested by another commentor for about 10 minutes.  These will be a regular dish at our house.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Jean nice idea to involve the family.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I also had trouble with flipping it to get it seam-side down. It helped if I didn't overstuff it. Tasty, even if it did fall partly apart.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: YouTube has several assembly videos.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Made it with plant-based ground meat (Impossible Beef), simply perfect! Never had Taco Bell before but this recipe is San Diegan approved!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: So fun to make these! A small scrap of flour tortilla may be all you need to cover the hole. I use shredded chicken thighs instead of ground beef.  I seasoned with taco seasoning and shredded with immersion blender right in the pan. Has anyone experimented on placement of the tostada to maintain crispiness?  It’s sort of breaks my heart when it doesn’t crunch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Click on the "Featured In" link at the top of this recipe for a photo of how the tortilla is folded.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: If you're trying to get that "authentic Taco Bell taste," you'll need to add a little flour and water (1/4 cup each) to the ground beef as it cooks. Otherwise you'll enjoy the flavor but the meat texture will be different from a real crunch wrap's. Probably not as big of a deal for most of the readers here as it is to me, but I grew up poor and ate a lot of Taco Bell and like getting as close to their recipe as I can for nostaglia's sake.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Fun, easy weeknight dinner. My teenagers loved it (so did I).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Excellent! I used a half can of chipotle pepper left from another recipe and extra lean ground beef   regular taco seasoning. My tiny grocery didn’t have tostadas, so I bought hard taco shells and broke them in half. Worked perfectly! I also followed two reader tips: put the cold stuff down first, and use your tostada/hard shell to cut a circle with another tortilla to lay on top.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe this weekend and it was fantastic! I made the queso and pico homemade and I think the homemade ingredients really elevated this recipe. I also used Honduran Crema instead of sour cream which made this also added a fun twist to the sour cream flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Honduran crema is the best!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: LOVED THIS. We used Cholula hot sauce in the sour cream (which we had on hand and saved us from buying a can of adobos) and that’s a fantastic sub offered in the recipe! The tortilla small circle tip was genius as well - we had no spillage. I’d love to make small ones for a party platter with chicken, veggies, meat alternatives - I think they’d be deliciously adorable.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: This sounds amazing even though I can’t picture how to fold it and I don’t know what quest nor tostado is (not in the US) A video for this would be epic, gonna sub quest with taco sauce and grated cheese and then taco shell crushed inside for crunch. Would this do?? Video please

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @GemHallJan in Tex-mex parlance, queso is a spicy cheese dip or sauce. There are lots of good videos on YouTube if you look up “folding a Crunchwrap supreme”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I figured this out nearly a decade ago, I even found a way to hack the taste and consistency of Taco Bell ground beef.  This recipe by itself is the reason my two children, now adults, hold me in high esteem to this very day.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @David in Baltimore would you please share your meat recipe? Thanks

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Used Tangy cheese Doritos instead of tostado **chef's kiss**

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Why on earth would you add oil to ground beef for cooking?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
@Sharon
To keep from sticking. Not all ground beef is high fat. Less than a tablespoon of oil is all you need. Use butter if you prefer which will help with browning the meat

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Never having had any kind of crunchwrap, this was a new experience.  Followed the recipe for the most part but substituted lightly pickled red cabbage for lettuce.  Got lots of positive feedback. Will def do again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried a panini maker for this? Cook both sides at once?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Added a healthy shmear of refried beans before the beef.  Did everything else as is except used Toufayan gluten-free restaurant-sized wraps because celiac (which, incidentally, are excellent)!  The only thing I did not love was wilted iceberg.  My question is this:  having grown up in a Velveeta-phobic household, and considering that this recipe is supposed to outshine Taco Bell, why not use a genuine mild or medium excellent cheddar for the queso?  On Wisconsin!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I can’t visualize how this comes together. Wish there was a video.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Wow.... this was so delicious I made it two nights in a row. I might try adding some beans to it next time, but the recipe is perfect as it's written.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Saw the NYT article and viewed some YouTube videos and gave it a whirl.  For the crunch tostado I bought a pack of "street taco" corn tacos, dabbed a little olive oil on them and toasted them on a sheet pan at 400 degrees, 5 minutes per side.  They came out great!  Did the assembly as described and kept them warm in the oven on the sheet pan used to make the tostados.  Only shortcoming was the tostados didn't stay crunchy in the wrap.  Still, pretty good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Any tips for baking an hour or so ahead of time and keeping them warm for a party?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: The largest tortillas we could find left a gap but instead of patching with a torn piece I topped the filling with another tostada. More crunch and no waste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Total stoner food. Loved it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I wish there was a vvideo of this!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Holy mother of god, this is great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
Of everything I've cooked for him, including many dishes that took much more effort than this..."those crunch wraps" are my husband's go-to suggestion of what I cook well 💀. 

This is easy & popular in my house (which is full of young kids who complain about most meals). If you don't stuff the tortillas with a ton of meat this can stretch to make five or six satisfying servings, but it's easy to double. 

As unnamed husband says: "It's the joy of Taco Bell without the next-day regret!"

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: This looks easy to make vegan or vegetarian. Use something like Impossible “meat,” and if you’re vegan make vegan queso (lots of recipes out there) and buy or make vegan sour cream. I am not vegan but cook for one so will be trying this! I might use Field Roast chipotle sausage instead of à ground beef substitute though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: In the recipe photo the crunch wrap is open on one side. Is that how it’s wrapped? With one side left open? Or is it wrapped up completely and then  sliced in half for the photo? I would love to see a video accompany this recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Anne the photo is showing them cut in half

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: What does tostada mean in this context?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: A large,round, flat, fried corn chip, essentially.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I used what was in the house and that happened to be ground pork, spinach, delicious Matteo's salsa, tortilla chips and corn tortillas.  I did have sour cream and used Sriracha as my hot sauce.  I used shredded Mexican blend cheese and should have used more.  I warmed the tortillas in the pan first, both sides, then built them in the pan (I didn't have large burrito wrappers) and placed a weighted press on top, that helped flatten and crisp things up as well as melt.  No trouble flipping-tasty!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Pro tip: if you have a panini press, it’s perfect for this! No flipping required and therefore less of a risk of the stuffing falling out.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
@Amelia
Did the tostada come out crispy after using the panini press?  How long did you press it, and what temperature did you use?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Fun! I’m going to double up on the tostadas next time because the second half of my wrap was soggy by the time I got to it. Otherwise it’s great as written — one tiny variation, I threw some pinto beans into the Impossible beef because I love beans. Recommended!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: This was such a delicious and fun treat! I don’t like fast food so have never actually tried the Taco Bell version but I loved this knock-off! I used Impossible plant-based meat and you really couldn’t even tell the difference. I did have to use tongs to tilt the package in order to get it golden on the sides.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Do not try this for a crowd.  By the time you have them all assembled and fried, it has lost its crunch. This is a make one at a time and fry and eat it quickly sort of recipe. Otherwise, it is delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: How do you fold it? Not clear from directions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Could someone please be more specific about the "tostada shell" ingredient?  Is it one of those corn tortillas cooked flat and crispy, and sold pre-made in a plastic bag? If not, please specify.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Yes, that is the tostada shell ingredient, sold in the plastic bag.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I know it’s a dumb question, but what does it mean to add a tostada on top of the other mixture? Isn’t a tostada normally a kind of salad on or in a shell?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Karen, the word tostada is used differently than an assembled tostada salad. It’s the hard crispy pre-fried flat corn tortilla. Very much like a hard taco shell except it is flat. The crispiness is what is needed here, and if you can’t find them in your Mexican food section of your grocery, you can substitute a few crispy tortilla chips…

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I’m so confused on the tostado part - can someone please clarify? I don’t see a tostado in the picture at all - it looks like a folded up tortilla/quesadilla? Also not sure how one would fold a tortilla over a tostada, it would need to be a massive tortilla! I realize the recipe calls for large 10” tortillas, but still. I saw a comment that says you can use tortilla chips instead of the tostada (to add crunch) which makes more sense to me… but would love to better understand the tostada element. Thanks!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Chelsey I’m having a hard time spotting tostadas in the photo accompanying the recipe, too, but it’s an essential element for that delightful crunchy texture (think potato chips on a sandwich). You can also use tortilla chips. If using a tostadas, try to find the extra large burrito tortillas (10-inches is a bit too small).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Roger thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: It's in between the ground beef and the lettuce. The tostadas are about 5 inches wide, so half the diameter of the tortilla. You put it right in the center, and when you fold up the sides of the tortilla to encase everything, the resulting package is about the diameter of the tostada.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Yum!  I had heard of this recipe but never tried it it was easy to assemble and delicious. I’m going to try with carnitas as one commentator suggested and add guacamole next time. What a fun Friday night treat!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Come on. This is so fun. So great. Followed to a T and loved.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: An easy, delicious, flexible recipe. I cook mine using our panini press, which eliminates the need for flipping them over. They seal perfectly every time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. But the tostada sogged out on us.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Tortilla chips hold up a little bit better. They'll add salt so I put very little in the meat mixture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: These are good. But I would not use jarred queso next time. It’s pretty awful. Shredded sliced Colby or Monterey would be best.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
Never thought I’d say “great idea Taco Bell”

You can vary the fillings with anything.  Chicken, pork, beans, etc.  very popular when custom made.!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Spot on. New family favorite. Used just one big tortilla and it was a tough fold but it worked!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Made this and it was great!!! I will say you can’t really batch these for the week since the lettuce is fresh and the sour cream will curdle but it was delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
Made as directed except I skipped the onion (dietary restriction) and took the tip of using tortilla chips in lieu of tostadas.  Warm the tortilla in a warm pan, makes it very pliable. 
College kid agrees that this is a good approximation of the fast food original!  It made 6 wraps.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
I have never eaten any kind of fast food crunchwrap so I don’t have anything to compare it to. These were delicious! They were a little time consuming for me because I made pico de gallo and queso, plus I had to fry my own tostadas. I would’ve preferred to buy them, but for some reason could not find them. 
Assembly and pan frying was easy and they made a delicious meal!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I recommend you add 20 pounds extra of ingredients to make it last a long time and make sure you char the crust of the Crunchwrap it tastes amazing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: this was ok as something different.  way too rich for my preference and you could easily make these smaller.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I cooked these using half the ground beef and the recipe made 5, with the 10 inch tortilla wrap covering completely, without gaps.  My only disappointment was that by the time i finished cooking the last one, the tostada part lost its crunchiness but they were nonetheless delicious.  Next time I will cook them simultaneously on my large griddle and see if that preserves the crunch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Subbed cole slaw and guac for the lettuce, pico and sour cream.   I didn't have the jumbo tortillas so I used a corn tortilla to cover the hole and put refried beans around the rim of the flour tortilla to help the it stick to the corn tortilla when I folded over the edges.  It surprisingly worked out like a dream and my family loved it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Use iceberg lettuce as a wrap, filled with all the ingredients. Voilà, keto-friendly.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
This was my go-to comfort meal during covid, but I made it full size with 4 large tortillas and 2 small ones in a cast iron skillet, with a variety of ingredients depending on what the fridge and pantry yielded. 
My favorite was leftover fajita filling with zucchini ribbons and shrimp. I’d lay large tortillas around the edge. A small one on the bottom to close the hole, dump in the pre-seasoned filling of choice (often just ground beef or shredded rotisserie chicken with beans, onions and grated cheese), top with the other small tortilla and fold over the sides to close. I sprayed with oil, but it isn’t necessary. Bake 40” at 350*F then turn out (upside down) on a platter. Slice in pie wedges. Less fussy, though less portable too. I was into easy, I wasn’t entertaining in the “pandemic times.” 
This recipe is GREAT for its portable, individual servings. Duh. It never occurred to me!! (I don’t do fast food joints. Apparently, I should try them occasionally for the culinary inspiration, right?!)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Huge hit in our house! Kids loved the idea of a Taco Bell “dupe” even though they’ve never had Taco Bell. Fun to make and fun to eat!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
Really liked this. Good change from same old tacos or tostadas. Next time I work use old El Paso taco mix for the ground beef. Also would not fill the crunch wraps too full. Made pico de gallo from NY times recipe. Loved the sour cream w/ adobo and store bought queso. Definitely put in our rotation.
Used Ole crunchy tostadas found in Mexican section. Heated them up first

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: @Pat M this is exactly what I thought when I was making it! Why grill the cold side? A little is fine to crisp the tortilla but if the hot stuff is on the seam side then you can really get it nicely sealed and crispy without heating the lettuce and pico too much. You can even put a layer of cheese on the seam side to glue it a bit.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: wow!  I've never had a crunch wrap from that place, but I bet these are 100% better!! added shredded Mexican blend cheese instead of the queso and crisped up a couple of flour tortillas to use for the "crunch", as I couldn't find the tostada's in the market.  Would definitely make these again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I thought these were just ok. They get kind of saucy in the middle with the queso and sour cream and feel heavy. The folding and griddling was super satisfying, though I definitely needed an extra tortilla patch to close the gap even with 10 in tortillas.  I think two tostadas would add crunch and improve the experience. I could see making these again with a lighter filling (chicken and tomatillo with cojita?), though I fully understand that health is not the point of these.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: We found the extra large, burrito sized tortillas and these folded, sealed and pan fried with ease. I think you will have issues with smaller tortillas. We followed the recipe exactly and this really does have the Taco Bell aura when using the jarred queso and sour cream fillings. It was a rich and tasty novelty to try, but  a bit too fatty for a regularly occurring meal in our house. Whatever you do, don't click on the nutrition info until after you eat every last morsel. Have fun with this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: This was a starting point for a great last minute meal. Sautéed some veg chick'n pieces with lots of spices, sautéed thin sliced onions, corn, leftover rice, and enough canned diced tomatoes to hold it together. Piled it into a large tortilla with veg cheese and (in one) lettuce. They held together in the pan and while eating. Great idea with so many possible cruelty-free iterations.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: We loved this! I was worried I was burning it when I put it in the pan, but no, the browned tortilla made it delicious! We decided to use shredded cheese next time...we love queso, but it was too heavy for us!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
worked out well. if you really like queso and sour cream, this is perfect. I personally prefer it with a lot less of both. instead of hot sauce I served it with salsa fresca, which went really well with it.
This was my dirty sin for the week, Monday I start watching my cholesterol again - which, by the way, I always imagined exactly like queso. do what you want with that information ;D
Happy Sunday!

commentBody:
So easy and good!! You can get by with a regular size burrito and half a corn taco shell. I find using a tbsp to pack ingredients together in the shape of the shell helpful: 3tbsp pico, shredded cabbage on top, slathered-side-down taco shell to clamp those together, then 2-3tbsp meat, shred cheddar on top and fold. 
Using chicken, cabbage, and greek yogurt in place of beef, iceberg, and sour cream was just as good imo!

recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t use any extra oil, let the beef start cooking and rendering fat before I added the onions and left a bit of grease in the pan to cook the tortillas. I followed another comment and put the cold ingredients down first, then the meat and so used 1/4 cheddar 3/4 mozzarella on top. I ripped one tortilla into quarters and used those scraps to cover the middle of the folded edge. Even the one that unfolded when I put it in the pan held together better than I had expected. Delicious and simple, I ate two leaning over the counter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
My tostada shells didn't stay crunchy so I need to find a solution - we used Ortega, didn't work. 
This recipe is definitely missing a cheese sauce (I made some).
Also, I noticed that the top "most helpful" comment says that the whole thing is built upside down - untrue. The extra layers of wrap keep the cold ingredients - placed on top, i.e. last, cooler. If you put the lettuce down first, it heats up FAST through that one thin layer of wrap. Don't do that, trust the process!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Our family is obsessed with this recipe—we’ve made it 5 or 6 times just this month! I prep all the ingredients, and everyone assembles their own before we brown them on the stove. The only issue is the tortillas never seem to close all the way… we might be overstuffing them.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I don't think you are overstuffing them - I think it is just their nature. Watched a video that fixed your problem, tried it and it worked! Take another flour tortilla (smaller, if you have a few on hand), and trim it to about match the size and shape of the crispy tostada. Lay it on top of the fillings, gently press down, and proceed to wrap with the burrito-sized flour tortilla according to directions. The smaller tortilla acts to cover the gap the middle.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
To make assembly and flipping easier without everything spilling out:
1. Use 10 inch burrito tortillas for the base
2. Use 6 to 8 inch soft taco or fajita tortillas for the top.
3. Wet your fingers with some water and apply to the entire outside edge of the burrito tortilla
4. Fold the tortilla in an octagon, grab the top edge and fold it down over the top. Press the tortillas together with wet fingers to glue them together. Then go clockwise folding the diagonal next. Then the right side, etc, until you finish all 8 sides.
5. Use a tiny amount of extra water to help glue the tortillas together as needed. 
6. Fry the top side first with seams facing down to seal it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Love all the gourmet options, but just here to say that these were also perfect with sour cream with Pace and Kroger taco mix.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody:
Adding extra tortilla helped and then sealing it in with cshredded cheese.

Replaced queso with shredded cheese

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Used a lot of reviewer suggestions and turned out to be a huge hit. Added layer of homemade mashed pinto beans, used cheddar shreds instead of queso, and served with guacamole on the side. For those who have access to Casa Sanchez products, I used the "thick and crispy" chips and they stayed crunchy until the very last bite. Also found really huge tortillas and no issues sealing everything in.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried freezing these? Seems like they’d lose their crunch in the freezer.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: We made this as best we could with what we had on hand and they were amazing. Used beyond burger with diced onion and poblano. Skipped lettuce (didn't have any) and made a very crude pico out of half a can of diced tomatoes mixed with diced onion & jalapeno, fresh parsley, and white wine vinegar (for acidity instead of lime). Subbed in tortilla chips for the crunch, and with the adobo sour cream and some homemade queso, these were excellent.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: In case anyone is wondering (doubtful, but still…) I made these with some ground venison that was languishing in my chest freezer and they were delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crunchy Queso Wrap Recipe
commentBody: I used this recipe as inspo for a fridge cleanout; my fam LOVED the result. Taco seasoning from TJs saved me lots of legwork with the ground beef. I also followed Pat M's tip to layer the cold things first: SMART. I oiled, salted, and baked corn tortillas to crisp them up since I didn't have tostadas, and that worked well. Next time, I'll try crumbling tortilla chips as a layer instead. I found the tostada to be hard to chew through, even after crisping. That's the only reason for 4 stars.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Tried this for the 2nd time this week (yes, it IS that good!) only I drained it even more and used convection heat instead of regular oven. DON'T do either! It was actually*too*crispy! A little more moisture in some pieces, d/t the uneveness of bake for different-sized tofu bits, is one of the things our family liked. I also simplified further by mixing the tom paste & oil in a large bowl, then added added the roasted tofu to bowl, for even coverage, before returning to the oven. Much easier, since the tomato goop doesn't really "drizzle."

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Idoknowbeans yup same problem. Note to self: no convection!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Idoknowbeans thank you for this suggestion!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
@Idoknowbeans yup. Thank you for this comment! I did convection first and was confused why ppl loved it so much. 

I’ll also second your tip on the draining: a tofu press is NOT your friend. Although I bet if you also  cut the cook time in half you could get similar results.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Oh la la!  So easy and so delicious.  Thank you for giving me a go-to midweek meal to make.  Followed directions except: I tossed the tofu in a bowl with initial ingredients.  The tofu came out so crispy.  Even my meat eating husband said it was superb.  Plan to make this ahead and re-crisp in the oven as needed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I quick pickled the onion and radishes and you should too!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Kyra great idea! Or pickup a jar from Trader Joe’s or Costco

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This is a delicious recipe. I was able to halve it easily. I didn’t have tomato paste so I subbed ketchup. I love crispy corn tacos and my husband loves flour tortillas, so I was able to use both! I added shredded lettuce, just because.  Enjoy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: For the record, I don't cook with, or even eat, tofu! The only other tofu entree I ever made was adding a can of Manwich to frozen tofu crumbles (pretty good, btw). Followed this great recipe to.the.letter, plus advice to mix the initial oil & seasonings in a bowl with the drained & crumbled tofu before putting on the sheet pan. I felt there was a tad too much oil in the tomato paste goop - the tofu was far crispier before adding that - and will reduce to 2 TBSP next time. Thanks for the YUM!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: WOW! For the tofu-averse, this is a total winner! I took the advice of others and ended up mixing the tomato paste, soy sauce and olive oil and adding it to the shredded tofu, and then tossed that into the bowl of seasoning and then baking it. Super flavorful and super crispy! My tacos were amazing. I'm so glad to have a crowd-pleasing meatless dish that I look forward to having again. Thanks, Alexa Weibel!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
I’ve done something similar to this recipe for years. I do a version w/ Asian flavors for lettuce wraps and Mexican flavors for tacos & salad.

First- grate your tofu in a large bowl and add your seasonings and oil/soy straight into the tofu & mix. Use parchment paper on your baking sheet. Less mess!

Other tips - get a tofu press (I have tofuture)!! It makes SUCH a difference. Use frozen tofu for a meatier texture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
Yes! Grated tofu and oil/ seasonings into one bowl. 

After making this last night, next time I would add the tomato paste at the same time and just watch the tofu as it bakes and crisps. 

I had used extra firm tofu and the baking time was half that indicated in the recipe.  Next time I will bake less, for firm but not too crispy tofu. Maybe it was the extra firm tofu,  but it got a bit too crispy for me.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @CM I found grating was too small as well. Next time I will rough chop to chickpea size and trim 5-7 mins from first round of cooking. Delicious though!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Juliana, what Asian seasonings do you use for the lettuce wraps? Sounds tasty!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Now is the kind of TACO I like. It was so delicious I ate one pan all by myself. The avocado cream is fabulous all on its own and I'll use it for other things.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: In 45 years I’ve never had tofu and wanted to try incorporating more meatless meals into our diet. The rave reviews and lots of seasonings intrigued me and OMG this is one of the best recipes I’ve ever made and best tacos I’ve ever had. I can’t get over just how good this was. I did t have onion powder but followed it otherwise to the T. I have to say tho that it started burning and crackling well before 20 minutes so beware time-wise. Probably would’ve burned to a crisp and set our smoke alarm into overdrive at 30 minutes. Definitely just watch and go from there. Adding this to the rotation!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Sounds yummy! I love tofu, and love tacos, so I am going to make them with low carb cheese taco shells, since I don't do carbs since my last doctor visit ;-)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Sylvia W, do you have a preferred recipe for those taco shells? Asking for another carb-restricted person (me).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Wilta Live same! Please share your low carb tortilla recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Wilta Live egglife wraps! Easy peasy

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Love the texture and flavor of the tofu -- In addition to the tomato paste I added chili crisp in that mixture. Also ended up quick pickling the red onion and adding like a half tablespoon of green chilis I had in the fridge.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
I’m making this for the 3rd time. We are a vegetarian household with large appetites. This is a new favorite. And the leftovers were great. 
First time cooking I used 1 block high-protein tofu from Trader Joe’s and one block firm. Both were grated it on the box grater. The high protein tofu was easy to grate with less moisture- so this time I used only that. Both types were good- I’ll use whatever is on hand but if I plan this in advance I’ll use the high protein style. 
I didn’t make the sauce but just mashed avocado either lime juice and packaged salsa. White corn tortillas warmed in the already-hot oven were perfect. A great departure from cheesy vegetarian tacos/quesadillas and my 1st vegan tacos!  !
I also used this method for tofu for fried rice using Chinese 5-spice for the seasoning - I didn’t add the tomato paste and didn’t crisp it. Used a splash of sesame oil - and it was very good. Maybe I’ll try a vegan shawarma next.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @sfrupe i’m going to try your rice idea – sounds great. Melissa Clark’s recipe on this site for cauliflower Shawarma is awesome. I don’t like to use quite that much oil; so I have both steamed it and roasted a it whole. If steaming, some of the spices rub off - so I add a little extra spice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Amazing recipe! If you want to skip the draining step, I suggest the traditional method of boiling the whole block of tofu for 5-7 min in salted water prior to grating it. This draws out the moisture and helps it readily soak up the spices and marinade.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: We enjoyed this! The flavor and spicing was perfect. I halved it and it was perfect for two. Sauteed some onions and peppers to go with. My crumbles were a little too small and thus a little TOO cooked. Next time I'll crumble in bigger crumbles. Will make again though!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
I made this into a salad!!  I made a simple dressing of lemon juice, pressed garlic, salt and pepper, and olive oil, tossed with finely chopped romaine. Topped with the tofu, the sauce and toppings.  I warmed a tortilla on the side. 

Amazing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Can the tofu be frozen after baking? I'm the only tofu eater, and even half the recipe is too much.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Rhani I’m the only vegetarian in my house too, so what I do is only grate half the block of tofu and store the other half in my fridge in a storage container until I need it for something else. if I ever make this just for myself I’ll probably quarter the recipe so I can use just half a block. But if you ever get curious and try freezing this, report back and let us know how it went! It’s possible that freezing would only improve the texture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I have a lot of issues with the recipe. Why 2 blocks of tofu? Halving it would make more sense unless feeding 8-10. The mixture of oil and tomato paste absolutely doesn't drizzle, so you need a different verb there. (It was really hard to incorporate that gloop). The foil is a bad idea--it sticks--parchment might work better? The cooking time is far too long. It went beyond crispy to rock-hard. I'm also not into the mix of avocado and mayo, and the red onions need pickling.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Malena I agree with just about everything you said

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic recipe! Try mixing  the tofu with the oil, soy sauce and spices before spreading evenly on the sheet pan. Add fresh diced tomatoes to the garnish for a touch of flavor and color.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Good recipe, but agree with some of the comments here- 1) crumble tofu instead of grate it, grating left it too fine and unwieldy for stuffing into tacos, 2) cooking time will vary based on your own oven so start conservatively and monitor as needed- times as written would have turned my tofu into ash, 3) tomato paste mixed with oil can’t really emulsify nor can it be drizzled so extra effort needed with that step, 4) avocado crema was great - but I substituted sour cream for mayo because I like tang.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
Absolutely delicious.  Would never expect it was tofu and at a complete loss to figure out what the taste profile is; certainly doesn't taste like tofu.  Will make this as a taco again but can see using this for a salad topping or over a fish filet.  

I used only one sheet pan and it crisped perfectly.  Don't use garlic powder, so minced fresh garlic and mixed it in with the spice mix.   Perfection.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I make these crumbles often but mix in bowl then roast to crispy mess, stirring halfway through cook time.  The crumble freezes incredibly well and is perfect for tacos, veggie/vegan bolognese, and the best stuffed peppers (just add rice and canned diced tomatoes…)!  I also add fresh crushed garlic to the mix.  Total yum.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Chale Do you freeze before or after cooking the crumbles? Thanks!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Rebecca S after! Then throw them in whatever un need them for, minestrone, chili, peppers, quesadillas…love them in stuffed cabbage, too!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: After 22 years of marriage, this recipe has renewed my wife’s love for me. It is now in regular rotation. I simplify the preparation, however, by mixing in a bowl instead of on the sheet pans. The partially cooked mixture is easy to remove from the pans when it is time to add the tomato paste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is life changing!  I just finished the last of my leftovers and I want to run out and buy ingredients for another batch asap.  The leftover tofu heats up well even in the microwave although it would be better reheated in the oven but I’m too impatient.  The avocado crema was good the next day as well - all of that lime juice helps preserve it.  My meat eating husband loved it as well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: where's the recipe for those gorgeous tortillas?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Try Almost From Scratch Tortillas by Mark Bittman on this site. Always good when I’ve made them

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: used leftover red sauce instead of tomato paste, crumbled tofu by hand instead of grating, used cumin seeds cause I didn't have powder, salt instead of soy sauce, served with sautéed chard in some crispy taco shells. 10/10! SO good omggggg

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
This was tasty. Ideal for vegetarian substitute for the usual ground meat in tacos.
