author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I wanted to like this recap but found it oddly bland and the tofu weirdly chewy. Not a fan.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I’m a vegetarian who doesn’t like tofu. NOW I DO! Fantastic recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This was not a winner for me. I found it messy, oily (even though I reduced the oil), and had a weird texture. For a vegan taco filling, I much prefer the walnut/mushroom recipe which can be googled.  Glad I gave it a try, but it won't be in my rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Delicious as written, with a little extra lime

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: WOW these are good! Don't question it. Just make them! Easy to cut the recipe in half to serve two. My husband and I devoured these.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This dinner has flavor, crunchy texture, and is healthy! I've made it a few times. Delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Wow - so good! The avocado cream was perfection. Tofu was such a tasty protein! Didn’t have radishes so subbed in sliced jalapeños and cokle slaw mix.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
This one is a keeper for sure. My husband, who’s not a tofu fan, thought the tofu tasted abit like bacon! 

I halved the recipe and got six generous tacos out of it, so I think a dozen is more accurate id the recipe is made in full. Like another reviewer, I also tossed the tofu im a bowl and added the oil, soy sauce, and spices and tossed it all together before putting it in the oven. That way, all the tofu was covered in the flavoring. 

And I didn’t bother wih the Cuisinart for the avocado crema. I just tossed the mayo, lime, zest, and avocado in a bowl and whisked the mixture hard.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I’m making this ahead to take camping for vegan taco and gluten free night with adult kid’s new partner. I tried this at home and thought this would be really great camping food and it would travel well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Super delicious (crisped up well next day as well). Just my own spice blend and a tofu press

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This was very good. Not sure it was over the top amazing, but definitely a keeper. The crema was delicious!  I tore the tofu because I thought grating it would create too fine a result. Made half recipe and got about 4 tacos out of it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: incredible recipe, this is the closest I’ve ever gotten to carnitas

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. My only change was to use one large avocado and add some Aleppo for the crema. Family devoured it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This is very good and adaptable to increasing/decreasing the quantity prepared. We look for ways to cut meat consumption and this fits the bill without feeling like a sacrifice. I've made a few times now and, honestly, I would prepare this recipe before I would prepare one with ground beef. I use shelf stable firm tofu, which means I always have all ingredients on hand in the pantry. Shelf stable tofu is not packed in a brine, which means it's ready to crumble without squeezing the water out.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I had low expectations but this was really good.  I added a little red cabbage slaw.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Made with and without tomato paste because of some comments.  We preferred with tomato paste. Only issue was some of it getting too crispy.  Will keep a closer eye on it and make sure pieces are close to same size next time. Good with avocado sauce, pickled red onion, radish, cojita and shredded lettuce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: It's hot today. Can I do this in a pan instead of roasting?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Place the firm tofu blocks on a deep dish and cover w a piece of parchment or similar then, put a heavy iron pan on top in fridge overnight to squish out extra water

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
Cooked this last night and it was delicious. Per the suggestion from another commenter, I used the cooking method from NYT recipe I Can’t Believe It’s Not Chicken (skillet). It was fantastic and fast. I suggest only cooking for about 10 minutes (stirring every two). 
I’m not a salt person, but I think the avocado mayo mix needs some salt. 
I paired this with a broccoli citrus slaw with chopped cilantro and minced jalapeno. It was great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Amazing!!! We made a second time just using guacamole and was even better. Leftovers reheat great and just as addictive. Highly recommend.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I made the avo mayo to go with my chicken tacos and it was so easy and delicious! will be making tofu tacos one day soon!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious but you can easily halve the avocado sauce and still have plenty. Would be good with some crumbling queso or similar mild cheese as well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Incredibly good. Especially with the fresh vegetables. Very easy to overcook. Also, my tomato paste doesn't drizzle- it dollops. So it was messy mixing with my hands. All worth it though and I will be eating it all week.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: These were sooo good. definitely don’t skip the avocado cream. We just mashed it instead of getting out the blender and it was great. 10/10 will make again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried freezing the avocado cream sauce?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: These were excellent. Thank you Alexa!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I made this following the recipe exactly, except for incorporating some of the tips to make prep easier and chickpeas crispier. It was wonderful! We had leftovers, so made them into a breakfast scramble. Yum!! Thank you, Alexa!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Very, very good! I made it exactly as written, and served it with shredded lettuce. I think I'll add fresh avocado slices (in addition to the crema) next time because the cool creamy texture of those will be nice next to the crispy spicy tofu bits. Those bits would also be good in a taco salad!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Will the cooked tofu hold well in the refrigerator or freezer?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I think it would be ok in the fridge. Not sure about the freezer. I'd try it out with a small quantity to make sure it defrosts well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This was really good! The fried tofu reminded me of those little fried clams you can get. This would be a great substitute for that with tartar sauce for vegetarians. I'd just wouldn't make the tofu too small.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Found this really crispy but totally bland- surprised by all the rave reviews!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Used soft tofu and broiled for the last 5 minutes it was delish

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Roasted the tofu for the minimum time. It still ended up quite crunchy with lots of charred, black bits. Crispy edges are good; burnt soy is not. Will definitely try this at a slightly lower temperature for slightly less time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I am wondering if there is any nutritional value left in the tofu after this process. Anyone know about these things?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
WELL I know its suppose to be Vegan and Vegetarian go to product for healthy protein.
Might have been in 1970's but no longer. Its a bean curd mixed with Round up. And that's not protein but poison

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Bruce Lindsay Rochester NY.    While concerns about glyphosate (Roundup) on conventional crops are valid, calling tofu 'poison' is a major exaggeration. Most tofu available in supermarkets today is certified Organic or Non-GMO Project Verified, which specifically prohibits the use of glyphosate. Even conventional soy remains a complete protein source. It’s important we don’t scare people away from a healthy, sustainable food source when organic options are affordable and widely available.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: To reduce steps, I plan to try this but to include all the oil and tomoato paste with the first step, mixing all in a bowl before spreading on the sheet pan for the oven. Maybe less perfect but worth the savings in effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Out of this world delicious. The crunch tofu is like crack. I can not stop eating it. Mine crisped up in 30 min, so I just topped with the paste mix and cooked a couple more...So good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I flattened the tofu a bit on the pan which might have been a bit much - ended up very crunchy, almost hard. But my tofu-averse daughter loved it (as did I) - will definitely tweak to suit and keep making it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Yum. Also good deconstructed on a bed of mixed greens dressed with OO and Korean red pepper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This was great, though the tomato paste at the end was too thick, some sort of unsweetened ketchup would likely be better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Can we substitute for the tomato paste?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Someone below said they used ketchup and it worked. That sounds like a decent substitute to me. Good luck! Let us know if you use it and how it turns out. I think we've all had a situation where we need some tomato paste so we don't have any.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Mine failed - burnt to a crisp, for 2 reasons - 1. author did not state - use regular tofu (it comes soft, regular, and firm/high protein). I used the high protein which has less water content - to author I suggest specify regular  2. I turned off oven but left pan in there as I was running air filter on high to get strong smell out of house - it was ok before - that did it in and burnt it to black unpleasant tasting - I suggest author state - take pan out of oven to cool immediately when done

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I recommend super firm. Easy to grate and needs just a gentle press to remove water from the block.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @LT I’m confused by the comment on smell. Tofu has a smell? I eat it twice a week and never noticed

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I'd like to take these to a potluck, but it does say serve immediately. I was thinking of cooking the shells in very very light sprayed oil and then bringing the tacos on a plate that's seven proof covered in foil and having them reheated in an oven at the party. The person doesn't care if I do that. What do you think? Will they be good?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: My avocados weren't ripe enough. Instead I made a base of coleslaw-cut cabbage mixed with Veganese, lime juice, salt, paprika and chili powder. I topped the tacos with Trader Joe's pepita salsa (you must try). I do think I overcooked the tofu "meat," which came out with some crunchy bits. Still, these are my favorite tacos of all time, and I'm from Southern California. My meat-eating husband loved them too. ... Can't wait to keep making these and trying them with the right sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Ok, I did some research and there is concern that charred, burned protein foods (in this case soy) could cause HCA's and/or PAH's and in general, there is concern that eating blackened foods can be carcinogenic.  Cancer medical research says small amount ok, but we should not seek out or make a practice of eating charred/blackened foods. Makes sense.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Good not great, the spice blend needs help.  Not sure what I will change but when I come back to this it will be very different.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and sooo tasty! Note: I had to sub guacamole for the fresh avocado on the recipe since I didn't have any on hand, and I loved it!  I even made the spice mix ahead of time for the second round I made to e following week!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was a hit! Both the tofu lovers and tofu ambivalent amongst our family loved it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This is SOO good!  Great for my veggie son with our Mexican slant in the kitchen.  Not a big fan of tofu, but this is truly yummy!  and looks good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I liked it but didn’t love it. Made 1/2 recipe just for two of us. There was no way to “drizzle” the tomato paste/oil. I added a little more oil that called for but it still didn’t make it drizzley. I’d have liked more red onion and radishes I think. Just didn’t think it was as great as others reported.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Love this so much and will be making more tofu crumbles tomorrow! I squeezed the tofu in a dish cloth and crumbled it all into a single bowl. Mixed all of the seasonings into the crumbles and spread onto a single foil lined pan - spray with Pam to prevent sticking. Added some coleslaw mix with lime, grapeseed oil and salt to the tacos as well. 10/10.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
This felt like a lot of work and cooking time for week night tacos, but my kids did enjoy it so that was win. 

Tip: skip the aluminum foil lining of the sheet pans. With all the mixing required of the tofu with the oil mixture, spices, and tomato “drizzle” my spatula poked holes in the foil, which I then had to closely inspect to make sure foil pieces were not getting in the food. Would have been much easier to mix and scrape without the foil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Really a lot of work for a not much flavor.   Really actually bad.  (And I love tofu).  I'd rather do mushroom tacos if I'm trying to go meatless.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Yup.  I made this last week.  Fortunately, it's just me so I had enough to stretch out over several meals.  A real keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Crumbles too small so will adjust next time. I always make per directions the first time, but wish I had blended all of the oil, soy sauce, and tomato paste together with spices, as others have suggested. I had issues with foil tearing when tossing so will try the parchment paper suggestion next time. I would love an Asian flavored spice mix as others have mentioned. It would make a tasty lettuce wrap meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic! Made as written, including the lime-mayo. Don’t forget the chopped onions. They add great contrast.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Oh, YUM.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Trying a tofu recipe for the first time. Thoroughly drained the tofu but perhaps too much.  30 minutes with a spin in the oven resulted in burnt edges and had to pry to tofu off the foil.  I didn’t cook it any longer just added what I could salvage to the tomato paste sauce and mixed.  Actually tasted pretty good. The true test is whether the husband will like it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Try doing a quick pickle on the onions and radishes. It adds a bright note to the dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This is delcious and quite versatile. I halved the recipe and it was perfect for us. I want to try this out with mushrooms next time. Also, we didn't have avocado so I made a kale, purple cabbage tahini slaw and piled that on top of each taco. Yum!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Holy cow this is great!  Next time I’ll cut down a bit on the tomato paste, and cut the initial cooking time by about 5 mins. The tofu was a little too crisp. The avocado mayo and onions and radishes(I quick pickled them with some ACV) were great additions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This is so tasty!! Some of the suggestions of mixing in the bowl would be helpful. The tinfoil is annoying it shredded as I was trying to unstick the tofu, parchment paper would work much better. Will plan to try again with the suggested modifications

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and looked just like the pic.  Thank you ❤️🌮

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Can leftover tofu be frozen?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Have already made this twice in a week. I added some chipotle peppers/chile en adobo and paprika to the tomato paste the second time. Still delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Excellent! I broiled it on low for the last minute. And I used low carb tortillas.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Loved this low effort magic.  Tofu for meat eaters .  I did skip the mayo/avocado sauce and made a guacamole of sorts.  We put the guac on the tortilla first and the rest of the fillings stuck nicely to the tortilla.  Will be making it for a family dinner next month and know the recipe will be requested by every household.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: We really liked this, although I think I squeezed too much water out and the tofu was quite crunchy. Paired with refried beans it was a nice textural element to the taco.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
Absolutely love this recipe and have made it over and over now. Two small things I changed that really made a big difference to me were adding a couple teaspoons of brown sugar to the tomato paste to give it a hint of richness and sweetness along with a couple extra shakes of cayenne so it was subtly sweet and spicy.

I did not like the avocado mayo mixture, so I simply mashed an avocado with salt and lime, and it was delicious like that.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: My husband is a resistant tofu eater but gobbled these up. Delish!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This is so good. I couldn’t stop eating it right out of the pan. I didn’t have tofu or tomato paste so used crumbled tempeh and a little tomato salsa. Mixed it all together and spread it out on a parchment lined rimmed cookie sheet. Baked 30 minutes at 375. Leftovers were great too. I served it on a crisped corn tortilla, topped with tempeh, a little cheddar, avocado salsa. Microwaved until cheese melted. Amazing and so easy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
Finally!! A tofu recipe that my husband and I enjoyed. In fact he was scraping up the last crusty remnants from the sheet pan. The seasonings really help and you must cook till it's crunchy. firvne that meant adding a few minutes at each stage but maybe I did not get enough liquid out of the tofu to start. and I could not grate it, ended up just breaking and chopping it. 
Super tasty.
Served with the crema and some quick pickled red onion and radish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: These are absolutely delicious!  One issue - it may be just me, but the tofu amount is very confusing.  As I understand it, two blocks with a combined weight of 14-16 ounces is called for.  But as written, it reads to my eye as 2 x 14-16 ounce blocks, or 28-32 ounces total.  Other than that, this is a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe. Very nice to have a recipe for crispy tofu.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I made this with seitan and did it in the air fryer for 20 minutes.  It works very well.  Will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: this was absolutely delicious, but I did burn half of it-even eight minutes for the roasting with the tomato paste and olive oil was too long. Probably five minutes would have been enough.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: My vegetarian kid loved this, as did her friends.  Substituted sour cream for the mayo, and served with additional garnishes (black beans, additional shredded cheese, chopped tomatoes, etc.)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight and enjoyed it well enough. A great way to get classic American tacos without beef. As others suggested, I used a bowl to add the soy sauce, oil, and spices. I also halved the recipe since it’s just me and my partner, this made one dinners worth of food. Also, I accidentally doubled the spices since I halved the tofu but it was still great. Might double the spices in the future too

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: My husband loved this one. It was a bit difficult to make because the tomato paste concoction is not easy to spread over the tofu. It also makes quite a lot! He was happy to use it for nachos the next day. Thanks for helping me introduce more meatless meals to the household!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Definitely definitely also add diced zucchini and chipotles in adobo. Do it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Amazing! Perfectly crispy and ideal for a taco!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
I love this recipe! The tofu truly takes on a meaty texture and we have used it in tacos, burritos, and quesadillas. My 12yo son (not knowing what protein was in his quesadilla) actually told me he really liked the “chicken!”  

Thank you to the commenters who suggested mixing the tofu & spices in a bowl, and putting the tofu back in a bowl to toss with tomato paste mixture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Shame on me for beginning to question if these could possibly be worth the time commitment (an hour-ish for tacos?). Part of the labor is my doing—I make my own grain-free tortillas. Anyway, these tacos are incredibly delicious. Perfect combination of spices and the right amount of crunch. I would be shocked if a meat-eater didn’t love them—great vegan option even if you are an omnivore like me. Wow. These will go into the rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
Love this, perfect tofu crispness!
Agree tossing tofu in bowl w oil and seasoning allows best coverage prior to roasting

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
So good and not hard at all!  I put all the ingredients in a big bowl and tossed the shredded tofu in it and it was easy and nicely coated.  used the mini food processor for the avocado and it came out so yummy and creamy.  

I toast the tortillas in a non-stick pan for a short bit and they are perfect.

This is now in the rotation!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: My favorite part of this app is reading your comments- always so helpful to hear what others have tried. I crumbled the tofu with my hands. I used the suggestion to mix everything in a big bowl (the step with the oil and spices). But I put it all on one pan (no foil or parchment) which I think helped prevent it from getting too crispy/hard all over -some parts were crispy and others softer. I mixed the tomato paste & oil directly on the pan with a spatula which worked fine and was easier than trying to mix in a bowl again. I can’t get enough of the avocado puree and added an extra tablespoon of lime juice to help it mix and to add some more tang. I will make again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I made this for a second time. I made a double batch of filling, splitting it between two sheet pans. I mixed everything in a bowl — tomato paste and all — since the tomato mixture not really pourable, as I learned last time. I used parchment instead of foil this time. No issues with sticking, but the parchment did tear in the last 20 minutes of baking, so I tossed the paper and finished baking on the bare pan. It takes longer to bake a batch of this size. You have to treat it like a granola, stirring periodically as it browns. The texture came out better than last time. I agree with another commenter that, as written, the texture is comparable to jerky. Anyway, I now have the quantity of taco filling I deserve. Cotija is a nice addition if you have it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Nice, but not as good as I expected. The tofu is unnecessarily greasy and a little…hard. It also shrinks down to almost nothing. My 9yo son and I ate it all in one sitting, which is quite a lot. I’m on a tight budget, and padding the tofu out with avocados was not my plan. Also a bit faffy. It’s nice; not thrilling, but nice. 7/10. May or May not make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
What a great way to make tofu! The cooking time was way too long. I tried the full length on the first batch and did less than 1/2 the time in the second and the second was far far batter. 
The dish was a bit bland so added oyster sauce, sweet chili, and something else that was kicking around and it made it much more palatable. 
Will certainly make again-easy to mod this recipe on the fly to make it better and it was a hit w/ the fam.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
I found this cooked up as described, but I thought it was high effort for tofu that was very ground-beef-like in the end. I did love the avocado dressing though. 
I didn’t drain the tofu much or press it at all, but I did use convection heat and glad I did or I don’t think it would have crisped. The flavors were fine but could have been even more aggressively seasoned for my taste. This could also use an umami boost from some miso or msg in my opinion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe is a great guideline to enjoy delicious tofu in a different ways. 
I think one teaspoon of salt and salt in soy is bit too much. I didn’t follow recipe to be exact but still came out great. We added some leftover baked lanb shredded to our meal with this crispy tofu. Next time we will try it with kidney beans and Kimchi tacos. Thanks

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I thought that this was just OK...honestly the best way to do vegan taco meat is with TVP. Very cheap and easy. You can crisp as much or as little as you want in a pan.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This kind of blew our minds. The crunchy bits, the chewiness… Picky kids loved it. Meat-lovin’ spouse loved it. The cook (me) loved it. We’re all excited for leftovers tomorrow.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I don't use garlic powder so added a couple of finely chopped cloves to the spice mix.  Works wonderfully.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
A wonderful way to make tofu. I added cooked brown rice to the corn tortilla shells before warming them. I topped the tofu with garrotxa (Spanish cheese) pieces. As usual a few other substitutions due to making this for one person & not having some ingredients. 

Wonderful crispy tofu. A wonderful recipe. I used a toaster oven as I made only ¼ the tofu.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
I found this recipe when I was looking for recipes to use up tofu in the refrigerator before I begin cleaning for Passover. I made the crispy tofu from one block and added half to barbecued beans I made earlier in the week. The crunchy was a nice addition. The other half will go onto pizza tomorrow night, to use up my OO flour. 
     My discovery was to use my tofu press to squeeze out the water from the block of tofu. From there I grated it with minimal crumbling.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: We liked the flavor but the tofu never crisped up. And I felt like it had too much oil. I thoroughly drained the tofu before grating.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I had a problem with the tofu sticking to the foil. Will spray foil slightly before adding tofu to pan to reduce that next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Crumbled the tofu with my hands instead of grating. Imprecise, but I didn't mind a few bigger, bouncier pieces. Mixed everything in a bowl instead of on the sheet pan. Used that same bowl to later mix in the tomato paste/oil. Was short on ingredients for the avocado cream, so probably only produced about half, but still well worth it. Despite using two blocks of tofu, since so much moisture is removed, the final volume comfortably serves maybe 3 (or maybe we just eat a lot).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
I agree with another response that you can't effectively drizzle the tomato paste mixture, something you can tell by looking at it. 
I put the tofu in a large mixing bowl and added that component, stirring briskly with tongs then put the result back on one cookie sheet, distributing evenly. It crisped up perfectly.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: THIS IS GREAT!  Super super yummy!  The avocado cream really steps it up a notch. I can’t wait to make this for my vegetarian relatives. A real winner!  I’m sure this will become a regular

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried substituting canned jackfruit for the tofu? It also is easily defined by seasoning but has a chewy texture similar to a meat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: delicious!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: What do you do with the other half of the spices? I read through all the comments and I dont think anyone has asked that. Does it go in the tomato paste of the avocado sauce?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Francesca it’s half on each pan.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I am avocado intolerant...any substitute suggestions? Thanks!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Claire B. I am also avocado intolerant. I make the avocado cream for my wife and daughter and a lime crema (1/3 cup sour cream + 3 tbsp mayo + zest and juice of lime + tsp of salt) to add instead of the avocado.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Claire B. I do not share your problem, but always love a challenge, so I went down a rabbit hole,  there are numerous online suggestions, but the one that stood out for me was an edamame spread. (Purée edamame with a bit of oil or I suppose one could use mayo, etc.),  it will give you the lovely green color and extra protein!  Hope that is helpful.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I am the same way. I always make a cilantro lime sauce but I use greek yogurt instead of sour cream/mayo. Easy to experiment with to find what you like!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This was sooooo good!  I added minced garlic to the avocado spread! Yum  Also, I had corn already cooked and cut off the cob.  Nice addition.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Delicious :) I ran way out of oil so I made a mixture of the tomato paste with some more soy sauce and mayonnaise instead, probably was not as crispy as it could/should have been because of that, but it still tasted great! Definitely a new staple.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: I didn't even finish cooking the tofu but I tried a little bit and its really good. I"m sure the finished version will be great. Based on this, I'd recommend making double portion of the tofu because people will want to load it up and probably you'll want more for later.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone used ‘tofu gan’ for this recipe? It’s Chinese pressed tofu, so it’s really dense already and usually comes refrigerated & vacuum sealed in a small amount of liquid

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Grating and roasting tofu is great. My carnivore, tofu sceptical brother-in-law thought my crispy tofu tacos were better than his beef tacos.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: What can I sub for avocado cream..I don’t eat mayo

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Regular guacamole would work fine. Or just layer on slices of avocado.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Anit Leave the mayo out. Make a more traditional guacamole by smashing avocados with a fork or in a molcajete or use a food processor and make it smooth. I make our house guacamole like this: for every medium to large avocado add the juice of 1 lime. If everyone likes cilantro, toss some of that in as well. For 2 avocados I use a handful that is likely 1/4 of a bunch and process totally smooth. Taste and adjust with more lime or salt if that’s your jam. I made this recipe over the weekend and we all loved it with the exception of the mayo. It got voted off the recipe card. We want the guacamole.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: @Anit Can you do a vegan mayo? You really need something to provide tang and creaminess. I tried regular guacamole and it wasn’t nearly as good. This is coming from a native Texan that loves guacamole!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: An incredible meal! When I said I was making tofu for dinner for the first time, my husband looked a little grossed out. Now he requests these almost weekly! the spice blend is perfect and the caramelization of the olive oil and tomato paste gives the tofu so much flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Wow, we loved this one. For the toppings, added cheddar cheese and sour cream and left off the onion and radishes. We got more than 10 tacos out of it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This remains one of my favorite NYT recipes. So easy and so, so, so delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: This is a terrific recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Crowd pleaser. Family loved this. Easy to do

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: My wife deemed these 'company worthy'.  I thought they were excellent.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: We’ve had this two ways: prepared according to recipe as crispy tofu and cooked a a little less time to a moister softer consistency (not crispy). Hands down we preferred the moister softer version. Cooking time was about one-third less time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody:
Amazing recipe!!! I will say this was my first time cooking tofu myself so I don’t have as much knowledge of the ins and outs of tofu, but I ran into no major hiccups! Very straightforward and delicious.

My one comment is that the super firm tofu grated a nice size, but after several rounds of tossing, transferring to a pan, back to the bowl, tossing again, and so on the tofu continued to break down into much much smaller pieces. That’s fine as a crunchy topping once baked, but in my opinion I would prefer bigger pieces, especially if it’s going to be served as a taco filling/meat substitute, like the recipe suggests. 

Next time I think I will try using one of those veggie cube chopper tools, or just dicing. Hopefully starting out with bigger pieces will let them crumble into the right size by the end.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: For the avocado sauce, make sure to pay attention to the size of your avocados! If you have a smaller avocado, use less lime juice so it doesn’t overpower. I made this mistake, but I was able to fix it with a few dollops of sour cream.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: My tofu-skeptic family loved these! Two blocks of tofu made quite a lot, probably seven portions for us. Will definitely make again! Might add some diced chipotles in adobo to the tomato paste next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Crispy Tofu Tacos Recipe
commentBody: Although I usually like grater tofu I didn’t love this recipe - it was quite dry and the tofu was overcooked (followed instructions to a T)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Maseri or Mae Ploy curry paste are great options here. Also punch up the paste with more garlic, ginger, galangal, turmeric if you have it accessible. Would also suggest a splash of Red Boat fish sauce or other higher quality to finish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Tracy Pepatphong i like the idea of freshening up a bottled mixed spice, of any kind, with a bit of the fresh version of anything in it. Usually garlic and ginger, but also in other recipes maybe parsley, basil, oregano, peppers, chives, lemon juice, whatever. Just one will brighten.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Do you by chance recommend a brand or brands of curry paste to look for?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @David Hays Buckley reason I looked in comments

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I have had some success with the brand Maesri.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Mae Ploy has very good green or red curry paste

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Where are the “tips?”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Right after step for in the above.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Under Step 4

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: The tips are at the end of the recipe’s cooking instructions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: For those of us in rural areas, with no access to lime leaves, what are options (I have no idea what they taste like)?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: It won’t be the same, but you could use basil.  Lemon basil would work great, but any basil would be fine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
@KatOSteele 

 I was wondering the samewould lime zest and juice do the trick?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @KEWambach I just add zest from a large lime, no juice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Some of us live in areas where it is difficult to obtain items like lime leaves, fresh or frozen. It would have been helpful if you had suggested a substitute if one is available.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: A very valid point, Mark — thank you for noting. We do mention that the ingredient, while authentic to the dish, is not totally essential — the dish still tastes great without it. That said, if you wanted a simple alternative, throwing in some freshly grated lime zest would add some additional citrusy brightness to the dish, as the lime leaves do.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I’ve ordered them from Amazon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Mark When I need to stock up my Thai pantry, I order from importfood.com. For people who love to cook Thai, it’s like a proverbial candy store.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: "King" (with a rising tone) literally means ginger in Thai. There is neither ginger or galangal in this recipe, so it is simply Pad Prik (stir-fried with hot pepper) not Pad Prik King. It's still a great recipe though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: That's the mystery. Despite the lack of ginger, it's still called Pad Prik King.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Kevin the curry paste typically has one or both. The one I got has galangal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night. It seemed a bit spicy so I added a large scoop of crunchy peanut butter and half a can of coconut milk to the sauce at the end. Then simmered a few minutes to thicken. Topped with minced cucumber and served on rice. A keeper!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Rebecca thanks for this tip! Added peas as well as the PB and the coconut milk. The extra liquid helped the chicken cook through in the wok as well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
Have only used 4-6 shredded whole makrut leaves for a recipe for four as the citrus leaf scent can be a bit overpowering. Just my experience and I'm not shy of over spicing and herbing my food usually.  Also recommend removing the centre spine of each makrut leaf before (very) thinly slicing the rest as the spine can be woody/stringy.

(Add up to 20 mins prep time as needed to cut chicken into strips, finely shred leaves, wash and slice beans and of course smash garlic)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Going to try this tonight, but with standard green beans... Which I assume must be different than long beans, because there's no way they would cook through in 4 minutes, so I'll be doing those on the side and combining at the end. Can't wait to try it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Roger that’s what I did - steamed them in the microwave for two minutes then used the water in the dish as the 1/4 cup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Can you make it without the lime leaves? Or use something in place of them?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: What might I do for a family member allergic to oysters? I spotted vegan oyster sauce the other day, would that be usable here or is it actually rather abominable stuff??  Thanks.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Lizzy Vegan oyster sauce is delicious. Wan Ja Shan brand is excellent and available online.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Marinate chicken with one tsp fish sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Lizzy we omit it for our oyster-allergic family member. I haven’t tried this particular recipe, but it usually doesn’t affect the deliciousness of a curry!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I wish you would recommend or describe particular brands of curry paste since that's basically what the recipe hinges on. There are so many and the couple brands that I have tried have been really not good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
This is from Pailin Chongchitnant of Hot Thai Kitchen:
Maeploy - my current go-to brand. It does contain shrimp paste, which is traditional
Aroy-D - another brand that I use quite often cuz it’s widely available; and if Mae Ploy isn't available this is the one I get. 
Maesri - the only brand that comes in a can, and also the only one with sugar in the ingredient list.
Namjai - another one in our lineup that contains shrimp paste.
Thai Kitchen - not a brand I often use

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
I use "Bright" brand Curry past.
It's tasty per recipe. If you don't have oyster
1 tsp fish sauce to marinate chicken
Sub sliced yellow onion for garlic and skip sugar as onion is sweet after cooking.
Parboil the green beans or fry with the onion. Paste. Chicken. Eat. Skip the cornstarch if you have fresh air chilled chicken.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Why would you skip the cornstarch for air chilled chicken? The corn starch keeps the chicken moist and can provide a more velvety texture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Made it with pork and fresh leaves.  Delicious.  An easier and almost similar route taste wise is to use the canned Maesri Prik King paste ( comes in a green can).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Thanks! I am planning to swap in pork for the chicken as well

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Lizzy - just leave the oyster sauce out. This is the first prik khing recipe I’ve ever seen that includes it.  I’d suggest splashing in 1 qtr cup of chicken broth instead of the oyster sauce & water.  Also marinate the chicken in a teaspoon or so of fish sauce at least 20 mins - half an hour beforehand if you can.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: What can I substitute for the lime leaves?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Doubled the chicken and beans while keeping all other ingredients the same quantities.  Tasted like takeout! Would only use thigh meat (I did 1/2 breast 1/2 thigh and thigh bits much tastier), will probably cook the beans separately for awhile then add to chicken to get them blistered.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: For those who can't get fresh or frozen lime leaves, order dried. If you don't want to use oyster sauce, just leave it out and try another recipe (from what I can tell, it isn't used in most recipes anyway), such as this one I am going to try: https://hot-thai-kitchen.com/gai-pad-prik-king2/

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
@Simon I cooked this recipe instead, and it was great, browning the chicken rather than velveting with cornstarch seemed right. 

I might add garlic next time as this recipe suggests, tho.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I made this to the letter, except I reduced the curry paste to 1.5 T (since I’d not used the brand before) and cut the oil in half. I would actually probably use even less oil next time, as it bordered on oily, but it was delicious! I might increase the curry paste to 2-2.5 T next time, or else add some more aromatics (or just find a new curry paste). I also finally discovered what one of the flavors I love about restaurant Thai curry is-lime leaf!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Makrut lime leaves = "Kafir" lime leaves, for those who call them by this other name as I do.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @RJ808 “Kafir” is an offensive term for a “non believer” in some cultures. It also has some very unpleasant associations with South African apartheid. So in recent years it’s been gradually being rebranded either as simply lime leaves, or Makrut lime leaves.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
This didn't turn out as I had hoped.  I will make several changes:
- better curry paste than the one I had in my cupboard.  The one I had was fairly bland.
- more garlic, and some fresh ginger.
- only half the oil (this was MUCH too oily!)
- I may just toss the chicken in dry cornstarch - the texture of the chicken was kind of strange at first (better after it rested a bit), and it was difficult to determine when it was actually cooked through.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Karen I agree with you on every point. It was SO oily. Also I didn’t like the brand of curry paste I used. The dish was a little lacking so next time, like you, will use better curry paste and add ginger. I may skip the cornstarch altogether and marinate the chicken in fish sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: As a Torontonian, I have enjoyed Ms. Regular's cuisine often. I made a few changes but the result was delicious: I thought it odd to velvet the chicken, so I didn't use cornstarch. I marinated it in a small amount of fish sauce and didn't use oyster sauce. I used 2 tablespoons of Mae Ploy curry paste; its first ingredient is garlic so I didn't add any, and I steamed the beans for a few minutes first - this is a vegetable that needs to be fully cooked.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: If you use conventional green beans, they take way more than three minutes to cook with the chicken and sauce. I suggest parboiling or steaming them first until they're almost tender-crisp. I added a tablespoon or so of fish sauce and used Aroy-D red curry paste; the dish was way too salty. So maybe skip the fish sauce and use less curry paste and taste; you can always add more. Next time I will dilute the sauce with some chicken stock or coconut milk.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Any recommendations for how to sub tofu for the chicken after the usual prep (press and drain)? Has anyone?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Use smoked tofu. Its dryness and firmness make it an ideal swap for meat in recipes. It won't crumble or break apart.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Four Winds Growers sells Thai Lime trees that you can buy online and grow at home. Thai Lime trees are one if the easiest types of citrus to grow as an indoor/outdoor plant because you are after the leaves and you aren’t worried about trying to get the tree to set fruit which is more challenging as an indoor/outdoor plant

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
It took me about 20 years to find the key ingredient in my local restaurant's Thai Curry - it was the lime leaves.  It's just not the same without them.
You can find them online, sometimes referred to as makrut/kaffir lime.  If you live in the deep south (as I now do), you can buy live plants at Home Depot if you're lucky, or in local oriental markets.  
I personally use Mae Ploy curry paste.  They also have yellow and green varieties.  All are different and delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: If you like your green beans really crunchy, 4 minutes is just enough cooking time. Make sure you get "French" green beans-regular ones in the US are too fat and won't cook in the time allotted. Living in France, green beans not a problem. I used dried lime leaves. They add a nice flavor, but next time I'll soak them in the water and oyster sauce before dumping the whole thing in.  Also tough to find boneless, skinless chicken thighs here. I'll use chicken tenders next time (aiguillettes).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: This is a pretty solid recipe.  I have a severe shellfish allergy and the maesri brand red curry is shellfish free (and vegan) and tastes much better than taste of India.  Recommendations from others to marinate the chicken in a solution that contains some fish sauce added extra depth of flavor/. Will make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious! But cooking in this order left scorched garlic and undercooked beans. Next time (and there WILL be a next time) I will cook chicken until almost done, put in a bowl, cook beans and put in bowl, cook garlic and curry paste then add everything back in with the sauce. 
It's so tasty and easy! Thank you for the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I made this a few days ago and we loved it....however the spice level of the Thai curry paste I used was through the roof. I had to make more rice to help us endure the heat of it!  Next time I'll heed the advice of others, and add the curry sparingly to start and then to taste.  Otherwise, a delicious recipe and it came together quickly on a busy weeknight.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: This was very good, though I needed to use more curry paste (Thai Kitchen).  It took about 20 minutes to cook because I moved the chicken to on top of all the beans so they had a good 10 minutes of simmering to make them tender.  I used boneless skinless breasts because of the short cooking time, but might try thighs next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I used green curry instead of red curry paste for this recipe, and it was very good. I would increase the garlic and add fresh ginger. The lime leaves add a citrusy flavor that could probably be gotten from lime zest. I had to special order lime leaves because my local Asian markets did not have lime leaves. Very easy and tasty recipe and I will make this again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Freeze your lime leaves, they last forever.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Definitely steam the beans first!  Otherwise by the time they’re cooked everything else will be overcooked.  Unless you like super crunchy beans. I think you can use less oil, maybe 75%. I’d also add a little less water when tossing the beans in. The photo makes the dish look less saucy than it was for me.  In fact the NYT photos almost NEVER look like what I make.  Who wants to write a cookbook with me where all the photos are actually what the food looks like when humans make it???

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Mae Ploy curry paste is my favorite. Many flavors. Lasts in the fridge for months

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Curious to know if the red curry paste is spicy hot, or just spicy flavorful?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: You can get the dried lime leaves on amazon with next day prime shipping for under $10.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I’ve never been able to find makrut leaves at stores in my area, fresh or frozen. I live in the CA Bay Area and go to Asian stores all the time, so I don’t know what I’m doing wrong. Any ideas, anybody?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Lime leaves are the difficult ingredient here. AI says "For lime leaves, especially Kaffir lime leaves, a good substitute is lime zest. You can also try a combination of lime zest and a small amount of bay leaf or fresh lemon thyme. Lemon verbena or lemon basil can also provide a similar citrusy flavor."

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Great quick recipe. If using Blue Thai Elephant red curry paste, 1 tablespoon was enough for us “well travelled but weak” individuals. I would add sesame seeds to top it off.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: There is a tremendous range of profiles in curry pastes - there should be a note appended to this recipe discussing which was used. The Thai Kitchen brand available in supermarkets has virtually no oomph - you need to double it just to get a semblance of what you are wanting. The Mae Ploy brand approaches an authentic taste: it has shrimp paste which gives Thai food it's essential umami foundation. It can be ordered online; here in CT I can get it at Walmart, so its hardly exotic.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Good & quick dish. However, for someone that likes spice, 3 tablespoons is VERY SPICY. I recommended going with 1 tablespoon

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I use the Sadaf Ground Dried Lime as a substitute in several situations, and should work here. Also handy to have around in general as a seasoning.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: This was good! I might advise a real scant 1/4 c oil, and 1tsp sugar instead of 1.5. But, that could just be personal preference. I did not have lime leaves, so per some comments, I used the zest of 1 lime and abou

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Liked this dish, but would up the curry paste next time.  Needed to allow more time for the green beans to cook, and I'd use a bit less oil also.  Didn't have oyster sauce so substituted half and half of Tamari and Worcestershire sauce plus lime zest and lime juice for the lime leaves.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I used 3TBSP of curry paste & next time I would use the full amount, and/or reduce the amount of oil. The dish was quite oily. Also, I would cook the chicken closer to doneness before adding the beans. The chicken needed more time than the recipe states & therefore the beans were overcooked a bit. I used lime zest & juice and that was a delicious touch! Overall, I would make it again with the modifications that I listed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: (1/2) Good base idea but I made it more authentic. Swapped out chicken for deskinned hotdogs which are closer to what they use if you know about this stuff. My friend Pradeep gave me the tip after I told him I was making this and I trust him. Mixed the marinade with the standard amount of Cornstarch and xanthan gum. One cup each to thicken it up.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
I've made this twice now. It's an awfully good recipe. Each time, I  used about 1.75lbs of chicken breast (we don't like thighs). But I forgot that I was almost out of lime leaves, so I added the zest of two small limes and about 6 lime leaves. It was very close in flavor to the first time I made it. I kept the amount of Mae Ploy paste the same and only used about 1.25 teaspoons of sugar. I had about homemade chicken stock in the freezer, so used that instead of plain water. 
I think it has become a family favorite.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Huge thunderstorm washed out our plans to get to our fav Thai restaurant (3 hour r/t from where we live) tonight.  But this recipe and a well stocked pantry (Mesri Red Thai curry paste) saved the evening!  Didn't have kaffir lime leaves but did have a Thai Basil plant in the garden so even though it changed the flavor profile it was quite taste with a big fistful of Thai Basil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I used Mekala brand red curry paste which is much hotter than the Thai Kitchen brand I usually use. I added 1 cup coconut milk and the juice of a small lime to tame it a bit. It was very good. Next time I'll use a milder curry, though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I used 4 Tb Mae Ploy red curry paste and it was so salty it was nearly inedible. Oops! Plan to try again with way less of the Mae Ploy paste or try a different paste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: This was exactly what I wanted to taste and eat. Delicious. Thank you NYT.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: delicious recipe. I used a panang curry paste, added some fresh ginger with the garlic, and fish sauce to the marinade. probably doubled the beans (Roma pole beans) because I have so many in my garden right now.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty. I recommend increasing the amount of green beans. Also, I par cooked the green beans before adding in step 4. Four minutes is just not enough time for them to get tender. I made with boneless skinless chicken breasts and they were moist and tender with the same cooking time per the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
Resolved to not be one of those people who post their extensive deviation from recipe. Then accidentally messed this up and made it vegan and it was still good, so decide to share. Realized after committing to making this recipe that I didn’t not have chicken, so I substituted 8oz extra firm tofu (lightly pressed excess water out with paper towels), cut in same size slices as chicken, into same cornstarch mixture, drizzled with 1-2 tsp fish sauce. Cooked at same point/length. Pushed to side and added 1 small thinly sliced onion and a scant cup of cherry tomatoes as had an underwhelming amount of green beans and tofu. Cooked for several minutes and then added green beans. Accidentally used 2 tbsp vegan oyster sauce. Might have accidentally made same mistake with brown sugar. Somehow happened to have lime leaves! 

Resolved to go grocery shopping more often and not cook while sleep-deprived. 

Ps. Used ~2-3 Tbsp red curry paste and it was perfectly spicy for the people who like pretty spicy Thai food.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I made a version of this with ground turkey. I added fish sauce and coconut milk at the end to create a saucier dish. It was a hit!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Why the cornstarch?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Simon it is used to thicken sauces and ‘velvet’ chicken. It’s a kitchen staple!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Simon helps prevent the chicken from drying out and gives a surface coating the enables the curry sauce to adhere to better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Simon it’s called velveting - the meat a velvety texture. It’s very common in Asian stir fry dishes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Corn starch thickens sauces

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Mae Ploy is an excellent brand of curry paste! Widely available.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Makrut lime leaves are apparently another name for kaffir lime leaves.  Now I know!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Can I use leaves from any lime tree?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Hi there! Makrut lime leaves are a very specific type of lime leaf that grows on the makrut lime plant (which produces makrut limes that are visually distinguished by their bumpy exterior). You can find fresh ones at Asian grocers and some specialty markets, or dried ones online. They add a slightly floral lime flavor, but the recipe is still delicious without them. In a pinch, I've thrown in lime zest, basil or cilantro leaves in their stead — not authentic, but still delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: The makrut limes leaves are much more intense in flavor.  From what I've read, the leaves and zest are the main products from the tree, as the fruit is rarely eaten.  I've found them to be a revelation in my Thai dishes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Does the corn starch create the same effect on chicken as baking soda?  I’ve been using the baking soda on chicken breast for stir fry and it creates the most tender, juicy chicken breast and it’s nearly impossible to over cook it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: The cornstarch helps create a creamy slightly thicken sauce as the dish cooks.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Is the marinade just water, salt, and cornstarch?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Can I use key lime leaves from my tree?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Is there a reasonable substitute for green beans, which I dislike fairly intensely?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Roger any strong green veggie will work. How do you feel about broccoli etc?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Roger I think sugar snap peas or mangetout (or the names in your specific country) are the most similar to green beans so use them or stay away from them, and instead try broccoli, green or red bell peppers, pak choi or choi sum, kale or collard greens and lastly cabbage is great in a Thai sauce - I tend to use red for substance or Napa/sweetheart hispi/Savoy for a lighter leafy texture

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Roger I have used frozen greens peas in a “thai food panic” and they were yum.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: My wife is allergic to all things from the sea. Is there a substitute for oyster sauce?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Kent plant based/vegan oyster sauce and fish sauce do exist but are harder to find. Easier to find is white or red miso which will give you the umami hit you need. You could also try soy sauce

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: If the curry paste needs to be sautéed in oil before making the dish. Does adding additional paste require sautéing in oil as well? Since the potency won't be pronounced until the food is fully assembled?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Christopher to get the best flavour yes. The paste is raw so it needs cooking out in oil or water. Using an oil base will bring out more flavour from the paste (google why, I don’t remember perfectly)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: 12 makrut lime leaves? Please provide a substitute. For busy working families, this serves as a disincentive. All else, the recipe looks good. Also, please provide a realistic prep/serve range. 30 minutes is unrealistic plainly, for the family that wants to cook healthy but gets hooked but stymied by the the unrealistic time frame. Do a range. You will not but I do suggest it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Mae Ploy pastes are great and are available online from you-know-who. Under no circumstances should you use Thai Kitchens (no flavor, no heat).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I love Mae Ploy red curry paste - I wouldn't use anything else. However, I found their green curry paste fairly bland - I had to use so much that the food was very spicy and salty.  Opinions? Thanks!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I really found this to be way too much oil. Anyone else??  And I just didn’t get the flavor I was hoping for.  Has to add salt. Help???

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Can kafir lime leaves be used instead of makrut lime leaves?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Bronwyn they’re different names for the same thing.  That’s the old name for them but it’s socially unacceptable because it’s a racial slur in their society.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
@Bronwyn

They’re the same thing. Makrut is a more polite make for them now :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Bronwyn they’re the same thing just labelled differently depending on “brand”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
@Food Enjoyer 
The best curry paste I have ever found is Thai and True. Where I live I can get it at better grocery stores, but it's widely available online. If you go to their website, they actually give you a heat rating for each of their pastes, which is very helpful!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Lime leaves substitute and quantity? Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
Downloaded this recipe yesterday, and I tried it tonight.
While it looked great, and the timing in the recipe was perfect, I have to admit to being slightly underwhelmed (a first for me with a NYT recipe). I did not have the lime leaves, but I took the recipe at its word that I'd have a good dish without them.
"Good" is exactly how I'd describe it. It didn't have the oomph I was expecting (Maybe more curry paste?); it didn't even taste especially curry-ish. Rather bland over-all. Suggestions?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Use Maesri red curry or Prik Khing paste - they have both. And lime leaves.  You have to use lime leaves.  It's one of the key differentiators from it tasting like a regular red curry.  Oyster sauce is not necessary (it's probably what muddled the flavour for you) but a qtr cup of chicken broth in its stead is.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
I used 3 tablespoons of Maesri red curry, which I found in the local Asian market.
Dish was great, maybe a little too spicy, but we really enjoyed this recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: We followed the recipe too closely, against my better judgement. It was all fine, rather bland

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: any way to make this dish while using up my three different tins of dried curry powder?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I made this dish according to the times listed in the recipe but the green beans did not cook right. They were tough and chewy. That needs to be altered. The oyster sauce was too strong and I'll use less next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I just made this and it was incredible.  I made a few adjustments based on other Pad Prik recipes.  I marinated the chicken in a teaspoon of fish sauce and also added a tablespoon of cornstarch to the chicken.  I used Thai Kitchen red curry paste as it was in my local market, and was able to purchase the Lime Leaves frozen.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I will make this again. I used Maesri red curry paste and added some lime zest at the end of the cook time. I served with some asian-style thin crunchy noodles on top - so good. I did find that the haricot vert I used took much longer to cook.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I made this with just a few modifications since we tend to not eat heavily spiced food. I only used one and a half tablespoons of the red curry paste, and while it was tolerable, our lips still burned. I didn’t have lime leaves, but used all the zest and juice from two small limes. Added some soy sauce to the pan as the chicken and beans were cooking and it turned out to be a lovely sauce to enjoy with the beans, chicken and rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is FANTASTIC! Get the lime leaves if you can. They add a depth of flavor that you will not get without. I’m a fan of Mae Ploy curry paste and find it less salty than other brands (including maesri). We were really happy with this recipe. If I ordered this at any Thai restaurant, I’d be thinking about it all week until I went back and had it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful and so simple! I used flash frozen green beans. I had a very good curry paste, but added more garlic, ginger and Red boat (as recommended by another comment). I also keep Makrut lime leaves in the freezer and they worked great. We are going to be making this with other forms of protein as well. I think I can make this in about the time it would take to get the same quality from takeout!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Lots have asked about red curry. In France, I used an entire pot (85g - about 3 oz) of Albert Menes red curry (Thai recipe). Adds a nice bite, but toned down for French tastes. Next time I'll add a dash of cayenne to "kick it up a notch".

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
Green beans from my local sources are tough. I followed the America's Test Kitchen method and blanched the beans in very salty water ahead of time. I cooked the chicken until it was done, then added the beans and remaining ingredients. 

Perfect! The quarter cup of water at the end made it just saucy enough

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
