commentBody: This is a great, speedy recipe perfect for weeknights. My curry paste is super spicy (it's all in Thai, I don't know what I bought) so I only did two tbsp. and that worked for us. And in lieu of green beans I used broccolini I had on hand-- delicious! Thanks for this excellent recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Here in Northern Minnesota finding lime leaves is not an option.  I added some lime zest so hopefully that will add some zing!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I marinated the chicken the night before with a chili lime salt rub and the red curry, fish sauce, etc. the flavor really came through in the chicken. We squeezes a lime on top at the end as well since we don’t have a clue where to find the lime leaves. Delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Made this with tofu and it turned out pretty well. I added some fish sauce (we're not vegetarian, just like tofu!), and I think it needed it. I did this in a wok, and it seemed like my beans needed more than 3 minutes to become tender. I used the whole can of chili paste. I found one that was explicitly for prik king.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I'd never cooked with makrut lime leaves before, but was able to find them at Whole Foods. What a revelation! They just smell and taste like Thai food! I didn't have oyster sauce, so followed a recommendation here and used a teaspoon of fish sauce with the cornstarch "marinade" for the chicken. We really loved this dish. It's always amazing, and fun,  to find an ingredient that makes such a big difference.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I made this as written but without the lime leaves as my local Asian market was sold out. I added a bit of lime zest and the meal was delicious! I will definitely make this again. I’m curious to see what the lime leaves add.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made without the oyster sauce? I am deathly allergic to all things seafood, but would love to try this recipe! Is there anything non-seafood that I can substitute?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Be careful, most Thai curry pastes contain shrimp paste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I made this stir fry for dinner last night and it was easy to make and delicious! The lime leaves are a new flavour to our household palate and they reminded me of lemon pledge with every bite. This was an unwelcome reminder of my grandma’s house - not that memories of her are unwelcome, but the flavour sure was! Next time I’ll try cooking them whole and removing them before serving or substituting them for lime zest instead.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: This was amazing! So few ingredients, so little effort but amazing flavors! I added a little red pepper flakes for a bit more heat and some basil at the end; didn’t have beans, so used asparagus. Can’t wait to make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: A good tasting dish - great flavour and texture, and satisfying to prepare.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious.  Thanks for the comments re lime zest for a substitute.  Fast and easy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Not a huge fan of the rubbery texture of green beans. I think I'll try broccoli or snow peas next time. Loved it as a rice bowl otherwise, though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: what makes this high in carbs? thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Joseph Maybe the nutrition info includes the rice? Or maybe curry pastes differ.  I made this following the recipe, except I reduced the oil, and plugged it into my nutrition app.  It shows it as 10.3 g of carbs per serving, 1.5 g being fiber.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
This was super tasty.
I thought there would be issues with the cooking method and step cooking of ingredients and there were- the string bean alternative needs a pre stir fry! The addition of ginger and sub if lime rind works really well. So does adding Vietnamese Basil. Thanks for an easy tasteful recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I used 8 cloves of garlic and chopped them coarsely, which lent another texture to the finished dish. Delicious combination of flavors.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I’m allergic to oysters. What would be a good substitute for oyster sauce?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @Char I am also allergic.  I use the vegetarian oyster sauce and it works fantastically.  It's not hard to find, either.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I agree about the American curry paste - Thai Kitchen used to be good a long time ago, but it is now so adulterated as to have almost no bite at all.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: This was great and only got better with a couple squeezes of lime and extra lime leaves

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I added tomato after the garlic and curry paste, it combined nicely with the dish. I agree that it could use a bit less oil, and I already had cut it by about 1/3.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: added because Mars liked it. Used only 1 tbsp red curry paste and 2-3 tbsp miso paste because i was worried about spice. was not spicy

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I always add fresh ginger and plenty of fish sauce, and top with a fried egg.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was delicious, even though my husband decided to put the whole jar of red curry paste (so more than twice what the recipe called for) into the dish. I'm going to lobby for it to go into rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I doubled and it ended up so liquidy!  I don't think a doubling of oil is needed. Also, the beans took forever to cook so next time I will get those going in the oven while the chicken is cooking on stove.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I've made this with chicken and also scallops. Turned out very well. Like the reviewer below me, I added more garlic., a very slight additional ginger and a Tbs of Red Boat fish sauce.  Really good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
Anyone tried a good vegetarian/vegan substitute for the chicken? 
I'm thinking of using tempeh fried up before starting in with the garlic, curry, etc.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: @JJ I think tempeh would be excellent! Firm tofu would also work.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I could not find lime leaves locally so added lime zest and the slightest squeeze of a lime to the end of the dish. I know it’s not the same but beggars can’t be choosers. Otherwise followed the recipe. The sugar was integral in my opinion. I did 2.5 Tbs of curry paste and that was plenty spicy for me.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I would parboil the long beans as they do take a while to cook through and add them to the chicken towards the end so the chicken does not get overcooked.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Made as written. The only thing I altered was a lot of grated lime peel for the   makrut leaves. The first few mouthfuls rated an okay. By the time I finished the dish, I was in love.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I was so sure it would be weird, but it was so good! I'll add cashews and probably a little dried fruit, or some banana. Tofu would work, too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: OMG! I wasn't sure this would turn out at all, and I was so wrong! This is gonna be a go-to, but I'll keep adding cashews and a little fruit, like currants or bananas. Man that was good! Just finished the last bite and am weeping bitter tears!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: This was easy and delicious. Those lime leaves are a must.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night. It was pretty boring. Will increase spice paste next time

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Made this with b/s breasts, non-fancy Thai Kitchen red chili paste, hoisin sauce instead of oyster sauce (oops), and extra green beans to use the whole 12oz bag. It needed less oil (2T instead 4), and some extra salt. I also added about 1T soy sauce as it was a little lacking in umami. Overall, though, it was easy and quick and tasty!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I added some red onions thickly slicked and bell peppers, which helped balance the salt and heat, but otherwise made as is - It was delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I made this with some leftover rotisserie chicken and it was super quick and delicious! Followed the rest of the directions as written while tossing in the “marinated” rotisserie chicken bits after the beans had cooked.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Love this recipe. Also use it as a formula, replacing protein and veg depending on what's at hand. Always add ginger to the garlic and back off the oyster sauce. It was wonderful the first time and each time I make it, gets closer to my ideal. Because of my willy nilly replacements, I cook the protein and veg separately and add them back at step 4. Bought a makrut lime, easiest citrus ever for inside/outside cold climes since you're growing for leaves. delish!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I use Maesri pastes because my Thai friends tell me that’s what they use in Thailand when cooking at home, and that’s what they use in Thai restaurants in the US.. They actually make a premixed Pad Prik King  paste which is heavenly. And you can order all the Maesri curry pastes on Amazon.  I grow my own kefir lime leaves because there is truly no substitute for their flavor, but you can order dried or powdered leaves on Amazon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: It’s funny to me that many recipes call for sugar and don’t make a distinction between white sugar and palm sugar which iso less sweet.   Palm sugar is easily found and has subtle differences than white sugar, with caramel undertones.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Very good. Use less lime leaves.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody:
As I've grown more mature, my taste buds just can't handle hot spices like they once did. So I cut down on the curry paste but it was still too spicy for me so I added a can of coconut milk. Wow, I really liked the new flavor! 
Thank you for this recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I just got some incredibly fresh kaffir lime leaves on Amazon. It’s the folks who ship them wrapped in banana leaves.(oct 2025) it might be a seasonal thing, so hop to it and freeze the rest for a year.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I just made this and it’s so delicious! I added eggplant and probably a little too much curry paste. Luckily, my family loves spicy food. Thanks for the great recipe. I go to NYT Cooking often for meal inspirations.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious and super authentic. Growing up this was my favorite dish at my local Thai restaurant. This was a perfect rendition - will be going into the regular rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: This is a winner - super quick and delicious. I cooked it for friends for dinner, and served with jasmine rice, the turmeric chickpea stew, a pumpkin lemongrass curry and a Thai slaw with red chilies, lime and fish sauce. They liked.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe! I'll be making this again. One change I'll try is keeping the makrut lime leaves intact (so they add flavor but can be avoided), or add sliced leaves a little earlier in the recipe. I have a makrut tree, picked fresh leaves, but they weren't tender enough with only 1 minute of cooking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: This went well. I was happy with how close it got to a restaurant Prik King. Man I love makrut lime.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I've made this like a bajillion times, it's so good! I find it does require the amount of oil stated (I tried to cut it down a bit but you really do need it). I find that green beans and lime juice work in place of long beans and lime leaves.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: EXCELLENT! Forgive me in advance as I did make some substitutions/additions as I could not procure lime leaves and was using average grocery store red curry paste. I used some items I had on hand: substituted flour for cornstarch (2 tsp), sauteed fresh ginger with the garlic, added red pepper flakes, sauteed basil just before adding beans, reduced sugar to taste (~1 tsp), added fresh basil and a squeeze of lime juice when plated. Outstanding - great for weeknight or dinner party.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: The quality of the red curry paste makes or breaks this dish.  I used Blue Dragon as that is what I had on hand and even with a very generous application it did not provide the flavour we hoped for.  I will definitely be making this again, but with a good Thai brand of red curry paste.  As suggested by some of the comments, I added some fish sauce to the chicken marinade; I will do that again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty.  Took me much longer than the listed times.  Struggled to tell when chicken was “not pink” since it was covered in curry paste.  Also had a hard time keeping the curry paste from sticking and burning.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: I have made this many times now, even growing a Makrut Lime tree for the leaves—it makes all the difference plus 4tbsp Maesri Red Curry Paste.  I recommend parboiling the green beans 2-3minutes before stir frying.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pad Prik King (Red Curry Chicken and Green Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! As often as I can get my hands on lime leaves, this will be in regular rotation. I accidentally picked up chicken breasts instead of thighs, but the marinading step made them completely tender. Just beware the heat of your curry paste (my husband went straight to the max amount, not realizing ours was so hot!).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Rainbow carrots   extra pistachios, absolutely fantastic and beautiful to look at! 10/10. You can go heavier on pretty much all the herbs/toppings if you want.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I don’t ever comment on recipes, but I had to on this one. This recipe is phenomenal. I grilled some chicken breast cutlets to accompany this dish - marinated with greek yogurt, lemon, garlic powder, olive oil, and S&P for about 30 minutes. Hoping our friends don’t find this so that we can make it at our next dinner party

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I love garlic, but I very rarely love raw garlic. If you're the same, try combining the minced (crushed, grated, whatever) garlic with the citrus juice and letting it sit for 10 min before blending the rest of the sauce. The acid will soften the heat of the garlic.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I want to make this tonight, but low on carrots and over-loaded on broccoli.  Does anyone see a reason why I should not substitute broccoli for the carrots in the recipe?  I have everything else on hand. Thoughts?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody:
@Joan
Broccoli would be a different taste experience, but may also be good - post your thoughts if you tried?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: @Joan I think broccoli can get quite earthy and bitter after roasting, while the carrots become sweet. I like that though. I have cooked a recipe before where the broccoli was laid on a bed of tahini and yoghurt mix but topped with peanuts, chilly flakes and and a drizzle of lemon juice on top. It was delicious too!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: @Joan I say go for it! Of course you’ll get a different flavor but the crispy texture from the roasted broccoli florets could make this a worthy switch. Curious to hear how it goes!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: this dish looks like it's a menu item at a restaurant in a gentrified neighborhood that charges $21 for a whiskey sour and features a cheeseburger that doesn't come with a side of fries. except, it's really really good and I made it myself for like $3.50 and the tahini from the Back Of The Fridge. was a big hit with the gals at the dinner party. would make again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe with rainbow carrots, lots of purples and reds and white as well. I used tandoori spice since I didn't have curry powder, and I doubled the pistachio topping and used less dill, more chive and added parsley. For the whipped tahini I doubled the garlic and added just a dash of olive oil, it seemed it needed more fat to me. Anyway this recipe was a lovely addition to dinner, I had it with filet of sole baked in Plugra on a bed of puréed and creamed leeks and spinach along with some gorgeous purple asparagus. Hello Spring!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I found the whipped tahini too tart. I used a lemon, so paerhaps an orange would help balance that. I ended up adding just a dab of agave to try balance the flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: As I noted, I did use orange and added more yogurt to balance out the tahini. Tahinis vary widely so a more mellow, less bitter one would be great here.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: This was amazing. Colored carrots make it festive. Add extra pistachios. Will be making again soon!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: How far ahead can you make the tahini dressing?  The day before?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Definitely can do, it'll thicken a bit in the fridge but it's not a big problem. PS I just decided to make it for Easter, bought waaaay too many carrots haha.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: @Anne You could definitely make it two days in advance! The carrots could also be prepared ahead and rewarmed. The only thing you can’t prep in advance is the garnish, as the herbs would darken and the pistachios lose their crunch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: @Lex Weibel The tahini cream is pretty ordinary for the amount of work it requires to chop up the herbs, nuts and grate the orange rind. I prefer just using homemade hummus along with the toppings.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody:
Great combination of flavours and textures. Strongly recommend using orange if you can, as the zest really adds to the topping. I forgot the garlic in the tahini smear and didn't miss it at all.
A big hit with the household carnivore - will definitely be making again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: This is a winner  exactly as written !

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: We had a very similar dish recently at a Jose Andres restaurant but made with roasted delicata squash. Can't wait to make this!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: These were seriously delicious, I will be making again. The carrots needed 30 minutes in the oven, although I did cut them a bit thick. I was out of curry powder so I used half garam masala and half turmeric

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe without the yogurt?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I feel like cauliflower (over broccoli) would be better with this dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I had to sub lemon for orange because I forgot to buy and orange. I like it with the lemon, but I'll try with orange next time. I also think I'll saute the garlic to mellow it out. We liked it, but the raw garlic was just too strong.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: A tasty combination of flavors and textures. It made a very attractive addition to Easter dinner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Made as written.  I cut the carrots into spears and they cooked faster. Depends on the size of the spears. We’ll probably have the sauce with other roasted vegetables to zhuzh up a plain meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I’d recommend going much heavier on the herbs in the tahini but this recipe is fabulous. I made this in advance and it was great reheated in the evening (also yummy when I snuck a cold carrot straight from the fridge to test it).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Made a carrot liker out my carrot hater—at least with this dish. Used oranges from the tree out back and a mix of parsley and cilantro since that is what we had. Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: wildly tasty! I added quinoa and paired it with homemade sourdough and a bit of leftover salmon for a delicious dinner. I didn't have dill, so I used mint (and thus of course went with lemon instead of orange) and wouldn't have it any other way. doubled the pistachios. Will add to the rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this as a Mother’s Day side and it was so easy but so impressive. I was a bit more liberal with the curry powder, used 2 garlic cloves instead of one, and didn’t put the water in the tahini cream (I didn’t feel it was necessary, but I also had a bit more orange juice in there as I had to get rid of two small oranges. I need to host a dinner party so I can make this for more people.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: If you wanted to make it complete meal and add whole grains or protein would you increase the tahini smear?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Yes! We just made this (scaled up by an extra .5ish) and I think everyone would have gladly scooped up more of the tahini smear if more was available. People were also dredging cuts of rib roast through the smear on their plates. I would say doubling the tahini cream can’t hurt for what you have in mind.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: @Kari even increasing the tahini sauce recipe a tiny bit makes it more frugal. A 5oz cup of plain yoghurt, four tablespoons tahini, squeeze of lemon and two medium grated garlic cloves will provide extra sauce and also not make you put back 1/2 a container of yogurt etc into the fridge

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody:
Tried this with lemons instead of an orange because I had lemons available but not an orange. 
 It was good, but it was a bit too tart.  Will try it again with the orange instead.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I used a lemon instead of orange. Roasted carrots in the toaster oven for 25 min. I sliced fresh radishes and placed them around the edge of the plate.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody:
Wonderful!  

I keep a container of frozen orange juice (remember those?) in the freezer and pop the lid off to scoop out pulp to use in these types of recipes. Works great - just need to adjust the quantity to account for the undiluted intensity. Lid clicks back on and it stays in the freezer for months.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Gosh this was good. I made exactly according to the recipe and used nice mandarins. Lovely. Agree you could make well in advance and simply rewarm and dress the carrots and whip the cream a turn or two before serving. The cream is exquisite and could be used in a thousand different ways. I will be making it anytime I roast veggies. Such a treat!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: yummmm!! use extra pistachios. I used a lemon this time, will use an orange next time for a bit of a sweeter flavor (there WILL be a next time ;) )

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: My tahini sauce was quite whipped.  I had to add extra tahini and then refrigerate for a bit to thicken it up.  I tried a new brand of greek yogurt so I'm wondering if that is to blame.  Overall, a nice and easy side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I've made on multiple occasions. I omit water in step 4! Maybe due to my Tahiti brand but with water it's too runny. I'd recommend holding off adding until you've blended.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: In my personal copy of the recipe I added the orange and juice to the ingredients list. Can't wait to try this !!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Cheated and bought "baby" carrots. Just made the whipped tahini (recommend going with a light colored tahini) using orange juice. Added more plain yogurt and orange juice to taste; would dial back the size of the garlic clove I used. It is SOOOO good! Almost like a frosting!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I take a little butter and blacken my carrots stove top in my cast iron pan. 15-20 minutes until they are really blackened all around. The char mixed with the sweetness is a wonderful contrast.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Could you please suggest a substitute for the yogurt to make this dairy free?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: You could definitely do cashew cream with a bit of lemon for the tang.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Made this for the first time last night - very impressed! I'll make it again for myself and would happily serve to others to others.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody:
Absolutely delicious. My tahini was a little bitter, so I added a tiny bit of honey to the spread. 

I made this for a casual get together, and my friend said that this is the dish to make if I ever need to impress someone.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody:
This was underwhelming.

My sauce came out much thinner than the recipe despite following the directions. It also didn't have the depth I'm usually looking for in flavors. I suspect this will only work correctly with a very thick Greek yogurt like Fage (I used Chobani Greek whole milk). I also thought the orange was too muted and lemon would have been a better choice. 

The dill-pistachio topping was pretty but didn't add much besides a somewhat discordant dill note.

Overall verdict: MEH.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. Made as instructed. No dill, so replaced it with fresh mint, so good! Don´t hold back on the salt, it´s a key ingredient!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Prepared exactly as instructed and it was phenomenal. It will be in my regular dinner rotation. My husband, not a native veggie lover, couldn’t get enough.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. I used pistachio flavored tahini and it added some extra nuttiness. Will make again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely loved this recipe!  Definitely will be making it as a new side dish at Thanksgiving this year!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: This is akin to an expensive side at an upscale restaurant. Unforgettable! I mucked with the recipe a lot which is par for the course for me. Added Calabrian chili oil to tahini whip as well as finished with smoked Maldon salt. Used lemon rather than orange. And went heavy on curry powder.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Very yummy.  I used skyr, lemon, and almonds because that's what I had on hand.  I heeded the advise of others and doubled the whipped tahini. It's a lovely, creamy bed for almost any other roast veggie.  Although, in hindsight I'd maybe adjust the lemon juice to taste.  Skyr is quite tangy on its on and doubling the lemon made the whipped tahini a bit too mouth puckering for my taste.  In any case, it's a very easy and tasty side dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Could I use a non dairy yogurt instead?  Cooking for a dairy-free and wondering if it will whip up well with one of the substitutes (coconut yogurt etc)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely! Forager or Kite Hill would wok!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I couldn’t get dill, so I used some mint I had on hand. Worked really well!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I replaced Greek yoghurt with vegan Greek Joghurt- delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Absolute banger. I didn't think dill quite fit the vibe so used mint and it was a good choice. Used runnier tahini so had to add more yogurt which was fine because it got eaten up so hast. Added some honey to balance the tart/bitter/earthy. Sprinkled in some pomegranate seeds on top of the yogurt for a little burst of color and flavor. 10/10 would make again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I made this with blood orange instead of regular orange. I used 2 bunches of carrots, probably could have gotten away with three. Loved it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: An immersion blender is perfect for making the tahini cream

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I made this for a late afternoon work holiday party with several vegetarians in attendance, and it was a huge hit! I had leftover ingredients, so I had more with my dinner last night and I'm planning to include it in my Christmas menu when I host my family. I used lemon, but will try orange in the future. I just love dishes with layers of flavor--this is definitely one! It's also quite easy and quick.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this four times this Christmas season already. Platter always cleared and friends always request the recipe!!!! I kind of wing the topping in terms of ratios — I like a lot of pistachio and I think it’s lovely with tons of topping!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious as is. Don’t change anything and use an orange, not a lemon. Yum yum.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Looooved this!  skipped the dill since I don't really like it.  I drizzled the sauce over the top and finished with the pistachio mixture.  I put a bit more freshly squeezed OJ in the sauce since I like a citrus kick.  Play with it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: My family loves this dish! We made it for Thanksgiving last year but now I make it for parties because it gets specially requested. It's fine served cold, but we really do love scooping up the dip on freshly roasted carrots and veggies.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: What would be a good protein to pair with this?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Chicken- fish - ground meat - lamb- carrots are great with them all

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I had some leftover silken tofu miso dressing, a fabulous NYT recipe.   I use that as my base for the roasted carrots and it was fabulous. Rave reviews from my guests.  I really like the combination of the curried roasted carrots, pistachios, and dill! I think it was important, not to add too much of the curry powder as that could be overpowering with the carrots

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I made this very successfully with Tofutti non-dairy sour cream

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Can I just note, you can't make this for Pesach unless you eat kitniyot. (Sesame seeds are kitniyot.)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Not only stunning on the plate….. also Delicious. You will not be disappointed if you make this. It was so good I made it three times for different guests in the week …. lol

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody:
WOW!!!

My husband and I ate the entire dish by ourselves. Incredibly wonderful. Such a stunning combination of flavours and textures - I will make this for every party I host from now on. Brilliant.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: These were such a hit!! They look beautiful on the table. Everyone was wowed by them. I made the tahini cream in advance to save time day-of. Perfect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Easter lunch, everyone LOVED it. I used lemon rather than orange. The carrot greens on our 2 bunches were really fresh so I chopped those up finely and used it with the other ingredients in the topping. It was a delicious addition!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed and this is, simply, an awesome dish.  It was so good, we made it again the following day -- and STILL loved it.  Truly a keeper!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I made this as a first course for a vegetarian dinner with Käsespätzle and salad as mains. Everyone loved it and it was a nice not too filling vegetable entree. Great recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I used roasted acorn squash sprinkled with za'atar, and drizzled it with pomegranate molasses and chopped mint.  I used lemon as the option for the whipped tahini.  It was a huge hit!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: This. Was. Extraordinary. Made it for myself because husband was going out, now I'm going to repeat it for dinner for him tomorrow. Made exactly as written, except I didn't have any pistachios. EXCELLENT.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: You could definitely swap out the yogurt for cashew cream! Thanks for the inspo!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: @Mary try non-dairy yogurt (soy, coconut, almond, etc )!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: What would folks suggest to use as a substitute for yogurt to make recipe vegan?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Can I make this with a non-dairy yogurt ?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: This was a HUGE hit! It was relatively easy and totally worth it; I served it at a dinner party and everyone raved about it. I didn't have dill or chives (store was out of both) but I used fresh parsley and it worked well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe is fantastic! I sliced off the green tops of the carrots leaving about 2” of green stems and sliced the carrots down the middle. Drizzled olive oil and sprinkled curry powder and S/P then grilled them. 
Followed the recipe but added a little honey to the whipped yogurt/tahini sauce and used parsley and chives for the pistachio herb topping and it was amazing. Definitely will make the whipped spread and pistachio topping thing for other grilled veggies… it’s so good. Very versatile.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this now twice — doubling the recipe both times— and there are never leftovers of this dish. I omitted the salt for a cardio dieter and it’s not noticeable.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: very good and will make it again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Wow! Absolutely delicious. Wouldn’t change a thing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, no notes!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I have had this in my “for dinner” list for 2 weeks. Finally got around to making it today and realized I am emergency-room allergic to one of the key ingredients (madras curry). I clearly was overlooking that key ingredient because the rest of it sounded so delish. I substituted with a mix of ground ginger, s&p, and tumeric. Still pretty good: the roasted carrot with the pistachio/herb/zest and the tahini schmear was fab. Served with grilled chicken.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I think Zaatar would have been a good sub for the curry powder considering the other ingredients. Not sure which ingredient you were allergic to in the curry powder

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Really great! Grilled the carrots instead of roasting and they were wonderful. But for the sauce, I should have held off on the water; it came out very thin and I had to build it up with more tahini and some olive oil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Really really great dinner party pleaser. I used a little extra orange and guests loved the zest. I also used walnuts instead of pistachios because that what I had in the pantry and was just as good. I would recommend using heirloom rainbow carrots for a bit of extra colour to the plate. Will definitely make this again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Did any one try without yogurt? Any suggestion on substitutions?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: This is a keeper. Use the orange. I used Madras curry, not sweet, as carrots are already sweet. Added Aleppo pepper to whipped tahini and a good squeeze of lemon to brighten. No dill in the house, tarragon worked well with the grated orange and pistachios. I returned the cooked carrots to the bowl I tossed them in and drizzled the oil runoff over finished dish with a sprinkle of Aleppo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I cut the carrots in larger chunks than called for in this recipe. I think larger pieces of carrots helps to bring out their flavor.  Otherwise I made it as written. Very tasty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: hey! i am allergic to pistachios (and most tree nuts) any ideas for subs? would this work with pumpkin seeds or peanuts?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: @Allison. How do you not know this by now?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: If you want to make this the main and star of a vegetarian meal, you almost certainly will want to double the tahini/yoghurt. I did and it was all gobbled up.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Meh... Alison Roman’s recipe for Sticky Roasted Carrots with Citrus and Tahini is better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Wow this was excellent! Needed 30 minutes for the carrots but otherwise perfect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Stunning and delicious on our Thanksgiving table!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Tried to use tangerine, it can't be grated. Use lemon or get the organic orange.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: These got mixed reviews - definitely a different flavor profile than normal roast carrots and some people were not fans (used berbere and OJ). They do not make a great leftover if you've assembled everything for presentation on a plate because you wouldn't want to microwave the yogurt/tahini sauce or the dill/pistachio topping (so we ended up letting it come to room temperature, ish, and just eating it at room temp). I enjoyed, but it's a bit too fussy and reviews were too mixed to make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: my friend made this for thanksgiving than I made it. Hers was exceptional but mine was lacking something. My guess is because I used orange juice and not lemon. I suggest only making the sauce with lemon. 10/10 !

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe but my carrots were not super sweet so felt it needed a few raisins or a drizzle of honey. Next time I will use sweeter carrots.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe! I subbed out a lemon for the orange and added some agave, was delish! Did the carrots on the pellet smoker, would highly recommend.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe a few times and it’s always a hit. If you don’t have pis I went with salted almonds and it

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe a few times and it’s always a hit. If you don’t have pistachios around, roasted salted almonds are a good replacement.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: This was yummy.  Made this pretty much as written with the orange juice from some mandarins and a tiny bit of lime.  The magic is the counterpoint.  Dark spice carrot with creamy, nutty sunshine and then herby crunch.  We get competitive around here and our household loves this kind of ammunition - where everyone wins, of course, because it's so good.  But, you know, we win.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Made this as a side for chicken shawarma and rice with a combo of yellow and ruby carrots, plus some kohlrabi I wanted to use. Everyone loved it! I am in nyc and had spreads from Breads Bakery in the fridge, so skipped the whip step and smeared my platter with their herby tzatziki on one side and their great and already pretty whippy tahini on the other. 🙌 The orange juice went on the vegetables and the zest/pistachio/dill sprinkle makes the dish. Don't hold back!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I made this with a combination of new carrots and sweet potato. Threw some cubed extra firm tofu on the sheet to roast as well. Lovely combination of flavours and textures! Will definitely make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I haven’t made this yet but was thinking of using the tahini smear as a dip with fresh veggies. Any thoughts?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Amazing recipe. Somehow transforms a simple vegetable into something entirely new and delicious and exciting. My wife said this has become her “new favorite”. Vegetables? I think it’s her new favorite food?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I absolutely loved this dish. I replaced fresh fennel for the prescribed herbs and loved every bite.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: The tahini part of this recipe in and of itself is great. I’ve been using leftovers of it to add to sautéed vegetables that I also add farro to as a great side dish. I think I could eat cardboard if it was dipped in the tahini sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: My first try was delicious - I used whole milk yogurt instead of Greek yogurt and it came out a bit runnier than I wanted. I'll try again with thicker yogurt!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: This outstanding ! Treat your self and make it! I agree double the smear.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: amazing!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Amazing!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Double it. You will not regret making more!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: My tahini cream is a liquid - not sure what I have done wrong? I blended in the blender - not a cream at all. Any suggestions?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: some tahini bottles have to be stirred as the thicher part is at the bottom; if you only used the top you got the loose/oily part

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I loved everything about this. It's definitely worth making extra whipped tahini; it makes a great dipping sauce for pretty much any meat or vegetable.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I don’t ever comment on recipes, but I had to on this one. This recipe is phenomenal. I grilled some chicken breast cutlets to accompany this dish - marinated with greek yogurt, lemon, garlic powder, olive oil, and S&P for about 30 minutes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous!! I followed the recipe with TJs pared rainbow carrots. . Loved by everyone and a beautiful presentation!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Double or triple everything! We loved this —served it as a side dish with Halibut and sautéed fiddleheads for dinner, and then had it the next day for lunch with the addition of chickpeas and pumpkin seeds. Beautiful, easy, delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Easy and tasty side dish. Highly recommend. I made extra pistachio topping.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I used parsley, lemon, and pumpkin seeds. Here to say it was INCREDIBLE! I toasted the pumpkin seeds then gave them a pulse in the dry blender. Made a double batch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious! I used lemon zest and juice cos that’s what I had, and mint as my herb - all great substitutes! I had some Berber spice blend (try to find it if you can), don’t skimp on it! And then make sure to drizzle the spicy olive oil from the carrots over the dish..  maybe even use extra for that purpose. Oh and extra pistachios. Ate the whole lot with pitta breads, and wished for a chilled glass of wine… next time!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: So good!  I’m not a tahini lover so I subbed it for feta and kept everything else per the recipe.  Will make again and loved it warm and also as cold leftovers.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this a few times. Amazing recipe! Couple of tips: don’t add water to tahini combo until the end as you may not need it. Also use tahini that has been stirred and refrigerated. An immersion blender better controls the whipped effect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Carrots With Whipped Tahini Recipe
commentBody: Making this for the third time! Lovely, textured recipe that is easy, as others noted, to prepare in advance and then assemble. Today I replaced half the carrots with okra because I have an abundance from the farmers market. Last Shabbat I subbed the curry powder for ras el hanout, with made the dish pair nicely with David Tanis' Moroccan Baked Fish With Onions. The whipped tahini is perfect smeared on challah!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: I always add turnips or parsnips for an earthy note.  But this is a great and traditional recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: This was a very nice side dish. I used purple cabbage which provides a much more appealing dish than the photo provided above. The crunch topping was a nice addition, however, I think a melting cheese may be a better option, as my cheese was very hard. Or maybe a dry cheese? I also baked it in the skillet I sauteed the cabbage in which worked well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: I made this last weekend, exactly as written, and served with a green salad. It was a delicious dinner and everyone loved it, even the kids!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: I made this for dinner this evening and we enjoyed it. I made it as written. It’s creamy and delicious- comfort food. Hubby gave it an enthusiastic thumbs up.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: Made it 90% as written. Just making it for myself so I didn’t need it to look as nice as it might with the panko crust. Skipped the panko and just added the herbs and cheese. Delicious! Excellent use of the glut of potatoes I have in the house.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: What a great way to use all the leftover potatoes and cabbage from St. Patty's Day dinner!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: Flat out delicious- I almost stopped when the base was done - it’s delicious on its own!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: Less potato, double white cabbage, double mustard, and the topping can be easier: mix panko, cheese, thyme and sage well, spread it over the dish and add some flakes of butter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: Sautéed a generous amount of sliced mushrooms before adding the brussels sprouts. Combined that with the potatoes. The mushrooms added some lovely texture and savory flavors.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: Interesting to see a Scot variant on the dish known as Colcannon in Ireland, or Champ in Northern Ireland.  Essentially cabbage and potatoes, butter and salt with options of onions, scallions, even leeks depending on your family’s traditions.  The cheddar is an option I’ve yet to try but will add it to my list.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody:
Made with cabbage. 
Delicious 
Froze well

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: We added finely chopped kale and onion sautéed separately and then added in at rumbledethump time. Could’ve cooked with the cabbage but thought of it too late. Anyway—Delicious!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: For topping, I had accidentally mixed dry ingredients with the cheese, so I melted butter,  let it cool, stirred it in just before topping and baking. Since topping was still crispy and good and this saved time, I'd do it that way again. Very yummy and a keeper. Manages to be dinner/lunch on its own. Worthy Thanksgiving side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: This was so good! So much flavour. I did not have breadcrumbs so just topped with cheddar cheese and broiled for a few minutes - worked great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: We used 1.5 lbs little yellow potatoes and 1.5 lbs sweet potatoes and it was really tasty! The topping was delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions for reheating the left-overs? I have half the dish in the fridge.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: This was great! I added some chopped ham for more protein. Filling, warm, comforting, and delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: I riced the potatoes which was a mistake.  The dish needs more bulk, so smashed is the better approach.  Less cream and butter would also let the ingredients shine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: We liked this a lot. Used a full pkg of coleslaw cut cabbage instead of brussels. Had to adjust the onion/sprout cook time down (since the recipe offers it as an option, some guidance would have been nice). The topping is awesome, don’t skip.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: Okay I admit it was the name that had me wanting to try the recipe and I am so glad I did. I make this with golden turnips in place of the potatoes and WOW! A new comfort food favorite, it makes for a popular potluck dish, with many wanting the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Rumbledethumps (Potato Mash With Cabbage and Cheddar) Recipe
commentBody: I agree on bumping up the mustard. At least 2 tablespoons, maybe more.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Get out your garlic press and use it to crack a single layer loosely packed layer of peppercorns. Remove with the point of a knife. Repeat. Nothing runs away.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Mary Great tip, thank you! The flat of a knife never works.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Brilliant! Thanks!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Mary, After cracking two small piles of peppercorns with my chef’s knife, I pulled out my mortar & pestle. I found it much easier to control and contain. I don’t have a garlic press. That sounds even better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Do a reverse sear instead=better.  You have a thermometer right? Oven at 330°,  put steak into the oven, get the core temp to 115°, remove from oven, let the SURFACE cool down to 110° or so (the core will not cool that much) get your cast iron or carbon steel pan up to temp, then brown out side of steak, flipping every 20 - 30 seconds, get to 125°, remove, and let carry over heat get it up to 132°

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Peter Luger's uses the method used in this recipe.  I have used the same method for years to perfection.  I don't understand why anyone would use a  meat thermometer.  Cooking is art, not science.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Powell How, exactly do, you determine when the core temperature of the meat before searing reaches 115° without a thermometer?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Alan Great question. I treated myself to an instant-read digital thermometer several years ago, and have not messed up a steak (or grilled chicken) since then. Bought one for each family member for Christmas the following year. All were very grateful.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Ouch. Looking at the picture of a lovely cut of meat cooked to medium is painful. If that looks tasty to you, make a personal resolution to have a bite of steak that is cooked medium-rare. The difference will astound you. If you like it, next try a bite of rare stake and see where it takes you.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: ribeye is better cooked medium imo, due to the abundance of larger muscle fibers in the cut

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Ribeyes have a lot of internal fat and this fat will not liquify and render until it hits around 135-137F. Cooking a ribeye to medium-rare is an absolute waste, unless you enjoy chewing and chewing and chewing on rubbery, unrendered fat. Save medium-rare for your strip steaks and filet mignon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I have cooked a lot of steak au Poivre in a too long career as a restaurant chef.  I have done the sauce à la minute using brandy and cream and a good veal stock or making the sauce ahead using a reduction of brandy with shallots and peppercorns (crushed green and black, the green can be canned) adding the veal stock and finishing with a bit of cream and some good Dijon mustard.  The steak can be whichever you like or have access to - a Bavette (skirt), hanger (onglé), strip, rib or even filet.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
@James 
YUM. Recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
1) You do NOT need Cognac. Cognac is just brandy made in a particular region of France but it's no better (especially for cooking) than just regular brandy. Any brandy will do, the cheap Paul Masson Amber is actually excellent.

2) In Paris the sauce is typically made with green peppercorns -- they're a nice addition, they add tang because the peppercorns are stored in a vinagery brine. They also make the sauce a little less hot. 

3) In Paris the steak would be cooked rare.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Agree. Cognac is for drinking. Paul Masson Grande Amber for cooking. If you have Cognac or Calvados, use it, but if you're buying for this recipe you can set your sights lower.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
@Dan
Great bourbon works amazingly w

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: What’s all the fuss about using a knife, or a skillet, or a mortar and pestle to crush the peppercorns? If you simply buy a good, relatively inexpensive, adjustable pepper mill you can have a full range from barely cracked peppercorns all the way down to fine powder. No muss, no fuss, and no getting peppercorns all over the place. It’s a basic kitchen tool that any even halfway serious cook should have.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
@Alan
Alan, I have 7 (seven) pepper mills in two locations, none of them inexpensive. I buy Tellicherry black and white peppercorns in bulk and store them in the freezer. You can’t get the same rough cracked pepper from a grinder. I use a mortar and pestle. Just saying. 😊

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I don’t have an adjustable pepper mill, and I’m not buying one just for this recipe—and even if I did, they often don’t grind course enough. That said, using the side of a knife is indeed a fool’s errand. Put in a snack-size ziplock bag and beat with a small pan or pot or rolling pin (or in my case I used a wooden muddler)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Alan I’m with you Alan. The pepper mill does the trick

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Sous vide the steak to 5 degrees less than your preferred end temperature (for me that’s 130F). Then do the sear, and sauce. Absolutely no issue then with timing, doneness or anything else. The inside will not overcook, the exterior will be perfectly seared and the steak does not have to rest.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
@Jeff P

This is what a sous vide does best.  I learned to set the sous vide temp less than my desired final temp 126 in my case because the sear always over cooked.  I also fo

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
@Jeff P 
I use the same technique. Sous vide about 4 or 5 degrees less than desired to account for cooking during sear. Also dry steak after sous vide to improve

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
@Bill
Maybe a silly question, but do you press the peppercorns into the steak before the sous vide?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I add a little Dijon mustard with the cream it adds some extra zip.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Christopher Molinari me too

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Step two is a fool's errand.  Put two tablespoons (that's what I use for any au poivre) in a plastic bag.  Put the bag on solid cutting board, cover with a towel, and beat the hell of it with a flat metal pounding mallet.  Or, do it their way and spend the next week picking up errant peppercorns.  They can have up to twelve-and-one-half food trajectory.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @LewI I put 2 Tablespoons in a plastic bag and beat them ip with a mallet. All I got was misshapen spoons.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Crushing the peppercorns with a cast iron grill weight (looks like an old fashioned flatiron, typically used for burgers and sandwiches) is safer, faster, and much better for your knife.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: no. using the flat part of your knife to crush something brittle and relatively soft like black peppercorns is completely fine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Schezwan peppercorns? that's a hard no.  They cook differently from other peppercorns—they're not actually peppercorns at all; mixed into au poivre sauce...well, not their best use. Do a variation using [pan toasted] schezwan peppercorns made into schezwan salt (75 gm of schezwan peppercorns, 25 gm of kosher salt, stir in a cast iron skillet until the color of the salt begins to change; remove from pan, crush in a mortar and pestle.  You'll have leftover Schezwan salt, use it on everything.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I make something 'similar' I 'invented' years ago. I first dry brine the steak by salting it with kosher salt and leaving it uncovered overnight in the refrigerator to dry which improves the flavor, texture and it sears better.  I then reverse sear it, sear it and add green peppercorns instead of black (without their brine) and vermouth instead of the cognac.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Eating rare red meat can lead to ocular toxoplasmosis causing retinol scarring and vision loss. This actually happened to a dear friend of mine. I stay away from it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I'm genuinely sorry about your friend. There are less than 5,000 cases of symptomatic occular toxoplasmosis each year in the US. I don't know the number of rare beef steaks consumed each year in the US, but my guess is that the absolute risk from this is pretty low... much lower than other risks people take routinely for things far less enjoyable.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
@Bob from Italy
At my medical school we learned that tpxoplasmosis was a parasite acquired from cats.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Paul from Calgary, that’s just one way to contract it. When pregnant, my husband cleaned the litter box, just to be safe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Why use chicken stock rather than beef stock?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Sunny it has much greater depth of flavour and umami. See Kenji.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Store-bought chicken "stock" (the stuff in the 32-ounce cartons) is acceptable to most foodies. But their general consensus is that there is no store-bought beef stock that's any good, meaning that it tastes anything like beef. But if you have the genuine article -- homemade beef stock -- that would certainly be preferable to anything you can buy in the soup aisle at a supermarket.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Sunny I think chicken stock is more neutral a flavor. With beef I believe it makes the end product “too beefy.”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Serve on a thick, heated-but-not-too-hot plate. Eat leisurely.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
I use a mortar and pestle to crush the peppercorns- much easier. Often I add some crushed whole yellow mustard seeds to the peppercorns before pressing them into the steak. Then proceed as written. 
Green peppercorns are good also, but I don’t press those into the meat before cooking or use the black peppercorns. Rather I coarsely chop a tablespoon of green peppercorns and add to the sauce with the stock and use more cream.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: My French family gave me the green peppercorn idea, too. Very simple and tasty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Green peppercorns in brine are another option for the sauce, though they are hard to find.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I would not use a knife to crack pepper corns, risks bending knife blade and you may cut yourself. I use the bottom of a small pan working side to side, easy to get the hang of it. Treat your knives with respect!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: When I make this I low simmer the peppercorns in butter for 5 minutes or so, before putting them on the steak, otherwise they are too strong. The suggestion to use only Szechuan peppercorns for this recipe would be a big mistake - only a very small amount mixed in with other varieties would work.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I have always used fresh green peppercorns preserved in water and brine, they add more flavor to the sauce and embed easier in the meat before cooking, Probably not available when the original recipes were published.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: We haven't eaten steak in a restaurant since I started dry ageing our prime rib...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
@Mark Beeflover
Same here. I have been doing a 30 dry age in the refrigerator for over 20 years. I am over 70 years old and my father was a butcher way back when the shop floors were covered daily with sawdust and the only way beef arrived was as sides that were hung in the cooler for a couple of weeks before being broken down into individual cuts. Wet aged beef is a mushy disgusting abomination.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
@BabbleFish
How do you suggest dry aging in the refrigerator?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: This is a lovely recipe but this is not the steak au poivre I ate my whole life in the many brasseries of Paris - you don’t need cognac to make the sauce au poivre, and the crust is accessory.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: If you're telling us there's a better way to do it, to achieve the steak au poivre that you ate in the many brasseries of Paris, then tell us that better way!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I put the peppercorns in a quart size plastic baggie and then used my wooden rolling pin to crack the peppercorns, first tapping them in a hammering action then rolling them. Afterwards, I simply poured out into a small mise en place bowl. Again as in earlier comments, no mess and little effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I hate pepper and peppercorns (I know.) Any suggestions from anyone about a good substituion?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @aig have you tried green peppercorns in brine? A different flavor and very good (some would argue the traditional ones used). If those don’t appeal then steak au poivre probably isn’t going to be the best choice for you.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: The name itself in French is "steak with pepper", so maybe use this recipe for general how-to cook steak?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: like asking for a kosher substitute for a roast pork,  steak with pepper needs pepper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: After you crush the peppercorns by whatever method you choose, put them into a small fine mesh strainer and shake. All of the fine pepper dust will come out and you have nice coarse pepper left, no dust which looks snd feels gritty and burns easily. Try it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
Times change. But filet mignon used to be THE cut for this dish. In fact, this or a couple of other sauces are typically called for with fillet, given the relatively mild flavor. It's also the best cut if someone absolutely insists on more well done than heaven should allow.

I used to use the plastic-bag-and-whack method for the peppercorns, but I've since found that just using the coarsest possible grind on my Perfex mill produces a fairly even large grind quickly and neatly.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I'd recommend beef stock for the sauce, instead of chicken stock.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Josh Many great chef's and people with great palates prefer ribeye steak cooked to a true medium.  See what Jessica said 5 hours ago.  So don't knock it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I banged this one out last night and was hailed as a champion of the people! Use a mortar and pestle for the peppercorns. Warm the serving platter if possible. Always keep a bottle of brandy around the house.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
1. Put peppercorns in a ziplock bag and hit with a pan or tenderizer hammer for even breakage of the peppercorns.
2. Browning steak 4 mins per side in a searing hot pan is way too much time and will result in a well done steak especially if you put it in a 425 F oven.  Try 2 mins per side for a 2 inch steak; steak will release and not stick to the pan.  Take the temp of the steak before putting into oven. 
3. Use beef broth instead of chicken broth to get better, deeper color on the sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: To add to my previous comment.  Yes, season the steak and let it sit at room temp for up to an hour.  Coat with a combination of black and green dried peppercorns crushed on both sides of the steak and sear in a hot pan.  Finish in the oven to the temp you like or in the pan with the sauce.  Voila.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Don’t shoot me but I prefer beef very well done. Temperature for well done?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: The sauce was bland and thin.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Followed directions to the letter. Result: overcooked steak, medium, not close to medium-rare, and broken, greasy sauce. Lesson learned. Next time: back to my usual method of reverse-sear, then wipe grease out of pan and proceed with deglazing with Cognac, etc.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious. We followed the recipe as written, and I wouldn't change a thing. we included the 2 T of cream, which I normally wouldn't do, but it was a special occasion - it's not like it's not a splurge dish anyway - and I would include it going forward as well. I only wish I'd tried this sooner!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Alas, there is no mention of sous vide as an option. Sous vide has the advantages of reverse sear technique, but is far more precise. Cook the steak sous vide to your preferred temperature (mine is 127°F, rare. if you prefer medium-rare, make it 132°F). You do not have to worry about an overshoot because the steak temperature is entirely uniform after sous vide. Then, immediately sear according to the recipe. Remember, the steak is already cooked, so keep the sear times short. Perfectly cooked.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
