commentBody: I made this for Valentines dinner. My husband and I both loved it. I used a thermometer to monitor it while in the oven and followed the recipe except I used tawny port because I didn’t have cognac or brandy. The smashed peppercorns made a crunchy crust after being fried in the oil on the stove and remained so after the oven. The sauce was delicious and we fried some eggs in the iron skillet the next morning in the remains. OMG!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Is there any suitable sub for the cognac or brandy?  Preferable N/A.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: The alcohol cooks off in no time, so I don't understand the need for an alternative.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
Peppercorn procedure is too fussy and they'll end up on the counter and the floor.  Put the peppercorns in a plastic bag and hit them with a metal meat tenderizer or a hammer.  It's really not as fussy as the recipe makes out.

I also agree that the reverse sear is a better procedure and avoids the overdone edges of the meat shown in the photo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I am a vegetarian since 1994, but I live with and cook for my son and his family. They get meat from their local farm here in middle Tennessee and I was to prepare steak for dinner this past Wednesday. So this recipe came up in my inbox and even though I grew up on a farm in ME, we never cooked our meat in this fancy way. The directions were so easy to follow and I had all the ingredients at hand so... it came out beautifully. I couldn't resist trying a tiny bit of the sauce...delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
Not sure where the chicken stock is used....  If it's for deglazing, 3/4 cup is way too much!
And, yes, medium is way too far overcooked.  I prefer the reverse sear method.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Bret Stock is used in Step 7

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: what is a "scant" tablespoon?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: About 2 3/4 teaspoons.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: My favorite cut of steak is porterhouse, NY strip on one side and filet mignon on the other and if you cook it sous vide for 2-3 hours the ny strip will be as tender as the filet with all the flavor. I have a butcher that cuts it with a bandsaw to the thickness I want. I’ve had it up to 3” thick, perfectly cooked and easily served four people.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: To make this recipe even more decadent, add a good knob of Roquefort ,Stilton, or Gorgonzola cheese ro the sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Why not crush the peppercorns in a mortar and pestle?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I found it much easier to use a mortar and pestle and "hammer" the peppercorns in small amounts than using the side of the knife.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I forgot how good and EASY steak au poivre is!! For the pepper corns I put them in a flat bottom plastic bowl and used a jar with a metal lid to smash them. It was quick and the bowl kept the pepper corns from flying away.  We got a rib eye and it was perfectly cooked without putting it in the oven. We rested it for about 10 minutes while I finished the sauce. YUM!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: We use butter in iron pan heated to almost browning and frying a salted and peppered flank steak about 4 minutes, turning over, then 3 minutes.  Turn off heat, leak steak in pan another 10 minutes.  Deglaze pan with heavy cream for a tasty sauce that goes with whipped or baked potatoes...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Any steak au poivre I’ve had in France is made with green peppercorns.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: put peppercorns in small ziploc bag and use heavy weight, cast iron pan or meat pounder, to crush. I've never had a pepper mill that would produce as coarse a grind as is desirable here.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Asking for a substitution without pepper corns is like Arthur Schwartz of old on WOR used to say is like asking for a kosher substitute for a roast pork.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I just cooked this tonight, and it was divine as written.  I used two  very thick filet from Trader Joe's that were superior to any I have ever purchased before...maybe 21/2 inches thick, that worked perfectly with the instructions for this recipe.  Smashed the pepper corns with my trusty mallet (!).  The sauce was perfection.  I read the comments after we finished dinner, and frankly, was stunned that people didn't just love this as written.  I will, however, try green peppercorns next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I made this last night, using one thick strip steak that we shared.  (More than enough.) Followed the recipe exactly and it was fabulous!  Nothing we’ve had in the finest restaurant has compared.  Can’t wait to make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I’m a competent to moderately good griller who has prepared surf and turf at home for Valentine’s Day for 15 years now and this is by far the best steak method I have yet tried. I used thick, high-quality filet mignon medallions. Because they didn’t produce much in the way of pan drippings, I added a dollop of beef tallow when starting the sauce. Also, the steaks were so thick that three minutes in the oven wasn’t nearly enough — they needed more like 8-9 minutes and still came out medium-rare. Probably the first time I would put a home-cooked steak on par with that from a high-end steak house. Still thinking about this beef days later and can’t wait to try it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Can anyone tell me why the salt in this recipe is kosher (which I assume is called for because of the larger crystals)? Reading the application of salt, i’m not getting why basic salt can’t be used? I mean, technically of course it can, but what is the reason/function for using kosher salt in this recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I think green peppercorns in brine, drained and added before the cognac is absolutely essential. Also crushing more than one pepper type at the beginning such as white, red, tellicherry and even Szechuan  adds more nuanced heat and taste.  But green peppercorns in brine is an absolute must.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I just made this for Mr Meat and Potatoes--He loved it. Didn't have a shallot so used half an onion. I didn't have cognac (or my usual substitute, vermouth) so just used 3/4 of a cup of beef broth.  1 Tbsp each of butter and cream and a dab of mustard. I've never done this cooking method and it worked perfectly.  It was delicious and I'll make it again, hopefully closer to how it is written

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I made the sauce and topped roasted cauliflower instead of steak. The combination is unbelievably delicious! I just sautéed the peppercorns in a bit of oil and added the rest of the sauce ingredients. A showstopper in our opinion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: So so tasty! I made it with small potatoes and it was very tasty! Took me less than an hour to make and perfect as a special treat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Made this as written and it was delightful.   I heartily endorse using a mortar and pestle for crushing the peppercorns and recommend you not  treat the heavy cream as optional.   A keeper for sure.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Over-the-top good! (Don't overcook.)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I had a huge rib eye - closer to 2” and 1.7 lbs. Needed 12 min in the oven altogether. Use that thermometer!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Hot Tip: Double the sauce and use it to dip roasted potatoes -- MMMMM.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: “Crack whole peppercorns evenly” or buy a Pepper Canon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Just wondering whether you could use a London Broil instead.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Wondering, NY from my experience, the London broil is a tougher cut which is often tenderized one way or another. It’s a great meat but the technique to prepare it would be different, unfortunately.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
London Broil is a recipe, not a cut of meat. What one merchant sells by that name can be just about anything, and by another can be anything else. They usually appear to be one of the poorer sirloin relatives.

Note that I typically go for flank steak for London Broil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Wow.  Wish we lived closer to each of you, were best friends, and were invited to your house for dinner on a regular basis.  What fun it must be to like actually "cooking".  Wonderful comments!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
Like someone had mentioned, I just used a coarse setting of a pepper mill and the pepper comes out the size needed.  I used less pepper than the recipe since 3 tbsp is a lot of pepper.  I also use a thinner cut of ribeye (less than 1” thick) and found I don’t need to pop it in the oven.  The 8ish minutes of searing was sufficient to cook the steak to the doneness of my liking which is medium rare.

I served it with mashed potatoes and family wolfed it down.  It’s a very good recipe, o easy to make and tasty.  The sauce is very good on both steak and potatoes.  I think I’ll double the sauce the next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Can you use a mortar and pestle to coarsely  crack the peppercorns?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: David Tanis offered a really good version of a Steak au Poivre in the NYT as well—we’ve referred to it many times.  He uses crème fraiche instead of cream.  Also, like other commenters we’ve mixed in green peppercorns.  The mound of watercress served with it is key.  Always a hit!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: There are no added peppercorns in the pepper sauce—just the residual from cooking the steak in same pan? Do I have that right?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Exact same technique i use for thick cuts of meat  except i crank my oven to 500° F.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this in Arles France for my wife on Valentines Day.  I closely followed the recipe except I used Filet Mignon and substituted Tawny Port for the Cognac.  I had this with potatoes with truffle sauce.  I must say it was incredible!  I wonder is it true that the food in France is just superior to what you can buy in the USA?  I have found that the food in the markets is soooo delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @aig  The name itself in French is "steak with pepper", so maybe use this recipe for general how-to cook steak?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: So... I am allergic to pepper. Is there anything else one can do to approximate the benefits of the peppercorns for the coating and the zing. Whole grain mustard, black sesame seeds????This may be an idiotic question, but I thought I'd ask.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Lots of opinions about the steak temp here.  I'm a mid-rare fan in general but not with a well marbled thick ribeye.  Medium is much more palettable, 130 ish.  I look forward to trying this au poivre sauce with my next cook

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Degrees C or F ?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Came out great but took a lot longer than three minutes in the oven.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Created exactly as advised (except I added more butter to the sauce) and it was superb. Also had a lovely grass fed puece of London Broil so I adjusted the cooking time appropriately for a perfect medium-rare. Hadn't made it in years ane a fabuloys Valentine's Day dinner. d we hav

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Too much oil! Sauce came out super oily. You would do just fine with 1 tbsp. instead of 2. You aren’t frying the streak. Otherwise flavors were very good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: For the love of everyone you love (it’s Valentine’s Day), please do not use canola or vegetable oil again.  Do the research.  Ghee, Avocado, coconut, or EVOL.  Love and health to all…

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: @Amy La Credible research indicates that vegetable and canola oil are fine for human consumption. The panic around seed oils is hype and misinformation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I haven't tried this recipe yet, but smashing peppercorns with this way could result in messy scattershot. Place the peppercorns in a small paper bag (like the kind that your prescriptions come in at CVS), flatten to one layer, and with a mallet (preferably a metal kitchen mallet), smack the bag a few times until the contents are the desired consistency. I do this with nuts for recipes. It will be a crushing success!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious.  Wouldn't change a thing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I used my electric pepper mill with the grind setting on coarse to get the desired large bits of pepper flakes. Worked pop perfectly

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Very good!  Rolled the steak into course pepper.  Used bourbon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Homemade stock is obviously the best, but if using store bought chicken or beef stock for this, blooming some powdered gelatin in the stock really improves the texture of the sauce. Just remember to add it to room temp or cooler stock.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Brilliant. I added a tiny bit of Dijon mustard in the sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe, but the two tablespoons of oil in a well-seasoned cast iron pan is a bit much. Nothing really to deglaze. I didn’t have brandy, so I used bourbon. The sauce was delightful. Of course, it was a bit sweet, but earthy at the same time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!  Prepared as suggested!  To die for!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I never slice steaks into thin strips on the central serving platter. It releases too much juice, and the steaks cool from optimum temperature much too quickly. Yes, the whole cut steak on the serving platter is Insta-worthy, but I’m not here for that. Let me cut my own pieces on my own plate!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Added mushrooms to the shallots and was delicious. Used oat milk instead of cream, and it worked well. Watch timing: Steak was about 1.5 inches and we decreased time (3 minutes each side, two minutes in oven) and it was medium.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Use a good quality beef stock. Also you can slow cook the steak in the oven at a much lower temp after searing if not in a rush. Much more flavorful

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Excellent option for when you want to cook to impress! The instructions were particularly helpful for this recipe. Watch carefully and use the meat thermometer to make sure you don't overcook the steak. Will definitely try some of the other methods for cracking peppercorns the next time I make this - I cooked this for Valentine's day and I'm still finding peppercorn bits scattered around the kitchen.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
This looks like a serviceable method and I'll try it.

But I'm commenting on the rather comical "rare meat" absolutism noted below. De gustibus, one would think, non est disputandum. But the rareness of meat seems, for some, a weirdly moral issue.

My preference for non-rare meat has to do with mouth-feel, not flavour; so, no matter how urgently yon Josh presses his personal preference on me, unless he can rewire my neurology I'll continue as I am.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: 132℉ is very nearly "well-done" in my book. Give me ~120-122℉ final internal temp to be happy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I didn't do the peppercorns or the sauce, I just did the basic steak cooking and I loved the result. It's now my go to method. Fast, easy and delicious!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Obviously,  check the done-ness of your steak with your finger (or instant-read thermometer if you feel more confident). However, in my opinion, 8 minutes in the cast iron and an additional 3 in the oven is likely going to hammer a 1 1/2-inch ribeye. Trust your feel. Otherwise this is a great classic recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: This was so good and easy.The sauce was the best!I didn't bother with trying to crush the peppercorns. I just used a peppercorn grinder.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Great sauce, but best to put the seared ribeye in a different pan in the oven. Then you can work on the sauce while the steak is cooking. It takes a lot longer to cook the sauce down before the steak is done. If want to reduce the pepper spice first cook the cracked(hammer works) pepper in oil, then after its has cooled and ready to use strain with oil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: This goes against every recipe I have ever worked with.  Now that we have all the cooking shows, this also goes against what they consistently show.  Learning to time everything is all part of learning how to cook.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: My steak was a tiny bit over 1.5” thick and cooking time for medium rare was just a bit under what she suggested. I did about 3.5 min per side, 3 min in oven. Didn’t have chicken stock or cognac so used beef stock and white wine. Lovely recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I assume you are using the terms "skillet" and "pan" to mean the same item.  It's confusing.  Since you specifically mention "cast iron skillet" I suggest sticking with "skillet" every time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: This was really fantastic. Everyone loved it and I felt compelled to tell 4 more people about it! It has only nine ingredients which is another huge plus. There’s a little piece left and it’s all mine!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I really enjoyed this meal. I didn't use heavy cream, but it still was very good. Also, I dont really think the type of steak matters very much although fancier steaks do tend to be a bit better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: It matters if you value tenderness and flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: This has become a family favorite! It's simple and delicioius.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: So good! Made this rib-eye and now will try it with t-bone and NY strip

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I made this and my steak went cold while making the au police sauce. Any tips on how to keep the steak warm? The sauce takes a while.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Made this recipe with my boyfriend, and we absolutely devoured it. It paired really nicely with this (https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1026296-endive-and-arugula-salad) arugula salad recipe. It was the perfect, crisp, bright bite next to the buttery richness of the au poivre. Would make it every weekend if I had the time!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
I made this with - chuck roast!  9.99 a pound!  I roasted the meat at 260 degrees after browning it. I added a bit of broth and covered it. A single pound for 3 hours. It melted off the fork. 

I made the sauce separately , using pan drippings. Then I added cooked broccolini, rolled it up in a pinwheel with Lavash bread and served as an appetizer with the sauce. 

Dog Field Devine!!  Super affordable. Lovely twist.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Very flavorful!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: This is INSANELY delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I tried Mary's tip about using the garlic press to crack the pappercorns and this broke my garlic press. The peppercorns were so hard that when I squeezed it, they pushed right through the bottom grating of the press. I had to buy a new one.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Lots of suggestions as to how to crush the peppercorn. Having worked in a French restaurant for many years, I suggest pointing them in a tray where the peppercorns can't run away from you and crush with the bottom of a small sauce pan. I say crush, not grind. Also, when cooking the meat, be sure to have your pan, whether you do an initial search of after sear, screaming hot to start. This goes for small filets of fish like sole. Get your pan hot to avoid boiling your meat or fish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Recipe is odd.  If you are going to sear 4 minutes on each side - 8 minutes total -- why bother at all with the oven for 3 minutes (might as well reduce the temp to medium/low after the initial searing and finish on the stove top so you can keep a eye on the temperature by feel or thermometer - much simpler).  Also, if you cook your steak to 132 degrees, it will be medium after resting for 5 minutes so you are going to overcook it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I have cooked steak of this thickness, 1.5inch regularly.  If you are using a hot skillet, ( I put the skillet on the burner for 3 minutes to pre heat it for searing) 3 minutes on each side is more than sufficient to achieve nice brown outside and medium rare inside.  4 minutes and you will have overcooked the meat.  After the 3 minutes, you don't need to put the meat into the oven at all, just let it rest.  I use a torch to finish the edges.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Fill a small jar of black peppercorns then fill the jar with brandy.  Let it sit in the back of your cupboard for a few months. It adds a phenomenal punch to this sauce!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Chicken Stock? Not beef?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
I followed the recipe as written, for once, I did not read the comments prior to preparing. This was THE BEST STEAK I have ever made. 

I made this for our Valentine Day dinner and that was a risk, you never know with a new recipe but the gamble paid off.  I will be getting a mortar/pestle as there are now pepper corns all across my kitchen. That is the only thing I will change… maybe I’ll play with the sauce, it was rich but, for a celebration meal it was great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I did a dry rub with a reverse sear. The steak was juicy and deeply flavorful. The sauce was velvety with an almost mushroom like savoriness. I went light on the peppercorns. Next time I won't hold back. Even so, this was a phenomenal steak.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I followed the comments and did a reverse sear. I made the sauce while the steak rested before getting finished in the pan. The steak turned out phenomenally!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: For a change, I use 1 tbsp whole yellow mustard seeds with 2 tbsp black peppercorns. I use my mortar and pestle to coarsely crush them separately, then mix and press them into the steak and then proceed with the rest of the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Be sure to make enough seasoning to really cover the meat and make a crust.  Ours was not so it could have been a lot better but I can taste the potential.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe. Instead of using the side of a knife, I had a very sharp chef knife, and I literally cut the peppercorns. (and I fear. I’ll have to send it back to the knife sharpener now ). Took more work. I wish I would’ve read the suggestion about using a garlic press. However, it was a simple and delicious recipe. And, given the ridiculous price of steak these days, it was an extravagance that proves once again you can make restaurant quality meals at home!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
Perfect. Perfect. Perfect. 
Did the steak for three minutes each side then into a hot hot even until thermometer read 132. Rested for 15 minutes. Best steak I’ve had in a long time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody:
I made this last night, thinking I was making the America's Test Kitchen Steak au Poivre which was one of the tastiest things I have ever made. It was a very large disappointment.  

I would suggest using the ATK recipe instead....far superior to this one, IMHO.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Made this for my husband for his birthday, it was excellent! Make sure to follow the instructions for not adding too much salt as my sauce came out a bit salty

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: I buy cracked peppercorns in bulk from Penn Mac in Pittsburgh. No need to crack them myself.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Is a non-alcoholic version possible for recovering alcoholics? Even if the alcohol burns off the taste of the liqueur is still there.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: "Cognac or Brandy" jangles. Cognac IS brandy. And for cooking ... any other brandy, I'd say. VS cognac will not cook down to be even slightly better tasting than any generic brandy. Maybe there would be some slight difference with a VSOP or XO, but I'd rather drink it than cook it to make a sauce, especially since it's wit black pepper that's going to blow the doors off subtler flavours. Think of people who want to make something nice for their special person, but don't have money to burn.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Steak au Poivre for Two Recipe
commentBody: Making this for dinner tonight and came up with a great way to crush the peppercorns. Put them between two paper plates and pound with a mallet. Worked great! Can't wait to cook!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
This was amazingly good, and made for great leftovers as well.  It was tasty straight from the fridge.

For the cashew butter, I made my own - I put about 1 1/2 cups of roasted salted cashews in the food processor and pulverized them into a powder.  Then with the motor running I added a teaspoon or two of oil, and it pretty much immediately turned from cashew crumbs to cashew butter after adding the oil.  From there, I added the silken tofu, checked the salt levels and adjusted.  Super easy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Daniel I think your concept of amazingly good and others are, perhaps, diametrically opposed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Cooking the broccoli in a foil pouch does not yield crisp broccoli. The next time I make this dish, I will roast the broccoli without covering it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Greg I wondered about that… why in foil, could not understand it! Thank you for pointing that out.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Greg came here to say this! Loved the dish but next time will roast the broccoli uncovered

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Haven't made this yet because that's what I thought about cooking broccoli in a foil pouch. That's steamed broccoli, IMO, and not a favorite. To roast broccoli cook it part way in the microwave then roast at 425 degrees for texture and better flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Go on, live dangerously..  throw some cashews in the blender and keep your seat belt loosely fastened. What's the worst that could happen?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Thank you.  Thank you.  At last a "creamy" dish that doesn't call for whole cocoanut milk or a 1/2 pint of cream.  Those are both total turnoffs for me.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Peanut butter in place of cashew butter? Garnish with chopped peanuts for a common stocked pantry item.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Allen I like that idea!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Allen or tahini?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Allen cool! I used tahini with a splash of sesame oil. It screamed for lemon juice so WTH. Fantastic!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
When I read the recipe I was intrigued; when I made this for dinner last night I was blown away.  
It was easy, fast and had a minimal number of ingredients, a winner from all angles

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I wonder if i could use food processed cashews instead of cashew butter. Is it the same thing?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Nancy yes! Same thing. I’d go with unsalted nuts :) cashews can be a tad dry so you might have to introduce a little oil or water to get it blending.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Nancy. I would pour boiling water over the cashews and let them soak at least 15 minutes before draining them. Will soften them without having to add more oil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Nancy I used a cup of raw cashews and it worked perfectly!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Terrific, easy, quick and tasty way to prepare broccoli.  Dinner plans were for roasted broccoli but I couldn't resist trying this since I adore chili crisp (Lao Gan Ma).  The spice notes were perfect for us and the broccoli flavor was not obscured by the garnish.  Since I was also baking frozen spring rolls, all I needed was one parchment lined half-sheet pan for both, with a half recipe for the broccoli.  With reheated fried rice, dinner was done in 20 minutes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Very, very delicious. I didn't have cashews at home so subbed with tahini and a peanut and sunflower seed mixture. I also had some korean-flavors inspired cabbage I had cooked early in the week and added that, too. Was so so good. Entered the regular meal rotation the second I took a bite. Wowza!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Very Peased thank you for some substitutions ideas for cashew, peanut allergies- tahini and sunflower!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: When something such as the cashew cream is listed as optional, is it included in the nutritional info?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Partner loved the recipe, this one's a keeper for a healthy quick meal. Not going to bother with cashew butter next time, will use peanut butter- I think the whipped nut tofu will go well in a future Asian noodle dish though!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: What about subbing in tahini instead of cashew butter? Cashew butter just isn’t something we typically have in the pantry.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I totally would sub, just remembering that the flavor of tahini is more Mediterranean/Middle Eastern, whereas cashews fit better with the Asian flavors this recipe aims for. Methinks a splash of unseasoned (unsweetened) rice vinegar might tie the flavors nicely if using tahini.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Emily tahini and a drizzle of toasted sesame oil would fit the Asian flavors perfectly. Or, if you’re lucky enough to have a source, Asian toasted sesame paste. It’s essentially tahini, but the seeds are toasted first.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: As a new bride decades ago, one of the first things I bought was a roasting pan with matching cover.  I never buy aluminum foil, which, like cement, requires a lot of energy to produce [usually from climate changing fossil fuels] and is a single-use disposable  item. Without a covered pan, I wouldn't try this recipe purely on environmental grounds.  There are so many other good recipes that do not involve single-use disposable items...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: It’s completely unnecessary I roasted it uncovered in about 15 minutes and it was perfect…seems strange to me to have to tent, aluminum foil over a baking sheet!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Mel Harte I use aluminum foil over and over by treating it gently and soaking and brushing clean with soap and water after each use. Let dry on drain board, fold nearly and reuse. Why throw away something that’s so useful when it still works?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Patricia I also repeatedly use my aluminum foil and was sort of shocked to learn, based on all the negative comments about foil, that most people do not.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Everyone liked this quick, nutritious meal.  We simplified by just using salted, roasted cashews and no cashew butter.  Cashews puréed down nicely with 12 oz of silken tofu.  I found this purée to be a little bland and added the juice of two limes plus some Aleppo pepper.  We took the top foil layer off of the broccoli for the last few minutes to get it a little crisper on top.  And finally, I did not cook the topping - just chopped cashews and mixed in the chile crisp.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
This is excellent. 
But aluminum foil not needed. Do not cover the broccoli with foil, and wait until the broccoli is almost done (a little crispy) before adding garlic. There. That's better. 
p.s. do not consider making without "cashew cream:" 1.5 cashews toasted, cooled, pureed in food processor. Add 1lb silken tofu and salt to taste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This is good but makes an INSANELY excessive amount of cashew cream, which is especially annoying considering how expensive cashew butter is. I'm amazed I didn't see anyone else comment on this. I have no idea how everyone is eating all this cashew cream. Especially because it's pretty bland as-is. I would make MAX 1/4 the amount next time. Honestly maybe even 1/6. And just try to find another use for the rest of the silken tofu.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Madeleine I completely agree. What a waste of expensive ingredients.  I can’t believe people raved about this.   I actually thought the dish was awful.   No one wanted to eat it. I will review separately, too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Elycia Boyer, I completely agree!! What a waste of money. The cream was too rich for me, and one must really have to love silken tofu to eat it. I love tofu, but not this. Looked better in the video than it really is.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous texture on the cooked broccoli and the chili crunch  is out of this world...had no idea what cashew butter is so just through cashews in small blender, added tofu( which had been sitting in refrigerator for a few months) and water..made a paste. voila....next time I'll shoot for fluffy status....and it was a delicious complement..of flavors..Best method for roasting broccoli ever.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This is an excellent vegetarian recipe using silken tofu and nut butters.  I used about 1 TB peanut butter with 1/4 cup ground cashews when I whipped the silken tofu.  I think 1 cup of a nut butter would be way too much.  I also used 1/4 cup chopped cashews for the topping - cashews are rich so a little goes a long way.  I had a lot of the silken tofu mixture left over, so I am saving it for another meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Made the broccoli and topping as suggested, but lacking silken tofu, I skipped the tofu cashew cream and served it over a smear of hummus instead. Dinner party guests loved it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Substitute roasted Brussels sprouts for the broccoli and skip the foil pouch. Cook the rest as written and boy are you in for a delicious meal!  Tofu cream could be warmed and that would make it a 6 star recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Can I make this without cashews (or any nuts)?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @DLJ I think so. What about flavoring the tofu with ground seeds? E.g. Tahini or sunflower? Or another sauce that would complement the tofu?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @DLJ I’m going to try this with hummus in place of the blended tofu/cashews since I’m allergic to cashews. Will report back

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
@Steph

The photo looked like hummus. I look forward to your review.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Pretty good! I roasted the broccoli uncovered as on other comments and it was great. Used raw cashews soaked in water to make the tofu cashew cream, and then I added a teaspoon of soy sauce and a teaspoon of sesame oil. Next time I would toast the cashews in a dry pan first.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Delightful! Delicious! And so easy to make. It feels like a restaurant meal with all the layers of flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I couldn't see the point of steaming the broccoli in foil, so I omitted the garlic and roasted the broccoli on parchment instead.  After  browning the chopped cashews and breadcrumbs I added the garlic to the topping just until fragrant. I also  added the juice of one lemon and one teaspoon of white miso to the cashew/tofu cream mixture as I thought it was a bit sweet and bland. With these modifications I think I would rate it 4 stars rather than 3.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
This was shockingly delicious and an easy prep! I made the suggested pasta to hi with it, but in the future I will just eat it over quinoa. 

I soaked 1/2 cup of cashews in hot water before adding them into the blender with the cashews.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I’ve been making a dish similar to this for years - love it. I prefer to marinate the broccoli in soy sauce, water & chili oil to plump them up with moisture before blasting them on high heat in the oven on parchment paper. This gets you crunchiness and moisture. I omit the cashew butter: 1 cup of cashew butter adds 1,500 calories! That’s a lot for a vegetable dish. Also there is black truffle chili crisp and honey chili crisp (or add honey separately) that are good to alternate!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Incredibly delicious and loaded with protein.  A keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Used tahini rather than cashew cream. Delish!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious. I ran out of cashew butter and topped it up with tahini. I think I’ll do that again next time! Served with a simple fried rice, this was a quick and easy meal that hit all the notes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
Made it with cauliflower and it was so delicious. 
Since cauliflower needs a bit more cook time, I followed the cook instructions and then removed the foil and cooked another 20 minutes uncovered, stirring once so it would brown evenly. Perfection!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Second note: I made the cashew cream with tahini instead of cashew butter because I always have tahini and never have cashew butter.  I had about half of it left, so I made it into a chocolate tahini mousse... just add the pepper later if this is your plan.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Really not very good.  The chili crisp is the only thing that saves it, and you’re gonna want a lot more at table.  The tofu/cashew cream, which is what I was excited to try out, (protein!) tastes like, well, raw tofu.  The panko/cashew topping is good.  But otherwise, this recipe generates a lot of dishes for a lot of meh.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Karen that’s why I’m gonna heat my cashew tofu mix when I serve it tonight

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: The family said, "Good, not great". Everyone agreed that the tofu/cashew cream was at best, bland. We'd all have the broccoli again (I did take the foil off for the last two minutes to roast it a bit). If I made a base, I'd do Ottolenghi's yogurt, lemon, and tahini sprinkled with toasted cumin, from his Plenty More cookbook.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Agreed: Slightly sweet, (expensive), and flavorless cashew-tofu cream with an uninteresting crumb topping. This was a very rare time of tossing food out as unsalvageable.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Will add less water to the cashew butter to make it thicker next time.  Would also like to find a way to make the dish a bit "warmer".  Agree that broccoli would be better roasted rather than "steamed".

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Just like everyone said- flavor profile & texture is amazing! Also very filling for a relatively small meal. I roasted broccoli covered in foil but it wasn't as done as I would have liked so then roasted uncovered for about 5 min.. Made my own cashew butter first.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: About to make. Any drawback to making the whipped tofu ahead of time? Does it keep well in fridge before serving?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
I know cashews are used in some recipes because of their mild flavor. We have a cashew allergy in the house, so I wonder if almonds/almond butter would work here
or are they too flavor forward? I will make this in the next few days and report back, should anyone be interested!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Did you end up making it with almonds and almond butter? I am hoping to do the same!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Amanda Hi Amanda, we have a cashew allergy in our house as well, wondering if you ever made this with an alternate and if so, how it turned out!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: what is chile crisp?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: There are many varieties; to my taste, this one is the best: https://www.laoganmausa.com/products?pgid=lqprp10a-7ab9b48d-e93e-4306-9447-e3e7d0457e5a

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Amazing recipe!! Fresh squeezed lime took it to the next level for us!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Recipe as written makes much more cashew cream than needed. I repurposed this recipe as a pasta dish with rigatoni: loosened cashew cream with pasta water, tossed with rigatoni, and then served with broccoli & crunchy topping on top! This was a bigger hit than the original (which we loved) and now when I announce I'm making this recipe I am asked to make it "with pasta".

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Sorry to be the naysayer but we (2 adults and two grown teenagers) did not like this at all. We are adventurous eaters but the whipped tofu and cashew butter was, well, too whipped and almost tasted sweet (I even went so far as to check the ingredients in both the tofu and the cashew butter).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
I found the cool of the "dip" didn't go well with the hot of the broccoli. I am curious what I 
can make with the left over tofu whip.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I really enjoy recipes like this because while different, the prep and cook is pretty easy and it tastes really good. I think a quality chile crisp really kicks this up a notch and goes super well with the whipped tofu and cashew butter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious! Like other commenters, I subbed 1.5c of roasted cashews and a dash of olive oil blitzed in the food processor for cashew butter. I served as a main dish so I filled it out a bit:  extra firm tofu cubes tossed with a splash of soy sauce and a spoonful of chili crisp, pan roasted in the oven along with a package of pre-cut butternut squash, also tossed in a little olive oil and soy sauce. I put the broccoli in the oven for the last 15 mins & served everything over the puree.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This was good! Based on the reviews, I ignored the foil pocket instructions and just laid the seasoned broccoli on parchment paper and it turned out wonderfully! I would double the chili crisp in the crispy topping. I like the tofu cream idea, but found the flavor to be lacking even with some rice vinegar and nooch added. Next time I might flavor the tofu cream with ginger, garlic, lime, and soy sauce to make a sort of whipped peanut sauce situation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: So why is the cashew cream optional?  It is where the tofu lives which is literally in the name of the recipe.  What am I missing?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This dish is a symphony of flavors and textures. Yes, that is a lot of cashew butter (in this economy?) but in my humble opinion, it is worth it as an occasional treat. I haven’t tried any of the many alternatives mentioned in these comments, but I think they would all bear experimentation, especially tahini. To those complaining that the cashew tofu cream is too bland, feel free to season it to taste. I thought it acted as a delightful, subtle base for the amazing pops of flavor and texture that is the broccoli and crunchy topping. As for the debate re: the broccoli roasting procedure, it is stated to cook until “crisply tender”, not crispy. My aluminum-tented broccoli turned out perfectly cooked; being tender yet maintaining beautifully crisp texture. Not soggy. Not crunchy. That was left to the delicious topping, which I will be making by the jar, to sprinkle upon any unsuspecting vegetable or noodle dish I come across.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Steps 3 & 4 refer to "cashews" - does this mean the casher butter?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Steps 3 and 4  refer to the ½-cup coarsely chopped cashews used in the chile crisp bread crumbs.  Step 5 creates the optional cashew cream.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Ellen step 3 and 4 seem the same to me?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: 800 calories per serving! Whoa! Cut out the optional ingredients and reduce the cashews to 1/2 cup.  Serve with regular tofu or a protein.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I just don’t know about this one, folks. I made it as written. I think the crunchy topping is the best part of the dish. As others suggested, roast the broccoli uncovered. They’re kinda limp and lose some color otherwise. I think I’m most perplexed by the whipped tofu/cashew butter. It requires a lot of salt, and I kinda felt like I was eating deconstructed cheddar and broccoli soup? I just don’t know. I appreciate the creativity, but I’m not rushing to make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: One of our diners is allergic to tree nuts, so I subbed in half tahini, half peanut butter for the cashew butter, and omitted cashews from the crumb topping. Still turned out delicious - everyone loved it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
This was awesome - I've been looking for more creative ways to use tofu.
I added aleppo pepper to the tofu, and used tahini because I don't have cashew butter.
To make into a meal, I roast za'atar chickpeas and whatever other vegetables I have in the fridge.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: My husband hates broccoli but absolutely loved this dish. For the tofu cream, I didn’t have cashew butter so I substituted almond butter.  Stronger taste, and I would have preferred cashew but it worked.  Added juice of one lemon to brighten it up a little.  Also, just roasted the broccoli without the extra foil, make it more crisp.  Omitted the soy sauce for the same reason, and used the garlic clove for the crunchy topping so the garlic wouldn’t burn.  Overall, a great simple dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I don’t get it. How in the world could this have been so popular? Maybe I didn’t salt the whipped “dip” enough, but as @Madeleine said, the proportions were crazy—at most, 1/4 of the cashew butter tofu mix would have been plenty. Aleppo pepper and lemon and lime could work, and of course, all the people said the broccoli should have been roasted were 100% correct!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I made the recipe as is. Next time I will use Tahini as suggested as I found the cashew butter bland and too sweet for my taste and way too expensive. I added some lemon juice on the broccoli for a bit of acid to cut the sweetness. Next time I would double the chili crisp topping and cut the cream in half.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
I roasted the broccoli, which was great - even used more than called for because we are fans… topping was great, but I am just not understanding the cashew cream… 
I would up with a HUGE amount. I thought a generous smear on the platter was what I was going for, but used less than half - even with the extra broccoli. Quantity felt wasteful to me - cashew butter is not cheap. I also found it bland, which is fixable, but am not feeling the five star magic. 
Would redo as roasted broccoli with yummy topping… might consider some marinated pressed tofu chunked up on the roasting tray…

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: what are chili crisps?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Chili Crisp is a Chinese condiment, which has become more popular/widespread, so lots of brands available. Think of it sort of like a hot sauce, but full of crunchy bits. Different types/brands vary quite a bit in spice level.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Marilyn Brockway came here to ask this question

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Encouraged by the rave reviews, I was excited to try this. I’m not sure what I did but I just am not a big fan. I did sub peanuts and peanut butter per one suggestion, so maybe cashews are the way to go. It also does make a massive amount of the whipped tofu, which I did not particularly like. The crispy topping and the broccoli were solid but I think the taste and texture of the cream was odd. It needed something else under it and should definitely be heated. I saw rice, quinoa, and pasta in some comments but none of those sound like they would be good to me as-is. Since I have a massive quantity of the whipped tofu now, I may try to make it into a base for Asian style noodles, heated and with a lot more flavor added.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Double click on not covering the broccoli.  Absolutely scrumptious with the chile crisp cashew breadcrumbs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I don’t stock cashew butter or cashews, so I used 4ish tbsp of peanut butter instead of the 1 cup of cashew butter (this feels excessive and expensive) and used chopped peanuts instead of cashews. Turned out pretty tasty! I’d recommend adding some lemon or lime for an acidic element.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: The cashew cream has a beautiful texture, but it was bland and tasted too much like cashews ;) The addition of lemon juice, garlic powder, and salt helped. I also heated the cream and drizzled it over the broccoli.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This did not strike me as great. I followed some of the comments that suggested roasting the broccoli uncovered to get a more crisp texture during roasting. This worked well. I used Momofuku's chili crisp and really did like the tang and heat of the topping. But the cashew / tofu cream underneath: no. This was not good and really dragged down the dish. I would consider trying the broccoli cooked liked this, along with the crisp topping, over quinoa, or farro, or maybe even sticky rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: The broccoli was too raw for my liking, so will stirfry next time. The topping, however, is absolutely amazing!!! Will use it again for carrots and peanut noodles. I did not make the tofubase, but did not miss it at all. Instead I think that stir fried tofu would also go well with this amazing hot crunchy topping.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
I hate broccoli (I know, it's a character flaw), so I use brussels sprouts in this recipe and it is AMAZING. 
 
Also, I almost never have cashew butter on hand but almost always have a jar of tahini in the fridge, so I've subbed tahini in this recipe as well, and it's DELICIOUS (I throw in some lemon juice too, because why not?). 

This has become a go-to recipe for me, and it really handles variations and add-ons well (like a few raisins, for a sweet/salty/spicy experience).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I loved this! I too roasted the broccoli and used part tahini part peanut butter.  I didn’t have any chili crisp and instead used chili oil. It was delicious. So easy to make. The whipped tofu makes it entertaining worthy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Damn delicious — even my kids were obsessed and nicknamed the sauce “super sauce.” The chili crunch cashews were next-level good, adding just the right heat and crunch, but not too much for the kids. I tossed the broccoli in the olive oil and soy sauce in a bowl before roasting it on wire racks so it came out ultra-crispy. I can see this working just as well with Brussels sprouts or cauliflower, and I might try it in the air fryer next time for an even faster dinner. Thanksgiving side dish?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This was seriously the best thing I have cooked in all of 2025.  Shared it with literally all the foodies I know.  Bravo Alexa Weibel!  A+.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: What the heck is "Chile crisp" and how do I find  or make it? Lois in Berkeley

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Lois Brandwynne, it’s a delicious spicy condiment, good on so many things. You can find several brands at Berkeley Bowl! :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Thought the cashew cream was blah so added lemon juice, garlic granules and sumac to elevate the taste. Cooked the broccoli as written but took foil off for last 7 minutes before serving. Next time I'll add some blistered grape tomatoes to the broccoli.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: After checking three stores, no one had silken tofu, so I subbed mashed cannellini beans and a little plain yogurt. Also cut the cashew butter by 1/3 and skipped chopped cashews in topping to reduce calories. Roasted broccoli in the air fryer for 10 mins at 400 to get some crunch and char going. I agree with others, the method in the recipe will just steam the broccoli. Having cleaned my plate, all changes worked well for me.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Tasty - but one significant observation: there must be a typo with the amount of cashew butter suggested. 1 cup is far too much. A few others have mentioned this as well. You only need a fraction of the cashew butter and tofu mix compared to the recipe- maybe 1/10 to 1/6, so thats a pretty serious adjustment

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I am stunned that there are so many positive comments.   I thought this recipe was very poor.  The amount of cream is ridiculous.  Steaming broccoli leaves it flavorless.  The panko chili crunch was the only saving grave and still weak in my opinion.   What a waste of expensive cashew nut butter.  We couldn’t even finish our small servings.   Virtually flavorless with goopy, creamy mush that overpowers any flavor that the chili crisp or broccoli may have.  Don’t bother with this one!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I am vegan for 10 years and I have eaten so much tofu in my life and it is delicious. This recipe truly disgusted me. I haven't seen any other comments from people who didn't like it so providing validation for those who felt the same way! Not sure what I did wrong, but the texture was disgusting and the fact that the broccoli is hot and the tofu is cold gives me goosebumps. EW!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This is very similar to a standby dish served at Alden & Harlow in Harvard Square. I’ve tried to recreate it myself but looking forward to trying this version.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Would frozen broccoli work in this recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I am wondering if PB2 peanut powder could be used.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @SusanDoesn’t that have sugar in it?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: What type of cashew butter?   Raw cashews or roasted?   This makes a difference in taste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This is such any easy meal, and tasty too!  I had everything on hand except enough cashews and cashew butter.  I subbed tahini for cashew butter and got... a cream that tastes like tahini.  I didn't have enough cashews so I used 2-3 tablespoons Fly by Jing's Chengdu Crunch instead of chili crisp to amp up the crunch.  I'm sure it's different from the intended recipe, but it's good enough to put in my rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I made my own cashew butter in the food processor per some other commenters then just dumped in the tofu and water to blend. Easy cleanup! Also just roasted the brocolli since we like it extra crispy. Just amazing! Need to think of some ideas for the extra cashew tofu cream....

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I also started with 1 1/2 C salted cashews. I ground them then added a couple Tbsp of olive oil. I only had firm tofu so I just used it. Added water until it was creamy and a shot of sriracha. My chili crunch is in a jar in oil. I’m going to top it with that and use it as a veggie dip.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night for my daughter and I. I didn't think my husband would be interested. It was amazing and my husband was so intrigued he helped himself to almost half of it. I was glad I put some aside for my daughter to take home before I set the plate on the table. What a perfect combination of flavors!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Is it just me, or is there something vaguely "fine dining" about plating something atop a smear of whipped something else (with an indispensable garnish)? That's cool with me, but my meat-and-potatoes husband was kind of confused by this dish. That said, we both liked it. Needed acid though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, although the cashew cream was bordering on having an offputting texture, at least for me. It would be better if it were the thickness of hummus - as it is, it’s a bit too similar to eating spoonfuls of aioli. (Maybe I’ll just serve this with hummus the next time…)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe is fantastic! Easy to make, super delicious, pleases everyone (meat-lovers included) - and healthy! 
I roasted the broccoli in the oven, removed the foil for the last 4 minutes for the broccoli to become crispy.
I just wonder what chile crisps are? We don‘t seem to have this in Germany. I used a mild chili instead, braised it lightly and added some bread crumbs and cashews.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Chili crisp is a jarred combination of oil, chili, onions, fermented soybean and seasonings. It adds spice, crunch and fat (lots of oil) to a dish and is usually in a jar. Most of the brands I can find originate from China.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: In addition to Erin's great comments, I have also found Mexican style chili crisp if you ever want to take a dish in that direction.  I know that Somos brand is quite nice.  I don't know if you are able to order from Amazon.com, but that might be worth a try for you.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Erin thank you! I cook a lot but have never heard of this and scannned alllll the comments to get this info! :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Hmmm… not crazy about it: tofu cream needs something - maybe nutritional yeast? And broccoli is mushy. I’ll try this again roasting without covering and without tofu cream. The chili crisp crumb was good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: What am I missing- how is this "chile crisp" without chilis? Also, for anyone watching their weight, PB2 makes nut powders  that contain less calories and fat with plenty of flavor- just add water. Easy to keep on hand.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Chili crisp is a jarred condiment. Most grocery stores carry it

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @Nancy the MalaMarket.com has the BEST chile crisp - it’s straight outa Chengdu and instantly addictive.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Anyone have any idea how long the cashew/tofu cream lasts in fridge?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I have about two cups of cashew cream remaining.   Any ideas on how to use this in other recipes besides swapping out broccoli for another cruciferous vegetable?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Cashew cream can be frozen. You might want to re-blend after defrosting.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Great idea. Will freeze extra for future use.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Thanks for the great idea, and I will search NYT Food for other recipes using cashew cream.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: What could I substitute for tofu (CANNOT handle mouth feel! and metallic taste!)--would ricotta work?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Normally I would roast broccoli (or cauliflower or asparagus) at 425 for 12 minutes but I've been using my new air fryer more lately and this that would also solve the issue of the broccoli coming out steamed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Is there a soy free version?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: @RJ Maybe try Bragg's Aminos?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: There is a fava bean tofu so not made from soy, Big Mountain brand. This tofu is pretty sturdy though, so you might want to use half a block and add more water to achieve the spreadable consistency? Otherwise, my first thought was Greek yogurt with less water (or maybe even no water, depending on how thick the mixture would be), if you do not mind the tang. Sour cream might be ok (I would skip the water in this case or add maybe a tiny bit at the time) but it will also have that tang. Good luck!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: What is chili crisp?!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Just made this, the motivation being broccoli in my refrigerator and my love of chili crisp. I made my own cashew butter by grinding a cup of whole roasted in my mini Cuisinart, which made 2/3 of a cup, so less than the one cup the called for. I added the tofu right in, seasoned it, and had a few doubts. I roasted the broccoli uncovered, perfect in about 15 minutes, the thin garlic slices crispy. It was delicious! Think of the tofu cream as a silky ivory palette for all the flavors and textures.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: OMG: You could serve me boiled shoe leather and I’d eat it if topped with the cashew/chili crisp/panko topping. I made this 98% exactly as written and it was a smash hit. I  used coconut aminos instead of soy sauce, and rice flour based panko crumbs to make it GF, but I can’t imagine that these mods changed the flavor or texture profile in any perceivable way. I finished off the broccoli under a low broiler setting for 2 minutes to crisp the tops  a little, the served the dish with coconut rice and sheet pan chicken thighs. The silken tofu/cashew butter whip was a great binder for those who liked to mix things on the plate up. (I.e., me!) Make this dish. You’ll be happy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone substituted hummus for the tofu cashew spread?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I did not but I had a side dish of spicy hummus as a painted base with chunky, chilled raw veggies on top with w drizzle of olive oil and salt, pepper, garlic flakes. Nice compliment to dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Frikken delish. Perfect way to cook the broccoli, IMO - (when roasted straight, the frizzled ends have the texture of hair which is so gross) but I did finish it uncovered for the last 5 mins. I want allllll that cashew chili crisp on everything. Added soy sauce and miso to the cashew tofu cream. I will make this forever and ever ( oh and   lemon wedges to serve.)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody:
I'm embarrassed that I had to stop myself from eating the whole batch.  I whirled whole roasted cashews to the tofu instead of butter, and added a bit of soy sauce to season and thin instead of all water.  Broccoli roasted uncovered.   And more chili crisp!  
I'll try this with peanuts sometime, too, maybe toss on some scallions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: We were disappointed with this.  The whipped tofu and cashew butter is just too heavy--it overwhelms the broccoli.  I'm going to use the left-over broccoli and small amount of tofu with other asian ingredients and veggies in some sort of bright stirfry.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: I made the tofu-cashew butter base and thought it made too much. Tasty but would cut in half next time. The panko topping is addictive. I plan to make more for other uses.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: No foil for crispier Brocolli! Fresh basil is nice on top :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This dish is a new favorite for "fast food" at home--delicious and so simple! Agree with others who did not roast the broccoli in the pouch, though I did roast it covered for 10ish minutes and then uncovered for the remainder which gave me nice, crisp-tender results. My tofu came straight from the fridge so it was strange to have cold crema with the warm topping; next time will prepare that part first and let it come to room temp while I cook the rest.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: Recipe is delicious. But I roasted broccoli open in oven no foil (because otherwise it will not get crispy). Recipe is incredibly rich and filling I think because of the 1 cup of cashew butter. Next time I will double the broccoli and use 1/2 C cashew butter. Or maybe even less. Thanks for the GREAT recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted Broccoli and Whipped Tofu With Chile Crisp Crunch Recipe
commentBody: This was so delicious! I've been eating plenty of broccoli, sometimes with rosemary marcona almonds and a little parmesan or pecorino. I imagine you could shift the flavors by swapping almonds or even hazelnuts for the cashews. Also love the tofu cream as I have been experimenting with tofu. This ain't your grandma's tofu anymore (and I'm the granny). It was a meal. I'll have to try it with rice or noodles sometime. So simple and elegant.

author: Alexa Weibel
