commentBody: Excellent! I would leave out the sugar next time and slightly reduce the butter to 1/4 cup.  My tomatoes were very good quality from Italy and I used only 1 tbl but it was still too sweet! Overall, a very satisfying soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Smoky Tomato Soup Recipe
commentBody: Nutrition info lists 1162 mg sodium per SERVING! How can that be right? There’s only 3 cups of stock for 6 servings so 1/2 cup per serving, plus canned tomatoes, which can be salty. But still, that’s a higher sodium content than even the saltiest canned soups!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Smoky Tomato Soup Recipe
commentBody: Halved the butter, but added some EVOO; replaced the sugar with a minced small carrot, added with the onions. Used 1/2 tsp ground Ancho Chile pepper and only used 1/4 tsp red pepper.  This is a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Smoky Tomato Soup Recipe
commentBody: This is probably my favorite tomato soup recipe and is a huge hit with my husband.  I make it just as it is (butter and all).  The only variation is that I accidentally added a teaspoon of the red pepper flakes instead of the half teaspoon.  Extra spicy, but who cares!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Smoky Tomato Soup Recipe

2.  Added 1/3 C toasted fideos.  Sopa de Fideos is a classic Mexican lunch or after school snack which we love. The toasted noodles add a little more heft to the soup. 

2.  I added 1/3 cup toasted fideos. Sopa de Fideos is a classic Mexican lunch after school snack which my family loves.  The noodles add a little more body to the soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
So, this wasn’t nearly as bad as some reviews made it sound. The prep was definitely on the longer side, but once it hit the oven, it was a simple “set it and forget it” situation. Bake, weigh it down, cool, and then into the fridge — easy enough.

I made a few tweaks along the way. I peeled the potatoes first, soaked them in cold water, then ran them through a mandoline before letting them sit in the cream and butter mixture. I held back a bit on the salt but added fresh garlic for flavor and a fun little twist — thin slices of prosciutto tucked into two of the middle layers.

The two-hour cook time was right on the mark, though the cooling stage before refrigeration took a bit longer than expected. All in all, it turned out beautifully — crisp Parmesan crust, creamy layered center, and that subtle hit of prosciutto running through made it feel a bit more elevated. Definitely worth the effort.

commentBody: These are amazing. Made them 3 days in advance for Christmas. The general consensus was if you wouldn’t rate them 5 stars you made them wrong or don’t like potatoes. commentBody: Following a near-identical recipe published by Thomas Keller, I’ve made this dish dozens of times. It’s delicious, looks great, and is a crowd-pleaser. Like a lot of similar classics, potato pavé takes time, but not extraordinary technical skill. Try it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
I really wanted this recipe to be awesome, but this one was a disaster from the beginning. 5lbs of potatoes do not fit in a 9” loaf pan, unless somehow you can defy the laws of physics. My pan was full to the top with 3.5 lbs, even with pressing each layer down as much as possible. There is also way too much salt and pepper. I would cut the amount in half, at least. You could probably add some thyme also, but that is a choice and cutting the salt is a must. As someone else mentioned warming the cream mixture as you layer the potatoes is helpful. Otherwise you will end up, as I did, with cold congealed globs of butter fat and salty pepper coating everything in sight. I used 3 lbs hand weights instead of cans to press the mold down, which worked well.
There was a glimmer of hope when I unmolded the terrine and it cut into the appropriate shapes, but then all heck broke lose again when trying to dip in the Parmesan and fry. The cheese did not stick to the potatoes, but it definitely stuck to the pan. Then the cubes fell apart when trying to extricate them from their cheese attached spot in the pan. I managed to salvage 6 pieces, fried them without the cheese and then put it on top and put the pieces under the broiler, but was also left with a mountain of hash brown like salty wreckage that was edible but barely. Great concept, but this one is a miss. The best way to describe this recipe goes beyond broken and requires the words of the Swedish Chef. It is “borked borked borked!”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Due to lack of close reading, I used small russets, large loaf pan, put hot melted butter and warm cream in a mixing bowl with half the salt called for. It just seemed too much. No separation of butter happened using warm cream and I let the slices soak until they got above cream, then layered in pan. I used finely grated parm. It worked. I didn't warm them up before cooking. I used a very good non-stick pan with plenty of oil. IMO, the recipe is fussy and not very helpful but the pavé was fab.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Yummy and if you’re looking for a good cooking project, this is it. I did a little experiment here - half of the terrine I fried as instructed. I had a pretty hard time getting the parm to adhere. As a result, I tested a second batch where I dredged the terrine chunks though a little heavy cream, then potato starch, and then parm. The parm, as you might imagine, still didn’t want to stick. You may think my experiment was useless and so did I UNTIL I was reheating leftovers. I noticed the batch that was dredged in the potato starch reheated much much crispier than the non-starch batch. So, if you anticipate having leftovers, consider adding just one more step. I mean, you’re already 80% done at the point of frying, so why not!

FROM THE FAQ ABOVE - Can you freeze potato pavé?
The baked brick of sliced potatoes can be tightly wrapped in plastic wrap and frozen for up to 3 months. To finish cooking it, simply thaw in the fridge for 24 hours, then slice and sear.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is not nearly as hard as the other comments made it seem - It just takes patience. I made this for the first time for my family thanksgiving and they were a showstopper! For tips, I would recommend once you cut the potatoes into their cubes before frying, keep them refrigerated or cooled until you fry them to help keep them together. The cream mixture acts as glue and if they warm, they will more easily fall apart when you fry them.  Also, don’t hesitate to be generous with salt.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Tried it for NYE dinner (started on 12/30) and was very pleased with the results of this project! Ended up using about 3.5 lbs of potatoes, cut closer to 1/16 in. Cut the salt down to 4 tbsp. Cream and butter mixture clumped up, but there was enough liquid to forge ahead and just flick off the clumps. Pressing down from time to time while layering the potatoes is a must! 

In the oven for 2:20. Took closer to 2.5-3 hrs to fully cool to room temp (pressed down by a 6-pack box of coconut milk from Costco topped off with a cast iron, and an olive jar on the side). 

Here’s what worked for me for frying: let the cubes rest for 30 mins, then press into microplaned parm on a plate (I gently flicked off the excess and regret it!). Once coated in parm, I put them back in the fridge (just resting on a plate) for about 20 mins while getting the rest of the dinner ready. Fried in a stainless steel pan—accidentally initially overheated the oil (above 400), ended up adding a little extra to cool down and fried at about 350. No sticking, no falling apart! 

I won’t make this often, but will definitely keep in my back pocket for whenever I need a make-ahead showstopper side! If you can enlist a helper for the peeling/slicing stage , totally worth the effort.

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I made these tonight. They were fabulous. I didn't have the recipe available when I went shopping and bought 5lbs of small russet potatoes instead of golds. Also had grated parm rather than shredded. Truth: I did the whole thing in about 5 hrs. Brought the temp down after baking by putting first in fridge and then in freezer. Didn't warm back up before frying. The potato cubes were very hot inside and crunchy on the outside. Devine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving. They make for a visually striking item on the table and the ability to make them a day ahead of time was killer. It feels like a very high effort for the outcome though, and somehow the creamy preparation looked really tasty on paper but didn't really make the dish stand out. If you're going for potatoes, I feel like a standard gratin dauphinois would have been at least equally good and a lot less labor-intensive

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Followed the directions except I reduced the salt. Lots of work...and frankly the final product was not special enough to justify all the work and complexity. Won't make it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I skipped the parm (opting instead for a dollop of chive drained fraiche on top after frying) but otherwise followed the recipe exactly. It came out perfectly and was such a huge hit. Yes it’s a lot of work and you need to plan ahead but it’s really good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I bet putting a bit of caviar and creme fraiche on top of the paves would make a very fancy hors d'oeuvre. Now if I can just persuade someone to make it for me!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: My motto since my late teens: anything worth doing is worth overdoing and this recipe exemplifies it. So worth it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: The dish is delicious and a major crowd pleaser, the searing process is dangerous but worth it, and the weighting with 14-ounce cans is silly—use a brick.  I also piled two cast-iron pans on top of the brick.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Had a similar dish prepared w bravas sauce at the once magical Ataula in Portland (although they used sous-vide rather than baking, wonder how/ if this changes texture?). Have been trying to replicate this long lost dish since- maybe replace parm w manchego? Excited to find an occasion for this undertaking- or as others have suggested, convince my food friend to tackle this undertaking…

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: @Sarah Oh, interesting idea!  They would have used a commercial vacuum sealer, and maybe added some extra starch, but I think sous vide could work really well here.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: The easiest pave recipe I've encountered.  Bravo!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I could not fit 5lbs of potatoes into one loaf pan. Ended up putting into 2.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I tried this recipe. My Kitchen Aid made short work of the preparation. Way too salty though this could be because I misread the ingredients and used 5 potatoes instead of 5 pounds as directed. Oops.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: We are in the middle of making this and we simply can't understand how five pounds of potatoes could possibly fit in a single loaf pan. Our pan is pretty much full with about three pounds of potatoes, maybe three and a half. Didn't see the comment about the salt until it was too late, I hope it turns out ok.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I used a mandolin and created very thin slices and all 5 lbs of potatoes fit in my large loaf pan.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
I had no end of trouble with this recipe. I will never attempt to make this again, but here are some tips that might help you. 
1. Slice the potatoes paper thin. Less than 1/16” thick.
2. Warm the milk with the melted butter so that the butter doesn’t clump up. 
3. I don’t have a nonstick pan but I guess it’s necessary. I usually use my de facto non stick cast iron and the blocks immediately fell apart. 
4. Much more salt. 
5. Good luck.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Just finished. Delicious and well worth itooo! And yes, 5lbs of peeled potatoes fit in our 9”loaf pan. (Slightly mounded, before cooking)

Made three changes…
Only used a tablespoon of salt and two teaspoons of white pepper in the butter/cream mixture.

Tried searing a test piece in a cast-iron skillet and as gentle as I was …the whole thing fell apart. 

Took two more test pieces …cooked one in 450 degree oven for about 5 minutes. Placed another in an air-fryer at 400. To avoid the cheese sticking, I placed both on a side without the cheese. (So cheese was on the sides)

Both came out intact and looking pretty much like the picture!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: The recipe calls for running a knife around a nonstick pan, which seems like a great way to ruin the coating.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is not really that hard.  Peel and slice potatoes.  Mix with the cream (I used dairy-substitute) and butter.  Layer them in the loaf pans... follow the directions as written and it turned out really well.  Only thing done differently was cut pieces into longer bars instead of chunks.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Whereas this was a good recipe, for all the carbs, salt, crème, prep-time and necessity to cook right before serving (meaning it’s not conducive to dinner parties unless you have help) it’s not worth it. But it was fun to experiment making.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I followed this recipe to the letter and was terribly disappointed with the results. The squares fell appart while browning them and I had to abandon the whole effort. I had a feeling as I was slicing that 1/8 inch felt too thick and should have adjusted. Would try again slicing much thinner - to 1/16th at minimum. I even weighed down with 3x filled HEAVY wine bottles vs the 3 cans suggested to ensure enough pressure but the potatoes quickly  started to come apart as they browned. Sad :(

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Wondering if I could finish them in the oven at high temp to get the crust, like roasted potatoes? Seems like a reasonable mod to buy some efficiency? Anyone?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Folks I just put these in the oven- not at all as time consuming as you think! Easier than mashed potatoes imo and far less mess!
Will slice and sear for dinner in 2 days. Easy peasy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: @Katy Balagopal update: made this today – sliced and let them sit at room temperature for 30 minutes as suggested, the parmesan stuck just fine. They were crispy on the outside very hot on the inside and delicious.  I’ll make these again for sure, but as with any sauté, there’s a bit of mess with oil splatter..

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I’m tempted. I don’t have a mandolin. Can I use my food processor to slice the potatoes?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I’ll pass on this one..it’s way too complicated and fussy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Not worth the effort. Rather make latkes or a gratin, but this hybrid not a great recipe. Mine also mostly fell apart

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: This was great, but critical to make sure the potatoes are fully softened. It took us two hours and will still a bit firm--suggest more like 2.5-3 hours of baking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: After having things stick and fall apart on my first couple of fry attempts I switched to doing a very hot oven with a parchment-lined baking pan filled to about 1/4 inch with oil. Worked very well (though my kids were already full from snacking on the crunchy failure potatoes from the botched first batches).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I made this recently. Sounded fun and delicious. My tip - go light on the cream mixture, and DON’T pour any of the cream mixture into the pan, even if you think it’ll add flavor. My dish was ok, but the slices didn’t fuse/stick together, so when I fried squares, the layers came apart. The only thing I can think of was the excess cream. I weighed it down, followed all those directions. Was disappointed w the end result. Hopefully, ymmv.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: The technique description is very good, however I do not think I would attempt this without some sort of "Watch This" How To Video to show how this is done.  That would be most helpful.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: This is much easier with two people: one peeling and one slicing on the mandolin.  Once you get the rhythm the entire process goes quickly.  Definitely slice on the longer side of the potato to improve the speed as well.  I found the parmesan crust to be annoying and ended up just frying without it and they were delicious. I saved the scraps from squaring up the "loaf" and stir fried them into the most delicious hashbrowns for breakfast.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I have been waiting for a snow day to make this! It is setting up in the fridge. Any pairing suggestions?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: @Brynn Melissa Clark’s salmon with anchovy garlic butter (https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1017329-salmon-with-anchovy-garlic-butter?smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share) was great with this, along with some arugula on the side. Makes for a pretty salty meal overall, though!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
I followed this recipe exactly as written but was unable to serve this to my thanksgiving guests. I used 1/8 inch slices per the instructions against my better judgment. I pressed as I went and weighted the potatoes as advised etc. When I turned out the terrine, it was chunky and fragile but looked as though it might work. Instead, it began to collapse as I handled it. The parm on the surviving pieces stuck to the pan. Good thing we also had mashed potatoes…

That said we baked the remains later on and they were tasty. I’m planning to try a few adjustments (thinner layers, a tweaked pressing process, and maybe even squaring the potatoes angles before cutting for a neater terrine) and hoping for better results.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I don’t understand these instructions: are the potatoes baked in slices first and then put together into “chunks”?  It is patently not clear in the instructions.  And if this is so, oh my goodness, lots of preparation for a home cook!  When i commented on this before, I was chastised for criticizing a recipe which I hadn’t prepared, but I see there are other comments like this too,

commentBody: This was A LOT of work, but I knew that going in.  I guess I was really hoping that there would be a big payoff for the effort. Alas, mine didn't really turn out very well. I could try again, but I think I'll just look for potato pave on restaurant menus from now on!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Don’t bother trying this recipe without a very high quality pan, or better yet a cast iron pan. Otherwise, your parmesan will instantly caramelize to the bottom without of your pan leaving you with sticky hash browns.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Worth the effort!
Yes, lots of prep but there was nothing tricky about this recipe. Followed directions and did the cooking the night before, making it easy to finish the last steps of cutting, coating and frying for New Years Eve dinner. 
TIPS: Definitely use a micro planer for the Parmesan, pressing the cheese lightly into your potato, and don’t flip them until the bottoms are crispy and release from the pan. I used the thinnest slicing setting on a mandolin and the layers were incredible!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: An epic disaster in my kitchen...super disappointing after spending so much time the day before.  Potatoes need to be sliced super thin - 1/16"?  Mine were too thick and the squares fell apart in the skillet.  I ended up with a cheesy, oily, gooey pile of potato chunks...definitely not the desired effect! I will take the remaining pressed cubes and toast them under the broiler rather than pan-frying them. Next time we will just enjoy the version served at our local steakhouse!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: OK YES, you have to faff about with a bunch of prep work and plan ahead BUT the end result is delicious and impressive. Great for a dinner party, all of your friends will think you are such a gourmand, and isn't that what hosting is all about... inflating your own ego..? ;)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I saved time by using a food processor to scallop the taters and it came out just fine. It was a lot of food for 2 people, so instead of frying them all up at once, we sliced off a couple of pieces each day over a few days.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful! We had these in a restaurant recently and found the NYT recipe duplicated them perfectly. Easy-peasy to make if you have a mandolin … you just need to plan ahead. I think we’ll make a batch to freeze so we have them ready to go next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe for making ahead. I love the idea of an elegant side that’s made days ahead of time and finished off just before serving. That said, the instructions call for slicing potatoes 1/8” thick clearly isn’t what was done in the kitchen for the photographer. In the days of Gourmet magazine the editors made sure that the recipe photos clearly represented what a home cook would create. That’s not the case here. As some have noted the potatoes need to be thinner, as in the photo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Potato Pavé With Parmesan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Put simply, there is no better preparation for potatoes.  I toss the slices in goose fat before layering instead of a creamy mixture, and once pressed and diced, I deep fry them in goose fat too.  It’s a labor of love, and I reserve it for holidays, hence I don’t mind the expense of copious amounts of goose fat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: If all you can manage is steps 1-3, you’re golden.  These little babies are so flavorful they really shine without any further embellishment.  Cook a bunch, because it’s hard to stop nibbling them as is.  The marinade et al sounds fab for any less stellar peppers.  And yes, these are such tender-skinned delights I don’t think it would be possible to peel them.  Enjoy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Truly tasty.  Made recipe as written except using grill pan on gas cooktop to char / slump the peppers.  The secret word is "immediately" in step 4 and the hot peppers absorb the dressing flavors.  Unlike red bells, Nardello skin is tender to eat and almost all the seeds are contained right near stem end so not a lot of seeds in the body of the pepper.  Removing seed 'cap' after cooking is easy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Isn't the grilled skin unpleasant to eat?  I usually put grilled peppers into a paper sack then peel off the softened skin for a silkier result.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: @Shorty Attentionspan, the skin softens more than other peppers, they’re best just as described.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: These have been found in English supermarkets for some years. I usually cut them in half lengthwise, remove the seeds, add the garlic, slivered, and halved cherry tomatoes. Bake at any temperature adjusting the time accordingly. They are good either hot or cold; immediately out of oven or refrigerator. The flesh is thin and the skin entirely edible.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I have grown Jimmy Nardellos for years: an entirely unique flavor of chili/fruity without the heat. I wish I could find them in local markets.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Yes, I wish I knew where to buy them.  Our grocery stores in southern Vermont are somewhat limited.  Maybe Trader Joe’s in Burlington?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous! So yum! Paired with roasted chicken and rigatoni with a vinegar forward pan sauce. yum.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Funny, I recently found a bag of these delightful peppers in Whole Foods in the Bay Area. I am going to try this recipe out. The peppers are so flavorful but, I didn’t know how to incorporate them into cooking other than salads.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Finally found some and they really are amazingly tasty. But the seeds, all of them, should be removed after grilling. They are VERY tough and took away from the final product.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Shining flavors

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe doesn't explain what to do once the peppers are tossed on the sheet pan! It jumps from there to... grilling the peppers.

Might be easier to toss the peppers with oil in a large bowl instead - easier to wash. And: Add an instruction for us literalists, e.g. - after tossing use tongs to place oiled peppers on the hot grill :D

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Jimmy Nardello Peppers Recipe
commentBody: So much better than you might expect. One of the most delicious things you can eat all summer. Put on salads, pastas, toasts, serve on a charcuterie board, or serve with burrata or ricotta and bread to dip. I usually eat a few all by themselves.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: as this is a complicated cake:   buy only the freshest ingredients.  Read through the steps at least three times to understand the time required.  Highlight resting times or chilling times. Set out all bowls and equipment and dry ingredients.  measure very carefully.   Dry the rinsed berries completely on  layers of paper towel and even a hair dryer set on air and low.  Do not  rush any steps. Do not go on your phone and check mail, instagram or tik tok.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious, especially the frosting and fruit combo.

I am a tad leery of only using almond extract after a couple traumatic over-the-top almond extract desserts. So I used my favorite Stella Parks “White Mountain Cake” extracts and their amounts instead: 2 T vanilla and 1 teaspoon almond extract. 

I altered the simple syrup from plain to a raspberry one using Chambord and raspberries.

This time of year grocery store strawberries are lack luster on the East coast. I envy those in California. I might use only raspberries with my Chambord simple syrup next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: If you want your simple syrup to have a delicate but definite raspberry presence without adding sugary red stuff I strongly recommend using *real* eau de vie Framboise. It is colorless and not syrupy. Also expensive, but a bottle lasts a long time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I don’t have a stand mixer, just a hand held electric beater. Does anyone know if the frosting can still be made with a hand held mixer?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Jessica think back to the  days before stand mixers were invented. Hand mixers can deliver equally lovely results, with a bit more effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Jessica With patience, I don’t see why not. It’s likely to take longer. Make sure it’s a good sized / deep bowl - and consider turning it round and round from time to time, running the hand mixer up the sides, to help incorporate the bits that get flung about in the beginning. Pause to scrape the bowl from time to time  and keep mixing until you arrive at the texture you wish. You might even consider whipping the cream separately until it’s starting to stiffen and then try folding that into the cheese mixture. Aerating the heavy cream within that heavier cheese mixture is likely to be the tricky bit with a hand mixer. Whipping it separately and then folding it in by hand should help, followed by a last go around with the mixer if need be to fully mix it all together.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Bea And god bless the cook with only a bowl, wrist and metal beater! Gone but not forgotten.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: My granddaughter and I made this cake in late summer before she left for college.  I certainly was time consuming but the fresh fruit  along with the frosting made it so worthwhile.  That said, if possible, I would buy it from Whole Foods (or the Bywater Bakery if you are in New Orleans) the next time (especially for a major function) just because nothing else much can happen in the house as you follow this recipe.  We had the time an enjoyed the results.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: In Step5, would it be good to beat the egg whites at least a little before adding them?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Barbara Ajami- no, it’s not necessary to beat the egg whites prior to adding to the batter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Beating the egg whites slightly and folding in will create a lighter crumb-- go for it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Having loved this cake at WF for years, I was surprised to see that the fruit on top was not glazed. A simple apricot jelly glaze can be  used to coat the fresh fruit on top. Lightly coat the fruit before decorating on the cake. Delicious!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: But isn’t this really ripped off from Sweet Lady Jane’s famous Three Berry Cake that took over Los Angeles in the 90’s?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Phil my first thought, a fantastic cake.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Jessica, re: hand mixer v. stand mixer. A stand mixer and whisk provides a more intense, comprehensive beating and incorporates air in a lot better. You likely can do it, but you will need to add time and hang in there longer, with your hand mixer. Worse case scenario: it might take you a try or two to replicate the full result, but you still get pretty great tasting cake.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: This recipe looks great, but I would really like to know what adaptations the author made before I make it. Is she serious about letting the layers cool completely in the pans? Never seen that before. Also, for those who would like high, even layers, I suggest using Magi-cake strips. Never bake a layer cake without them!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I just made this - the cake did remind me of the Sweet Lady Jane triple berry cake. I left the sugar in the cake the same, used the simple syrup, but cut the powdered sugar in the frosting down to 150 grams. Perfect balance of sweeter cake, with lightly sweetened frosting and sweet berries that way. It tasted great the next day too after a night in the fridge. If I made it again, I might try a different white cake layer, but the frosting + berries was a great replica of the Sweet Lady Jane cake.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: This recipe reminds me of the really lovely (and simpler) Hungarian Cream Cake in Craig Claiborne's New York Times Cook Book.  It's a three (or 2) layer cake with a similar flavor profile.  Light & delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
@Maria
I made that cake for my son’s first birthday - I remember it well - he turns 35 this summer

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Family declared that this may be the “best cake ever”. I did take some of the advice from readers and used 2 teasp almond extr t and one of vanilla, which was perfect. Otherwise, followed the recipe exactly. It was amazing!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I used Cup-4-Cup gluten-free flour in place of cake flour, and it worked very well. Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: The recipe worked using two 9-inch pans.  The 2 cakes rose to around 2 inches and I cut each in half to make 4, 1-inch layers.  The bake time increased to around 45 minutes.  Next time, I will also increase the amount of frosting by perhaps 25% to accommodate the larger surface area and additional layer.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: AMAZING!!  I cut the recipe in half and made 3 6” layers.  Also cut the frosting in half and had plenty of frosting!  (And I like a lot of frosting) LOVE this cake and will definitely make it again.  Thank you NYT Cooking!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Thanks for this tip! I didn’t need a huge cake for my boyfriend’s birthday and this halved recipe created a perfect little 3 tier 6” layer cake! I also did half vanilla / half almond extract and it was amazing :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Phil, I had the same thought -- that this reminds me of the oh-so-delicious Sweet Lady Jane berry cake.    It is time-consuming with many steps but pretty straight-forward and easy.  One suggestion I have is to maybe increase the cake ingredients so that the cakes rise as high as possible!  the recipe here makes two really good sized cake layers but I had to start over for a third cake.  Looking forward to doing the frosting/filling tonight and assembly Thursday.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Just made this cake for the first time - it was delicious.  Instead of the simple syrup I brushed Disaronna on the cake layers. Yum!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Nice cake, worth the effort.  I realized I only had 8 oz of mascarpone so I reduced the cream as well.  Frosting was really tasty, not overly sweet like most.  I only had enough for the layers and crumb coat, so it was a bit naked looking, but this was the first time making it.  I have some good, super easy recipes that I generally make, but I'll this one again.  Decided to skip dinner and just have the cake the day I made it!  Very yummy, especially if you do not like super sweet cakes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: The recipe is fail proof! I was a little nervous seeing all the other comments, but if you follow the instructions, everything goes so smoothly. This is the most beautiful cake i have baked, and the recipe is really easy. By the time the cake cools down, the frosting will be chilled to perfection and ready for icing. Don’t make this in a rush. Enjoy the process!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I’m a novice baker. Taking up a bit of baking in retirement. There’s a big payoff to making this cake according to the recipe and listed ingredients! Best cake I  ever made and most compliments ever. I was so pleased everyone loved it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I was told that the simple syrup was not just sugar and water, but also apricot jam was in the water to give a more rich taste and aroma.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I made a little extra simple syrup, and coated my decorating fruit in it. It made for great glossy fruit and made the top of the cake look especially nice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: This is an incredible cake. I made it in 2 9" inch pans, and I think they took 47 minutes to bake in my unpredictable oven. It's exactly what I wanted, but even better. The berries and frosting are perfect together. The cake is dense and textured. I had to give most of it away so I wouldn't eat it all. Would 10,000% recommend. Everyone who had it raved about it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
I'm not a huge fan of almond extract, so I used 1t vanilla and only 1/4 t almond extract, but also, I substituted 1/3 of the flour with almond flour.  

Also, I didn't have half and half, so I used whole milk and a little extra oil to increase the fat. Turned out great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: For high altitude cooking I increased the 1/2&1/2 by 1/3 c and decreased the baking powder by1/4tsp (5000 ft) they came out beautifully!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
This cake was a complete miss. And nothing like the WF version. The cake was dry and a bit tasteless. The icing was very loose (as noted by another baker). More like a whipped cream topping. But very yummy. I could not ice the cake with it. Ended up piling it on top of the cake. (The icing/whipped cream would also pair well with say a carrot cake or pumpkin cake.)

My recommendations would be bake a classic yellow cake (like Magnolia Bakery Vanilla cake) in a single layer. Pile the icing on top and then pile lots of fresh berries on that. If you want an iced cake, make a buttercream frosting, because this icing recipe will not hold up.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: It was fun to take on this challenge, spent the better part of a day working on it. I would only make this in summer when the fruit is local and delicious. This was for a dinner to serve 4, so I cut it down to two layers. That would be enough for a larger crowd as well. And it made it so there was no left over cream, cream cheese or marscapone. I recommend taking the cake out of the fridge a few ours before serving to let it warm up and soften. It was good on day two as well, sent the leftovers home with my daughter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: What size pans are used for this?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Per the recipe, three 8-inch pans are used

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Can you substitute gluten free cake flour. It’d be nice if recipes included how to make them gluten free for those of us who have celiac.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Can you use frozen berries for the filling and buy fresh berries just fot the decoration?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Vidya the frozen berries will have water retention and can make the cake/frosting more wet. Fresh berries that have been washed and fully dried should not cause that issue.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Reverse cream method works better here given the quantity of eggs, especially if you’re using GF flour.  Also there’s no acid to activate the baking soda if you aren’t using a “cake flour” which has a lower protein % than regular flour, and particularly again if you are using a GF flour.  Make the baking powder an even 1 Tbsp and skip the soda.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Made this as written and it's FABULOUS. I know it freaks some people out that the whites aren't whipped prior to adding, but just trust the process! This cake puts all others to shame. Can't wait to try it with local strawberries in season!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: This was a fantastic recipe.  I made the cake one day in advance in case it needed adjustments for high altitude in Denver. No adjustments were needed. That was the best frosting I’ve ever tasted. A very generous amount! Everyone loved the cake and asked for the recipe. Was not difficult to make, butit’s important to follow the directions exactly. I will definitely be making this again for special occasions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this as a sheet cake?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Swapped the most of the berries for mango (alternated blueberries and mango in the layers; mango piled on top); cut sugar in half in both the cake and frosting; no simple syrup wash - perfect sweetness level and not dry; used thin 8 inch layer pans (12 mins baking) which made an impressive 5 layer cake. This made a lot of frosting but needed every bit of it with the extra layers. Did not have any issues with runny frosting. Added an apricot jelly glaze to the fruit topping. Excellent cake!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: This was the first cake I’ve ever made and was quite easy to follow. Was delicious and a crowd pleasure. I served it right away but found it was better the next day as maybe everything sank it. Would recommend!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I made 4-inch bento cakes with this recipe to ask my bridesmaids to be a part of my wedding party. Because of this, I made the cake in a sheet pan with a slightly shorter cook time & cut out 4-inch rounds using a mini cake pan. I drove the cakes between states w ice packs & they held up well, and the flavor was a hit with everyone. :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Mine is a question, once I’ve completed my cake, how is it stored in the refrigerator? I wouldn’t want any refrigerator smells to infiltrate it after all that work! Also I think kumquat slices could embellish the look of the fruit display on top.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Nan If you do not have a cake carrier, you can partially insert some toothpicks into the top of the cake and then wrap the entire cake with a high quality plastic wrap. The toothpicks keep the wrap from sticking to the cake. Be sure to not leave any gaps in coverage because you are right that other smells will spoil the taste of the cake.  Also covering the cake preserves the texture of the frosting.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Could this be made as a sheet cake, split into layers?  I don't have the 8" inch cake pans, but I do have a pan for a sheet cake.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Question:  can this cake be frozen?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Can you use the reverse cream method on this recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Lovely cake. A big hit at work. Took 4 hours, not the 6 reported.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Would this cake turn out well with gluten-free flour?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Decided to make a special “Engagement Cake” for our nephew and his new fiance. It was a big hit! Shared with neighbors and they all loved it!  Will make again…

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: This year my son had the audacity to request the grocery store version of this cake for his birthday over my own chocolate cake—so I challenged myself to make this one.  I learned a lesson:  to read through to the very last line of a recipe before deciding to make it because the last line says it’s recommended to make it the day before. I suggest that go into the notes! It also took me  a full 6 hours as stated without much down time.  My cake layers turned out to be on dense and dry side— not when I expected from such a fluffy batter. I would love feedback because I did notice that the batter deflated substantially after the flour and oil was slowly added.  After only 20 minutes at 350 the cakes had pulled away from the sides of the pans, and no crumbs, but the top was so soft I left it in for another 5 minutes, perhaps that caused the dryness?  The frosting is exquisite. The birthday boy and everyone else loved it.  Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @JeannieL i’m curious did you use the simple soak as well?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: was just at whole foods looking at their cakes -- the cheapest/smallest size was $35....

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: This recipe -which I followed exactly- is AMAZING. The cake part is soft and tender crumbed. The frosting is so good you'll eat it out of the bowl. Both components together with the berries is magic. The only thing I wasn't sure about was how small the cut fruit should be: I think they should each be no bigger than the raspberries. Also next time I'll try to press them slightly into the frosting so the layers are glued together better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: 6 egg whites, in this economy? No thanks. I halved the recipe and baked it in a brownie pan (9x13). I cut two 6 in rounds with plenty of leftovers (maybe I could have pieced together a 3rd layer if I had been careful, but I just ate the scraps with leftover frosting and fruit). This made a delicious and cute little cake that was enough to feed four, with leftovers. I made the foodwishes salted caramel custard with the 3 remaining egg yolks (1/3 of the recipe, makes two 6 oz ramekin desserts).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
This cake turned out dry and kind of course textured. It was a big disappointment considering I followed the time consuming recipe exactly and weighed out all the ingredients. 
I think it is odd that the recipe does not have you whip the egg whites.
This is my 2nd NYT recipe that I tried and both were flops.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Three cups of heavy cream was way too much for the frosting. It came out so runny, even after sitting in the refrigerator overnight. I would maybe use half of that or two cups next time. Everything tasted great but the cake was ugly due to the runny frosting.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I’m concerned about starting the frosting with cold cream cheese as the recipe indicates. I’m worried it’ll be lumpy in the end. Can anyone speak to this?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Hannah I had no issues!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Hi - this looks amazing. Thanks to the commenter who made a GF version, as I have celiac and will do the same. Question: my family can't handle cheese (cream or mascarpone) in the frosting. Would subbing a simple chantilly cream work or would a Swiss buttercream be better? Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
I made this for my birthday. I had high hopes and it delivered. It was a fussy process with the timing and the chilling but was also really clearly laid out. I have a pair of 7 inch pans and 10 inch pans so I opted for two thinner, bigger cakes. I scaled the frosting down to fit the 250 gram mascarpone container and it was a good amount as the ratios seemed right for my shorter cake and I lost a little to tasting and even had some left over. I was shy about the almond extract based on other posts and wish I’d done a bit more although it was detectable and still good. I had to make cake flour with cornstarch, mix my own half and half and use cream cheese that already seemed whipped (the spreadable kind, not the block), left the cake to chill during all the steps but for longer blocks
 and it all turned out.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: How would you adjust this for high altitude? I live at 6,000 ft. Thanks!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I love the cake part of this recipe (sans frosting)  so much that I made about 10x, to build my daughter's 4-tier wedding cake (12-10-8-6 inch tiers) as well as a trial cake. Used baking strips for the pans, and pasteurized egg whites from a carton which was a time saving, egg saving, *gamechanger*!!! I filled the layers with Easy/Faux Meringue Buttercream and raspberry compote.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @hz How did it hold up with tiers? Did it slice clean? Im considering this for a wedding cake as the top layer and doing red velvet on the bottom tier

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Kelley The cake was absolutely perfect! I filled, crumb coated and froze (for 2 weeks prior to the wedding) 4 tiers, with sugargeekshow's easy "faux" meringue buttercream (highly rec!) and a raspberry compote filling from frozen berries. It cut cleanly, and was moist (not dry at all).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I wish I could make their chocolate angel food cake at home! Please, help.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
I have made this recipe for my family for several years. It is SO good, they call it “THE Cake.”

I use whipped cream (made quickly and stably in a food processor) as the frosting and use organic almond flour, 

It is an exceptional dessert.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I made this for my birthday this year. It was a stunning success. Like a few other commenters, I reduced the amount of almond extract and added vanilla. Otherwise, I made it as directed. It was a time-consuming recipe, but so worth it. Everyone I shared it with raved about it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Still comes out great if you just use 8oz of cream cheese and 8oz of mascarpone instead of 12oz of each.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Baked in 9 in pans, 23 min?, left in the pan to cool and then into the freezer. 2 days later used hubby's homemade orgeat as the simple syrup. 16 oz mascarpone to use it all up, 12 oz cream cheese--this frosting is so delicious. Show stopper of a cake, and my niece said it was her favorite of all the cakes I've ever made. So I guess I'll be making it again someday :) Is it a project? Sure is. But do-able. I even melted apricot preserves and ran it through fine mesh to glaze the top fruits

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Made this with my son for Mother's Day. I used two 9" pans and split the layers. Skipped the simple syrup as the cake was very moist. Feel the frosting didn't benefit from the marscarpone and added 2T Grand Marnier to enhance flavor. I only had 40 minutes to chill frosting prior to whipping - no problem at all. Will absolutely make it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Please explain what “crumb coat” means

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Carol putting the first layer of frosting (not decorative) over the top and sides of the cake to create a smooth base for the rest of the frosting.  It tames any runaway crumbs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: A crumb coat is a very thin layer of frosting, especially around the sides of a cake, that "glues" crumbs into place and prevents crumbs from getting into the final layer of frosting.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
It is a thin layer of frosting applied to the cake to seal in the crumbs.
You let that set a while, then go ahead with the final layer of frosting. No crumbs.
Happy Baking !

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: What would be a suitable yellow cake recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: May I ask, please, what ingredient is referred to as “half and half”? Writing from Dublin in the Republic of Ireland.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
@Ne Plus Ultra
Half and half is the following: half cream and half milk. It is commonly used by Americans in cooking and coffee.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: To be more specific it is half milk and half single cream, for a fat content of about 10%. (Have lived in the UK, so am experienced with dairy product conversion!)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Alexis. how about defining single cream?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Is this recipe the original yellow cake version or the newer white cake version. I’m guessing the white cake version, since there are no egg yolks.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I'm not familiar with the term "crumb frost." Could someone explain it please?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Sara To apply crumb coating you lightly frost the entire cake, very smoothly. Some crumbs might visually appear mixed in with this layer. When chilled it creates a sort of barrier that keeps the crumbs from getting mixed in with the final frosting. After chilling the cake the final coat of frosting is applied on top of the crumb coating. Some bakers freeze their individual cake layers before applying the crumb coating but that won’t work here due to the fresh berries

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
Crumb voat is a thinly applied frosting to the sides and top of the cake. So thin, cake can be seen. As the instructions note, use 1/2 of the REMAINING frosting.
Then chill the cake and frosting an hour or more. This allows the cake to firm up. When you apply the rest of the frosting, there are no cake crumbs to contend with.
Great trick when frosting any cake.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: What does the small amount of oil do?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Keeps the cake moister. Oil remains liquid at lower temperatures than butter and provides a moister mouth feel, while butter gives it the rich flavor you expect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: When berries are in season I make strawberry shortcake with whipped cream. And earlier in the season it might be a sponge cake - quite high - or even a Passover sponge cake. With mixed berries and whipped cream both cakes are delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Anyone know how to make the mango version of this cake? I remember it as being more than just cut mango added to cake layers.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried without the simple syrup? The recipe sounds amazing. As there is sugar in the cake and frosting I wonder if I can dial down the sweet. Or is the simple syrup important for assembly or texture of cake.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: if you don’t own 3 8- inch pans, it is possible to bake the cake in one taller 8-inch tin - remember to adjust baking time - and cut afterwards into three rounds. i score a line an inch from the bottom with a sharp knife (rest the knife on a 1-inch tall cuttingboard, for example). then a nestle a long thread into the line all around the cake and draw the ends carefully together. that cuts one layer off the cake. gently lift the top piece off and repeat. that makes three layers out of one.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: There are much easier ways to make a delicious whipped cream, mascarpone frosting. A quick internet search will reveal a dozen options.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: This cake. Oh my, outstanding! I did map out a plan for the timing, making the layers two days ahead and keeping them in the pans, well wrapped.  Made it for a work birthday party and the looks of bliss on people's faces said it all.  I did add a teaspoon of vanilla extract to the frosting, but other than that stuck to the recipe.  The amount of almond extract in the batter was a little daunting, but the resulting flavor is perfect, and the contrast of the fruit and creamy frosting is divine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: What is the purpose of the simple syrup?  I'm thinking about leaving it out.  I made my favorite yellow cake in lieu of this one, and I don't see a reason for more sweetness?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: @Theresa the simple syrup is for the cake to have some moisture and the berries, especially the ones that are on top. It adds some sweetness but isn’t excessive.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
@Theresa
The cake is very dry. The simple syrup adds moisture. I did not use the simple syrup as I did not want to add more sugar. Cake was too dry as a result. I

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly half the recipe following the stated quantities (thanks for metric!), which yielded 12 perfect, fantastic cupcakes. The technique was very simple, and resulted in cupcakes with evenly risen, spectacularly light, delicately crumbed texture. I topped with my own buttercream, but will be making this again in entirety, as a test wedding cake. Love the almond extract in this, but will need to sub for nut allergies...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful. I made this for a friend and it was a big hit. I used 2 9-inch pans because that was what I had and I ended up slicing one cake in half (with the help of a YouTube tutorial). There wasn't much of a difference I thought but I was a bit worried since the cake is so delightfully spongy. I thought it would collapse. I had extra Chantilly cream as well as fruit so maybe next time I won't buy so much fruit. I printed out the directions and went through each step and ingredient and crossed them out. I prepped a few things before I started like making the cake in the morning and the frosting in the afternoon and weighing everything ahead of time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I understand the need to refrigerate after assembly. Using fresh washed and throughly dried fruit, does the fruit bleed? So if assembled the day before will there be an issue?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Delicious.  I used three nine inch pans, which still resulted in a beautifully tall cake.  It was great to be able to make the cake the day prior.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I found that the three layers were each around 480g of batter - much more than what the recipe says. Did anyone else find this too, or did I do something wrong?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
I made this yesterday for Easter and I really recommend splitting the frosting into two loads. It would have made making sure it was mixed correctly and well much easier. First load could be for the crumb coat and the second could be for the finishing. I did it in one go and it was unwieldy and I had to put the collar on my stand mixer to prevent overflow.  
Other than that, this was delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Bywater Bakery no longer makes this cake from scratch. The frosting still is, but the actual cake is from Sysco. With that in mind, you could focus on getting the frosting right and still end up with a wonderful cake. It's a shame that the bakery is comfortable misleading customers like this though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: It took a little over 7 long months from the time that I read about this cake until today 5/11/25 when I finally got to taste it. Oh my God, was it worth the wait!!! Best cake ever

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Do you live at high altitude? If so, you'll need to reduce the amount of leavening and sugar to compensate for the reduced air pressure. At high elevations, without adjustments, cakes rise too much too fast, before they develop enough structure. Sometimes they run over the pan, sometimes they collapse.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: This reminds me of a single slice of cake I had decades ago at Chateau Marmot and it was so delicious I never forgot about it. My husband let himself down when I busted him stabbing a fork into the cake never even bothering to take it out of the refrigerator. Having no egg yolks in the batter makes for a dense angel food-like quality.  And the icing has so much volume in it…I gave my neighbor a generous slice and she was annoyed with me that it was way too big. Then came back the next day to return the plate confessing she ate the whole piece in one go.  This is my new favorite cake. It’s perfect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: I made this cake in an hour. Who knew?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: This cake actually originated at Sweet Lady Jane in Los Angeles. I'm pretty certain Whole Foods took it from them.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: The cake is a multi-step concoction that is otherwise pretty straight forward. It is a show stopper in presentation & has a lightly dense texture with a good tasting filling -- fresh very sweet fruit is key. I also made several other recipes to compare with this version, & a friend brought over chantilly berry cake from Whole Foods -- 2 families sampled them all & wondered what the hoopla was about. The recipe versions were all good, none WOW, but all better than the WF version.

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Overly sweet, the Chantilly cream as written was not thick enough to properly frost the cake as pictured (probably needed gelatin). The strawberry cream layer cake tastier, more balanced and way easier with a fraction of the calories

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Great cake actually while a lot of steps are involved if you move slowly and premeasure eveything it goes rather quickly in terms of putting it together. I just did two layers in part because I don't have 8 inch cake pans mine are nine inches.  It was pretty and a show stopper would not make it again as it didn't really seem to appeal to those in my household.  I actually stumble upon this when I typed in Chantilly cake.  I was really looking for the Hawaii version of a Chantilly cake.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Hi! I want to bake this for my daughter's birthday, and I was wondering how well this cake freezes. I want to make the layers in advance, but I am not sure if it is a good idea. TIA!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
@Julia
The slices freeze wonderfully for consumption over the course of a week. However I’m not sure about freezing before presentation. The chantilly cream - denser it might be - still flattens resulting in a sad looking cake. I suggest making the cakes (saran wrap overnight), the cream, and prepping the berries a day ahead and put it all together the next day.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Before I invest half of my life to bake this cake, I have a couple of questions: is it sweet or not very sweet? The amount of sugar in the icing and the layer of simple syrup on every layer makes me think it will be too sweet for my liking but if it’s light and airy and creamy as it looks in the photo, I’m in. I should explain I’m a cook but not a baker, so if I’m going to bake a fancy cake, I really hope to like it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody:
@Lola 
It’s definitely an indulgent cake that will be the pièce de résistance at a party. I personally wouldn’t call this frosting light or airy, though certainly delicious and addictive. It’s made with whipping cream, mascarpone, cream cheese AND lots of sugar. You should try a slice of it from local Whole Foods before committing to making it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Funny! My decade in New Orleans (including being a licensed NOLA tour guide) I always heard this called: Gentilly Cake. Still hear that when I go back. Yes, it was made with Chantilly. But we all thought it was named for the neighborhood. Then again, maybe that was when she was with WF? Lol I remember when Bywater Bakery opened. This is great! As a pie person this is my FAVORITE CAKE. Moved back north in 2017 and I've missed it.

Also, can this be stored in the fridge/freezer before icing? I.e. bake cakes on one day, ice the cake the next day?

Thanks in advance!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chantilly Cake With Berries Recipe
commentBody: Made this just with strawberries (but otherwise exactly as written) for a friend's birthday and it was a big hit.  A couple of suggestions:  Use ALL the frosting -- if you have a heavier coating it makes it easier to smooth out the surface; use toothpicks on the top and sides to keep plastic wrap from sticking to the surface; keep everything very cold, especially cake and frosting before, during and after assembly.  I would also probably use less almond extract next time, or do part vanilla.

Even though I decided it was worthy of finally buying a cake carrier instead of carrying my cakes in an upside down lidded pyrex bowl and the handle of the carrier failed and the cake dropped and no longer looked pretty. Was delicious and I think the people who asked for the recipe didn't do it out of pity...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
I love the idea of melting the cheese and grilling the bread before putting the tuna in the sandwich. I usually make open face tuna melts and broil them, but then the tuna warms up. 
Honestly, I don’t understand why people have to vent their food preferences in a commentary on a specific recipe. Just use your favorite mayonnaise, mustard, bread or whatever and stop complaining about the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Made as instructed. Cape cod kettle cooked salt and vinegar chips stood up with a satisfying crunch, even in the saucy tuna salad. Delish. Food should be joyful, this one hit the spot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: What’s with all the “preferred “ name brands? I understand people have their preferences.  I prefer the way Ina Garten states it.   “Use a good quality mayo” or whatever the ingredient may be.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Reminds us of our childhood tastes. We have gotten all fancy of late, but there’s a place in the world for French’s yellow mustard, and old style mayo. American cheese is pushing it though. I have all this stuff except the cheese and intend to make a half recipe, as there is only me. I’ll eat it for a couple of days. On pumpernickel bread.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I think it is because the recipe specifically called for Hellman's.  Had it said, "good quality mayo", likely nobody would have balked. :)  I think Hellman's is great, prefer Duke's, and really prefer Kewpie, but yes, I would just go with what I have or prefer without calling it out.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @TOM G saying “use a good quality mayo” is very subjective - mayo that I love may be sweet or particularly runny, but because I love it I might think it’s good quality. Noting a specific brand of product ensures the recipe tastes as intended/designed. Obviously recipes are open to customisation which will inevitably alter the end result - neither a good or bad thing as long  as it’s enjoyed when eaten!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
Why Hellmans Mayo? Dukes mayo has no sugars, never had 'em, never will. We've scrubbed of supermarket list of "added sugars." Never missed 'em. Never will. 
Your body thank you.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I read this before, then looked at the ingredients of both brands online. Both have added sugar, the their webpages are to be believed. Very little, it doesn’t bother me much. You might make your own?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @NJ's Dr. Bob - because the recipe seeks to replicate one single version of a recipe, and the person publishing this effort believes one Mayonnaise gets the end product closer to the original than any other.  If you just want to make something you prefer to that original, use a different brand of mayonnaise.  It is pretty simple.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @NJ's Dr. Bob. LOVE Dukes!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
Sick of the American cheese hate! It’s not plastic, and will NOT kill you! The ingredients are: 

cheese, milk, water, salt, annato (like cheddar), sodium citrate (sodium and citric acid—can be made from baking soda and lemon juice), sodium phosphate (found in baking powder), calcium phosphate (main form of calcium in milk and our bones). 

None inherently bad for you! As a melty, mildly flavored cheese, American is perfect for tuna melts and grilled cheese—everything in moderation.

recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Seeing as how I was looking for a recipe to use up a soon-expiring jar of bread & butter spears that was bought accidentally, this one is going on my Labor Day menu. Based on many years of making tuna salad a variety of ways, we'll leave out the oil entirely & use water-packed tuna...the oil from the American cheese & the butter has always been enough w/any tuna melt we've ever made. Other people's mileage will differ of course!

commentBody: When I make tuna melts, sandwiches or salad, I swap in chickpeas, mashed or not, for one of the cans of tuna. A bit less animal product and more fiber.

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: If you want to make your own "American Cheese", just use some sodium citrate, plus your favorite cheese (can be anything from parmesan to cheddar to brie), and some liquid (beer, milk, water). Sounds fancy but it only takes 15 minutes and turns any cheese into a beautiful melty "American" style cheese.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
This is great because it celebrates the Tuna Melt, a grand invention.  Do as you wish with the ingredients (I prefer a big dot of mustard but no more and just a dab of chopped onion for example); so make it work for you.  Meanwhile the chips were a novel touch, I had just plain ones which were great changeup.  Long live the Tuna Melt, up there with a top notch Patty Melt.  

Celebrate great diner food!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: As I read the recipe, the cheese is divided among 4 slices of the bread, not all 8. So, the calculations are correct. That is, only one slice of bread gets the cheese melted on it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Agree with Anthony, just use a great canned tuna in olive oil and use the oil it was packed in. My favorite is Genova. Disregard adding olive oil to your recipe. Voila, even more flavorful.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @ I live in a very rural part of NM.   Quality tuna not available so I order the Genova in oil from Amazon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This is a wonderful pantry recipe as you can see that everyone is added what they already have available. We didn't have that much bread so we made open face sandwiches and put the potato chips over the cheese underneath the cold tuna. It was absolutely delicious. Heck, we even had vegan Gouda cheese and it all worked.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Love the idea of the crunch potato chips. And I have nothing against American cheese, but the only cheese for my tuna melts is Swiss.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Follow the portion sizes of each ingredient in the recipe carefully and you'll be making a perfect tuna melt. And don't fret over the brand of the ingredients--use what you have on hand; you'll be fine. (I didn't have yellowfin tuna around, but did just fine with albacore.)  I passed on using tabasco, and just put a small squirt of Sriracha in it instead, but you do you. But DO be sure to let it the mixture get cold overnight; it holds together MUCH better the next day and is far less messy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Jumping on the Heated Rivalry train. NYT, we see you 🤣♥️

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Recipe sounds delicious, but American cheese isn't cheese, it's an abomination. C'mon Sam, find another cheese that melts well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @The Dude stop it. Let people eat whatever cheese they want

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Fun sandwich. Cape Cod chips add an ideal crunch until it's all eaten. How I reached my late 60s without ever having a tuna melt beats me.  That said, the prescribed American cheese sort of disappeared into oblivion unnoticed. More of a more assertive cheese would be better.  Everybody has their own favorite tuna salad recipe and this one will not replace mine.  However, the whole concept is good fun and worth repeating. Give it a shot with an eye to your own preferences.

author: Alexa Weibel
