author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Typically hilarious nyt comments.  Pickles expire?   News to me.  But this was outta the park delicious.  The tuna salad recipe alone was superb, but adding potato chips and frying the bread separately was ingenious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: The epitome of comfort food. Love, love this recipe. I only regret I didn’t make more of it. I will next time. I made it exactly as prescribed in the recipe. I always do, before making any adaptations or changes to please my Latin taste buds. Next  time I make it, I’ll add a tad of cilantro and just a slight taste of jalapeño slices.  The recipe as is, however, is already a 5 star recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Directions say to place 1 and 1/ 2 slices of cheese on 4 slices of toasted bread.  So, 6 slices of cheese over 4 slices of bread = 1 and 1/2 slices of cheese on each sandwich.  Or have I missed something?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Nope, you haven't missed anything -- your math is correct. Why 1-1/2 slices of cheese per sandwich? Because rye bread typically is larger and more oval than regular square-shaped sandwich bread, thus requiring more store-bought sliced cheese (in square slices) to cover the bread's larger & longer surface.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe

Used two slices of cheese to cover my rather large rye bread slices.  Crunchy, tangy, rich, creamy, very filling!  Can make the tuna mix ahead and finish the sandwiches quickly when you're ready to eat.  Don't leave out the salt and vinegar chips!

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
A long time ago on Weight Watchers Week One, ( IYKYK), starving and cold ( probably January) a tuna melt was suggested.
I do not come from tuna-melt people, but I was desperate. 
Tuna salad mixed with shredded cheddar cheese on a TOASTED English muffin, wrapped in foil in the oven for a few minutes.
I still remember.
It was good, but this sounds better. Lunch tomorrow!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Can someone tell me why the recipe specifically calls for canned yellowfin tuna? I’ve only ever used albacore for tuna salads and am spooked to use something different - I need someone to talk me into it! 🙏

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
@Ali Harris
The albacore tuna packed in water is drier. If you look at any Italian recipe or French recipe, when they use canned tuna, they use yellowfin tuna packed in olive oil. It’s better. Try it. It is all I use. I haven’t used albacore tuna packed in water in a can in 15 years.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
@ChefValerie
I've done this with both Cheddar and Gruyère. Works great. All hail Modernist Pantry!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I can't abide by yellow mustard. Otw this is my tuna recipe.  I prefer dijon mustard. Trader Joe's is solid. don't need  crunch in my sammie, I put the chips on the side. Sometimes I'll throw in some chili crisp, or kimchi. Feel free to throw some sauerkraut in there, and you've got a tuna reuben. A sammy I invented more than 30 years ago when working at Stage Deli in West Bloomfield, Michigan. Try different cheeses 2.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Amazing, my wife groaned when I told I found a tuna melt recipe, a huge hit. I used tuna packed in olive oil, and only had sliced Havarti cheese on hand. Worked well. Thank you for this amazing tuna melt recpipe Goden Diner and Mr Sam Yoo and Alexa

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @Hoffer great idea about the tuna in oil. Heading to the store to make this awesome recipe again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: For a variation, I love Swiss cheese and very crispy bacon slices along with the tuna salad.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: We love this recipe!  I thought adding olive oil was weird would make the tuna messier, not tastier, and skipped that one ingredient.  Today I ran across the recipe as published by the Golden Diner in a Vogue article from April, 2020, during Covid when restaurants were beginning to struggle, and although olive oil is listed, it is poured into the skillet and heated for grilling the bread.  It is NOT whisked into the tuna filling.  I will probably continue to grill the bread with butter.  Yum!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: If you squeeze all of the liquid out of the tuna, the oil is necessary for consistency and flavor. Others say they used tuna canned with olive oil. I don't like the generic oil used for that purpose. If you squeeze out water, you can add whatever olive oil you like... just not a weird variety.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
@Richard X
I don’t think you can find yellowfin tuna packed in water. I only saw albacore tuna packed in water.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Enjoyed this enough the first time that I've made it a couple of more times. Much preferred my second attempt using dijon mustard instead of yellow mustard, and my third using English mustard from a jar I hauled home from Mason & Fortnum's duty free shop at Heathrow Airport [after airport security confiscated my first jar because it was bigger than what was allowed :-( ].

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Dill pickle potato chips ( rt 66 or cape cod) for the win !

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Best tuna melt I ever made, and super easy. I love the technique of grilling bread and melting cheese first, then adding tuna salad. The spicy dill chips I added put it over the top.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This is my goto tuna salad recipe. The only modification I make is my local grocery store doesn’t have yellowfin tuna in water, only in olive oil. So I omit the olive oil and use the entire can of tuna in oil. S&V chips on top are the chef’s kiss. It’s my favorite sandwich.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This sandwich is fantastic.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
Mother of God! This is one of the most satisfying sandwiches I've ever eaten. 
I used a good sharp cheddar, cornichon instead of bread and butter, green onions (white and dark green parts), Crystal hot sauce, crunchy jalapeños, tuna in olive oil and a bit more mayo. Perfection! I actually thank God as I savor every bite. I've been making sandwiches with this for the past 3 days. Yes, the tuna salad does get even better after sitting a day or two in fridge.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I never write reviews but I can tell you this is the best recipe for Tuna melts. This is the only way I will make it

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @Janne me too!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
Shane Hollander and Ilya Rozanov brought me here! 

The recipe is fantastic! So good

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Love potato chips on a sammich!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Tuna melts always contain sliced tomatoes. No garlic or onion powder please. Bread & Butter pickles on the side along w salt and vinegar CapeCod potato chips. It’s a classic!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @Name Linda Criss I never mix tomatoes with tuna melts because the acidity highlights the “fishy” taste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This recipe proves that tuna melts do not ALWAYS contain tomatoes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I don’t hand out 5-stars often but this is utterly fantastic. I’m not even really a huge lover of tuna sandwiches but this thing is something else. Wow

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly according to the recipe because it looked delicious and I happened to have all the ingredients in my cupboard already (including the salt and vinegar chips!), except for rye bread. I had whole wheat, so I used that. But I think rye would be better, so next time I will be sure to buy some rye. It was in fact delicious and very satisfying comfort food, which I will certainly make again, especially this winter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: When I order a tuna melt I ALWAYS specify that I want a grilled (Swiss!) on rye, then add a scoop of tuna salad. Hot Tuna is a 60’s/70’s rock band, not lunch! There’s nothing better than a tuna melt, but Swiss cheese is a deal breaker!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This was great. We substituted a small diced hot pepper for the tabasco. Worked really well. Next time we might add a few capers.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Made with local bakery’s NY Rye bread, no name brand other products because that’s what I had on hand. Subbed in rice vinegar for distilled white. Used tuna in oil because it needed using and skipped the olive oil. Also not a fan of American cheese so thinly sliced some Monterey Jack and used that.  Delicious.  Don’t skip the Tabasco! Also wonder how Dijon would be in this recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Only change was to mix up the cheese to swiss and cheddar. Paired with a bowl of Melissa Clark's Corn Tomato and Basil Chowder was perfect on a cool Maine evening.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is amazing! It's our go to tuna sandwich now!  YUM!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I made this a couple of nights ago. I made it pretty much according to recipe, unusual for me, because my husband loved the list of ingredients.  I halved them, as there were just two of us, and I didn't get to refrigerate for the entire hour, because I had run out of time.  No matter, because it was delicious.  My husband is already asking for it again!  I didn't mind the Hellman's suggestion, because I always find I like its flavor best, but I did use the low-fat version.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely the best Tuna sandwich and or grilled cheese ever!  I added a couple of thinly sliced tomatoes but otherwise followed the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Too spicy, sorry. Good tuna (I prefer Tonnino) needs little more than mayo to enhance its already rich (meaty?) flavor. So many recipes these days are ruined by an onslaught of hot/spicy condiments. What's happened to taste buds?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I agree that Tonino tuna is too good to use for a sandwich containing many other ingredients. I use good-quality canned tuna in evo instead, and it was excellent. Please don't knock it until you've tried it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Don’t skip the step for chilling the mix before adding it to your sandwiches at the end. Because of the amount of liquid/oils/fats, this is a very loose mixture, so not chilling it results in the filling exploding out the sides with every bite.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: No. Mayo + olive oil + butter +  chips = artery grease Not worth the textual stuff that these chefs constantly pitch as "taste". Easy pass.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I love comments like this attached to a recipe with 5 out of 5 stars!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Alexa Weibel is the goddess of modern food. I used vegan mayonnaise and it worked well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I will too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I did use tuna in oil, drained it and whisked in when noted. Better than plain EVOO.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Made this and loved it. I didn’t add the extra olive oil nor did I let the tuna mixture sit prior to making the sandwich.  Really great baseline - next time I’ll play with other cheese types and bread though the rye really amps the flavors up.  Salt vinegar chips a must.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Made as written - and it's delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! Definitely the tuna melt of my dreams. The technique of pregrilling the sourdough bread/melting the cheese was brilliant. I only used salt, fresh ground pepper, fresh Italian parsley, and dried dill which suited me today. The fresh celery and red onion were perfect for crunch. I had only yellowfin tuna in oil in my pantry, so drained it well and lowered the mayonnaise amount. I also used dill pickle potato chips as I others were in the house. Thanks for this great recipe that elevated a classic.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I used regular sliced cheddar...this was very good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Really, really delicious.  I mostly followed this recipe and even went out and bought the chips, and it added that extra something something.  The only bad thing about this sandwich is that it's probably at least 1000 calories.  Thank you NY Times for putting up this recipe.  I tried to get into this restaurant once and there was a 90 minute wait.  We decided to go elsewhere in the end.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: QUESTION: Do we not have a distinct oversight in instructions? Getting ready for 1st try here and baffled as to whether tuna/scoop goes on bread toast pieces WITH cheese or WITHOUT cheese?  Shirley  we should specify? ALSO: Picture sure LOOKS like the sandwich has cheese on BOTH slices anyway, under AND over.  Which couldn't be a bad idea, right?  But the recipe is pretty-darn specific, cheese on ONLY four slices.  Have I missed something? Or is this a rare omission in  NYT recipe? Please help!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: tuna onto cheesy bread slices, top with chips, close with non-cheesy bread - my reading of the directions

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
"divide the cheese among 4 slices of bread, tearing cheese to fit in a single layer (1½ pieces per slice of bread).
8 slices of bread for 4 sandwiches. but as per the directions, you only place the 6 cheese slices on 4 of the slices of bread ... and use the other 4 slices to cover and make the 'sandwich' ...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Granted, this is a careful, next-level version: but as a lad crisp and melted cheese sandwiches were de rigeur to soak up an evening's indulgence. Top marks for using salt n vinegar crisps...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Minced bread in the sauce? Is that just confusion about the pickles? Is AI writing the descriptions?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I think they are talking about a bread-and-butter pickle.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
@Sylvua
Instead of “minced bread”, the recipe calls for “¼ cup minced bread and butter pickles”.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Minced bread and butter pickles, a type of thinly sliced, sweet pickled cucumbers. Not minced bread.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: In the west, Hellman's Mayo is packaged as Best Foods. There is also Dukes Mayo in the Midwest and the slightly sweet mayo varieties. I recommend a couple of things. Dab of  Sweetish mayo, celery and onion minced real real fine and enough mayo to make the tuna whirl to creamy shreds in the food processor so creamy, ever so slightly sweet and slightly crunchy via small dice. Smooth and sublime. Cheese melt extraordinaire. Toasted Rye a must. Butter, no.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: As a European I’m curious what American cheese is? Is it a type of cheddar?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: It’s a processed cheese that melts beautifully

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: If you can find Cooper's cheese, it's a really good version of American cheese.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: As far as American cheese, the Kraft singles are bad and I don't think Velvetta is even cheese.  The best American cheese I have found are the Kraft Deli Deluxe and Boards Head.  And I do think that to be a legit tuna melt the bread has to be rye.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Who doesn’t love a solid tuna melt? I like a nice slice or two of salted and peppered beefsteak tomato on it and a pickle on the side, myself.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Love tuna melt!, the way I make them includes toasted bagel halves, and the tuna mixture includes diced tomato(meaty part not the seeds) and 1/2 tsp Old Bay seasoning, 1 tbsp lemon juice. I top bagel halves with tuna mixture and grate Swiss cheese over the top, broiling until cheese is golden…yum yum!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I make tuna melts frequently with my granddaughter, who loves them. But for the life of me I can't imagine why I should add olive oil to this mix. It's got mayo (real mayo, yes!) and that's all it needs. When first reading the recipe I thought this oil was a misprint. Totally unnecessary fat (in addition to mayo, cheese, butter and chips). What is the point of the oil?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I get sliced Boar’s Head American cheese sliced to order at the deli counter of the supermarket. And sweet pickle relish could sub for the bread & butter pickles in a pinch, I’m sure.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Tuna melts should be open face.  Preferably with a slice of cheddar.  Period.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
I can understand why Hellman’s is recommended. But, as many have said, use what you have and/or what you prefer. Make it your own. 

For even more crunch, how about a few cucumber slices added to the pile?  We love tall sandwiches!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: It’s always better to have cheese melted on the food not on the bread!! This is true for burgers too!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: But a tuna melt is not a burger. The idea here is to keep the tuna cold for contract. (Not something a care about personally.)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Just made this with some substitutions based on what I had available - oil-packed tuna versus water-packed, havarti for American, kosher spears for the pickles, thick Italian slices rather than rye, and I omitted the onion powder.  I also was hungry so couldn’t be bothered to wait for the salad to chill.  The result was still fantastic.  Just make this with what’s on hand and don’t sweat the specific ingredients too much.  It’ll still be excellent.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Why not tuna packed in olive oil?  Seems silly to squeeze the water out of tuna packed in water and then add olive oil. Also, I do think mature chatter is better than American cheese for this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I used pickled red onions as a substitute for both the pickles and the red onion and it was absolutely delicious. Can’t wait to make another tuna melt.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I'm sorry Dukes fans. After years of Helmann's, the taste of Dukes comes off as a bit off. I have a half filled bottle I am going to chuck out.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I always make mine open-faced. To keep the tuna salad from heating up, I place a thick slice of tomato (or two) on top of the tuna, then the cheese melted over that.  Even in a closed sandwich it's not a tuna melt for me without tomato.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Huge hit at our house.  Super yummy and easy to make.   Like others, next time I will just used tuna in oil rather than buying in water and then adding oil.  Also, I doubled the amount of spices. This one’s a keeper!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: The recipe didn't say what type of American cheese to use so I picked Alpha Tolman from Jaspar Hill Farm.  The sandwich was great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: My hack today was adding to the mix a liberal sprinkling of gochugaru. Yum!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: You specify the mayo, the mustard, and the hot sauce by name but any old brand of tuna will do? Never mind. Like many others, we'll use the mayo, the mustard, and the hot sauce we have laying around. WOULD be interested in which tuna you use, however.   This one sounds good and I appreciate the suggestion to try rye bread.  Will do.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: If I ate this at a restaurant I would be very happy with the chef. Instead I made it at home and am currently very happy with myself! The perfect diner sandwich. I have the  leftover tuna left in the fridge and am already thinking about my sandwich for tomorrow!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Toooooo much butter!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: There is noooo such thing as tooo much butter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
Best tuna melt ever. Rich in flavor and texture.
I liked the fact that the tuna was cold, wonderful addition of the potato chips.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Since childhood, I've loved BBQ potato chips in my Tuna Sandwich, melt style or plain. Usually eaten solo as a secret sin food.:}  Love a dash of Berbere or Curry Powder added to the tuna.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: As my daughter would say, this is just "basic." There's nothing special about it, but nothing really wrong with it either. If you don't already have a basic tuna salad recipe that you love, then I guess start here, but otherwise I don't see the point.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I guess the point is that this is the recipe as used by a very successful NYC diner. Question for you: how many "basic" recipes get 5 star ratings?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: CAVEAT EMPTORTR: Tuna melts are just about the most delicious sandwiches you can make when correctly made, and the one in the photo seems to hit all the right notes.  I was so curious that I went to the restaurant's website, and saw that they delivered to my area.  But then I looked at the photo the restaurant had posted on it's website of the tuna melt in question - it looked ANEMIC!  The bread was barely toasted and there was much less tuna used as compared the one in this article's photo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Great method, but substituting the American cheese for a quality cheddar or Gruyere greatly improves the sandwich.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is for the tuna melt as served at the Golden Diner. The Golden Diner uses American cheese.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I subbed Colby-Jack for American and it was great. Otherwise I cleaved to the recipe exactly and it was so delicious that it will go into my repertoire. I also used kettle-style salt & vinegar chips, which added both crunch and zing. Genius.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Best tuna sandwich I’ve ever had!!! Yumbo!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Some observations on this recipe:  First, so fresh!  I added tarragon and chives because I have them in the garden and it made the sandwich pop.  Oddly, what I tasted most prominently, was butter.  Maybe I was overgenerous when I slathered my bread, dunno, but it was toasty and warm and great.  I used Dave's multi-grain breads and it was crunchy in itself, and so I did not pick up the chips, which is a real shame.  Next time will use less crunchy bread/or omit chips.  Added white beans for fiber

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: The taste of the included potato chips was horrible.  At least I tried it as written.  Not again tho.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I'm 77 years old and I have included potato chips in every tuna sandwich since I was 15. Buy better potato chips.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I don't get it.  Not sure that I understand what's the big deal here.  I followed the recipe exactly and got a very ordinary (but too sweet, from the pickles) tuna sandwich.  Ah well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: If your first bite of the sandiwch was too sweet, why not take the rest of the pickles out of the sandwich? So next time don't use the pickles. Ah well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent tuna melt. Made the recipe as written, except: I used ~1/2 the mustard, omitted the tabasco sauce, used Japanese loaf bread (Pasco), and added some cheddar to both bread slices (American cheese to only one).  For chilling, I put the tuna salad in the freezer and it was cold in a couple of minutes.  I used plain ruffles chips and they were perfect.  
It's very filling as written, I might try an open-faced sandwich next time, which is the way I recall seeing tuna melts in the past.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: American cheese is an abomination. Use a mild or sharp cheddar like a sane human being.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Did you notice that this is the recipe for a tuna melt as it is made at the Golden Diner. The Golden Diner uses American cheese. American cheese melts well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Amazing!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Wow. Just wow. Made this today and followed the recipe with some exceptions: brown bread, Dijon mustard, plain Cape Cod chips, Colby cheese. Delicious, especially the wonderful crunchy texture added by the chips!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Elevate this with a grilled tuna steak. You will never eat tuna out of a can again, I promise.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: And make a patty melt with a filet mignon. You will never eat hamburger again, I promise.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Brings back memories of childhood, except this adulthood version is better,haha!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I didn't love this. I'm in the minority in my household  because my husband thought it was the bees' knees. I followed the recipe to the letter using marble rye. On paper, it sounded great, and I really wanted to love it like the other reviewers, but to me it was just so, so plus, the potato chips didn't add that much.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
Fish with cheese…mustard with mayonnaise…and all the subtlety of a demolition derby of bad taste. 

Your readers will love this! Yum!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: For anyone else getting home too late to pull off the whole thing and using the recipe as a base: I skipped the extra oil, added a bit of Greek yogurt, and should have doubled the spices. I plopped it on mixed greens and a slice of homemade whole wheat. I like the flavors and hope to try the whole thing later.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
I really liked this sandwich!

But the tuna mix was very salty to the point that I definitely couldn’t add the chips to the sandwich. 

I think the successful grilling method will stick with me for making a tuna melt, more than the actual tuna salad recipe. It deals with the issue that my mom doesn’t like: she thinks warm tuna salad is weird. 

Just make the tuna mixture whatever way you like!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: The "point" to which the tuna mix was salty was completely under your control. The recipe says only to "Season to taste with Tabasco and salt." Knowing that potato chips were yet to be added, you might have added less salt. Don't blame the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I really liked this. personally if I made it again I might use plain chips, because between the vinegar and mustard I felt there was already enough acid, but that said I did enjoy it very much.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight according to the recipe. The only change was to use Bubbie's Spicy Bread and Butter Pickle Chips and my homemade caraway rye. It was very good, but too rich for me; I could only manage half a sandwich. Next time, I will toast the rye bread in the toaster, place the American cheese on it, and let it melt under the broiler. Then I'll proceed with the remainder of the recipe. The salt and vinegar potato chips were a plus.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @Lex homemade caraway rye!! Oh my goodness sounds amazingggg

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe but I used full fat Greek yogurt instead of mayo and skipped the olive oil. Healthier and still tastes delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Made as written and the sandwiches were delicious. Though next time I'll try half the olive oil or eliminate entirely--it seemed unnecessary. However, I'll never eat another tuna sandwich without adding potato chips. Genius!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I love the recipe for the tuna, but I find I like just toasting the bread, omitting the cheese, and just using the tuna and chips. It’s just as satisfying and less heavy without all of the butter and cheese

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: That's not a tuna melt, is it? The topic here is Tuna Melt.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I HAVE TO MAKE THIS!!!  I have not made it yet, but I can tell it is going to be AWESOME!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I like the recipe. . . except for the Salt and Vinegar potato chips.  Don't get me wrong, I LOVE Salt and Vinegar chips, but unlike ordinary potato chips that consist only of potatoes, vegetable oil and salt, Salt and Vinegar chips contain artificial ingredients.  A bridge too far.  If the author knows a brand of salt and vinegar chips that contain no artificial flavors, that would be helpful.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: this was … so dank. i added a little fresh dill to the tuna mix and used sharp white cheddar instead of the singles. i’m full and im still thinking about it. 10/10 will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I use 1/3 cup Wickles Relish in lieu of chopped pickles. Delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Oh my goodness. Amazing. Definitely use salt & vinegar potato chips. 2nd time I made it, I only had sea salt and it made a difference.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Made this recipe as is with gochujang substitute for hot sauce since that’s what I had. I made it with Gouda slices (on hand) and it was excellent!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Potato chips inside the sandwich really made it! Didn't have yellow mustard (yuck), but used dijon and a dash of mustard powder and it was very tasty. Rye bread and sweet pickles made it right for me!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This tuna melt was a HIT!! I used yellow fin tuna in olive oil since I couldn’t find it in water.  Just drizzled a little olive oil in the batch I prepared.  Used a little more mayo (Dukes) and more finely diced bread and butter pickles than the recipe called for.  Added all other ingredients.  The Tabasco sauce adds a nice flavor to the tuna, but I didn’t make it spicey.  Brushed melted butter on both sides of sour dough bread and grilled it (so nice and crunchy!). Added 2 slices of deli quality yellow cheese slices on each piece of bread while grilling.  Put generous amounts of tuna on sandwiches along with vinegar and salt potato chips.  Served with  sliced dill pickles on the side.  Delish!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Reader beware, this is the most addicting tuna sandwich in the universe. I often halve this recipe just for one sandwich with zero sense of guilt.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I really enjoyed these, and I was skeptical of the potato chips in the sandwich. I will definitely make these again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
@Christy
When I was a child, I layered every sandwich with potato chips (and hamburgers, with French fries on top); it was all about texture and taste. Potato chips enhance any sandwich!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I just made this as-written for lunch and it was eyes-rolling-back-in-my-head good. I would NEVER put pickles in my tuna salad (basically blasphemy in my mind) but figured I can’t knock it till I try it … and I am so glad I did! Try it, you’ll like it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: So, to make this fabulous tuna melt for lunch, I only need almost two hours to prepare them, along with 16 different ingredients. Upon reading this recipe a second time, I think it makes more sense to go to the diner instead!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: A heated tuna meltdown inspires a rivalry. IYKYK.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is delightful! I’m vegetarian but really wanted to try this, so I used a mixture of mashed chickpeas & marinated artichoke hearts as a substitute for the tuna. It was perfect!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This was an interesting and different take on a tuna melt. Made as directed except we had no salt and vinegar chips on hand and had to substitute sweet green relish for the bread and butter pickles because didn't have those. Thanks for the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I will definitely make this but will never use Tuna packed in water again. It's my understanding that Tuna packed in water was cooked in the can and that's why it has that "tinny" taste. After converting to Tuna packed in Oil the taste is far superior as, again, my understanding, the tuna is cooked outside the can and then packed. Very curious about using Kewpie mayo, too. Also, are we all understanding that the recipe is calling for wet mustard and not dry?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: What is minced bread and where is it in the instructions?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: It says minced bread and butter pickles. Not minced bread. It would have helped had it been written properly, as in “bread-and-butter pickles” where “bread-and-butter” together modify “pickles”. That kind of pickle is available most anywhere, and is commonly used. I prefer dill pickles and would use those instead.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Minced bread-and-butter pickles. They're a sweet pickle chip.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Made the recipe as written. It was just a tuna melt and what’s not to like about a tuna melt? I didn’t find anything particularly special about it, but it was tasty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Personally, I hate american cheese, but I'd bet this would be great with swiss, provolone, cheddar, muenster etc...Love this recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: West of the Rockies Hellmans is branded as Best Foods mayo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: West of the Rockies, Hellmann’s is branded as Best Foods mayonnaise.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: The best Vinegar and Salt chips by far are Boulder Canyon Malt Vinegar and Salt. IMO zero chemical taste like so many others

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Double the cheese please!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: In response to misteranthony, you’re right! Using tuna packed in olive oil works great for this recipe. You end up using less mayo, but the added olive oil is a terrific touch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: And, olive oil is much healthier than the oil that the mayonnaise is made with (unless you're using olive oil mayo)!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: We use Wickles original Relish in our tuna and it has just the right zing without the labor involved in chopping pickles. Name brand used here because we have found it to be the best IOHO.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: There’s a sandwich shop in Providence,RI which steams the cheese before adding it to the meats and greens of the sandwich so you are able to taste all the flavors at their ideal temperature

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
Pickles on the side, then layered whole
Tabasco, well 'Some Like it Hot'
mustard?  vinegar?  olive oil?  why?  Oh maybe to get the 'schmearing' to work 
garlic & onion powder? (if you need it, fresh is best)
salt?  (tuna is fish, fish is salty)
Now the grand finish....grated mozzarella into a non-stick pan, then drop your sandwich on it.  Longer it cooks, the crispier it gets.  Both sides are even better!  Enjoy & you're welcome!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
Tuna isn't salty, naturally.  It's low sodium.  One ounce (28 g) of fresh tuna has only 13 mg of sodium.

It's the water or oil in the can that is salted.

One ounce of tuna packed in oil has 118 mg sodium, while one ounce packed in water has 70 mg sodium.

Rinsing canned tuna can significantly reduce the sodium content.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: For anyone with an aversion to canned tuna, I use frozen tuna steaks that I sear and cook to medium and then crumble. I find it to be much more of a pleasurable texture and taste and I also have the peace of mind of knowing what's in my tuna. I used to love canned tuna for all my, salads and casseroles, but after using the steaks because I had them and no canned, I couldn't make myself eat the canned stuff anymore. Worth giving a shot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Nice combination of flavours in the tuna mixture. If you don't eat American "cheese" slices, which we don't, this sandwich is great with cheddar cheese. For faster melting, we grate the cheese and sprinkle it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I really dislike salads made with pickles, though I like pickles themselves. I make my tuna with mayo, lemon juice, salt, pepper, celery, onion and green pepper, which would also work well here. If I want to grill, I don't use a lot of butter, but lightly spray with olive oil. I serve these with a side salad or vegetable to balance nutrients and reduce overall fat content.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This was a little overwrought for me. If make a more traditional tuna salad next time. I also ended up burning 3 slices of bread because the cheese didn’t melt but that was a me thing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Delish!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I’m 3/4 of the way through this recipe and I’m stumped. Half of the sandwich is toasted and the other half isn’t? That’s not what the photo shows and I’m not reading in the recipe anything about toasting the top slice. What am I missing?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
No, all the bread is toasted. Only half of the bread gets cheese added.

"Meanwhile, lightly butter one side of *each* slice of bread. "

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: why is the tuna cold? This is where every tuna melt I get falls short. I used to go to a one man hole in the wall diner in Chinatown where the cook heated  tuna straight from the can on a pan on the stove - so much better

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I think the tuna is cold because most people prefer it that way...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Did it exactly as written but swapped out the butter for ghee. Heaven.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This recipe has some great ideas. I will try it with MY favorited ingredients. It’s the preparation I am interested in. Nothing more to say.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: A classic, this was so good!! I added a few green pimento stuffed olives and black olives, sliced in half.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: The tuna mix was too oily and I didn’t even use all of it. It would be better to use tuna in oil. I prefer Kewpie mayo and American cheese is a no. I used Gouda and sharp cheddar.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I thought the cheese was supposed to be MIXED with the tuna and the whole thing griddled....

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: How prominent is the mustard taste here? This sounds yummy except I am not a mustard fan, but if it gets melded with the other flavors, that's different. As a mustard disliker, leave it out, reduce it or use it?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: If you don't like mustard, it would make sense to leave it out. Why punish yourself?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Everybody seems to like this recipe except... me. This is the only Times recipe that I've really disliked. I made a couple of changes – used tuna in olive oil and substituted sharp cheddar for disgusting American cheese – that can only have improved it. The result was, I'm sorry, an unappetizing mess.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: What is the best brand of canned tuna to use?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Canned tuna is not known as a gourmet food. Use whatever you like. Solid white tuna is more expensive than chunk light tuna.  Yellowfin and albacore cost more. Jarred tuna (Tonnino brand, for example) is excellent but quite expensive. Find what you like at a price you are willing to pay. I make this recipe often and I love the sandwich. But American cheese and potato chips don't elevate it to "fancy" level. For that reason, ordinary canned tuna is just right.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Decadent, delicious and easy. Maybe too rich to have often; great for a treat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Add slices of crisp apple. They provide a bright and juicy addition for the taste buds.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Not bad. But you don't need to use the endorsed brands. Other mayonnaises and mustards work just as well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This was the recipe used at Golden Diner. The "endorsed" brands are what Golden Diner uses.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
5 ounce can is 142 grams. 
So two 5 ounce cans would be close to three 95 gram cans

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious. Left out the onion and celery as I don't like them. Could have done with a crunchier chip as they were getting a bit soggy by the end

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Seems like a lot of people like the “crunch” of potato chips. My wife doesn’t like them. I Jesé’s my regular tuna salad recipe and added some guyere.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Unfortunately, I didn't think the tuna salad was any better than a typical recipe. But what really made this sandwich, in my opinion, was the salt & vinegar chips. Could only give it 3 stars.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: a variation on the old fashioned classic Patty Melt sandwich

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Right. And tuna steak is a variation on a ribeye.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I used fresh fish from the grill instead of canned. It was a big hit with my fisherman. Subbed slim sliced medium cheddar.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Our go-to tuna. Just as good on a toasted bagel without the chips and cheese :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: What is this French's /Frank's mustard. Never heard of them but they apparently have stolen French's logo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
Definitely a tuna melt. We used Gouda and havarti cheese. One on top, the other on the bottom. Will
make again. Don’t skip the kettle chips if possible, but still would be good without them.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Tuna is usually tinned with olive oil or seed oil which are low quality oils, extracted with heat or chemicals and they are saturated oils. I find that there is a huge difference in taste when using extra virgin olive oil or another cold pressed oil. Maybe that's why the recipe discards it. @misteranthony

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @ChefValerie doesn't sound fancy at all. It sounds like a giant pain...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Huge hit in our house. They loved the salt and vinegar chips in the sandwich. Fun twist! I personally don't love American cheese, but it sure did melt easily.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: So so so good!! Definitely super heavy though — restaurant food for sure.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Replaced the American cheese with a Wisconsin 12-year Cheddar.  Fabulous.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: A "HEFTY dose" AND a "HEFTY scoop" -- this is a a powerhouse of a sandwich. Will I be able to even pick it up?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Am I reading this correctly that only one side of the bread is grilled not all 8 slices for the 4 sandwichs?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @Rebeka all 4 sides are grilled.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I've tried two grocery stores near me (I live in Harrisburg, PA) and cannot find yellowfin tuna in water. Both stores only carried yellowfin tuna in oil. Can 'regular' canned tuna in water be used instead?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: @David DeKok. Just use the yellowfin in oil. Utter precision/duplication is not a useful habit in cooking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: We live inHawaii and had leftover seared shibi (small ahi tuna).  Combined 5+ ounces of that with canned tuna.  Otherwise followed the recipe and it was fabulous.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
Substitutions:
Sour Cream for Mayo
Dill pickle for bread & butter
Pepper Jack cheese for American
Salt and Pepper chips for salt and vinegar chips.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Could be the best tuna salad sammy ever

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: This sandwich was just "okay," not great. I accidentally put cheese on the top and the bottom pieces of bread. I used a ciabatta roll instead of rye bread. That held up well on the griddle. Probably won't try this recipe again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe is naughty in the very best way possible. Crunchy, creamy, toasty goodness. 
Perfection.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Was definitely one of the best tuna melts we've made but be warned, they are hearty, filling sandwiches! We all only ended up eating half of our sandwiches, so just keep that in mind. Really good though!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Made it as listed but should have added a bit more hot sauce. Also, didn't have any rye on hand and subbed a seeded oatmeal bread. Bad move. But will make again! Now I have to finish that bag of chips!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Really tasty and a nice easy weeknight meal.  Scaled down to 2 sandwiches and used slices of a fresh homemade loaf of flaxseed rye sourdough.  Stuffed leftover tuna salad into piquillo peppers for a bright appetizer.  My wife had a chuckle at the organic American cheese slices I found—seemed like an oxymoron but worked really well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Super delicious. Added some more pickles and onion for more crunch!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Made it with 2 cans of Fishwife’s Tuna in Spicy Olive Oil and reincorporated the olive oil from the can into the recipe. This is going to be the only recipe for a tuna melt I ever go back to.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe! Made as directed and turned out great. I’ll make my tuna melts like this from now on.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Hands down, one of the best sandwiches I’ve ever had. Definitely the best tuna melt I’ve ever had. Make this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I used an In-n-Out spread packet (or two) since I didn't want to get bread and butter pickles just for this. My sister said it was the best tuna salad she's ever had. What was different for me was all the olive oil in it. It adds like buoyancy or body to it as well as a nice flavor. There's a huge difference letting it sit for a while. It's better for it to be mellow upon first tasting cause it's a lot of punchy flavor later. Next I want to try it with kimchi-flavored kettle chips.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made a lot of tuna melts in my life. This is by far the best. Wowza!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: i made this tuna melt following the recipe fairly closely except i used hook's 3 year cheddar instead of the foul american cheese and omitted the onion (can't tolerate raw onion), used plain crunchy chips to cut down on salt.  later i read the review placed by someone who used grilled onions; i will try that next time.  the melt was very tasty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: “foul American cheese” . Best melting sandwich cheese ever! You’ll come around some day😂

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: The recipe is the recipe is the recipe! But I I don’t needed the cheese and added the yummy tuna-mixture on 1 bread above thinly sliced oxheart tomatoes! Delicious!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Microplane some ginger and lemon zest. Substitute lemon juice for wine vinegar. This compensates for the fact that canned tuna has a tinny (stodgy) taste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Let me suggest a substitute for American cheese a beloved, albeit not by everyone, ingredient. Google for precise quantities. Grate whatever cheese you desire, add to water and sodium citrate. Heat until it all blends together. Cool this and then slice for your DIY American cheese.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  I would call this a tuna reuben.  The cheese could be omitted and would not be missed among the other tasty flavors and textures.  I buttered and griddled all pieces of rye bread but put cheese on only half of them.  Lots of crunch!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: My nephews moved a beautiful patio set for me and this was perfect for a Sunday afternoon late lunch.  I used a nice, dark pumpernickle bread; followed directions as stated. Wouldn't change a thing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Tuna melts.. Memories of coffee shops all over Manhattan & Bronx in the 1970s So simple. My tuna melts have been improved with the toast bread before melting cheese, I keep it simple. Hellman's mayo, lemon juice (bottle or real), diced onion, diced celery, salt & pepper - dill pickle on the side, I like sourdough bread and American cheese (Kraft yellow deluxe American or white if I don't have yellow) some decent potato chips on the side, miss the coffee shops.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Nutrition information is way off.  Do your own calculations if this is important.   Example, it lists 612mg sodium per sandwich, but just the cheese and commercial bread alone exceeds this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: Good but used a cheap sharp cheddar because that's what I had on hand but would like to try again with a more melty American and potato chips (thought I had some until I realized I didn't at the end).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Golden Diner’s Tuna Melt Recipe
commentBody: These are really good. I made a batch of the tuna salad and one sandwich at a time, as it's just me. I don't like yellow mustard, so I substituted Dijon. Overall comforting, joyful (as another commenter pointed out), and flavorfully colorful.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: Interesting recipe for the lamb. Tried it using 1 pound of ground lamb as finding a 1 lb boneless leg of lamb was not feasible. Served with Naan bread.  Good blend and made a good burger. Probably very good with sliced lamb,and I may use the rub  next time I cook a whole boneless leg.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: Costco has a 2# boneless leg of lamb that can be cut in half.  Freeze half of the leg.  If you have access to a real butcher as opposed to prepackaged supermarket meat, they should be able to give you 1 lb of boneless leg.  Even my Safeway meat cutter will pretty much give me what I ask for.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: Per the suggestion below I obtained a 3 lb boneless leg from Costco - the smallest they had (and a beautiful piece of meat, btw).  After trimming I used half for this and upsized slightly.  Results were delicious and very much like the original dish.  Vacuum froze other half to make again in a few months.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: Best not to call these a “burger” else it’s easy to misunderstand and use ground lamb. Needs to be strips of spiced and grilled lamb meat. Made correctly these are one of my favorite street food dishes in Chinese cuisine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: Made this with the spice mixture using ground lamb.  It was good - served with warm Naan.  Would use the spice mixture as a rub for a butterfly leg of lamb.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: Did you add the cornstarch to the ground lamb?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: I think the spice mixture sounds divine and have the same cornstarch question.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: I am using ground lamb and if here is a reason to use the cornstarch, I am not.  Will update with the result.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: The nearest Xi'an is often my first food stop on my many trips to NY. Now if only I could translate these flavors into the dumplings with that spicy, oily red broth, all my problems would be solved (mostly).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: @Dave T Genuinely believe buying a jar of their chili crisp oil is the answer! They ship it! And sometimes they sell it on Amazon too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: The lamb was delicious, great flavor combo.  I sauteed all of the veggies, including the onions and chilis, before adding the lamb, because I didn't like the idea of nearly raw onions and jalepeno.  The result was less browning, but a lovely blend of pungent flavors after I added the cumin and chili powder.   The "buns", however, were a total dud.  Dense hockey pucks that were raw inside and inedible.  I would recommend using Meera Sodha's recipe for naan on this website; its amazing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: For the spice mixture Chile powder I used gochugaru and cayenne. I also pre-marinated the lamb in some baking soda, soy sauce, msg, and shaoxing (small amounts of all for ~2hrs). Only other sub was jalapeno peppers. The meat came out wonderfully spiced, tender, and moist. Definitely a keeper. I'm not sure what, but I think the buns could benefit from a topping, something light, maybe an acid based slaw to add some crunch and freshness.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: I had to knead the dough for twice as long, ten minutes, to get it to be smooth & supple.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: HORRIBLE! Cumin completely overpowered this dish. What a waste of time and ingredients!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: I used thinly sliced lamb that I got from the Asian market in my small midwestern city.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody:
Thought the recipe was way too heavy on the ground cumin, to the extent that I couldn't really identify the meat beneath all that cumin.  If I were to make these again, I'd reduce the cumin by half.  

Also, I used normal-sized burger buns in lieu of making the buns.  I found that the amount of cooked meat was way too much for normal burger buns.  I could have easily made twice as many sandwiches.

Also, not being a fan of raw hot peppers or raw red onion, I cooked both with the lamb.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: To make this vegetarian, I'm thinking of substituting vegan chicken for the meat and making it a shawarma style Middle Eastern style dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: Considering doing this in a cast iron tray in my pizza oven at very high heat… thoughts?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody:
Be sure the wok/skillet is VERY hot, as the lamb will release a good bit of fat.  I made mine in a wok and had a hard time getting the lamb to brown, so I finished it under a broiler for some texture.  Next time I'll use a large skillet with more surface area.  

That said, the flavors were great (as always, lamb needs a generous amount of salt) and we loved it paired with a simple shirazi salad (not Asian of course, but still a nice complement).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried kneading the dough with the hook attachment of a stand mixer (instead of hand kneading)?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody:
I have used ground lamb to make Merguez for a very long time.
The recipe for it has more spices than this recipe and I suspect tastes better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody:
I did not make the bread. Not sure why anyone would, ha. 

That said, I loved it. I wasn’t brave enough to add all that cumin at the end. I did about half. Felt excessive, trusted my instincts. Maybe next time I’ll add it all. Enjoyed it on Hawaiian buns with feta and minty yogurt and killer homemade herby fries.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: I don't wrap food in plastic, rather close it in a tupperware or in a towel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: Lamb is delicious.  But as others have mentioned, the buns are a DUD.  Something is definitely off in the proportion of ingredients, the instructions, or both.  For example the ratio of water to flour is WAY off - the dough is a dry, dense mess.  And the timing of the instructions doesn't make sense (2 hours if making the buns, when all activities including frying as written take only 40 minutes??)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: The cumin tended to be very overpowering so I tempered it with light soy sauce along with XO sauce for some earthly flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: This is for people that don’t like lamb.  I only used 2 T cumin and it was overwhelming.  Didn’t try the rolls.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: Yes, corn starch to ground lamb

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Cumin Lamb Burgers Recipe
commentBody: Meh. I like cumin, but this was too much, as was the salt. The idea is great, though, and it comes together quickly. It would be good with half the cumin. Next time I'll serve with tzatziki and harissa sauces on pita.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: The best sandwich is the simplist one. A few years ago a neighbor gifted us a couple of large ripe heirloom tomatoes. I placed a few slices on white bread, added a little salt, dabbed a decent smear of mayonniase and took a bite. Wow! It was a revelation that something so simple can taste so good. Tried sprinkling a little parmesan but it killed the pureness of the tomato. I truly believe a well constructed tomato sandwich can stand up to a cucumber sandwich any day of the week.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: While the flavors were good, it was much more oily than our family cares for. Mayo on the bread to toast, more mayo on the sandwich, oil to cook the halloumi, dressing on the arugula, then remaining dressing drizzled over it. I'm not sure I'd dress the arugula if I were to make it again. I might also consider simply toasting the bread.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: @Maura agreed - it was overly and needlessly greasy and heavy. Not making again.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: @Maura you could also try grilling the halloumi rather than fry in oil.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Placing tomatoes directly against the bread changes its texture by making it moist. The riper the tomato, the wetter the bread will become, especially if the sandwich is packed to be eaten later. The solution is to place dry ingredients against the bread slices. Therefore, the order in this sandwich should be: bread-halloumi-tomato-arugula-bread.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This is why the mayo and oil is important - mayo and cheese provide a barrier to keep the bread from getting soggy.  Whenever you make a sandwich always put the cheese on one side and the mayo & mustard on the other and the juicy fillers like tomato and meat in the middle to keep the bread dry.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I switched out the halloumi for bacon and it was delicious!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: @Kristy360 — try the halloumi! It’s even better. Or, you know, have both (heh heh).

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
I want to make this over the next few hot days. I have a question: in the ingredients for the garlic pepper mayonnaise, I don’t see any pepper. Do I just add my usual ground black peppercorns to taste or do you have a specific type of pepper in mind?
Thank you

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: The instructions in the recipe say to season to taste with salt and pepper. I'd go freshly ground, personally. I think if the recipe-writers had a specific pepper in mind, they'd have said. That's my guess.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Added some Aleppo pepper to the garlic mayo spread to give it amazing depth without extra heat. Have made it twice and found the greens need a very small amount of dressing. I also found draining the salted tomatoes over a bowl/sink helps to concentrate the flavor and make the tomatoes just the right amount of juicy. Have also added pickled red onions or banana peppers for an additional flavor profile. So good any way, will definitely make again.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: What an awesome sandwich! I used olive oil to toast the bread and did not make the dressing for the arugula due to some people saying it was too oily. Great suggestion!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Halloumi can be hard to find so I substitute Queso De Freir.  It's very inexpensive and has a bit less sodium than the Halloumi.  Same end results - glorious and squeaky.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: If you made a different sandwich, why post about it? Just eat your sandwich!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I’m pregnant, which means I go through hyperfixation meals for several weeks. This might last me the rest of the pregnancy. It’s so good. Used ciabatta instead of sourdough.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: If you read the instructions, you will see that you season the Mayo with salt and freshly ground black pepper.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: @Dan Not in my version.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
I made this sandwich using all the ingredients and following the directions exactly, with the following exceptions only:
- Light Duke’s mayo (don’t hate lol)
- Power greens subbed for arugula (I had them on hand)
- I added red pepper flakes to the Greek dressing for the salad (I love heat)
- I used pickled red onions because yes

I thought it was delicious. 

Would I maybe use less mayo next time? Sure. Might I stack it differently next time? Quite possibly. Add pickles? Yum. Is less often more? Yup. And so on. 

This sandwich, as the recipe dictates and as I bowdlerized it, was fabulous.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Much ado about little. I’m going to toast bread in the toaster (wow!) and proceed to make a BLT subbing grilled halloumi for B and arugula for L (maybe dressed, or not) and good old Hellman’s. The T I’m leaving. Some things just don’t need to be that fussy.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Only toasted bread in toaster to avoid oiliness. Made sure to sandwich tomatoes in middle of sandwich to avoid sogginess. Would consider adding honey to halloumi next time to add some sweetness to contrast savory

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: My husband said this was one of the best sandwiches he’s ever had. I’d opt for a bit less oil or mayo or dressing next time - it was pretty sloppy. Also we put the pickles IN the sandwich. Maybe next time I’ll pickle the red onions first for extra acidity/ crunch. We used a gorgeous fresh cob of sourdough. So yum.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
Incredible sandwich that does not take an hour.   We scraped some time off by toasting the bread in the toaster without sacrificing flavor.

Yum!!!!!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
responding to Rafael's suggestion of tomato sandwich.
Here in North Carolina, we call it a "mater" sandwich which is a nice white bread, Duke's mayo on both slides, giant fat ripe tomatoes that you salt and pepper.
you can't beat it

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. Next time I will grill the halloumi (on the pannini grill) as it came out a little too chew/ rubbery this way for my taste.   My husband doesn't like halloumi so I substituted fresh mozzarella and that worked nicely.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Nothing beats a clasic tomato sandwich.... delicious ripe tomato, mayo, salt on good bread. Less is more.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
Flavors were great - would make again! 
I read the comments beforehand regarding greasiness and decided not to dress the arugula. There is enough tanginess coming from the other elements that the arugula does not need to be dressed. We did toast the bread with mayo, which was yummy but probably not strictly necessary. Toasting in toaster would lighten it up — just depends on what you’re looking for in a sandwich experience.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: OMG I usually think mayo is the grossest thing to have in the fridge, but I made this sandwich completely as written. I may divorce my husband so I can marry it.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Am I missing something here? The recipe is for 4 sandwiches, apparently 1/2 per person. Yet the nutritional info is for 18 servings. Is that a typo and it should instead read 8 servings? That presumably would more than double the calories, fat , sodium etc shown per serving.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Halloumi is one of my favorite cheeses and this is a great way to serve it.  Also love that the recipe builds in a side salad.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This is outstanding for a summer evening. I am Greek and I would not substitute Feta for Haloumi as some have suggested. Feta is an entirely different taste spectrum, and it can’t be fried. Haloumi is easy to find. This not very ’oily’, the amount of mayo and oil used for 4 big sandwiches is not too much at all. Try to get the best tomatoes you can, that’s key. And be generous with the cheese!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious and perfect for dinner during a heat wave. Kid doesn’t like halloumi so I made them one with pan fried tofu. Everyone enjoyed it.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: My groc store that reliably carries arugula and halloumi was out of both. I used the idea to make a paneer and tomato chalupa instead. For the garlic-pepper mayo I used chaat masala spice mix. Added finely chopped mint to the mayo. I had a bagged “Asian salad mix” that I used instead of the arugula.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: We've had these 2 days in a row now (had to use up the halloumi!). We followed the recipe to a T first, and it was good but super messy. Today we just used a little plain mayo and a squeeze of lemon and it was outstanding! I found all the oil yesterday blunted the flavors. Today the sourdough, tomato, and arugula were allowed to shine. This "dumbed down" version is definitely going into our summer rotation.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Added fresh pressed garlic to mayo instead of garlic powder. So much better than garlic powder. (To answer question below - S&P added in directions but not listed)

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I thought to myself “I mean I like halloumi… I like arugula … I like tomatoes… but is this sandwich really good enough for the nyt to post a recipe for it?” The answer is YES. It’s perfect. No swaps, no notes.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This sandwich eats like a burger. Delicious!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: The flavors are fantastic, but next time I will toast the bread without mayo. Just a tad too greasy, and not necessary.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This was the best sandwich I’ve had in years! When I read the recipe I thought it had too many steps and I was tempted to skip most of them (like spreading mayonnaise on the bread and toasting it or adding dressing to the argula) but I am so glad I followed the recipe as written because the end result is a delicious, filling, restaurant quality sandwich that is so easy to make at home! Can’t wait to make this again!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Don’t change a thing. In no way does this take an hour to prepare, though. At most it was 30 minutes and I was prepping side dishes at the same time.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Way too salty.  Too much dressing for 5 oz arugula. Notice the halloumi slices in the pictured sandwich is about 1/4” slice, not 1/2” per directions. The larger slice overtook the sandwich.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
