commentBody: The halloumi needs to be sliced really thin to get crispy to replace the bacon.  !/2 " was way to thick IMHO.  The mayo needed lots more pepper to ramp it up.  I also did not like the mayo fried bread.  Next time I will toast in the toaster.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: For me, this is a better idea than recipe. Too salty, heavy, and unbalanced as written. We dry fried the halloumi and used a small amount of the lemon mayo inside the sandwich along with thin sliced avocado. Cut the halloumi 1/4 inch. Not sure why you would need more oil on the arugula? We popped these in the panini press but would be fine with normal toast as well.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Already a summer favorite. Waiting for August heirlooms..

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I added gochugaru to the mayo for a subtle heat. I pickled a jalapeño with some added Worcestershire for more umami. Toasting the bread on the panin press made a world of difference. Excellent.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I made a version of this where I grilled all of the ingredients (except the arugula & mayo), plus red pepper, and it was one of the best things I’ve ever eaten. Perfect summer sandwich.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: AMAZING sandwich.  Toast bread in toaster.  No need for mayo.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I made some beetroot hommus and used that in the sandwich. It’s a winner

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I had this with regular toast, no oil on the arugula, avocado, and shallot instead of onion because it was what I had. So good for something so simple!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: instant classic veggie sandwich

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Love this! Just yesterday I was wondering what to use in place of bacon for my favorite summertime sandwich. HLT is the new BLT! I made this with local tomatoes and skipped the extra dressing. A winner. :)

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I turned this into a salad with chickpeas and sourdough croutons and I don’t think I’ve ever made a better decision (threw some olives in too)

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Great sandwich. I skipped adding oil to the arugula like some other people suggested and used less mayo. Also thinner slices of cheese. I think adding basil or roasted red peppers would be great.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Omigosh. Made it twice in as many months and there will be more to come as long as I can get a great heirloom tomato. When I add arugula in a sandwich, I kind of ball it up and smush it down. Stops it from being so unruly. I didn't toss it with the dressing first, just drizzled it on top. Made 2 gigantic sandwiches. Didn't find it too oily. Just too delicious.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: My husband declared “eat your heart out, Panera!” Great way to celebrate the start of peak tomato season. Unlike most NYT Cooking recipes, took way LESS than the listed time- only about 30 minutes. Also calculate your own calories, if that’s your thing- for some reason the estimate is for 18 servings. (I used much less halloumi and oil/mayo than prescribed, and my sandwich turned out around 560 calories.)

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: this was delicious, and took closer to 30 minutes! I toasted the bread in the toaster and cooked the haloumi on the top rack of my oven under the broiler rather than in the pan. I find that this method browns the halloumi more easily without making it gummy. brush with olive oil first. I used butter lettuce instead of arugula, and did dress it, but made a dressing that was more lemon juice than olive oil. (mostly because I was running low) the sandwich wasn't at all overly greasy.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Add bacon to sandwich

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Given the amount of prep to make this sandwich, I was underwhelmed with the result.  For my liking there was just too much lemon, too much mayo and not enough tomato. It's also a very messy sandwich to eat. We decided that we love the ingredients, but would rather have them all in a salad, cubing the halloumi, and adding a really good homemade crouton to replace the sandwich bread.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this substituting feta or some other cheese for halloumi?

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious!! I couldn’t find halloumi at my grocery store, so I breaded and fried up some feta, worked perfectly with the flavors!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Make Labneh and use that instead of fattening mayo for the spread. I've been eating this forever, very Middle Eastern

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Assembled this as a salad with sourdough croutons.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: As noted above tomato sandwiches are amazing. Might I suggest Duke’s mayo specifically?  Salt and pepper to taste on toasted bread. Only tomatoes that haven’t been refrigerated, as well as, only those that haven’t had the taste bred out of them.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Quick and simple.  Made it for 1. I used just a few thin slices of tomatoes and a few slices of halloumi on quinoa bread.  Just a touch of mayo on the outside for toasting of bread and the flavoured mayo inside (too taste).  A handful of lightly dressed arugula from the garden.  Really nice flavours.  Looking forward to making it with my garden tomatoes when they are ready!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight for supper and it was amazingly good, even with the second-rate GF bread I am reduced to (which fell apart half way thru). I loved all the flavors and textures, and finding a new way to use halloumi. Highly recommend!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: We enjoyed this sandwich very much. I dressed both the onions and greens in the Greek dressing (to which I added a touch more lemon juice and some garlic powder). Didn't make the mayo mix, but instead toasted the bread in a lightly oiled cast ion pan. Spread one slice of the sandwich with some, but not a lot. of mayo and sprinkled on coarsely ground black pepper. Will definitely make again.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Great sandwich. Since my wife doesn’t like arugula or raw onions, I skipped the arugula and marinaded the red onions in the Greek dressing - perfect! Moving this one to our high rotation recipes!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I forgot to buy tomatoes, and it was still delicious!!  Definitely a keeper!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Arugula != lettuce

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Made this yesterday for lunch. Messy but delicious.  The halloumi took some time to brown on medium high heat, not the 2-3 minutes suggested. More like ten minutes.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This is really good! Made it as written except subbed fresh dill for oregano in the dressing since I had some to use (and I think it was even better), and romaine for arugula (what I had). Very low effort but tasty payoff. This is a good weeknight dish- definitely going into the rotation!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This is a fantastic sandwich. It’s an elaborate sandwich - sure, you could just throw things on some bread - but you could also zhuzh it up and you wouldn’t regret it. Not sure I ever followed a sandwich recipe before but I don’t regret it. I felt like we were eating at a fun and fancy sandwich shop instead of eating a cheese and tomato sandwich at home. I followed the recipe exactly and I wouldn’t change a thing. I served it with Charlie Bird’s Farro Salad which was a great compliment.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I never leave public comments, but I think this was the best sandwich I’ve ever made/eaten. Truly impeccable. I subbed vegan mayo throughout and it worked beautifully.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I'm not a mayo fan so I spread toasted sourdough with spicy labneh topped with sliced tomatoes, halloumi, and the dressed arugula (used the leftover lemon/garlic dressing from a favorite salad https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1015707-lemon-garlic-kale-salad?unlocked_article_code=1.2k0.nCr2.sJmZ6RrHKJLb&smid=). I forgot the onion but the whole thing took 7 minutes and it was really very delicious. I am trying to think how I can package this for lunch tomorrow and have it stay crisp...

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Wow, was this fabulous! We are putting this on the regular rotation. I made one tweak, out of frugality; I had 1/2c oil marinade leftover from artichokes, so I used some of that instead of the olive oil. Worked well. Not usually a fan of arugula, but it worked perfectly here, cutting some of the richness from the cheese and mayo. It took only 25 minutes, start to finish.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This sandwich was absolutely delicious! The garlic pepper mayo and the punchy arugula really make the whole sandwich. Will definitely be making this again!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Even though I forgot the onions, this was delicious. I cooked the halloumi in the morning to avoid turning on the stove later in the day, and it was a perfect hot weather vegetarian meal.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
I never thought I would ever want children, but if I could with this sandwich- I would. It’s amazing.

The recipe forgets to add black pepper to the mayo ingredients, be sure to add it. I also added fresh garlic to the mayo spread as well as the vinaigrette for the arugula. But, I’m a garlic girl. Shake off extra dressing before putting on sandwich, as mine was a little juicy. 

This babe will go into permanent rotation!!!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This was...fine. Way too lemony, nothing to counterbalance all the acid. So many steps, components for something that was meh. My kitchen is trashed and for what? Will never make again.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Be sure to follow the instructions to toast the bread until golden in the pan. Otherwise it has a soggy texture. Learned that the hard way! Otherwise very tasty.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic! I did not have onion or arugula. To save calories I ate open faced. Still delicious!!!!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This is so amazing and delicious, but very, very greasy. I am going to try it again without grilling the bread with mayo on the outside. Toasting the bread seems like it would be crunchy and lighter.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: White distilled vinegar or white wine vinegar?

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
Cut recipe in half for two so cut all ingredients in half as well. I was cautious bon lemon due to remarks from one viewer. I think it would have been fine as long as you are not heavy handed putting lemon on the halloumi. It’s very filling! 
As for someone’s remarks about trashing the kitchen… advice from years of experience…clean up as you go… put ingredients away as you use them and a sink of soupy water to wash as you use items.. no big deal.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This was really tasty. Howver, with how salty halloumi is on its own, I didn't add salt to anything else. Will definitely make again.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
Sooooo oily! 
If I make this again, I would toast the bread in the toaster, add only a thin layer of mayo with lemon juice on the inside of the sandwich, and no dressing for the arugula. I couldn't even taste the halloumi under all of that mayo and oil.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I like the idea of this because I love halloumi, but when it’s sandwiched between bread, it really loses a lot of its appeal and its flavor. This dish would be much better as a dressed arugula salad with pan fried halloumi and tomatoes.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. I recommend reducing the mayo content and only using the barest layer when toasting the bread (or possibly none at all), to keep things getting too oily.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Perfect as written. Can't wait to make this again.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This is a good sandwich and very filling. We used one block of cheese and four slices of sourdough for four sandwiches and it was plenty. The cheese is very salty, so no extra salt is needed. Avocado would be a good addition, and the pepper mayo and salad dressing are fine, but not necessary if you’re in a hurry.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: One of my favorite sandwiches ever! I actually like it better with the arugula dressed with just lemon juice and salt and pepper, and I don’t toast with mayo, I just toast in the toaster.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Grilled halloumi first and used leftover oil to toast the bread. Soaked up excess oil before adding lemon. Lightly dressed the sorrel with just a small part of the dressing.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This is the best sandwich I've had in years, I think. The only changes to the recipe were (1) I could not find halloumi, so I substituted that for bread cheese, and (2) I used grated garlic instead of garlic powder. Great recipe!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious!
I went toasted with Guldens spicy brown, halloumi, heirloom tomato slice, sweet onion, arugula, halloumi, spicy brown, toast.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
Pretty good and simple for a weeknight dinner. I mostly followed the recipe except that I toasted the bread and I grilled the halloumi…no sauté pan to clean. DH liked it and so I’ll probably make it again. 
FWIW I used only as much dressing and garlic mayo as I wanted.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: The garlic mayo is delicious. We skipped the salad dressing and just piled on the arugula. Also just toasted the bread, didn’t fry in more mayonnaise. It’s a delicious sandwich.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This is a great flavor combination. I put the pickles right on the sandwich. With all of the juicy tomatoes, dressing, and lemon juice, the sandwich gets soggy quickly, so I want to try this with a thicker bread, like ciabatta.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: We made this delicious sandwich last night and agree with previous comments that the arugula would be perfectly fine without the dressing. At the last minute we had to substitute the sourdough for seedless rye (discovered a tear in the wrapper of newly purchased sourdough- ugh) and will try it again with sourdough.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: MARVELLOUS! I don’t like sandwiches but I just made this for myself and my boyfriend and we made FOUR EACH IN TOTAL !!! Every ingredient works to perfection together. Make sure to have a sturdy bread (no holes). Red onion elevates this exceptionally. Pair with a dry white wine!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: @nancy you each ate four sandwiches in a sitting?

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely incredible…finger licking good

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This was an excellent sandwich!  We used purchased garlic mayo and our favorite Greek dressing we order from PA (Gazebo Room).  Only issue was with the bread falling apart so will look for a more sturdy sourdough next time.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: It seems to me a panini press would simplify the preparation of this sandwich—has anyone tried using one?

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Maybe I'm being overly optimistic, but this doesn't look like it takes almost an hour to make. Am I missing something?  I added up approximate times and it looks like 30 - 40 minutes at most. I guess I'll know more when I make it (which I'm planning to do).

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
Took the oily comments to heart:
- Toasted the bread without mayo
- Very lightly oiled the Arugula
- Was sparing with oil for the cheese.

 Honestly I wish I had dabbed the fried halloumi slices with a paper towel before applying lemon. Either way, rave reviews from the man.

Also, the halloumi was super expensive, so I bought some bread cheese to try (Juusto, I believe.) Just as good, and a little more sturdy that halloumi. And like, $5-6 cheaper.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: If you have an ALDIs nearby, look in the cheese section for pouches of Cheese Fries Halloumi -Style Cheese Sticks. The cheese is cut in thickish strips that you can pan fry. I often cut it into croutons. MUCH cheaper than imported Halloumi for a pretty good product

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: My wife and I loved this! Especially my wife—the mix of salty and tangy has her number. Agree that the whole thing is a little greasy. While this didn’t interfere with our good time, I’d consider either dry-toasting the bread or grilling it with just a bit of olive oil. I added avocado because we had one and it fit right in. Also opted for open-faced.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: So good! swapped the mayo for quark (mixed with garlic powder, lemon juice, a little bit of olive, kashmiri & italian chili powders), and did a quick pickle of shallots. Don't have patience to watch halloumi in pan so baked it in oven & then added in thick seeded bread to toast

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
The NYT Cooking Nutritional Analysis indicates that these 4 sandwich account for 18 servings which suggests that each sandwich serving would be cut into about 4 servings per person.  

It also suggests that the nutritional information should be multiplied by 4 for each ingredient such as sodium which would provide close to 3,000mg of sodium per sandwich.  Seems excessive.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I loved this sandwich! I didn’t put dressing on the arugula, but I did use the mayo as directed.  It was delicious.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
This sandwich is delicious. A little confused by the one hour cook time considering it took less than 15 minutes to throw it together. I made it for one so didn’t measure/made some adjustments. 

Skipped seasoning the mayo, just seasoned the tomatoes and arugula heavily. Didn’t want to cut a lemon for a tiny squeeze so used apple cider vinegar instead and just a drizzle of olive oil for the arugula. Added avocado to be extra decadent. As many have said, layer order is key to any sandwich. 

Great use of halloumi. Yum yum yum.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Agreed with other comments that it was quite greasy. I don’t think the arugula needs dressing. I grilled the halloumi which is def the way to go with halloumi. It was delicious and VERY filling. I could do with half a sandwich. Served on my husband’s homemade sourdough and it was fantastic. Would make again but with less oil and split with the hubs.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Can I do this with paneer?

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: It was tasty! Wouldn’t toast with mayo and just regular toast next time. Didn’t make the dressing just used plain arugula and used some pickled red onions I had in the fridge. Really good! Also had to use bread cheese because no haloumi at the store. Still delicious! Kept its shape

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: One of the best sandwiches I’ve ever made. So simple, bright and flavorful

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Very hard to find Halloumi where I live, so I use paneer, either store bought (Indian groceries) or homemade, which is also far less salty. The rest of the sandwich is unbeatable.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
a banger
swapped the dressing for samin's house dressing cuz its what I had on hand

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Made this as written, and next time I will probably just toast the bread in the toaster like I do when we make BLTs. It was highly flavorful, but a touch oily, and I would be willing to sacrifice that and remove a step or two from this yummy (but eight step) recipe.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: While this was pretty good, I found that the lemon overpowered the taste of the halloumi and the arugula. I will try without the vinaigrette next time.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Goodness. Pure goodness!

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this as written and it was good but it took way too long for a sandwich or even a weeknight meal.  I made it again lightly toasting the sourdough in a toaster oven, no mayo, dressing the arugula with olive oil and lemon juice, putting plain kewpie on the bread when I assembled it.  Equally as delicious and half the time.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: I made this twice, once as written.  The second time I toasted the bread in the toaster oven, used plain kewpie mayo, Roma tomatoes and quickly dressed the arugula with lemon juice and olive oil.  Equally as delicious and took much less time and fuss.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Use thin slices of bread.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This was insanely good. I made it as written except I didn't dress the arugula because (1) I'm lazy and (2) I'm not a huge fan of salad dressing anyway. But it was excellent without. Like jaw-droppingly good. I'm excited to make this in the summertime with some tomatoes fresh from the farmers market. Yummmm

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: This was a delicious sandwich! Followed the recipe exactly.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Amazing recipe! Used a blender for the marinade. I was a little doubtful about pouring the marinade on top and leaving it in the fridge but it dries up and I think ultimately acts as a dry brine. I followed the recipe exactly including the basting. The flavor penetrated into the meat and incredibly delicious! Highly recommend.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: @coffeespoon  absolutely agree . Made exactly as stated . Delicious!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I made another recipe almost identical to this. and stumbled upon this recipe while looking for the source of what I made (luckily I wrote it down!) The only big differences are the salt, ginger, and lemongrass. The fish sauce and soy sauce (the other recipe used less soy sauce) gave enough salt to the recipe. I marinated boneless chicken thighs and I thought it was the best marinade ever. Absolutely delicious, hot or cold. So I would not hesitate to try this one (maybe without the salt).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: This was the BEST chicken recipe I have ever made!  I have a rotisserie and an oven, but not a rotisserie oven, so I cooked outside on a Weber.  I cut out the backbone of the chicken and divided into two big pieces, each with a breast/leg/thigh combo.  I used a food-processor to puree marinade.  Used all of marinade in two separate bags and refrigerated for ten hours.  Cooked over direct heat, skin side down; turned; cooked over direct heat backside.  Moved to indirect heat to finish.  AMAZING!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: The introduction and the menu at the original restaurant mention garlic. Was it left out in the recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe. Used this on boneless skinless chicken breasts (10+ oz each), cut crosswise. Grilled on indirect side of the grill for 20 minutes. Served with a Thai peanut sauce from another recipe and a raman noodle salad made with coleslaw mix. Such a good dinner. Lots of flavors banging around there.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: This is my third time making this in as many weeks. These are the changes I've made. I use the Thai Blend in a tube from  the refrigerated section in the produce aisle. I also use the recommended Thai Seasoning instead of soy sauce & sweeten the marinade to taste. Throw the chicken into a reusable plastic bag with the marinade. Leave it in the fridge for two days and spatchcock before cooking at 425. It's impossibly delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Could you spatchcock the chicken and do this on the barbecue?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: VERY salty.  I left out the added salt but with oyster sauce, fish sauce, and Thai Golden seasoning, I think you could also skip the soy sauce.  Needs acid and sweetness to balance the saltiness of the marinade—maybe add lime juice?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I agree that it was a sodium bomb and also left out the added salt.  It was tasty but not sure it was worth the effort (I think I prefer a simple roasted chicken seasoned with fennel salt).  I froze away the extra sauce to make again at some point when I want a change in pace

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: @Chef Egon There is another Thai roasted chicken recipe on NYT that gives great results and is simpler, though it doesn’t have the pedigree as this recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I spatchcocked mine and barbecued it. Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
I'm doubtful of this recipe as I know from experience that blending fibrous items like fresh ginger and lemon glass always produces an unpalatable paste of tiny, hairy fibers that would end up ruining the dish.  Perhaps my blender is not that good?

Also, seems like cutting the chicken up into individual pieces would allow for much better penetration by the marinade, unless presentation outweighs taste.  

Lastly, I would probably braise the pieces in some diluted marinade to keep them moist.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I had no problem blending the ginger and lemon grass.  The marinade penetrated the whole chicken just fine.  It was very juicy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Prepared according to recipe (for a change) and the flavors were intense and powerful.  If I make it again, I'll cut the ginger in 1/2.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
I tasted the marinade before combining with the chicken and it didn’t seem great to me. I tested two pieces of chicken marinated overnight and really didn’t like the taste. Maybe I’m
Not a berbere fan. I ended up making chimichurri chicken and it was a big hit.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: This was so salty we couldn't eat it, even with plain white rice. The flavors seemed unbalanced as well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Thai BBQ Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Used this with bone-in thighs which I barbecued. When I froze the extra amount of marinade and used it for another batch, the new chicken was too salty. I will make this again, but with less added salt in the marinade.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was my favorite dish at the long-gone, much lamented Bruno's Ristorante in L.A.  When they closed, the chef gave me the recipe; it was similar to this, but used sun-dried tomatoes softened in olive oil instead of canned tomatoes (Costco's sun-dried tomatoes in olive oil work beautifully) and added white wine and a hit of cream. I generally don't add salt, since sun-dried tomatoes are made with salt. Delicious over pasta, alone or with grilled chicken.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Interesting, but 28 oz of sun-dried, per this recipe, or some other proportion?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: LOL, there were no amounts on the recipe the chef gave me, just a list of ingredients jotted on a postcard, so I add ingredients until it tastes right.  The Costco jar is 35 oz, and I probably use 1/2 to 1 cup of wine and maybe a quarter cup of cream? Not much oil other than what clings to the tomatoes. I use at least 4 cloves garlic and 2 tsp red pepper flakes, but adjust to taste. Don't add salt until you taste it, because the tomatoes can be very salty. Sometimes I puree some of the tomatoes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: You can reverse too much “heat” by adding a raw peeled potato to the pot and cooking gently for 10-20 minutes depending on how much kick you want to remove. I use this whenever I overdo the cayenne in my Manhattans clam chowder

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Unexpectedly: an olive oil heaven. 1/4 cup instead of a tablespoon in this simple combination changes everything.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Try spitting the recipe with 14oz canned and 14oz fresh tomatoes.  It’s less dense and very flavorful

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Whole, peeled tomatoes would not become dense with 20 minutes of cooking. Yes, dense, if "cooked down" for 3 hours as someone suggested. This is marinara with spice, not spaghetti sauce with red pepper flakes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Running a half carrot or so through a blender/food processor and adding before the garlic/red pepper should add some richness to the sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I suggest get Roma or plum tomatoes. Cut them in half and place them on a baking sheet and broil them until the skins are browned and blackened and the tomatoes are soft. I use Indian chili peppers, dried, and break them up and along with  seven or eight cloves of garlic, sliced in half length-wise. I let that saute in olive oil until the garlic browns. Turn off the heat. When the tomatoes are ready, I place them one by one cut side down over low heat with garlic and pepper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Made as written and it was fine but not amazing. I would definitely cook again if I forgot to buy an onion or Parmesan for my favorite pomadoro recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I would recommend crushing the tomatoes in a bowl UNDER the water line unless you want a nice tomato stain on your clothes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
To avoid burning the garlic, remove pan from the stove when it starts sizzling and let it cool for a minute, then drop the tomatoes. 
When the sauce is ready, drop the boiled pasta and some chopped fresh parsley (leaves only) in the pan and toss for a couple of minutes.  Perfetto!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Before the garlic starts to brown, remove it altogether. That's what Italians do. They rarely eat garlic, only use the flavor

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: 1 tsp of your regular crushed red chile flakes and 1 tsp of Aleppo pepper flakes is the golden combo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
BC— rdr’s comment with sliced Jalapeño’s is same for me except my exposure was many years ago working in NJ. We’d go once a week for penne Arrabbiata with sliced sausage “extra spicy” which got us the Jalapeño’s. 
I’ve made at home ever since. Make the Arrabbiata as described then sauté any extras (Jalapeño, sausage, vege sausage) with splash of pasta water, pasta and sauce for each person’s heat tolerance.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I loved this light, flavorful vegan sauce!  I’m a wimp when it comes to heat, so I started (and ended) with 1/4 tsp of red pepper flakes.  Didn’t  need salt after the initial 1 tsp called for in the recipe. Tossed in rigatoni along with a sprinkling of Parmesan and a heaping tbsp of ricotta per serving. Perfecto! Just what I was craving!  Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Followed to a T. Perfect. Went with just one teaspoon of red peppers and the heat was fine (I’m a fan of spicy). Didn’t add any more salt at the end. Didn’t need it. Used three spoonfuls on 100g of spaghetti which is my favorite pasta. Saved the rest on the freezer for another day. This one is a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Use  jarred calbrian chile..not crushed pepper..much tastier

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Sautéing a minced onion along with olive oil, garlic, and crushed red pepper is the way to go.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Your way to go. Arrabbiata is spicy marinara; neither traditionally has onions. But you go your own way.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight and had it on penne. It was spicier than I expected with 1 teaspoon of pepper flakes, but tasty. Next time I'll use half the oil, a quarter cup made it a little too greasy for me. I also realized as I was eating that I had under-salted it (used the 1 teaspoon but didn't salt to taste at the end, just chowed). Super easy, though, especially if you go the lazy garlic press route, as I did.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Simple and good.  I agree with taking the pepper and garlic off the flame.  I added a teaspoon and a half of red chili flakes.  Served with rigatoni bc that’s what we had.  I’ll make it again for sure.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Added a handful of chopped basil and went heavy on the red pepper flakes (two full tbsps). Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I made it exactly as per recipe and it was excellent, but I think a little tomato paste browned with the oil/garlic/chile would be a great addition. I think it would help both the flavor and texture. I used jarred San Marzanos from my garden last summer, and I still think some tomato paste would improve it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Arrabbiata is just fired-up marinara sauce. Tomato paste would make it heavy and thick, not what this is meant to be. Add tomato paste if you want spicy spaghetti sauce. Canned San Marzanos have just the right flavor for this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I added some sauteed zucchini for a vegetable.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: If you’re planning to simmer the sauce for 20-40 minutes, do yourself a favor and only use 1/2 to 1 tsp of red pepper flakes. Learned tonight that 2 tsp will burn all your loved ones

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I put my stove on medium high and the garlic immediately turned black when I put it in. Seems like it should be medium low for the first step.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Many years ago I’d get a terrific Arrabbiata at a Boston trattoria in the North End where the chef sautéed sliced jalapeños in a pan with garlic before ladling in marinara. Not traditional, obviously, but delicious. Have made it that way ever since. Don’t bother to seed the now uniformly mild jalapeños, just slice and sauté.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I've been looking for a good arrabbiata recipe and this is it. I just made it and loved it. I do wish there were instructions for saucing the pasta, as well as pasta shape and cheese suggestions (I used rigatoni, as in the photo, and parmesan, which was great).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Note that this is a recipe for sauce... not pasta with sauce. You're on your own for pasta shape and cheese, and it sounds like you have managed well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I thought this was pretty bland, despite the red pepper flakes.  I'm not a person who enjoys spicy foods, so this was a big surprise.  Also, I wondered if perhaps the sauce needed some salt.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: So why not add some salt?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: It says to season to taste with salt?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: The variability of dried chili flakes in their heat release moves me to sub in something like Trader Joe's "Italian Bomba sauce": crushed Calabrian chilis in olive oil.  Always know more or less exactly how much one teaspoon supplies in heat to a cup or two of red sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: The picture shows grated cheese. Don’t do that: pasta all’arrabbiata must be without any cheese!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Cheese is sacrilege for this dish. Agreed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Oh, I had it with cheese. I liked it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I also think cheese is good on this dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: The sauce should be cooked much longer - three hours or more - and you need sweetness to offset the tartness of not fully ripe, or tinned, tomatoes. Maple syrup works a treat. And you need more than most people think. As for salt, try fish sauce. The Vietnamese version is great - yes it's fermented fish. If it was good for the Romans (and still today it's used in parts of Italy; google Colatura di alici). While this is optional, added sweetness is an absolute must.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Arabbiata  is not a sauce that requires hours of cooking. It's similar to marinara sauce, requiring minimal cooking, but with the spicy addition. Indeed, there are sauces that benefit from hours of simmering to develop flavor, but this is not one of them. Colatura di alici is made from anchovies, which is not as far-fetched for an Italian dish as maple syrup!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Some Italians add some sugar to any tomato sauce made with canned tomatoes--to take away the metallic tang.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I can't even imagine the consequences of adding maple syrup to this and frankly, i don't want to

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Easy, but just fair.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: this sounds good I will recommend it  to my mom tonight.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Had to tone down the heat cause we have a child who doesn’t like heat. It was pretty tasteless. Needed a lot of extra spice—salt, oregano, additional herbs. Wouldn’t make this recipe again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Bright and delicious flavor--super simple to prepare! We used 1 tsp. of crush red pepper; it was plenty spicy with that! We topped our rigatoni with Parmesan and whole milk ricotta. Perfection!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Try this recipe, it's delicious, easy and so few ingredients. I followed the recipe to a 'T' and the results perfect on pasta or wherever a semi-spicy tomato sauce is needed - and it stores in the fridge for a few days. Follow the technique, timing and heated pan instructions. I didn't deviate from the amount of salt in the recipe - found it sufficiently salted at the end of preparation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Super simple and amazingly good. Followed the recipe exactly. Served it with strozzapretti pasta tossed with steamed cauliflower and beans.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I think this will be a pretty good recipe! Only thing is that minced garlic cooks really fast in medium high so be ready to add the tomato pronto when they start to go hah

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I think this will be pretty good! Only thing is that garlic cooks fast so be ready to add this tomatoes pronto. It won't take two minutes haha

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Simple recipe merits simple comment: perfection!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Arrabbiata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Just made it and it was so delish!! I put it over pappardelle noodles and it was perfect. I used the chili flakes and then added in a little chili crisp as well to up the heat and it got rave reviews!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
To make this vegan:
I used a can of coconut cream instead of cow cream & added about a teaspoon of whole grain mustard and a tablespoon of nutritional yeast to the beans.
I added about a teaspoon each of nutritional yeast & paprika to the bread crumbs & mixed the arugula with a good glug of the sundried tomato’s oil.
Devine! Bright! Rich! Will be a staple!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Your vegan alternations were brilliant! We made it last night.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Ann I was thinking to myself I’d love to make this for dinner but I wasn’t sure how I’d swing the heavy cream since “vegan” alternatives tend to be expensive/ weirdly textured/ bad. Thanks so much for your thoughts! Brilliant.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Ann great tweaks! For the cream, I used an ounce of raw cashews and blitzed it in the vitamix with 1 1/3 cups water, the tomato paste, mustard, and nutritional yeast. I also used only 1 can of beans and added a package of crumbled tempeh. Fantastic results.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious! I used approximately 1/2 cup of Greek yogurt mixed with 1/2 cup of 1% low fat milk and it was a great substitute for heavy cream, and baby spinach instead of arugula. I’ll make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
@Dee  

Great idea! 
Even better, I suggest goats milk, which is naturally homogenized and has more fat than cows milk and is very low in lactulose. 
I don’t know why so few people cook with cow’s milk or cream, when goats milk is so much tastier and healthier for you.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Rachel Scott I don’t know why so many people insist on telling other people what their food preferences should be or what is “healthier” when they actually have no idea or business doing so.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @RhuLu The poster was just giving information others may not know about.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
This is simple and amazing!  My husband thinks it should be renamed “Marry Me Beans.”

My own substitutions - chopped tomatoes instead of sundried and half and half instead of cream. The lemon in the arugula is such a great counterpoint to the richness of the sauce.  And leftovers were fantastic - it smelled like our favorite Italian restaurant on the Upper West Side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I guess I’m not the only who noticed the ingredients are virtually identical to those in Marry Me Chicken.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
@Sarah
What is the restaurant?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Sarah. Do you mind sharing the name of that restaurant?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
I’ve made this twice - first time as the recipe is written, and I thought the full 1 cup of cream was both overwhelmingly heavy and unnecessary. 

Second time, I cut the cream to 1/4 cup and doubled the amount of sundried tomatoes, and it was *fantastic*. My family is always trying to get more legumes in our diet, so this one (with these modifications) will go into regular rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Nikki Agree it was too heavy with a cup of cream (not to mention 6 tbs of oil). Will follow your advice next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Nikki great suggestions. I made this last night and I agree the cream is a bit much.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This is a beautiful recipe, with so much unnecessary added fats (oil and cream). I change the macros on this by using only a teaspoon of oil (a tiny coating) and then make a cream out of 1/2 cup of pumpkin seeds  with hot water to cover, blended well to a cream, in lieu of heavy cream.  Pumpkin seeds add fiber, polyunsatured fats, and 5 more grams of protein per serve but still make it creamy. And instead of 650 calories per serve, it's at about 450--with a lot more protein and fiber.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Linda Are they raw pumpkin seeds? Please explain the process of using them in more detail.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Linda try with cashew cream— I found the cashews to be extremely delicious and to taste like heavy cream!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Linda wow.  Thank you.  Will give it a try

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Made it with whole milk and great northern beans. DELICIOUS!! My favorite NY Times recipe ever!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: For anyone looking to add some more plant protein some crumbled up tempeh works great in this!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
@Sam H
Thank you Sam H! That’s exactly what I want to do. Did you pan fry the crumbles first?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I'm new to cooking-- and I made this!! And it's delicious and beautiful! I'm so thrilled!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This is yummy comfort food—and relatively healthy.  The white beans add creaminess, so there doesn’t need to be a huge amount of cream and cheese.  The arugula and lemon salad add a fresh tang, and the bread crumbs a great crunch.  I used Pecorino Romano because I thought that the flavor of Parmesan might be too subtle for the dish.   I’ll definitely make it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Did you cut down the cream? Seems like so much fat for a vegetarian meal. I'd love suggestions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Beth plus over 800 calories a serving. Not what I’d call “healthy.”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Beth I cut back to 1/4 cup cream and about half the cheese. I used the aqua faba from one can of beans to increase the liquid. It was amazingly creamy even with the smaller amount of cream.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. 10 out of 10. I used finely grated Pecorino instead of Parmesan since someone said it would be more flavorful. I also wanted to make it healthier so used 2% milk mixed with cornstarch instead of heavy cream. I accidentally bought Pecorino seasoned breadcrumbs and it was delicious. I would probably use them again. I would definitely recommend making this dish and am excited to make it for my vegetarian friends.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This is so good!!! Of all the NYT recipes, this is the one I make again, and again, and again… immensely satisfying and so simple. I really like the flavor of a little bay leaf in here, so add a couple, three. The lemony arugula breadcrumb salad is fantastic (actually keeps for a day in the fridge, much to my surprise). Can’t overstate how much I enjoy this recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I have made this a few times and find it to be delicious! The only thing I do differently is to grate the lemon into the cooked breadcrumbs, and drs the arugula with the olive oil and lemon juice. I serve the beans topped with the lemony arugula, and then top with the crumbs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This is the ultimate comfort food for a bad day. I never have cream. I use milk. It’s fine. I often don’t have sun fried tomatoes. Also fine with fresh grape tomatoes or a brick of crushed tomatoes. This dish is straight up yum. Eat it with a hearty rye for the best beans on toast ever.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Following Ann in the vegan direction: can’t do coconut so subbed a box of silken tofu blender’d up with couple tablespoons nutritional yeast and a tsp mustard for the cream and cheese elements. Yum.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Karen tried this to up the nutrition a bit and wow, thank you for encouraging me to use silken tofu. It was my first time eating it and it won’t be the last! (I made a vegan mousse au chocolat with the rest, also absolutely fantastic!) As I’m not vegan I still added the Parmesan and also a dollop of pesto that I had on hand.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Karencicinut, while one commenter said they liked it the coconut milk, it just doesn’t go with Italian inspired anything. Tofu imparts very little flavor buts adds the creaminess factor..good choice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Karen cashew cream is also fantastic here (1/2 cup raw cashews and 1/2 cup water blitzed in a high speed blender ). I’m not vegan and this tasted like heavy cream to me.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I like how commenters say it's great AFTER they put their own spin on a recipe, totally changing it....IMHO make it the way it's written the first time, then decide if it needs a spin...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Paula that’s how cooking works … it’s a creative endeavor! it seems like everyone’s spin on this one turned out great

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Paula You do you. I enjoy seeing the modifications

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Paula I love reading the comments and different ways people have adapted the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: added some chopped garlic and oregano while cooking, topped with tons of fresh parsley. used half and half, which was plenty creamy. simmered more than 5 mins to reduce the liquid. gets better after a day in the fridge imo!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
I mean, it's fine if you want to replace every ingredient with your own thing and comment on how amazing this recipe is; we all know that's what people are going to do, however unhelpful it is to comment on a recipe you didn't make.

But it really renders the star rating meaningless if people are going to rate some random dish they made up instead of the one that's actually in question.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Exactly! I completely understand when you don't want to trudge to the store for one ingredient that you can confidently substitute. But, when you try to turn something vegan or go completely off the radar with like 5 changes, perhaps you should consider another recipe, or at least not waste other people's time by writing a review for a recipe you did not make.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Lisa The whole point of the comments section is to see what other people did. I often try the same substitutes or changes. So while that may not be to your liking, it is for many others. Plus you can’t assume that everyone who comments has also given it a star rating.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
@Peacock Blue
Thank you. I like to read also substitutions as may not have everything in my pantry.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This is so much greater than the sum of its parts. No cream on hand so replaced with 2% milk, subbed lacinato kale for arugula and added half the cheese to the beans only, none to the greens. Tasty and the greens are a bright complement to the toothsome savoriness of the beans. Bread crumbs are a must.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I made this with 1/2 cup cream and 1/2 cup of milk instead of the full cup of cream. It was incredibly rich and very satisfying. A great meal worth making and so rich it's more of a special treat. I just couldn't put in over 800 calories of cream along with the oil soaked tomatoes and additional 6 T of vegetable oil into a single dish. The fat content takes you to a "burger and fries meal" level. Next time, I want to melt in an anchovy or two when cooking down the onions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Richard a teaspoon or so fish sauce gives the same umami bomb

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as in the recipe but found this dish to be too sweet with a gross, fatty mouthfeel. Would omit the cream next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious. The only modification I made upon the second time making this was forgoing the panko. I used homemade croutons instead.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Really good recipe. My wife at first said, "I don't really like cannellini beans," but after eating this she said "wow, I was wrong! This one is a keeper." I did reduce the cream to 3/4 cup from one cup, and did just a sprinkling of crushed red pepper. Also, the tomato paste I had on hand was Hunts Tomato Paste with Pesto, and since that's all I had, I used it. Maybe it added a bit of extra flavor? Regardless, I'll make this one again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
After reading through notes decided to *start* with 1/3 heavy cream, and go up as needed for flavor. Idk, this seemed fine to me. Would add a bit of acidity like Tabasco or straight vinegar. Used about 4 cups cooked mayacoba Peruvian beans (find dried in Latino stores) which are very creamy texture. 
Big hit with my partner

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Name I sub Peruvian beans for white beans and pinto beans all the time. They are among the highest fiber beans so great for those of us needing our 40 g of fiber daily.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious..even better the next day! I had to use diced tomatoes, rather than sun dried, but they worked well. Oops, then discovered I had no arugula, so I roasted broccoli rabe. That worked too. It’s a very forgiving recipe which provides great contrasts in taste. Next time I’ll make sure I have the listed ingredients.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I have cooked this multiple times and have found that substituting a subtle tasting Greek yogurt for the heavy cream works well if you want to make it a little bit healthier and up the protein content. I have used the sprouts brand low fat Greek yogurt so that it’s not too thick of a consistency.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: It was excellent as a main dish but can also see as a dip, per another poster, or just to put on garlic toast (like avocado toast). I made it ‘as is’ with no ‘excepts.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: We love pantry meals and this is a winner. I was surprised how creamy it was before adding cream. Would add less next time. Lemony arugula was a perfect counterpoint!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: A big hit with our family. I used Royal Corona beans (which are gigantic!) from Rancho Gordo (cooked them the day before in the crock pot.)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Trust the process and absolutely do not skimp on the arugula and breadcrumbs - they make the dish!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Completely delicious. I served with meatballs and a baguette on the side. I also did not serve the salad on top of the saucy beans, because for leftovers I knew it wouldn’t be good to store the beans and salad together. So I served them separately. Still good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Don’t cinch on the lemony arugula. You will think: “wow that is a big pile of arugula”. But trust me when I say it compliments the richness of the beans so well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as written, loved it, no plans to tinker with it for later iterations.  Chopping the sun dried tomatoes in oil is a mess.  Leaving the panko out would be a tragic error.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I used a cup of 2% milk instead of cream and left out the water. Otherwise, I followed the recipe. This was very creamy and delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
I love this! Homey, comforting and also that bit of zip from the arugula. Yum Yum. I will do this again. 

i did not have cream and, as others have said, milk was more than fine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This has become a regular at my house, and for good reason - it’s invariably delicious! In response to someone who made the comment that “chopping the sun dried tomatoes in oil is really messy” : l totally agree. Having made this without chopping them (not the optimal approach), I have arrived at a solution. I chop the tomatoes in the jar using a pair of kitchen shears. Mess averted, recipe still delicious! At the risk of angering purists, I will add that I routinely use whole milk rather than cream and sub the water with white wine or dry vermouth.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Maximum flavor for little effort! I used Romano. I highly recommend adding  a generous amount of halved cherry tomatoes to the arugula topping. Great with crusty bread (Acme Walnut Levain.) Second time I used lime zest and juice as I didn’t have lemon; equally great. Next I’ll try soy milk or maybe 1/2 & 1/2 ,  instead of heavy cream.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: How is this so good? I didn’t have sun dried tomatoes so I used a can of stewed tomatoes instead. Same with sour cream for heavy cream, croutons for panko. I was having a bad day and this meal felt like a warm hug. One of those rare, perfect recipes that is cheap and fast but somehow decadent. A much needed reminder of how far a handful of pantry staples can go.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Made as written, using Parmesan cheese, and served with garlic bread on the side. Too many breadcrumbs, not enough delicious chunks of tomato. Will halve the breadcrumbs and add a can of diced tomatoes next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: So absolutely delicious!! Followed recipe, and it turned out great. Leftovers were fantastic on toast topped with a runny egg for breakfast!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This was an excellent dish. I'm looking forward to making it again and trying something other than cream (trying to decrease my cholesterol). Thank you to those who have offered suggestions for substitutions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Jenn I have been using cashew cream as a heavy cream substitute because I always have raw cashews in the fridge and appreciate their protein/fiber/unsaturated fat profile. No one believes me when I have told them that’s what I used— they all think it’s heavy cream. To make cashew cream, blitz 1/2 cup raw cashews with 1/2 cup water in a high speed blender. Tasted like tomato vodka sauce, yum!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Annie it sounds like you don’t soak (or simmer) the cashews first, something I’ve done in another recipe. Have you tried both ways, and determined pre-soaking isn’t worth it?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Maria in Minneapolis the soaking is supposed to remove an unhealthy chemical in the raw cashews. Discard the soaking water.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I added red wine vinegar to cook with the beans, about 1-2 T. per can of beans to give brighten up the dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Incredible! Easy enough for a weeknight but feels decadent and so delicious. My partner and I swapped the heavy cream for nonfat Greek yogurt to lighten it up, and we both agreed we don’t think we missed a thing. Next time, we may also lighten up the breadcrumbs. They’re definitely a necessary complement to the overall dish but seemed to overwhelm the arugula mixture, and we ended up with extra. Will definitely make this one again, very soon!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: How would you know you didn't miss a thing when you didn't even follow the recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
@Paula
I’m thinking you had an awful day the day you posted two cranky comments. It’s okay. Try to keep in mind that part of the popularity of the app is that real people are out there perusing recipes, comparing them with what items they have on hand and taking a chance. It’s an adventure. Let’s enjoy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as written. Amazing!! And I’m usually not a bean girl. I paired it with the NYT speedy no knead bread recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I'm with Jim.  I would like to hear from those who follow the recipe as written and then critique their results.  I followed these instructions exactly.  The dish is delicious.  The arugula and panko topping is the real star. The sun-dried tomatoes add a great tang but I used them right out of the jar and they could have used a finer chop. I salted and peppered my serving on my plate, but noticed that no one else at the table did. Makes 4 generous servings.  I would double the recipe next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I agree with chopping the sun-dried tomatoes finely and with doubling the recipe. I did add some additional salt and pepper (mainly) during cooking. My family could have done with maybe a 1/4 tsp more of the crushed red pepper from a hot perspective.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Deborah I followed it exactly. I agree with the commenters who found it too rich and somewhat greasy tasting. Will cut back some on the cream and oil next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I'll never understand why bean recipes don't start with "Use dried beans" (preferably Rancho Gordo), then suggest canned beans as an alternative.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Because it dramatically changes the time and the prep? It's not a straight swap, and many people are looking for weeknight-friendly dishes. People who prefer dry beans tend to know to swap and plan ahead.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Make this tonight. Join the Bean Cult.  You will be transported to mystical realms. All hail the magic beans.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Lots was great about this recipe but I think it was too rich for me.  I will see what I can do  to adjust that. So much was so good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Rancho Gorda are the best!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I have to add that these beans are great the next morning for an English style breakfast. They are great cold (or room temperature) on toast, or heated, accompanied by eggs, sausage, and bacon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and simple! I make this recipe so often that I’ve started freezing tomato paste in this exact amount whenever I open a new can. Easy to halve if cooking for one or two. I omit the panko altogether and swap milk for the cream, and I don’t miss either in the slightest. Great left over, too!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Wowza! It blows my mind (and palate) when simple ingredients can be elevated to this unctuous level! I substituted spinach for arugula only because it’s cold and rainy and I didn’t want to leave the house today lol. But other than that, followed the recipe to a T. No further suggestions, it’s THAT good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Deeeelish! Used soy milk instead of cream and added a fresh red chili to complement the dried flakes. Topped the salad off with a handful of chopped smoked almonds, and served it with some seedy sourdough. Got a standing ovation at the table, and ended up without any leftovers because the kids totally destroyed all of it. This recipe will definitely go into heavy rotation. I often make a vaguely comparable recipe from Max LaManna with mozzarella and mushrooms, but I’m afraid that dish has been usurped by this one, at least for the foreseeable future. Sorry, Max.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
Fry the onion and garlic in the sun-dried tomatoes oil.

A few adjustments:
-Skipped the tomato paste and went with a full jar of sun-dried tomatoes
-Changed the breadcrumbs for croutons right before serving
-Added some spinach instead of the arugula salad on top

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: @Mel. Super suggestion to use the tomato oil! I will next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
@Karen L Davis— Totally agree !!! Why
throw out all that flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I did not have sun-dried tomatoes on hand or tomato paste. Working with what i had in the pantry, I substituted Raos jarred marinara for paste. I also sliced button mushrooms which I needed to use up, I browned separately on high heat and added to the bean mixture at the end. Followed the rest of directions/ingredients as written. Delicious and can't wait to try with sun-dried tomatoes!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I’ve been making a similar dish for years. I suggest you don’t need the cream if you purée half of one can of beans—adding a little liquid of your choice, i.e., water; stock or the juice of the beans. The flavor is enhanced: creamy and bean-y.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody:
My favorite bean dish I’ve made from the app!

Few adjustments based on comments:
- used almond milk instead of heavy cream. I can say with confidence heavy cream is NOT needed

- used 2 tbl spoons olive oil not 4- you don’t need 4

- didn’t do any breadcrumbs. Served with a baguette instead

- only had spinach so did that instead of arugula and it was great!

This will absolutely be in rotation at our house moving forward

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This was very good and we enjoyed it a lot. That said, I made it with whole milk and it was still very rich. Can’t imagine what it’s like with heavy cream. Salad is essential thought I found the breadcrumb to arugula ratio to be off—too much breadcrumb. Overall, nice vegetarian recipe and Will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Too rich for me. I’m adding water to the leftovers to thin this. Next time I’ll make with milk not cream.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I think this dish is how we get folks into cooking again…in less than 20 minutes we had a flavorful, sustainable, affordable, accessible, lovely dish of vegetarian persuasion that can easily be addended or updated to the tastes of the home cook. This dish has officially been added to the weeknight repertoire of go-to fare. Yes. Please. More. Thanks.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night exactly as written. I love beans and eat them regularly but have never prepared them with cream and sun dried tomatoes. The concept is great and the two components need each other to make the dish work! Next time I'll try subbing Greek yogurt for the heavy cream as I found that made the beans too rich and made an otherwise pretty healthy meal less healthy. The panko and lemon zest is a must.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! We loved the panko in the salad. Great complement in flavor/texture. I cut the cream by a third and added bean liquid from the Rancho Gordo Marcella beans I made for this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: We had oat milk in the fridge after the visiting vegans left. I used that for both the cream and the water. I am sure it was a bit more delicious with the heavy cream but this was pretty darn good with the oat milk. We luckily had baby arugula in the garden so I made half the topping for the first night and then fresh with the leftovers. Definitely a do-again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I made this vegan with Trader Joe's non-dairy parmesan and vegan heavy whipping cream.  Such a lovely dinner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Loved this, it’s going in the rotation.  I bet it would be almost equally delicious with less cream as well!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: Amazing.  Subbed chicken stock for water.  Can’t wait to make again.  Wow, just wow.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is delicious, but as written it has 52 (!) grams of fat per serving, 21 grams of which are saturated. I'm not a low-fat cook or eater by any means, but this is truly excessive. I made it a second time with 1/4 cup of heavy cream and 1/4 cup whole milk, and it was even better tasting with improved mouthfeel, in addition to having a more reasonable nutritional profile.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: The beans are good but on their own wouldn’t be anything overly special. The arugula salad on top with the lemon and breadcrumbs is easily what makes this dish. I was skeptical that the breadcrumbs on their own could add that much crunch… but I was wrong to doubt!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
commentBody: I loved this recipe, especially following the tips from another commentor who told us how to make it vegan.  That meant substituting coconut cream for dairy heavy cream. Adding 1 t. of whole grain mustard and 1 T nutritional yeast to the beans. Also adding 1 t. nutritional yeast and paprika to the toasted bread crumbs mixed with the arugula along with a glug of sundried tomato oil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Creamy, Spicy Tomato Beans and Greens  Recipe
