commentBody: This was easy and delicious, I was surprised how well it worked, given the comments here! I used radiatori and the swap in pasta shape caused no problems.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Added extra garlic, some mushrooms sautéed separately and roasted the broccoli floret tops. Was delightful. Will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was not as good as I expected.  Broccoli gets too mushy.  I would cook the broccoli separately and add at the end.  I like the idea of anchovies.  I would substitute Italian breadcrumbs for panko.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: 10 minutes cook time for broccoli florets and pasta seems too long. I would expect the pasta and broccoli to both be mushy by that point. 8 minutes would likely be sufficient, and I would start checking at 7.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: At the very end, turn heat off, add 1/4 cup heavy cream and 2 tablespoons butter. Bon appetite!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is alchemical.  Simple ingredients and easy preparation somehow result in a deliciously lemony mix of pasta and veg that would be great even without the breadcrumb topping.  I was surprised and over-the-moon pleased.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Simple preparation, outstanding flavor.
Adaptable in many differnt ways. 
This will be a regular in oir menu rotation

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this as written and I think it is delicious. I will be curious how it is tomorrow because I only cook for me.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I was a bit apprehensive to try this after reading the comments but I am so glad I did! Delicious and versatile. I used a whole pound of pasta, added a little more broth, and probably doubled the lemon juice. I followed the previous advice to prep the breadcrumbs earlier and set aside so the same pan was used for everything. I skipped the drizzle of oil at the end. Next time I would add the garlic to the breadcrumbs instead of the broccoli. And add some beans for protein. A keeper!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The processed broccoli stems, onions and garlic did not come out slushy nor would they without any liquid. So that was weird as far as instructions went. This came out ok. Not very flavorful. The breadcrumbs added crunch but the recipe really didn’t need them. I probably won’t make this again. Too much work for not enough payoff.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly (which I almost never do!) and it turned out fantastic. So great to find an alternative to red or cream-based pasta sauces. For the reviews where people said this recipe didn’t work for them, most clearly didn’t follow the instructions so just read closely and you should be more than fine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Should have spent more time cooking out the blended bits, ended with a bit too much raw garlic taste. 
Looked ugly at first but trust the process, turned out solid. Don’t skip the bread crumbs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Cooking broccoli "to death" makes it very sweet and transforms it into a completely different vegetable.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made as written except used orzo since that’s what I had. Turned out delicious! Helped that I had homemade chicken stock in the freezer to use.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I fried a package of cubed pancetta in lieu of adding olive oil. I then added the broccoli paste to the pot and followed the rest of the recipe steps. My whole family enjoyed it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Peel the main broccoli stem of it's outer toughness and you have the best tasting part left of the broccoli.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Added sausage and halved grape tomatoes to the leftovers. Even better!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: One star for how hard we laughed while making this. Followed the recipe to a T and it was borderline inedible. Maybe it would be good if we had never seen pasta before. Serve it with garlic bread so you'll at least have one thing that tastes good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I think that it's best to leave out the onion and use more garlic (maybe 5 cloves), but sauté that first in a small amount of oil with the chili flakes, then add the broccoli purée, etc.  I would also cook the orecchiette separately as it stays al dente and is a difficult pasta to cook evenly, or perhaps use a penne or small rigatoni instead.  I also throw a bit of parmigiana and pecorino into the pasta at the end in addition to having some on the table.  Finish plates with a glug of olive oil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe, paying attention to the precautions re: temperature and proportions, and it was delicious and tasted very authentic.  Nothing needs to be changed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Don’t let the comments scare you. An easy and delicious recipe that works perfectly as written. 1/4 tsp of red pepper flakes gives plenty of heat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
This was delicious - the proportions worked well for us.
The panko crumbs are an integral part of it.

It's a lot of work- chopping, food processing , etc.

But once I'd relaxed I said I'd make it again. Husband loved it - nice kick from the red pepper flakes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I had high hopes for this recipe but was left with a pasta that was under cooked while the broccoli was mush. The flavor wasn’t bad but the texture and color were bad. The best part was the breadcrumb topping. I ate one serving and threw the rest in the trash, I won’t be making this recipe again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I took a risk and used chickpea rotini - pasta took a bit more minding than was intended, but it came out great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Came out kinda soupy mushy.....not saving it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: here in Italy this cooking technique is called pasta risottata and i love it but though i am never fussy with  al dente notion i would never cook pasta for 15 minutes

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I added cubed sautéed chicken breast for protein -- this dish was a big hit. Definitely wait to add the broccoli at the end of the cooking time or it'll get pale and mushy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Reminds me of the Knorr broccoli-chicken-noodle package meal I ate growing up. Only this is delicious and nutritious. Will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Very good.  Used 16 oz rigatoni and increased volume of broccoli by 1/3.  Used 4 c. Better Than Bouillon veggie stock to start, and then added 3-4 c. more water along the way to keep it saucy after taking the lid of the pot.  Could have added the broccoli florets a little later in the cooking, but did not find it overdone when cooked per instructions.  Will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: For those of you like me who don’t have a food processor, dicing the broccoli stems, onion, and garlic and then pulsing them in a blender seemed to do just fine

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Would another veg work in place of broccoli?  I’m allergic. Other crucifers are fine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Since you're allergic to broccoli but other crucifers are ok: Cauliflower would behave similarly but have a different flavor. If you tolerate romanesco that would also work. You might also get a similar effect (again, different flavor) with squash. The cooking time of the squash would be different of course (less time for summer squash, more time for winter squash).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Not a chef, but off the top of my head I'm thinking zucchini or chard might be nice?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Reserve 1 cup fond before straining pasta and broccoli
No need for food processor
Add feta

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: There is no draining of the pasta in this recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this almost exactly according to the recipe but wanted to add some protein, so started by sauteeing some chicken sausage, removing it, and then cooking the puree in the sausage-flavored oil. Then I added the sausage back at the very end with the lemon zest etc. It was delicious!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made a big sloppy overly-complicated broccoli mess.  I recommend making the bread crumbs first and setting aside, then wiping out pot to make pasta, saves one pan to wash.  Could use more lemon, maybe anchovy in the sauce.  Just didn't work out for me.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Did not like the texture of the broccoli. I would use these ingredients but not the method and make a better pasta dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Perfectly pleasant but not wow, . Agree some anchovies would lift it a little.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I am with Jacques Pépin: broccoli stems (peeled, of course) are the best part of the broccoli. Step 1 in this recipe is confusing. Perhaps I am just being too literal: "Trim and discard the thick, woody stem, removing it where the branches begin." Why would you trim it if you are going to discard it?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: "Discard the thick, woody stem, removing it where the branches begin...  roughly chop the remaining broccoli stems."

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: We found this a little oniony. I’d use half an onion, though you might need to add a bit of water to make up for the juice. I also waited 5 minutes to put the florets and added a handful of chopped basil at the end

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
I ended up cooking the pasta separately and adding less broth

Then I additionally added some cannellini beans for some protein 

Yum the kick of the spice and lemon! Hits just right.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made a version of this recipe last night, with a few modifications: (1) to make it easier to blend the stems, I first cooked them with the broth until tender, then spooned out and blended with a bit of liquid before returning to the pot, (2) for a bit more flavour/texture, roasted the broccoli florets before adding them at the last minute to the pasta. Would make this again, next time a bit lighter on the seasoning for the broth, as once absorbed by the pasta it was a bit too salty for my taste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I have been making broccoli pasta for years. Much easier way to do it: steam broccoli, sauté onions and garlic, add steamed broccoli to pan with onions and garlic and mash . Add anything else you wish (no broth). I add only a little salt, pepper and grated  parmesan or pecorino or both.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was tasty and easy. The broccoli florets do get a bit banged up with the very frequent stirring towards the end of the recipe. I added some sautéed sausage which probably overpowered the broccoli taste and used some garlic breadcrumbs from another NY Times recipe-my bad. It was still delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious- a great weeknight meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: It's not the same with whole wheat pasta, and the lemon zest gets lost.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Would gluten-free pasta work?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I implore you to NOT discard broccoli stems.  Once you peel the tough exterior, you'll find that they are delicious.  Why would you throw away so much usable material?  Perhaps it doesn't fit into this recipe (which is on deck to try) but if you dice into small pieces, you'll be delighted with the unique flavor of the stems.  So Good!  Raw or cooked - but peeling is a MUST~

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this following the recipe as closely as I could. I realized as I went on that adapting a modified risotto technique to a white wheat pasta would produce a heavy and starchy result. I am a big fan of this site. I have never before objected to a recipe. I won’t try this one again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Add spicy sausage for flavor and texture

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Looking forward to trying this recipe. I plan to use a different pasta though. Does anyone else have the issue I always do with orecchiette sticking together (it’s like 2 or 3 of them are spooning) so those pieces don’t cook enough? I’ve tried rigorous stirring throughout cooking and no luck

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Recipe didn’t come together in the food processor well. Use of 12 oz of pasta is so odd. Very much a side dish and doesn’t have that herby flavor you are probably seeking. Didn’t really finish our meals. Won’t make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: We love this recipe, but I don’t like when a recipe has you leave a partially used box of pasta or broth leftover. I use a full box of pasta, a full 4 cups carton of broth, and two heads of broccoli. Season a little more heavily with salt and pepper, and you may need to add some more water as suggested during the final stage of cooking but it’s perfect! We love it mixed with chicken sausage that I pre-fry in the same pan that I’ll use to make the breadcrumbs. Great pasta recipe for my lactose intolerant husband.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this with 12oz of Banza gluten free pasta and the whole 4-cup box of broth, and it was absolutely delicious. To keep the recipe gluten free, I toasted walnuts on the stove in a little bit of olive oil instead of the breadcrumbs. A fabulous sunday dinner! Salting throughout is critical for the dish to not taste flat

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: For two of us, I used 8oz pasta (orecchiette) and 21 oz broth, but kept the quantities of broccoli, onion, garlic, and oil. There were leftovers. Ratios worked well (though I agree with other commenters that orecchiette is a challenge to keep from sticking).  TBH, I skipped the panko--this was a quick weeknight meal--but I'm sure it'd be lovely.  The lemon zest and juice brighten the dish.  I might be tempted to add a few anchovies in the initial saute of the veggies next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Could this be made with broccoli rabe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Added some pan sautéed prosciutto and pecorino to topping.  Toasted breadcrumbs super on it. Blanched broccoli separately.  Tasty! It’s a keeper.  Used tortiglioni pasta and added a bit more ckn stock.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
In case you don't have broth, you can use water, add nutritional yeast to make it flavorful, or bit of soy sauce or miso. 
Alternative to breadcrumbs is using fried onions, crushed. I usually buy them when they are available during holiday season. They can be a crunchy garnish to many things including, salads, soups, pilaf etc
I would refrain from using different shaped pasta. I suggest adding the broccoli after 6–7 minutes of pasta cooking, to maintain texture and color of broccoli.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Braised Broccoli Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Tasty. I added the broccoli after the first 10 minutes of cooking so they stayed bright and not overcooked. Also threw in some leftover rotisserie chicken at that point. Will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I'm Korean and tried this recipe and it was very, very good, getting a heartfelt approval from the many mouths that I feed. I followed the recipe (used linguine) but added a few touches. I sauteed onions with the scallions (and a bit of garlic), and added fresh wild sesame leaves at the end (kkaenip, similar to shiso leaves) (instead of the parsley or basil that accompanies pasta dishes usually). Added a bit of Parmesan(not olive oil) to mellow out the heat of the gochujang. A keeper!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: As an savings idea, I'd get smaller shrimp instead of paying a premium for big ones and then cutting them up...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Just curious. If you’ve already combined everything in Step 5, then how (or why) are you dividing the pasta amongst bowls, and then spooning the shrimp and sauce over it?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Yes — you are spooning the (sauced) shrimp pasta into bowls, then transferring any errant shrimp and sauce that remain in the skillet on top of the plated pasta. (The bite-size shrimp tend to fall to the bottom, and you'll want to save/enjoy every last bite!)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I’d like that clarified, too!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Hi Don.I think they are assuring all ingredients are divided equally after you put the pasta in your bowl, as it most likely will fall back into the pot...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I dislike supermarket cherry tomatoes in general and especially when they are cooked. They are mostly tough skin and bitter seeds. They don't add much tomato flavor to a sauce. For most recipes, I substitute canned, diced Italian tomatoes along with some of their juices until tomato season arrives. Diced tomatoes are also more likely to cling to the pasta and meld with the gochujang to form a sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was good, but next time I'll add garlic with the scallions.  To the person who wanted to turn the heat down, use less gochujang or add coconut milk.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Question on skillet pasta like this. Is there a reason to put all ingredients in skillet at end vs. put the pasta pot? I use the pasta pot after draining bc my skillet never has room for all ingredients together.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
@Dawn

You are ‘supposed to’ add the pasta to the sauce and not the other way round and then a little of the pasta water that you’ve saved to get it to all amalgamate

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Good, but proportions didn’t work for us. NYT always suggests more pasta than I would use. For 1 lb shrimp, I used   1/2 lb dried pasta, 1 10oz container of cherry tomatoes (cut in 1/2), 1 small can of diced Italian tomatoes, 1 medium onion (chopped), 2 cloves of garlic (chopped), about 1.5 T gochujang. Added some spinach near the end of cooking. And of course we cheated by adding parm cheese. Yum!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Sauce worked perfectly.   I used Thai rice noodles but next time would use spaghetti.   Quick and easy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Almost a 5 star. My gochujang came from TJ's, so maybe wasn't the best, though it was made in Korea. Sauce didn't seem very 'saucy', at least mine didn't look much like the photo. Hubby added a serious amount of gochujang at the table and I would definitely increase the quantity in cooking next time. I used bucatini because that's what was in the pantry and I think a more delicate pasta would hold the sauce better. Nevertheless, it was a satisfying dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Gochujang   shrimp = can’t go wrong. Didn’t chop shrimp  but added baby spinach and kale at the end for a final stir.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I haven't made this yet, but know from other similar pasta dishes that when you divvy the pasta, the pieces mixed in, such as the shrimp and tomatoes, fall to the bottom. You then spoon them to each bowl/plate separately on top.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Using Gochujang in a pasta dish was a bit of a revelation, although once I cooked this dish, it makes total sense! Easy and quick.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Supper tonight, exactly to the recipe. What fun to make a sauce out of cherry tomatoes, instead of roasting. Tasted result on stove: buttery, pasta showed itself, and sauce was more than gochujang and tomato. Yet just before serving, I broke the leash and added some cilantro. Nice. Lemon juice? Maybe next time. Extra-firm tofu might be a good sub for my vegan friends.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night with half the pasta (more than enough for 1 lb shrimp!), 12 oz tomatoes (that was what I had) and left the shrimp whole. Really liked it, but after reading comments might consider adding some garlic next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Is there a compelling flavor reason to use large shrimp here rather than smaller? If you're already chopping them up, seems to me you could save money & buy smaller shrimp!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Laura I’m not sure, except that I’ve never gotten the flavor wallop out of small shrimp that I get out of Alaska spot prawns or royal reds. They maintain that strong sweetness even when cut up. That’s a possible reason. But sometimes the budget decides!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Substitute canned diced tomatoes for the cherry tomatoes, especially outside of tomato season.    Can substitute yellow onion for scallions if no scallions on hand.   Use a little less than 1/4 cup gochujang for most palates, 4-6 garlic cloves, 3/4 lb. pasta plenty.  Grated Parmesan at serving.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Very easy very delicious.  I went a little heavier on the gochujang.  I also made it  with small shells instead of long spaghetti.  It's what I had handy and made it easier to eat with a spoon for a take to work lunch..  Will def make this one again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Added some lemon after trying it and helped brighten the flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and so easy to make!  I would use more shrimp, scallions, and tomatoes next time - so much pasta left over and not enough of the good stuff to go with it.   I used wild caught Argentina Pink shrimp and cut them into thirds.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and simple recipe - after reading the comments I decided not to half the shrimp and add garlic to the oil during the shrimp cooking process halfway through. I did mess up and cooked the green onions and tomatoes at the same time. But this helped by adding some more garlic and pepper. I also added a bit of the pasta water while cooking the sauce and seasoned with more salt. Loved the depth of flavor in a simple recipe! I threw in some arugula for greens - did want to try the other comment of adding perilla leaves but didn’t have time to go to hmart.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with chunks of lobster instead of shrimp?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
For less spicy - 

I used ssamjang instead of gochujang for less spice. Ssamjang has gochujang in it, so it does have some spice, but much less because it's mixed with Doenjang, which has very little (or no?) spice. Ssamjang also has more savory profile than the sweeter gochujang. 

I will make this again and may up the spice and sweetness by replacing some of the ssamjang with gochujang. I did add some Huy Fong Chili Garlic sauce, but that didn't add the sweetness that gochujang would've.

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made as written, except halved. Will make again but might add more of something green. Seems like a great recipe for when you've been hanging out and someone says "hey do you guys want dinner?" More or less pantry ingredients. Someone can run to the store for shrimp or tomatoes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I was nervous at first with this recipe, but it ended up being a wonderful alternative to the usual frutti di mare & arrabbiata recipes. I love spicy seafood noodle dishes! Will definitely be trying this again with crab, sardines, scallops, clams, squid, etc -- and I think it could use a burst of sweet green peas to counterbalance the zippy gochujang. Why not!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I really love how quickly this came together, but it's really important to follow the measurements on this one, to make sure every bite gets a bit of shrimp in. Otherwise, it's a spicy pasta and that's no fun. BUT when the measurements are followed and there's enough shrimp, paired with a bit of those sauteed scallions, mmm yumyumyum. I am definitely making this again and again and again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Used smaller frozen cooked shrimp (peeled/deveined)- thawed and then threw them in at the end, it made this recipe even easier! Also added about 4 cloves of minced garlic- one was really big so maybe more like 5/6. I used the Trader Joe’s gochujang and I feel like I used more than 0.25 cup, but I didn’t really measure, just kept dumping it in until the vibes were correct. I LOVED this recipe. So quick and easy and vibrant!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is good, but it needs some umami. I sprinkle some MSG on after plating, and it goes from good to great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: A  quick  few ingredients  complex tasting pasta dish that is on my keeper list. I didn't have fresh cherry tomatoes and substituted  a  can of diced tomatoes from my pantry.  Thank you Alexa!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Yes what Janet said a year ago: supermarket cherry tomatoes are hard little acid balls.  Canned whole San Marzano style tomatoes, 'filleted' like a fish, are the way to go.  It's a bit messy but it only takes a few minutes to cut out their little stem knobs, then splay them open and wipe away some pulp.  Then, cut in long thin strips to go with the pasta shape.  You're welcome.

author: Alexa Weibel

commentBody: Is there any reason why you couldn't just add the raw shrimp for the last 2-3 minutes of Step 4 or to Step 5 to cook through?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was really good! I’m a coward about heat, so I less chili crisp. I steamed broccoli spears and added them to the bowl, but I could, I think, have cooked them with the tomatoes. Cooking for one, so I made about a quarter recipe and loved every bite. Whole grain spaghetti added a bit of nuttiness and had good consistency. Oh, and I used tiny shrimp which were a total pain to clean. Might use larger ones and chop them next time, as suggested. Good idea, though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: We loved it!  I added a lot more garlic, some ginger root, and some shiitake mushrooms.  Cutting up the shrimp is a good idea; I cooked large shrimp and it was a pain to cut them once plated.  To ensure noodles were crisp and not mush, I transferred the shrimp mixture to a bowl, then to empty wok added more oil, garlic, ginger root, and sautéed cooked spaghetti noodles.  Added some nam pla and chile crisp while sauteeing noodles.  Then we spooned the shrimp sauce mixture on top. Fast & Yum!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made this without shrimp (vegetarian), but otherwise followed the recipe. Maybe the meat makes a difference but the sauce has 2-notes so if you LOVE gochujang and tomatoes, go for it. But that’s it. No nuance, no complexity, no reason to make it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Smashed a couple of garlic cloves in w shrimp sauté. Used only 1/2 lb spaghetti. Otherwise, made sauce in quantity as directed, adding a bit of pasta water as the tomatoes cooked down. Fast, easy, tasty. 
Had snap pea, radish and mint salad on the side. May not be the flavor profile to complement this dish, but the cool crunch was welcome.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was wonderful. I overshot on the gochujang for my spice comfort level, so to compensate, I added a few tablespoons of coconut milk. It added an excellent creaminess that combined well with the olive oil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: It was ok but not the best use of shrimp. The gochujang and scallions tasted a bit off, not bad, but nothing special either. Olive oil with garlic, anchovies, pepper flakes and lemon juice would have been better partners of shrimp or scallops. I used canned cherry tomatoes.

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel

Keep shrimp whole and cook at the end with

author: Alexa Weibel

commentBody: I had on hand a pound of bucatini and a pound jumbo shrimp. Cut each shrimp into thirds and used a 1/3 cup of the gochujang. I used a brand called to Sempio. Although it is from Seoul, Korea, it is not very spicy. I would not recommend that brand. That being said, The dish turned out wonderfully. author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. It’s very easy and goes together quickly. My husband doesn’t even like shrimp and was licking the pot. It’s wonderful!

author: Alexa Weibel

commentBody: Good. Not your classic pasta as it is made with the Gochujang sauce, but if you are a fan of that you will love this! Husband and I discussed whether it deserves parmasen and I believe it does but he disagrees. The olive oil does make a a difference in spice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Q:  Should I use gochujang sauce or paste for this recipe? Another recipe said to use paste not sauce so wonder if same applies to this one.  TIA

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe with only a few ingredients. Used the only pasta we had (penne) and it worked out fine. Added a few squirts of lemon to the top of the served plates and it made the whole dish sparkle.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe. I modify by cooking the tomatoes for about 15 minutes instead of the recommended 6 minutes. This makes the tomato sauce reduced and ensures the tomato skin and flesh is soft and cooked rather than firm. I also slightly overcook the pasta to make sure it’s full of starches for the sauce to stick to. I personally love it this way because it beings all the flavors together in the sauce and helps the sauce stick to the pasta.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with chicken?  We have shellfish allergies in my house.  Thanks and I'm looking forward to trying as we all love gochujang around here!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was great! Strongly agree with doubling the amount of gojuchang for good flavor. Also, I didn’t have tomatoes and they’re not in season anyway, but I did have a big 28 ounce can of san Marzano tomatoes, and that worked perfectly. Didn’t really need much pasta water because I think it had the right amount of juice to give it slip.

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is an easy and quick dinner. I used less gochujang and it was spicy enough for us.

commentBody: Does this recipe use gochujang sauce or paste? They are different. I have both. Which one should I use?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gochujang Shrimp Pasta Recipe
commentBody: So delicious! I tried it once as written with cherry tomatoes and second time with canned tomatoes. Honestly preferred the cherry tomatoes as I found it more silky and gochujang forward. Canned tomatoes were good but it tasted like more of a generic red sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Caramelized Shrimp With Lemongrass Recipe
commentBody: Freeze the lemon grass stalks. You can then use a microplane to grate as much as you need. Saves the time and effort involved in peeling and pounding/mincing the stalk.

author: Alexa Weibel

commentBody: We love fish sauce.  But 5 tbs seems excessive...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Caramelized Shrimp With Lemongrass Recipe
commentBody: For me it was just the right amount.

author: Alexa Weibel

commentBody: Curious if anyone knows whether gojuchang paste is a decent substitute in this recipe for the “roasted chile paste.”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Caramelized Shrimp With Lemongrass Recipe
commentBody: Not really.  The "ground chili paste" would be better although it is not really a paste in the same way as gojuchang.  Sambal Oelek by Huy Fong is once available by Amazon which is my favorite.  I think you could definitely find a chili-bean paste in your supermarket.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Caramelized Shrimp With Lemongrass Recipe
commentBody:
@webhill
I used Gochujang and liked it - I guess it's a matter of preference. I was cooking for a family member who couldn't take too much spice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Caramelized Shrimp With Lemongrass Recipe
commentBody: Used less sugar, more fish sauce. Added chopped asparagus and it worked really well!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Caramelized Shrimp With Lemongrass Recipe
commentBody: @webhill gojuchang sauce has more umami-- many recipes that call for it and suggest subs will sub with a 1:1 mix of miso and siracha. Vietnamese tưong ớt or roasted chili paste is more straight chilis with a bit of vinegar, think chunky Siracha.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Caramelized Shrimp With Lemongrass Recipe
commentBody: 5 Tbsp doesn't seem excessive for 2 pounds of shrimp.  Anything less and you won't have a sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Caramelized Shrimp With Lemongrass Recipe
commentBody: Very good recipe. I made it as directed except reduced amount of shrimp because I was cooking for two. I added julienne snow peas at the end for color and crunch. Will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Caramelized Shrimp With Lemongrass Recipe
commentBody: Made the first time for a party of 10. Best dish on the table by far - and it faced stiff competition. Fish sauce was perfect as stated in the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Caramelized Shrimp With Lemongrass Recipe
commentBody: Using 5 Tbs of fish sauce in the syrup makes the final dish way too salty. I will try this recipe again with 2 tbs fish sauce:3 tbs water & see how that tastes. All the other flavors are very nice.

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Normally I wouldn’t comment on a recipe I haven’t cooked (it’s now on the grocery list) but want to offer a tip.  Anytime I’m using boneless skinless chicken thighs in a recipe that asks for a searing of cubed meat, I butterfly the fat side of the thigh to even out the thickness and sear them whole. May take a couple of batches.  Then cube the chicken and back to the pan for finishing.  You get a much more even sear and prevents overcooking.  Bon appetit!

author: Alexa Weibel

commentBody: Can you use lemon grass paste as a substitute?

author: Alexa Weibel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Though the flavor isn't as fresh, it should be fine to substitute puree for the stalks. The general ratio is 1 teaspoon puree for 1 stalk of lemongrass.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Yes, as long as the jar has not been sitting in the fridge for months.  In fact if you are a lazy mincer, it might work better than fresh - even "fine chopping" fibrous fresh lemongrass would be too coarse for this recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was just ok. Needed some lime zest or juice, fresh basil, or something else to brighten it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Soooo my middle aged post Covid brain forgot to add the lemongrass to the lemongrass chicken. It was still delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: This was good with coconut rice. 1.5 cups white rice, 1 can coconut milk, fill that can with water and dump it into the pan, a squirt of lime juice, and some salt.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: It was good even though I used oyster sauce instead of fish sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: i thought the Thai chile overwhelmed the delicate lemongrass. next time i will use a jalapeno.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I have to disagree about puréed lemongrass !  The flavor is totally different.  Fresh lemongrass stalks taste fresh and live; puréed lemongrass in a tube adds almost nothing and sometimes deadens other flavors.  Go for the real thing!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I made this as written the other night, except for substituting a serrano pepper as my grocery store did not have thai chili peppers. It was delicious! I served it over jasmine rice as suggested with a side of baby bok choy/shiitake mushrooms. There were 2 of us, and no leftovers.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
Used 1tbsp fish sauce and 1tbsp soy sauce, and it was still really salty. I think .5tbsp of each and 1tbsp of water would have worked out better. 
Tossed in some steamed green beans at the end  with a teaspoon of chili oil. Worked out great (aside from the salt, of course)!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: This was easy and fast but the sauce needed more punch. I doubled all the ingredients except the fish sauce, and used a bit of gochujang paste in place of Thai chili. Will keep playing around with the proportions....add a dose of lime maybe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
made this many times. fam favorite! i add ginger too!
i use Red Boat fish sauce and Siracha. ALWAYS great

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
Made this in a double batch as instructed, but trimmed excess fat off the thighs and rendered it in lieu of using canola oil.  Came out PERFECT.  I've longed for Pho Binh's (Memphis, TN) lemongrass chicken that they offered during their buffet prior to COVID where they shut it down.  That buffet was wonderful.  The rest will be missed but this lemongrass chicken is a formidable clone.

This worked well with quinoa as a grain, too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I don't eat fish sauce.  Ideas for substitution?  What flavor is it contributing to a dish?  (I do not like the taste of fish)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Lynn, here's a webpage with six substitutions (found by googling "Fish Sauce substitutes"): https://food52.com/blog/22057-how-to-substitute-for-fish-sauce-in-a-pinch -Fish sauce is salty, but also has an umami funk that some folks find off-putting. Personally, I like it, but I'd never eat a spoonful of it by itself. I also wonder if a combo of soy sauce and lime juice, maybe with a pinch of sugar, might be a good sub.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
I have not made this particular dish (yet), but fish sauce adds an umami - that *something* extra. A deep soy sauce (like a mushroom soy sauce) or oyster sauce might give you a similar oomph. 

Also: I cook with fish sauce and detest the smell, which is overwhelmingly fishy, but the flavor doesn't typically overpower the dish with a fishy taste. If a recipe calls for a lot I add a bit at a time and taste a bunch to ensure I'm getting a flavor I like without the fish sauce overtaking the dish).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I had the same attitude until I tried it in a couple recipes. Fish sauce adds a special umami without being fishy. You might start with a lesser amount, say, 1 tablespoon. A substitute is soy sauce, vinegar, and a pinch of slt...but it isn't the same.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: It’s ok- a bit heavy on the fish sauce, next t time I’ll try the soy sauce, lime and sugar mix. Will add broccoli and some peanuts to garnish. I will use the lemongrass in the and the fresh  really wasn’t ‘fresh’.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Delicious simple & quick! No modifications needed. Paired it with a Japanese pickled cucumber salad and butter lettuce. The Thai red chilis add a punch which we loved but maybe would go half for someone sensitive to heat. Will be making this one again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Made it with thin chicken breasts because it was what we had on hand. Delicious!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Used lemongrass paste and it was great.  Served over coconut rice.  Wonderful  Will definitely make again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: We made this with frozen lemongrass. It was a bit spicy for us so will go lighter on the peppers. Also we really missed and acid. Next time we will have limes on hand and also some coriander/ cilantro for freshness.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I appreciated how fast & easy this was - it looked beautiful coming together. The chicken turned out very tender and perfectly cooked, but the sauce didn't taste as flavorful as the ingredients made it sounds like it would be - I mostly tasted umami and little bursts of lemongrass, which is ok but not the flavor bomb I had hoped for. Next time, I'll marinate the chicken in the ingredients for a couple hours. I added cilantro, fresh lime at the end, and harissa (instead of the thai chile).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
Loved this as written and served over jasmine brown rice. Garnished with cilantro, mint and fresh lime wedges. 
After an epic culinary trip to Vietnam last fall was craving the great foods and lemongrass chicken we enjoyed.  

*Don’t be afraid of the fish sauce. 
It adds such an umami depth of flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Using the term "sauce" is kind of overrated here. This is a pretty dry dish as is a salt and pepper tofu for example. It's still really tasty though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: This was not good. Fish sauce and sugar didn’t caramelize & dish just smelled like ammonia.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: First time user of fresh lemongrass from the garden and it won't be the last! I didn't add the Thai chile pepper because of spice averse children but we really enjoyed the dish. Next time I would add minced ginger and maybe a little cornstarch and water to create a bit more sauce. So yummy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: appreciated how simple the recipe was. I did use an entire Serrano pepper and regretted it. Way too spicy. Also topped with sweet Thai chili sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I have been eyeing this recipe for a couple of months and finally made it. I substituted Lemon grass paste cause that's all I could find. It turned out great! I grow Lemon grass in Florida but forgot to bring a few stalks home with me this season!! I used 3 Tablespoons of paste where the recipe calls for 1/4 cup of stalks. My hubby just said this recipe is a keeper!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe. Next time I will double the shallot and maybe add a little ginger. But the lemongrass and fish sauce combo is awesome. Fast, healthy, and delicious. Weeknight winner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I used boneless breasts because I like my chicken bone dry :). This was really good, but I would just suggest being sure to mince the lemongrass very small. I had never cooked with it before, and I think I left the pieces a little too large since they're kind of tough and fibrous and difficult to chew.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
This was delicious and quick. My favorite combo. 

I keep lemongrass stalks in the freezer. Everything else is in the pantry. I subbed TJ’s green dragon sauce for a thai chili (tonight was a “grab what we have” night). I made rice with coconut milk and served with broccoli in garlic oil. I did deglaze the pan with water to grab the brown flavor bits. And I put a little soy sauce at the table. There’s no such thing as too salty for me. 

Yum. Thanks NYT

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: PS: Forgot to add that I threw in some chopped up broccolini towards the end and it certainly added a little something (as well as a punch of colour!)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Can I substitute lemongrass powder?  If so, how much?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Loved the recipe but will use half quantity of fish sauce next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I used the zest from two lemons and the juice from one to substitute for the lemon grass. Came out great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
Thanks to notes contributor Portland for their lemongrass substitution suggestion (I also used lime)—I was uncharacteristically out of lemongrass paste & wanted to make this!
Used homemade vegan fish sauce,tripled the garlic, & marinated the chicken in tamari & spices for 30 min. 
Used green onion as a sub for shallot, but can see how shallot would lend necessary sweetness. 
Served w/steamed broccoli, squash, & brown jasmine. Ridiculously good & easy! Next time will make coconut rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I can only purchase dried lemon grass.  Anybody ever reconstitute and then chop and add that to the re ipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Two tablespoons of fish sauce for one pound of chicken was WAY too much for us. In fact, I had used a pound and a quarter of chicken and the fish sauce flavor was unpleasantly overpowering.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I cut the fish sauce to 1 and 1/3 Tbs and still found that it was too salty and fish saucey for me, and I'm Thai. Next time I'll use only 1 Tbs. I added some napa cabbage and loved the end result. The flavor overall was good and I'll definitely make this recipe again. In fact, I packed leftovers for my son's lunch and he texted me from school to say, "This lunch is really good!" which was a first!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions on what to pair with this dish? What vegetable or salad?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Turned out well and as a fan of heat, this was a good one. However, 2 Tbsp fish sauce and 1/2 tsp sugar does not make a sauce for 1lb of chicken. Drier than I expected, but still good. Although I will always seek out heat and the pleasant funk of fish sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Just made this dish and followed the recipe as written. I’m not sure if 2 tablespoons for fish sauce is a typo, but it was way too much. Definitely reduce it to 1 tablespoon. Despite being too salty, the lemongrass flavor was delicious and the dish came together easily. I served it with rice and steamed broccoli, with a squeeze of lemon juice to mellow the saltiness.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Excellent, flavorful weeknight meal, and I appreciate the Vietnamese flavors without all the sugar that gets added to restaurant Vietnamese dishes. I used two red chiles for extra heat and substituted a small amount of minced yellow onion for the shallot, which worked out fine in a pinch. Perfect to two people with a small leftover portion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Any subsitute for a thai red chile?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: not a fan of chicken thighs. Has anyone substituted with boneless breast?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I actually made this with chunks of chicken breasts. You have to be very careful not to overcook the chicken, or you wind up with dry, rubbery chicken nuggets. Based on other recipes I have made, chicken thighs hold up much better to being cooked. I’ve always eaten chicken breasts, for a recipe like this, where you are cooking small chunks, I did not even notice a difference.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I loved this recipe! Spice lovers: Use two red thai chilis instead of one to give the recipe a great amount of heat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: This didn’t turn out well, too much fish sauce, I recommend using half of the sauce.  The lemon grass was a bit chewy and gristley…I would not make this again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
I made this. I was rereading the recipe afterwards to see if perhaps I had left out some ingredients. I hadn’t.

Perhaps my lemongrass was not fresh enough, but there was nothing about this that makes me want to try making it again, not when I have a recipe for Chongqing Chicken.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I used chicken tenders instead of thighs, and seasoned them with some Asian fried chicken spice mix.  More shallots, garlic and used lemongrass puree.  Outstanding!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: To me, this dinner just smelled and tasted like straight fish sauce. We were very excited to try it and had to force ourselves to eat it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Followed as is but used vegan chicken. Way too salty!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Vegan chicken?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I added 3 TBS soy sauce to the recipe as it was too bland.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: The comments really helped. I cut the fish sauce by a quarter, added ginger, an extra half teaspoon of sugar, and a tablespoon of lime right at the end, and this was really delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I made this with tofu and vegan fish sauce and it was great. The flavour of sauce/marinade is quite powerful so the subtle taste of tofu worked wonderfully. Would make again with something green on the side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
Plan to make this over a grill? 
Use oyster sauce , honey , water mix and brush during cook ing

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: The fish sauce overpowered the dish and didn’t just add umami. Just smelled and tasted like fish. Couldn’t taste the fresh lemongrass that smelled so great when I was mincing it. Total disappointment.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Re: fish sauce...I read somewhere that the inexpensive ones are more "stinky." I used some in a recipe where you add it to a hot pan, and the kitchen smelled like fish sauce for a week. One review recommended Red Boat (among others) and I've been using that with better results.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Confirm red boat upgrade, will be doing more experimentation.  Side note: can confirm better, Japanese mirin is also worth it in recipes that call for that.  It has a darker color compared to the clear, cheap, mirin.  A Japanese friend said I would probably not be able to notice but it was a big difference.  Yes, the imported ones here have salt to get around some strange import tax about mirin with alcohol, but even so the salty Japanese dark mirin was more interesting by far.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I always throw in Thai basil into this recipe, adds a great herbacious note.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I just made this tonight, and it was great.  I put tons of pepper and distributed it evenly by mixing the chicken with with fork.  I made sure my chicken had no strangest pieces in it, eg silverskin or tendon.  I used a lot of salt also as spouse and child love that.  The fish sauce as primary sauce was weird and smelled bad while cooking, but it was so good after cooling slightly.  I used a large jalapeño as my store does not carry fresh Thai chiles.  This was so good!  Way to go!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Very good but if you don't mise en place you will make everyone cough from smoke!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: This was easy and delicious! I served with baby bok choy, roasted with olive oil, salt, pepper, sesame oil,  a little garlic.   I followed the recommendation to seer the flattened boneless chicken thighs and then cut into 1 inch cubes.  The most delicious part of this meal was that the lemongrass flavor came through. Yum.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I wish I had read the comment on lemon grass paste.  The recipe was good with the exception of the minced lemon grass.  Every bite was crunchy but not in a good way.  The lemon grass chunks were small enough but still woody and unpleasant.  Disappointed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe! I make it with boneless chicken breasts and add extra shallot. Bright and delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe as is but thought it needed a little more flavor so I added a dab of peanut butter, roasted peanuts, sweet chilli sauce and fresh lime juice. That made all the difference! It was savory, tangy and absolutely delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I made this for dinner tonight and it was FABULOUS! The family loved it and the 14-year old hopefully asked if there was any more (there wasn't!). Next time I'm  going to double the quantity so we can all enjoy seconds!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: It was looking and smelling fantastic, but the fish sauce buried everything. Recommend less, something different or a combination.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: I sautéed the lemongrass as lengthwise pieces and removed and drained back into pan before adding the chicken. Then added it again during the aromatic step. Easy to tong most of it out before serving. Good lemongrass flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody:
@Christopher

Squished the stalks with a mallet too to release the oils.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: This was pretty good. Next time I'll up to 1.5 times the servings.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Lemongrass Chicken Recipe
commentBody: Just made this and it did need a few adjustments but it turned out to be worthy of five stars. I agree that as written it lacked a bit of flavor so I added more fish sauce to taste, a bit more sugar, several squeezes of lemon and most importantly some MSG. That really made this dish pop. Really good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious, great complexity of flavour and wonderful sweetness from the onions. I believe that 1 cup of oil is correct - I used a half cup to be cautious (coconut oil for the flavour) and it wasn't too oily at all. It makes sense in proportion with the full 20 oz of onions, you need a lot of oil to make the sauce saucey! Also, don't be tempted to skimp on the chilies - if you keep them whole as directed they impart a bit of heat but it wasn't too spicy (just don't bite into them!).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: I agree, 1 cup of oil is not a mistake. I also cut the oil in half and it turned out a little on the dry side. Those pureed onions can soak up a whole lot of oil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: My lawnmower uses less oil. Instead, i used 2-3 tbs in the dish, and used the remaining oil to separately fry sliced onions until browned and crispy, which i added with the fresh coriander.  The result was fine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: Two tablespoons of vegetable oil or ghee would be ample.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: I think the idea is cut one onion in half and slice both halves into thin slices.  Chop the other onion and then blend it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: Not sure about the onions. Half an onion is sliced. That is clear. Chop the remaining onion. Can I  assume that this means the remaining half plus the second onion?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody:
IMO, the directions indicate that you slice one onion in half and thinly slice both halves.  "Coarsely chop the remaining onion..." To me that means the remainder of the 2 onions.

One onion is halved and thinly sliced enabling thin half rings rather than thin whole rings.  One onion is coarsely chopped and put into a food processor to make an onion purée.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious.  I used just slightly less oil because I imagined that the dish would end up very oily.  I shouldn't have been worried about it---it didn't have an oily taste or mouth feel and could have handled the whole cup.  I used extra virgin olive oil as it's what I had. No other changes except use only a tsp of salt as my shrimp was already quite salty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: After finding that the peppers I had gotten were very mild, I added about a tablespoon of red pepper flakes which made it pretty spicy. I served it over brown rice. The time for cooking the peppers seemed to be very short, and I will probably use less oil next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: So tasty! Used whole tomatoes   tomato paste. Actually had a hot jalapeño…so I’ll take the pith out next time (perfect heat for R, too hot for me).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: It was OK, but it's missing something.  The heat was good, but otherwise bland. I added some lime juice, which helped,  but still...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: Followed recipe as is. Whole house enjoyed. This dish will most certainly become a mainstay.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe! Especially using two forms of onion. I will definitely make this again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: used 5 serrano peppers - excellent

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody:
Remember to buy Thai chilis
Make plenty of sauce and don’t reduce too much
Serve with fresh tomato salsa - tomato, spring onion, jalapeno and cilantro

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: Cooking for one, so halved the recipe.  Excellent.  Oil proportions are correct.  Used a Vidalia onion, which provided a nice sweetness; kashmiri chile powder for gentle, rich heat, 3 serrano chiles which I cut in half but did not seed.  I knew better than to bite down on them.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chingri Dopiaza (Shrimp With Onion, Tomato, Chile and Turmeric) Recipe
commentBody: I've made many delicious dishes from NYT Cooking, but this is the first one about which I felt compelled to comment. It’s ridiculously yummy. I swapped out the thai chiles for a scant amount of crushed red pepper (I don't handle heat well), but otherwise followed the recipe as written. Can't wait to make it for the rest of my family!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Good grief, all these comments about what's not right.  It's a recipe not a sacred text, which means you can change it to suit your taste.  If you don't want sugar, don't use it; someone else may lie it.  If you want broccoli instead of brussels sprouts, go for it.  Even better, learn to cook, investigate different ingredients, without adherence to recipes. Be brave in your own kitchens.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @KDW I want to applaud for this comment. On point.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @KDW finally someone who understands. Thank you.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight and it was spectacular. No Brussels sprouts, so used romanesco instead. Also Reblochon instead of Brie. Put in rectangular pan instead of faffing about with that circular nonsense. Absolutely delicious. Will definitely make again. Thanks, NYT!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Love the Reblochon idea!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Great idea to use a pan! What size did you use? Did you still have to roll the pastry?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Firstly this is an awesome recipe, even better if you use all the ingredients stated. If you don’t like sugar then leave it out… but the small amount of sugar helps keep the greens green! 
I suggest you try this with some guests… they’ll be impressed!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Peter CS sugar helps keep greens green? Did not know that.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I’ve just finished cooking all the vegetables and am reading ahead to baking the pie. It doesn’t say how hot to bake it. Is it baked at the 425 degrees listed at the beginning for roasting the squash? Seems hot, but??? Thanks! Excited to taste this dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @lori - my puff pastry package included a couple of recipes that baked at 400, so I tried that for 45 minutes and it worked well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: As any Great British Bake Off fan would know, this is a pithivier.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @MGM I didn’t know that - thanks!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Either cut the Brie into smaller chunks or use a cheese without a rind. It doesn't seem to melt and incorporate into the veges

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Jack use a ripe brie to get it to melt. And for extra security cut a cross in the top.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Jack the Brie infused itself perfectly in ours. Just seemed to roam through the veggies and was everywhere.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Jack My Brie did not melt much either but I liked being able to grab a bite with or without Brie.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I left the rind but the Brie really didn't melt and spread throughout the filling. Everything came out pretty good. But next time I'm going to cut the cheese into smaller parts and spread over the vegetables or maybe use a different cheese.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this as turnovers for dinner since it was just 2 of us. Cut the recipe in half, used slices of Brie nestled among the vegetables. Delicious and would make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: omg yay, I was just looking to see if anyone had done this! Did you just use one sheet of puff pastry and cut it in half then fold for each turnover? Did you have to change the bake time? I've never used puff pastry before, so not sure how to even begin to modify this recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
This is a savory recipe, with such lovely vegetables and great gooey cheese, herbs, etc. 
It is very hard to understand the honey and the brown sugar added. It’s such a shame to see even in this recipe the ever present and unnecessary sugar.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: You’re eating puff pastry filled with melted cheese and you’re nagging about an added 3-4 grams of sugar per person.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I would imagine that one could just leave both out without doing any harm to the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: The honey is to sweeten the squash and is completely unnecessary. The sugar is to create a sweet-sour balance with the vinegar. Personally, I'd omit the sugar and cut back on the vinegar, but you could add a teaspoon or so. For the critics, doctors and nutritionists recommend avoiding added sugars. And I think we can all agree that cheese is a fat not a carb.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I’m planning to make this for some guests this weekend. Can it be made in a 10 inch springform pan instead of making a free form round?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: It would probably help to score the top of the Brie or Camembert. Just a big X on top to cut through the rind. That’s what I do when I bake Brie to eat with crusty bread like a deconstructed grilled cheese. Just a thought!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: The sugar and vinegar are added to the onions and they are cooked until jammy. The idea is to create an onion jam. Sweet jammy elements are very common in baked Brie. The honey may not be needed for sweet squash, but 1 tablespoon is a nominal amount. In the entire recipe there is only 3 or 4 grams of added sugar per serving. The recipe creates a savory, sweet, sour salty, fatty mix of flavors. Awesome.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I haven’t made this yet but seriously considering for Thanksgiving. Having created a roasted veggie burrito at a restaurant I used to work at I might try roasted beets - would add some sweetness without the sugar & works well with cheese and always a big fan of leeks.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: It's kind of surprising that there are no instructions for creating the design on the top crust in the photo.  Someone inexperienced with puff pastry would need them to replicate the wow factor.
