recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried making this in a pie dish rather than freestanding? Feels like it would be easier to assemble and serve, but I don't want to mess that up.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Make the bottom crust 11-12 inches because 10 inches can't hold the ingredients and leave a margin for attaching to top crust.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this 3 days ago. Used broccoli instead of Brussels sprouts. It was beautiful! Looked just like the one in the recipe. Only problem was the Brie did not melt ooze over the vegetables. I was disappointed. Next time will cut Brie in chunks. If I had baked hotter than 400 degrees or longer than 40 minutes, it would have burned on top.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Keep in mind, it's not truly vegetarian unless you make sure to buy brie without animal rennet. This is not always easy to find. Look for microbial rennet or vegetarian rennet in the ingredients on the label.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I’m so excited to try to veganize this with Nuts for Cheese Brie and a nut milk to brush puff pastry in place of the egg wash. Can anyone verify if they baked it at 425 - same temp as the roasted squash?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe with Camembert and really enjoyed it! For our family's personal preference, I would scale back the sugar and honey, but it tastes great. I took someone's recommendation here to try scoring the cheese to make sure the rind didn't stop the cheese from spreading, which worked wonderfully. I also wasn't sure about baking at 425 the entire time and ended up doing 400 instead. Got a lovely color. My only other note is that I would definitely like to do the veg prep in advance.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Kathleen any recs on how far in advance? Thoughts on cooking the veg then freezing until needed? Or prepping the whole thing then freezing until needed?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: The recipe directs this order operations in laying down the filling: 1st: vegetables. 2nd: Brie. 3rd: onion jam. But the photo shows it was actually 1st Brie. 2nd: onion jam. 3rd: veggies. You can see a clear layer of onion jam on top of the Brie with the veggies on top, and the cheese hasn't melted through the vegetables to sink to the bottom. What gives?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: The recipe says to put half the veg on the bottom and half on the top, with cheese and onions in the middle.  HTH.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Very good.  I used about three quarters of the vegetables, there was just no more room.  I used 500 g of Camembert and when it was done it had seeped into every nook and cranny and made for a very nice sauce.  I might chop all the vegetables a day in advance.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I made this for Thanksgiving last year. It was totally delicious - a huge hit!
I made it in a large glass pie plate which solved all the assembly issues.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I chopped my pinky finger off and added it for a dinner guest to find like finding a Christmas pickle on your Christmas tree with the bonus that it was tasty!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Simpler: roast all the veggies together and season; assemble the pie with the roasted veggies and brie, bake, voila. Less washing, less time, same results.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Have made this several times with different roasted vegetables. My favorite random combo: carrots (with honey or without), zucchini, asparagus, sautéed romaine lettuce! I have also used a couple of tablespoons of red pepper jelly in place of the jammy onions and enjoyed that version very much.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Made this for Friendsgiving and it was good but not fabulous. My guests liked it, but I was a bit disappointed with the amount of time put in compared to the product of my work.

As others said, the Brie did not melt fully (even after I cut it into pieces, as one reviewer suggested). I think I would try this again with a grated/shredded cheese, and perhaps use something sharper than Brie for a bit stronger flavor and more salt. 

Also, I don’t know if it’s just me, but the preparation of the vegetables (chopping, sauteeing each item separately) took longer than described—around 90 min. That was ok for me because I was prepping the filling the day before, and assembly was fairly easy the day of my event, but if you plan to do it all in one day, plan accordingly! 

I took others’ advice and cooked this in a square pan — it would have been very tough to assemble otherwise! I would add one more recommendation: drain any excess liquid from the bowl before adding the veggies to the pastry. Maybe even pat them dry a bit? My veggies had a lot of moisture, which made the pastry disintegrate a bit.

Finally, I could only find 14-oz packages of puff pastry (I used Dufour). I do wish I had a little more pastry to work with. Does anyone have a recommended brand that comes with the right shape/amount?

I am not an experienced baker, so I think much of my problem was user error! Just leaving my two cents in case it helps someone else who does not know much about making pies.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I used Dufour too! I used one box/sheet for the bottom and one for the top. it was just the right amount in our case.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
@Emily
Trader Joe’s butter puff pastry comes in a 1lb package with two separately wrapped pieces.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Shannon Thank you for this suggestion. It worked brilliantly.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it was a hit with vegetarians and meat eaters alike. I followed the recipe with two small changes----I put a vent hole in the top to release any steam and at 45 minutes, when the pastry was golden brown, I temp'd the cheese (8oz Brie disk) through the vent hole and it was only at 100 degrees F. So, I  tented it with foil and left it in for another 15 minutes and got the cheese over 140F. After resting, the cheese was nice and melted and the pastry wasn't burnt.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: The honey and the sugar are totally unnecessary. Whilst cooking the red onions, you can add a tablespoon of balsamic vinegar which helps to caramelize the onions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This cries out for some meat–sausage perhaps (venison), or some lamb?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This pie was delicious and will definitely enter the rotation in our household. I found that the amount of filling was too much so only used 2/3 of the filling in order to get the 12in top piece of puff pastry to fit over it. The leftover filling made for a delicious veggie bowl that I ate with some cooked quinoa for lunch.  I waited 30 minutes for the pie to cool before cutting but the brie was still liquid at that point, so I recommend waiting at least 45 minutes so it stays together better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Use frozen veggies. They are precut, have more nutritional value, and can be defrosted in the microwave. Make life easier

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Make sure the Brie cheese you buy doesn't have animal rennet if you are vegetarian.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I cannot wait to make this! I think I’m the only one in my family who will like this, but I can do it “justice”.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this exactly as directed except that I used camembert (because I like the flavor better) and trimmed off 90% of the rind. It was delicious, impressive and fun to make/eat. Perfect for a 6-person dinner party, paired it with the fennel & beet salad linked below for a nice contrast of flavors and textures (and you can roast the beets while you have the oven on for the pie). https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1016820-fennel-beet-and-orange-salad-with-cumin-vinaigrette

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made a written for the first go and it was fabulous. I am vegetarian but my husband is not. We often eat separate meals because he thinks he can't get enough to eat with just veggies. That was not a problem here. Satisfying and savory enough to satisfy any appetite.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I love this recipe. 

It doesn't have to be round!  A rectangle looks good too, and you don't have to worry about trimming the dough.

It works beautifully with firm winter vegetables that can maintain their textures. It's also great with broccoli, and the sweetness of leeks cooked with butter and lemon zest.

If you haven't time to make the onion jam, pomegranate or cherry molasses dribbled over the cheese adds a sweet-sharp note.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: After reading some comments, made a rectangular version as part of Thanksgiving celebration. It was terrific. The combination of flavors made every bite a joy. Notes: Brushed the bottom layer of pastry with egg wash to keep it from getting soggy. Added a roasted sweet potato [roasted with olive oil, salt, pepper] to the veg mix. Made two lines of mozzarella & brie slices down the length of the pie & then added the onion jam.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can you freeze this? Does it have to be defrosted or can you cook it straight from the freezer?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I was thinking about doing the same, but I think the ratio of puff pastry to filling will prevent the filling from cooking fully by the time the puff pastry cooks. And if you lower the temp, the butter will run out of the puff pastry.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Laurie did you figure this out? Interested!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe, even though we changed some veggies around. My advice would be to cut the cheese into pieces, as otherwise, its centered only in the middle and doesn't distribute through the rest of the dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Use half of the Brussel sprouts, 1 tsp of honey/sugar and double the mushrooms

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I baked this in a 10 inch deep dish pie pan and put the larger sized pastry on the bottom and the smaller (10”) on top. I baked it at 400 degrees. I used local oyster mushrooms instead of shiitake. Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I took a lot of liberties with the recipe and it came out deliciously. I didn’t make the red onions “jammy” or let them cool before adding. I sautéed them without adding water while I roasted the sweet potatoes, mushrooms and brussel sprouts on a baking sheet. I used a 9 by 13 inch glass baking dish and cut the wheel of Brie up and spread across the top  based on other people’s notes. It was really pretty, delicious, and I managed to do it on a school night.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving. I made one that was onion and garlic free for a family member, and one as the recipe states. Gotta say that the jammy onions really made a difference. They work so perfectly with the Brie. I was also confused about the temp for the oven, but cooked them at 425 for 30 minutes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: the combination of flavors - and the sense that I'm cooking something mildly healthy - makes this recipe a winner. As a lazy nonbaker, I bypassed the puff pastry and freeform shaping parts of the directions, instead implementing 2 premade refrigerated pie disks. I baked the "pie" in an 8-inch square glass pan and followed the recommendations to cut the cheese into chunks. Came out great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: It truly does take 4 hours from the time you begin to make it to the time you put the first delicious bite in your mouth.  Making the vegetables beforehand is a great idea but no regrets here.  It tastes even better than it looks.  In fact, it's one of the best things I've ever eaten - and I'm a big foodie.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Pamela McLemore, haha, it took me nearly four hours just to cook the fillings, but I had to take a break cuz my feet were getting tired. 😂

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this yesterday (Christmas, 2024) for the second time using Dufour puff pastry and a really nice triple cream Brie (I did remove the rind and cube the cheese which then melted beautifully into the veg). It was a fabulous centerpiece for our family holiday. I do not understand why anyone would complain about the addition of a tablespoon of honey and brown sugar. The brown sugar makes the onions jammy, while the honey pops the flavor of the butternut.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Christmas mainly for me and my sister because we don’t eat meat, but in the end everyone had a piece of it, it was so good. I made the filling and jam the night before, i recommend that because it took forever to cut all the brussel sprouts… And i used sweet potato instead of pumpkin because i don’t like pumpkin, it was very good and complex. Will make again for sure!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: A question: Some in my family turn their nose up at mushrooms. Any thoughts on a substitute? Thanks

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: When we were kids my brother wouldn't eat onions. My mom would saute them, puree the cooked onions in the blender, and then add to spaghetti sauce or whatever. Sauted mushrooms have a similar kind of slippery texture to onions (which is what he didn't like) -- maybe this tactic would help?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made a personal version of this for vegetarian, gluten free, Christmas guests. Used Dufour plant based pastry, and vegan mozzarella between layers ( don’t think there is a vegan Brie yet) It was a success even with traditional eaters and I could imagine trying again like this. Vegetarian   gluten free centerpieces are hard to find so this was a win.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: It was very savory and delicious. I substituted broccoli florets in place of the brussels sprout, and cut the brie into slices which I used to form a layer over the first layer of the vegetables. While everything was delicious and savory, I felt it could have been cheesier. Next time, I will use two rounds of brie, or a round of brie and a round of camembert, and make a layer of vegetables, layer of cheese, layer of vegetables, layer of cheese, topped with the onion jam. Hopefully it works.

commentBody: is it okay to assemble this a day in advance?

recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Baking: I baked at 400 for about 35-40 minutes. Check at 35 and if the pastry is golden, you’re done.

I made this as directed, though didn’t bother trying for a circle and made it “rustic.” Everyone liked it, but I’m not sure I’d make it again. A bit awkward/messy to serve. The 7 oz Brie round seems to have sort of disappeared into the crowd of vegetables. The veg filling was immense. I couldn’t even fit it all in (will probably use the rest for a hash) but the flavor mix was really nice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as written up until the assembly stage. There was absolutely no way I could fit all the veggies and cheese inside the puff pastry as specified. I had maybe 2 cups of the veg mixture leftover. Absolutely delicious!

recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I scored the cheese and it still didn’t melt through, but I realized it’s because I didn’t bring the cheese to room temp first so it went in chilled.

I think next time, I would perhaps cut the rind off the cheese and then put the whole chunk in the middle in order to get the melted cheese effect in the picture.

Using  soft cheese without rind next time. Also just did a rectangle with a few slits i stop and was equally impressive. easy to make.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe.  We did do a couple changes.  Used hot honey, took the rind off of the brie and cut it into a few pieces.  Did not have to cool the onions as long as the recipe called for - and would also cook the onions first so they can cool while you cook remaining vegetables. We were very hungry, so cut into the pie after it rested for about 15 minutes.  Was perfect!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. It took a while but you could make the veggie mixture beforehand. Kept the leftovers in the fridge and were delicious the next day heated up in the microwave! I used a very mild Brie but I would recommend a sharper one because the flavor was completely drowned out by the vegetables. Overall, everything was amazing and everyone ate more than one slice!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I'm sorry, but this recipe is OTT (over the top.) Why so much ....  stuff inside the puff pastry? I'd pare it right down to just the cheese, and serve the vegetables and/or a simple green salad on the side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This is a vegetable pie, not a cheese pie.  Nothing over the top. Vegetarians don't want carbs and cheese alone.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Take the rind off. I cut off the sides only and it melted in great, but the top and bottom rinds were still there and a bit weird as far as texture. Next time I will just slice the whole rind off.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is a bit of work, so I looked through the Notes to see how others fared.  Just 7 commenters in the "Helpful" column containing 40 comments had actually made the recipe, and the rest were personal opinions and rhetoric (not useful).  It was a great pie, though I did not succeed with the attractive pattern on the finished product.  Like Marie, I couldn't use all the vegetables.  My brie melted perfectly.  Joy's experience with non-melting brie:  perhaps yours was a bit underripe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: It took 3 1/2 hours from beginning to tossing it in the oven, then 45 to bake and nearly an hour to rest, but I have to admit that it was worth every minute. I did all my gram conversions very carefully (ahem, NYTC) and followed the recipe closely, except that my squash gave more like 6 cups than 4 so I had filling left over and my puff was a bit scant in places. But for Veggy Thanksgiving, I highly recommend. Even Very Picky Husband was impressed, and that doesn't happen often.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Oh, I made a salad with thinly sliced raddichio, beans, garlic, a hard-cooked egg, and feta cheese, with pumpkin seed oil and balsamic dressing, and its slight bitterness paired very well with the sweetness of the pie. Just sayin.'

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Planned on taking this to Thanksgiving dinner, so made a test pie with left over veggies and puff pastry.  I didn't have an extra wheel of camembert or brie, so used smoked gouda that I had on hand.  It turned out great and was absolutely delicious!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this today and simply followed the recipe. This is a very lovely savory dish. Everyone loved it including me. I did make my vegetable’s the day before and the onion jam. It made the recipe a breeze.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this for my vegetarian son for his main Thanksgiving dish, and it was a hit! I took the advice of cutting a cross on top of the cheese rind, and it worked great! I held my breath cutting out the first piece, and I was so thrilled when the cheese oozed out just like in the picture! The flavors are so wonderful—it’s really a showstopper!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Yes: the recipe is sketchy on the temp to bake it.

I tried 425, but watched it carefully and it started to brown to quickly so I turned it down to 375, and that allowed the cheese to melt without charring the pastry.

Also, deleted the mushrooms and added more squash sprouts, and a little more onion.  It was a real crowd pleaser, so the recipe is pretty resilient, I think.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Did anyone test this recipe exactly as written? If I used a 10” pastry circle, the tower of veggies and brie was about 8” high.  I used the weights of veggies in the recipe and followed everything to the letter.  I had to redo it with a 16” pastry circle, which got kind of thin, so I plugged up the wholes with leaves made from extra pastry.  I can’t imagine how the whole thing is supposed to fit between two 10” pastry circles.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this for a dinner party and it was a hit. I did super thin slices of brie wedged in the filling as the comments suggested and it got perfectly incorporated with the vegetables. Other than that made no changes and it was so worth the effort!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving for my vegetarian husband, by far his favorite Thanksgiving dish ever. I used Trader Joe's puff pastry (only available during the holidays) it is made with butter and by far my favorite pre-made puff pastry. I also used already peeled and cubed squash which cuts down on the work involved. I think the small amount of honey boosted the flavor of the squash, it did not seem overly sweet. The layering of the different ingredients is key, as are the fresh herbs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Serving for guests tonight. Trial run yesterday turned out great. Took 90 min for the cooking, then there was wait time for cool before assembly & bake, and after cooking, which accounts for about 3h30m to 4h total. 
Delicious taste but a bit dry bc the cheese remained clumped and hadn’t seeped through the filling. Baked at 400F/40 min. Will research more on melting cheese and re-post if it goes better second time around.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this with Miyoko's plant milk cheese (lemon rosemary limited edition, don't know how long it will be available) and non-dairy puff pastry to test for vegan guests over the holidays. It's great! Like many have suggested, next time I'll cut the cheese into smaller parts and spread around the whole pie, because it pretty much stayed in the middle. Half of the vegetables were enough to fill the crust entirely. And the pie held together beautifully when cut.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I cut the rind off the side of the camembert wheel and it oozed very satisfactorily all over the pie.  Absolutely delicious but as many commentors suggested, you can skip the sugar and the honey, it is very sweet and delcious on its own.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Worth the effort. For those with no experience using puff pastry or pie making - like me - just follow the instructions and take your time. I don't have a pie plate so i just used a dinner plate and a ruler to get the proper dimensions for the top and bottom crusts. The only modification I made was cutting the side rind off the brie. I served this to vegetarians, and they loved it. A triumph!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: We had this last evening, made according to the recipe, and it was fabulous.  Yes, I agree with others, the cheese didn't melt as expected.  When I make it again I will cut the cheese into 6 triangular pieces and arrange it in a star formation in the center.  I will also arrange bake it on a rack placed in a shallow pan, as the bottom crust was pretty tough to cut.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent and worth the time and effort for a special occasion. I agree with others who recommend rolling both top and bottom crust to at least 12 inches to accommodate the filling. It won't work otherwise. I baked this on a round (pizza) baking sheet and used a serrated bread knife to cut it, and both worked fine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Followed the directions to the letter, which I normally don't do, I must say that it was absolutely delicious. There was filling leftover, so I made pasties with it to have another time. 
Yum!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this for thanksgiving, without the cheese (plenty of cheese on our thanksgiving table already!). It was a HIT! I got large mushrooms and didn’t cut them smaller which let their meaty texture shine. I also always start cooking my mushrooms just in water - really brings out the flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: super rich! not quite the best main dish but super yummy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as directed and it was a major hit! My 15-year old had three helpings - if that doesn’t get a five star review, nothing does!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: One of the best pies I ever cooked and ate. Spectacular!! The only note is that I see no reason to roast the butter squash, stir frying it gets the same result. Also, I used GF puff pastry and would use it again as it is not as soft and elastic as the regular doe. Fantastic recipe!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Marina H. Please share where you found GF Puff Pastry, and brand? Thank you!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Was quite skeptical of this recipe going in but needed a vegan appetizer for my Thanksgiving table, so I removed the cheese. Still turned out amazing

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: There were questions about oven temperature for the final bake. We did 400 F for 40 minutes (for about 9 inch pies) and they came out well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Surprised at the lack of enthusiasm in other reviews — we found it delicious! Used Camembert instead of Brie which possibly made the difference. This was a test run for Thanksgiving and happy to say I’ll be making it again in a couple of days!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
This was the centerpiece for our vegetarian TG last year. It will be back on the table tomorrow by popular demand! Used 2 sheets of Dufour and combination of brie and camembert (cut in small strips or cubes). As many have mentioned in the comments: be bold and go forth! I coated the bottom Dufour sheet with harissa (nice bite!) and added onion jam toward the top of the heap.
Carbs, fat, salt... what could go wrong?!! Happy Eating!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was tasty and enjoyable, but it was a lot of effort for not the most spectacular result. In particular I felt that a lot of the vegetables merged together, and I could not taste the distinct flavors well enough to make all the different mechanisms for preparing them seem worthwhile. no problems at all with the recipe just not sure it’s worth it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: A big showy hit with the vegetarians at Thanksgiving.  The vegs could be made ahead of time, final assembly was quick.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous! I followed several suggestions and cut the rind off the sides of the Brie round and it melted well. Next time I will score the top and bottom rinds to see if it melts more because there will definitely be many more times making this amazing dish for my veggie-loving family!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I had this at a friend's house true to form, and then I used the veggie mix in an egg bake that was a hit. I'll continue to use the veggie mix for other applications.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Where is the cheese? It’s not listed with the ingredients

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Yes it is...read thru the assembly. It is the last item

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Spectacular. Have made it multiple times, occasionally substituting broccoli for Brussels sprouts, experimenting with different mushrooms, etc. There's just no way to go wrong - this is a 5 star vegetarian recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I finally made this and came to the decision that I don't care for squash and melted cheese.  It's too rich of a combination.  With all the steps, baking and wait time, saving your appetite for the first velvety bite- it didn't add up for me.  I prefer Dorie Greenspan's Everything Good Baked in a Pumpkin recipe from Around my French Table, for a savory squash entree.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions for substituting the puff pastry for something Gluten-Free? Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Gigi I purchased Schaffer GF puff pastry in the grocery store freezer section. It was not real flaky, but still very good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Gigi dufour makes gluten free puff pastry

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: We made this at Christmas because of a vegetarian family member. It turned out to be absolutely delicious and everyone loved it as well as being the first time in my life I have ever enjoyed eating brussels sprouts!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Have made this twice, both times it was great.  If you love mushrooms, this is a must try.  I've followed the recipe exactly, it was all good.   perfect dish for vegetarians.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I liked that the veggies could be cooked in advance and the assembly method was clever and produced a very beautiful dish. However the actual end product was underwhelming. I didn’t think the veggies had a great flavor or texture once all mixed together in the pie (just too much going on) and the brie just got rubbery rather than melty and didn’t merge at all with the rest of the fillings. I think I’d reuse this assembly method but probably select completely different fillings.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this for one holiday meal and the following year teenagers asked for it again. A lot of work but delicious and really beautiful on the table. Room for tweaking here and there as needed - you know what to do.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Ah, the cheese? how much???

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: As stated in the recipe: "1whole (7- or 8-ounce) round Brie or Camembert."

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Sounds yummy. Question - would you cut the rind off the top and/or bottom of the cheese so it would melt into the veggies, or leave it on to keep it as a separate “layer?”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Do you cook the Brie cheese in the rind? I don’t eat the rind when I eat Brie. I am assuming including the rind help the structure?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Should I cut a couple of vents in the top to release steam?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, but definitely NOT vegetarian with 8 oz of animal fat-containing cheese in it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: It's not vegan, but cheese, which comes from living animals, is absolutely vegetarian provided it does not contain animal rennet.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made without cheese? Will the ingredients hold together? Thank you.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Would this work in a round pie plate rather than on a baking sheet? Might be harder to cut / serve, but seems like it would be easier to piece it together.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Do you need to cut any little vents in the top of the pastry to let steam escape?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: It looks good but I question the Nutritional Information. Firstly, the photo makes it a 6 serving pie and the nutritional info is based on 11 slices!!, so you can double or triple the fat %. There is oddly no cholesterol info and with all that brie....expect a lot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Umm- I can't do mushrooms- but I lean vegetarian- so I'm constantly looking for a good substitution for mushrooms in veg dishes. Usually I just leave them out and add more of the other ingredients. Anyone have a great substitution suggestion? I'm also not a great cook- I don't know things. I can follow a recipe but often find myself texting chef friends going "what the f does this mean?" So simple substitutions would be great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: You could leave them out and it probably wouldn’t suffer, or substitute another savory vegetable like eggplant, more onions, or more Brussels sprouts/cabbage/brocollini.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this with regular pie crusts instead of puff pastry?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can you cook this in a pie pan or baking dish instead of free form?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: The addition of brown sugar, honey, soy sauce, and shiitakes remind of delicious East Asian flavors, which is lovely in a homey pie like this. For those with criticisms (on the sweeteners in particular), pause to  consider the palettes outside your own norms.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with a gluten free pie crust instead of gluten puff pastry?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I read this recipe through looking for what type of dish to in which place the puff pastry- reading through the comments it looks like it’s mad free form?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Onions are off the menu. Can someone suggest a substitute for the onion jam?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Perhaps cranberry sauce/chutney

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Is any harm done to texture or flavor if it is baked 3-4 hours before the meal? I assume it could not be reheated? (I'd like to try this for TG but have a two hour drive and then an hour before dining).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Great. Really not that hard with puff pastry from the store. The onion jam was not that distinctive from the rest of the veg but the Brie was- very yummy. Pretty easy and pretty veg centerpiece.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this for a family birthday brunch. It was beautiful and delicious! Next time I’ll either assemble it with freshly cooked and still very warm fillings, or be sure to reheat the fillings if cooked in advance and refrigerated. And I’ll use a slightly warmed Brie round that’s either scored or cut into smaller pieces. Though it was an amazing entree and the pastry browned perfectly, the Brie didn’t melt. The recipe doesn’t specify what temp for baking so I did the usual for baking, 350F.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
R :  See "Step 1 - heat oven to 425 degrees"
That'll melt the Brie!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Making this weekend for vegetarians (not vegan). Making exactly the way it’s written except for two small tweaks after reading comments. I’m slicing the top runs off the Brie to aid in melting adding a few dried cranberries for added color. How can it be bad? Happy Thanksgiving everyone.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and impressive…how to turn veggies into a spectacular main dish! I wouldn’t bother making it round. A rectangular pie would taste just as good. I don’t know how you would fit all rose uveggies into a 10 inch round.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with pie dough instead of pastry crust?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I am sure I saw a picture of this recipe that included a blob of cranberry sauce on top of the brie. Your thoughts? Good or bad idea?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: That's the caramelized onion!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: If this is Thanksgiving entree, what works well for sides?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: OK, I've got it all assembled 26 hours before Thanksgiving dinner. Now what? Par-bake it, bake it completely now or bake it tomorrow?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Do you think the crust would hold up if baked 4 hours before eating (but warmed in oven right before eating?)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Have not started cooking this yet, but I was dreading all the prep. Was able to find a 1 lb bag of frozen sliced Brussel sprouts, and also cubed squash, so happy to proceed without some of the hands-on work

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: So so so so good. Wow

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I think adding a touch of honey or a jam (apricot, marmalade, or fig) really adds to a baked Brie. 
I can’t wait to try this recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Took 30+ minutes for the onions to get jammy, cut the rind off the brie per other comments and it melted in great. Baked at 425. Made a double batch of the filling because that was a lot of effort. But. Absolutely delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: To make this truly Thanksgiving main, is gravy a plus?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this last weekend, used camembert instead of brie. It was phenomenally tasty and I will definitely make this again. However I do recommend to pair this with a light salad or something of the sort, because it is, to no surprise at all, quite heavy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Aaarrgghh! My phyllo is tearing!!! Any suggestions?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Big hit as a Thanksgiving main dish in lieu of turkey! I prepped all the veggies and onion jam the day before. After reading some comments I sliced off only the top layer of rind on the Brie, and had the perfect layer of cheese “goo” throughout. Tastes great with cranberry sauce, and might even substitute that for the onion jam next time!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Hi All,
In order to get the cheese to melt, score it deeply with the rind on, the way you would for any Brie en croute. And yes to everyone's else's adaptation to this recipe very customizable. I followed the recipe with the exception of adding leeks because I was out of shallots, and filling out the Brussels sprouts, with a bit of spinach and a zucchini because I did not have enough on hand. This was the vegetarian Thanksgiving main dish for my vegetarian husband.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this as written for a Thanksgiving side because it incorporates so many of my favorite Thanksgiving flavors. It came out delicious--moist inside, flaky crust, lovely golden color with the egg wash. Make sure to rotate it in the oven, if your oven, like mine, cooks better from one side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly per the recipe minus the mushrooms and added artichoke to the Brussels instead. Absolutely stellar, can’t recommend enough.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Followed the instructions exactly and it turned out perfectly!! The perfect thing to replace turkey on our thanksgiving table. Might become a yearly tradition, but I doubt we’ll wait that long to make it again! :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was good, but not amazing. I think 425 is too hot, so the puff pastry cooked before the vegetables got hot enough to fully melt the cheese (and I took the rind off), but it was a hit at Thanksgiving. Used the filling to make one regular, and a smaller one with gluten free pastry dough. Probably won’t make it again, but a fun twist!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe this afternoon. I created it following the steps and ingredients as written. Only one change - I substituted a variety of on-hand root vegetables to use in place of the butternut squash. I did bake it at 425. Great suggestion from another reader: put slits on top of the brie (I scored it like a pie before putting the red onion “jam” on top,) The cheese spread evenly. The caramelized red onions should not be skipped! A showstopper! To save time, do some steps days in advance.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I took the advice of a few people and took the rind off of the Brie, cut the Brie in half, and then made squares and put it in between the layers. Well it disappeared sadly. Next time I will do as the recipe says and bake the entire round as it says, or if I cut it lengthwise in half, I will leave the rind on. I added some acid - Lemon zest and a squish of fresh lemon to the veg mixture.  I also toasted Walnuts (for crunch) with a pinch (tsp) of Red Pepper flakes (for heat) & added. Was Yummy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was disappointing- the Brie would have been better swapped with a sharp cheddar and the thinly sliced Brussels sprouts lost their flavor in fine chopping.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Turned out really well! Was a hit with the guests.
I went low with cheese and it still turned out pretty well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe for our Thanksgiving table this year, and it was good but not as much of a showstopper as I was hoping for. It felt like it was missing a textural component; perhaps it needed more mushrooms to add a meaty chew, or some chopped walnuts or something. We enjoyed it enough, but I don’t think I’ll be making it again next year, not for Thanksgiving anyway. With some adaptations to the texture, this would still be lovely to serve at a dinner party or a nice family dinner though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Found it over done with the rosemary.  Cheese really didn't do much for me.  Liked the idea, but wasn't over the moon on the outcome.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Came out looking beautiful but it tasted just OK.   It just wasn’t very flavorful.  Maybe I didn’t season enough. I used Bella rather than Shiitake mushrooms which maybe contributed.  Tips for people making this: you can slice the Brussels sprouts in the food processor with the slicing blade.  Also make small cubes for the butternut squash … maybe 2cm… would make assembly easier.  I roasted the squash for the time recommended … but maybe not long enough to bring out flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Wasn’t going to do a Thanksgiving spread until I was bewitched by this recipe. I couldn’t stop thinking about it. So glad I made it! Pretty much as per recipe - but the brand of puff pastry I had was not quite big enough to encapsulate all those veggies (or I could have been bolder in my rolling), resulting in a higher cheese to veg ratio than intended. Still delicious - I didn’t hesitate with the salt and pepper at each step. Delighted to have a showy vegetarian “ta-da!”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe and the filling was really excellent, although I ended up with a lot of extra. Has anyone tried it with a different cheese? I found the brie a little bland (maybe some gruyere would be good?). Otherwise a really good vegetarian centerpiece recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
One of the best recipes I have made so far from NYT - Cooking. I had frozen discs of homemade pie dough so subbed for puff pastry, and made into a 9" pie. Subbed yam for the squash and sautéed in a fry pan until al dente. Was a little careful with salt, and was just right. Also, I cut the Brie wheel into segments and distributed evenly. A rich, flavourful dish that I will make again. Family raved.
I agree with KDW's comment that recipes aren't sacred. I tweak recipes all the time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it was the star of the show. So delicious that the vegetarians at the table had to guard it from the meat eaters, who also loved the depth of flavor enhanced by the brie. Next year, I will make two -- one that's vegetable-only and another that incorporates roast chicken. I did have the amount of brussel sprouts and divided the cheese into six pieces so they could better spread throughout the pie. A keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it was the star of the show. So delicious that the vegetarians at the table had to guard it from the meat eaters, who also loved the depth of flavor enhanced by the brie. Next year, I will make two -- one that's vegetable-only and another that incorporates roast chicken. I did have the amount of brussel sprouts and divided the cheese into six pieces so they could better spread throughout the pie. A keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Truly outstanding! Served as the star of a charcuterie board so guests could graze for “lunch” as Thanksgiving dinner is served in the evening. I substituted broccoli for the Brussels sprouts because we were already having sprouts with dinner. Cooked the veggies and onion jam the day before so all I had to do was assemble. Took the top rind off the Brie- left the sides and bottom rinds in tact. Amazing!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this as the star of a charcuterie board for “lunch” on Thanksgiving as we eat dinner in the evening. Took the top rind off the Brie leaving the sides and bottom in tact. Substituted broccoli for the Brussels sprouts as we were having sprouts with dinner. Prepared the veggies and onion jam the day before so all I had to do was assemble. Delicious and a beautiful presentation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: As others have experienced, this was the star of our Thanksgiving dinner and there were only two of us! Made as directed, with excellent results, but next time I’ll divide up the Brie, I think that’s a good suggestion. Also, I used the 1 mm blade on my Cuisinart to slice the Brussels sprouts and ended up with too much volume there, so left some out. In any event, a home cook, not a professional, can achieve a most satisfying result! Labor intensive but worth the effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is nuts, completely over the top delicious. It is very rich and has me questioning how I will safely eat all the leftovers ;)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  The downside is the amount of time it takes to prep, so I did it a few days in advance.  There is also a LOT of filling, so I ended up making it in an oval Corningware dish (8x10).  I followed a suggestion of cutting up the Brie, so it must have gotten absorbed into the vegetables since it didn't ooze out.  The 425 seems like the correct temperature.  No need to cut slits in the top crust like you would a regular pie.  Recommend!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving and it was delicious! Thank you, Alexa! I put a removable bottom from a 10" tart pan underneath it on the baking sheet to make it easier to maneuver onto the serving platter. The tart pan bottom also made a perfect template for the dough. I substituted a mixture of aged Gouda and Gruyere instead and it was great. Next time, I'll mix the shredded cheeses in with the vegetables, as the cheese stayed in one place and didn't mingle the way the brie would have.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was so delicious! I had success with decreasing the prep time by enlisting my various processor blades. This will be on the next dinner party table!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic, just fantastic! I added some roasted sweet potatoes to the veggie mix, and I would say in the end I only ended up using about half of the roasted veg in the pie, but I'm not mad about having leftovers of that! Other than making up my own veggie mix I followed this recipe to the letter, and it was brilliant! I offered this up to the vegetarians at our Thanksgiving dinner and it was popular with ALL non-meat and meat eaters alike. Those jammy onions and the oozy brie?  Zero leftovers.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: We made this for Thanksgiving and it was delicious. Be careful the top of the pie doesn’t burn in the hot oven. Once it’s brown a sheet of tinfoil on top is a good idea.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was the "great success" main for my vegetarian children for our expat delayed weekend Thanksgiving.  Had to swap the order on the brown sugar and honey. (Local honey being too thick.  The puff pastry I found was Gluten free, so I followed suit on the soy sauce.  With four celiac guests, this made them happy.  6 of the guests ask for the recipe. Note, I followed the guidance for the reference recipe and removed the rind from the top of the cheese.  The cheese melted as desired.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. I was surprised at the flavor it packed. We found the squash a little too prominent. Next time I would remove one cup and do more mushrooms and onions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this for the first time and WOW does it have all of the makings of an outstanding dish to impress guests. I need to refine my technique and check my ratios of veg to cheese. The Brie seemed to disappear, but I expected it to ooze out after letting it sit. Coincidentally I watched an episode of the Great British Baking Show tonight where one challenge was to make a Dauphinois Pithivier that looked very similar. Could this recipe be a version of a Pithivier?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: All in all a whole lot of work, and the outcome was rather disappointing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this and ended up with tons of extra filling. Threw the leftovers over chickpea pasta with more Brie. So so good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Yes, nice recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This is a perfect savory vegetarian dish for a fall/winter holiday meal! It combines a lot of our favorite veggies and herbs (so what’s not to like). And the puff pastry seals the deal with added texture and an elegant presentation. Just allow plenty of time for the prep.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving it was …amazing. Followed the recipe with two additions. I added chopped carrots because I had them (sautéed aldente). I also added cashew cream. This will be in the regular rotation especially for holidays!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Made this tonight without cheese or eggs
Delicious. 
Layer of fried cubes of silken tofu between the vegetable layers. 
Oat milk glaze worked well on pastry

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This stole the show at thanksgiving! I thinly sliced the squash and layered it at the bottom of the pie (followed by other veg layers) to give it layered look upon slicing. Will make again as beautiful vegetarian main.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Excellent as an elegant appetizer or dinner alongside tossed arugula. So lovely!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this to bring to Friendsgiving and it was a huge hit with vegetarians and omnivores!  Substituted broccoli for Brussels sprouts. Sprinkled some salt flakes on top before baking. Highly recommend!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was FANTASTIC. The only change I made was scoring the brie and adding some broccoli to the Brussel sprouts. I will make it again and again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: If you double the recipe and use a 2 lb whole brie, how much puff pastry do you need and how long should it be baked?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Also, the brie did not melt…

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This pie was very tasty. This first timer made the crust too thin on top which caused a minor melt down towards the end of the cooking time. Baked at 425 for 40 min. Either oven too hot or cut the cook time. It was a hit with the guests even with a fissure running down the middle.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Amazingly delicious. made as written with a slight modification of using hot honey on the squash. next time, May add a pinch of cayenne pepper. really like that you can prep all the veg in advance. Just make sure to bring it to room temperature before assembly. Also popped the assembled tart in the fridge for a quick chill while the oven heated.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: 1st: this tastes really good! 2nd: it’s a real time-sink, so be prepared. 3rd: I used an 11x11 inch square of puff pastry on the bottom and it was still barely possible to pile the ingredients on top (my top crust was more like 12x12). 4th: after 40 min. at 425 and waiting for 20 minutes, the cheese was barely melty. Even my spouse who likes his food cooler than I do thought it needed to be warmer. Note to self: avoid gluten-free puff pastry dough. Glad I tried it; probably won’t do it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was quite a lot of work, requiring a good 4 hours and $40. For all the effort involved & premier ingredients I expected the result to be glorious. The stuffed pastry was good, but not outstanding. En fin, this wasn't worth the cost or the time involved. In my repertoire there are numerous other recipes that are much tastier & memorable (not to mention less time-consuming, more economical and healthier than this one). NYT recipes are becoming a regular disappointment. Mo

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried adding Cherry tomatoes?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Christmas eve and it was a hit. Made the veggie mix and the onion jam the night before and then assembled and baked day of. I wish I had salted the separate components a little more generously (I thought I was at the time) but the flavors were great! I did cut my brie in half and score the sides to help the cheesy goodness. The red onions were the star of the bites, so feel free to up the amounts.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I had a lot of leftover vegetable filling because I could not fit all of it on the pastry while leaving a 1 inch margin. I would have preferred to have more vegetables in the pastry than what I ended up with. Trying one of the alternatives suggested - like making it in a pan - may be better to accommodate the volume of vegetables. 

I baked it for 40 minutes at 400 degrees and let it sit for 45 minutes. That worked well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Followed this recipe almost exactly and it was a HUGE hit at Christmas. My only issue was cook temp and time for the pie. I lowered the temp to 400 and it was dark brown after only about 25 minutes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: As it happened, I only had about a cup and a half of Brussels sprouts. I went with that, leaving everything eke the same. I thought the balance was just right and I’ll do the same next time. My Brie came as a wedge so I cut off the rind and then cut it into about 8 triangles which I arranged into a circle. Scattering them would have been fine too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe as written but it was strangely flavourless. Looked good though

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I was super excited to make this recipe and had been drooling over the photo for a few weeks! I made it for New Years Eve dinner with family and although it looked beautiful, it was surprisingly not as flavorful as expected. Followed it to a T but very disappointing and will not make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful recipe as is. We used 9" round with rind and it was great. Wonder how it would be with chicken added. My husband insists on animal protein.  Anybody try that?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Show stopper center piece for Thanksgiving, excellent flavors. Definitely remove all the rind off the brie. Will make again. Cook veggies the day before. Re sugar: use honey or palm sugar, the latter is not as sweet as regular sugar, more subtle. Or, don't use any sweetener.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Very bland and too much work. Won’t be making again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Try adding herbs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Wow - spectacular! Looked & tasted fantastic; very dramatic slicing it for dinner guests! Can taste each component with none too strong.
Made veggies & onion day before-highly recommend. Only change was omitting mushrooms/soy sauce part as I am allergic. Trimmed all the rind off the cheese, so melted & blended with everything. Cooked for 55 minutes as everything was cold to start; don't be afraid to get it nice & brown. Waited 45 minutes to slice-was hot but not at all runny-perfect!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Generally tasty, but all of the flavors get muddled together - could have saved time and cooked all the veggies together. I would also recommend slicing the cheese and scattering throughout. The wheel in the middle sounds fun, but when serving/ eating slices, its a bit of a mess and naturally, the cheese is not evenly dispersed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This was a terrific vegetarian alternative to turkey. Really delicious and earthy in the best way possible. My husband the vegetarian loved it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: This is great, I think there are definitely ways this could be shortened/simplified, but it's delicious if you are willing to spend 2-3 hours hands on.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: The flavors just didn't work for me. I like Brie and I like roasted vegetables,  but the combination was challenging. Perhaps others used a better cheese than I have access toth

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I added some acid to the vegetables once they were all cooked and mixed (lemon juice & the zest and juice of a clementine), and it really punched up the flavors. The onion jam was perfect to offset the rich Brie, though like a few others my Brie didn’t melt throughout. Next time I’ll score the rind like someone suggested. Delicious and a definite crowd pleaser at my office’s Friendsgiving!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Quite good. Cut the recipe in half and made two turnovers instead which was enough for several servings. I still had filling leftover even though I axed the mushrooms altogether and replaced the Brussels with broccoli. I cut up the Brie and had no problems with melting. Very tasty, the jam is a nice touch!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: My 16 yo daughter and I prepped the veggies on Wednesday and then baked yesterday for our vegetarian main course for Thanksgiving. We loved it!!!! Leftovers today are excellent too. Def a new tradition.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can someone suggest a non-cruciferous vegetable to sub for the brussels sprouts?  Trying to satisfy a guest...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @BG how about Dino kale stems removed and thinly chopped

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Broccoli or cauliflower

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: @BG leeks would be a fantastic addition!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: So i made this and it was delicious except the bottom of the puff pastry burned … any tips for keeping the bottom from burning when you cook it at such a high heat?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I agree with another post that said 10 inches is too small for bottom round.  I had much more slippery filling than I could get piled up and still keep a 1-inch border (and I measured the fillings by weight when I prepped them).  So I piled as much as I could and put the rest aside.  Would try making base a 12 inch round in the future.  Another idea I thought of was to build the whole thing upside down in a bowl lined with parchment, then invert onto the baking pan.  May give that a try next time.  Dish is DELICIOUS by the way, don’t want to leave that out.  Will def make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Prepared for Christmas 2024.  Guests loved it, especially the jammy onions.  The cheese only partially melted even though I removed the rind from the sides.  I think the cheese is too insulated by the veggies to fully melt.  Next time I might partially cook/warm the cheese before assembling the pie.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I have now made this recipe twice, and first I will point out that 35 minutes prep time <> be possible in a professional kitchen (just) but it is a gross underestimate in my competent-but-far-from-professional one. Be aware. Forewarned is forearmed. Plan accordingly. Secondly, I highly recommend using a pie pan. Skip the freeform convolutions. And thirdly, I used my own recipe for pie dough and was delighted to skip the puff. Otherwise, a fabulous recipe. 5 stars.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I made this a few years ago as a centerpiece and it turned out great. Decided to adapt it as an appetizer this year and loved it even more. 
I followed the same assembly order (brussels, cheese, jam) and folded it in individual puff pastry rectangles. Huge hit at the party. I ended up with a lot of leftover vegetables so I’ll adjust the amount of brussel sprouts next time, but overall the recipe was super adaptable and will probably make it like this moving forward.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Made this for the vegetarians at Thanksgiving last year. Such a big hit I'm making it again this year.

A few people made comments about the brie not melting well so I cut the top of the rind off and put it gooey side down on the vegetables in step 8 and then the full piece of on top of that. It was a much appreciated save.

This year, I'm going to cut the brie in half and do the same... one gooey side down, other piece on top, gooey side up.

Other than that, this recipe is ready as is.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: The store was out of puff pastry so I ended up with phyllo dough instead. I used 8 sheets, lightly brushing each layer with butter and crisscrossed each layer like a plus sign. Then the veggies, cheese, veggies surrounding the cheese and onion jam on top. Last, I just folded it all up and tried to pleat the phyllo dough into a decent looking top, brushed the top with butter and put in oven. It was all good and my family ate it all up and asked me to keep in the rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I used asparagus cut into 1/2 inch pieces instead of the Brussels sprouts and I really liked how they tasted would recommend. I also added juice from 1/2 an orange to the onion jelly to give it some brightness. Overall I really loved this recipe and will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I always find the rind on brie a little bitter, so I shave most of it off with a vegetable peeler before adding.  It melted right in with the vegetables and was fantastic.  Otherwise, I followed the directions but did make the top puff pastry round bigger once I saw how tall the mound of veggies was.  I baked for 40 and cooled for 40.  I have limited experience with puff pastry dough and didn't end up with the prettiest presentation ever; nonetheless, it was delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Made it for the vegetarians last Thanksgiving (I always want there to be an entrée for vegetarians at my table.) Huge hit.

This year I subbed Camembert for the Brie. Did not like it.

The Camembert overpowers the dish!

Oh, and I shaved and glazed a beet and put in a layer of that. Very nice addition!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I read all the comments and I changed vegetables to suit my 14 yr old vegetarian’s taste (no mushrooms or butternut squash - added broccoli and chayote and carrots and walnuts).  It was very meh.  I REALLY wanted to like it and I was very excited about it, but even with all the seasonings (and I followed the directions exactly) the veggies were all so-so and you couldn’t taste the Camembert which didn’t melt well (I scored the top and peeled off the rind around the outside, leaving top and bottom).  The bottom of the pastry also burnt - 425 was too hot for the parchment paper.  I did have fun making it but was definitely not a great return on the effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: A flavorful feast!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: A friend of my daughter's brought this to a Thanksgiving Day dinner last year and then we duplicated it at Christmas when my other daughter and husband came to town.  Fantastic.  I made the puff pastry and we partially cut through the camembert (instead of brie).  Ooey gooey cheesy wonderful veggie centerpiece.  We are doing it again this year but with store-bought puff pastry.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: 35 minutes prep time?? Im on hour 2 and havent even started on the pastry part.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: It’s very hard to believe that a serving is only 2 grams sat fat.  I feel like I took in 15 grams just reading the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Amazing recipe! I made it for Christmas and I will keep it as a special dish from now on. Complements to Alexa, this is spectacular!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: That's a LOT of veggies. I couldn't fit them all into the pastry.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: I cut down on prep time by combining the squash and brussel sprouts in a big pot with all the shallots and garlic and cook those down, adding brown sugar and balsamic at the end. Then I do the mushrooms and onions together. Combine the two and assemble on your pastry dough. Still a lot of cutting but I find the flavour is similar and prep time more manageable. 10/10 appetizer that I always make when we have dinners with any folks who are vegetarian.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Followed the recipe as is. Was fun to prepare and cook with two friends. Between the chopping, and carmelizing the onions, theres something for everyone to do. The assembly was also exciting and simple. 

TIP: Cit your brie into chunks and spread around the pie for better cheese distribution.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody: Making this for New Year's Eve dinner party.  It's a fabulous, versatile recipe, and you can't go wrong with whatever veg you use. Tonight, it's being served over polenta (I'm tired of puff pastry). Made the filling yesterday, put it into a lightly oiled Le Creuset braiser and dotted with brie. The beauty of this is: bulk of the dish is already done and will be in the oven during cocktails, so the only time I spend at the stove will be to whip together the polenta.  Perfect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Baked Brie and Caramelized Vegetable Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Maybe it was me but as a really experienced cook I rarely have disasters and this was epic. I used my scale to make sure the weights were correct but I had way too much filling (which I saved and will be delicious alone). I scored the brie but it did not melt. I had the oven at the given temp and cooked it for 40 minutes.

It came out very dark and I can't say there was any puff to the pastry (all I could get on New Year's Eve was a store brand) It was just very disappointing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: If you saute the shrimp, and then bake them for 15 minutes (or until the feta is softened), won't they be overcooked and tough?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I'd like to echo this - seems too hot for too long, especially for medium shrimp.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: @Shari. I sautéed the shrimp to nearly done, they were coated in paprika, salt, and chili powder, and then added back in the last five minutes of the 15 minute bake to avoid that issue.  I subbed capers for the olives as well.  Turned out amazing!  Will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: How can you cook shrimp for 15 minutes after they’re already sautéed? They will be overcooked! Put them in the sauce a couple of minutes before serving.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Could also add fennel

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
