commentBody: This sounds like it would be fabulous with squid. I would skip putting the squid in the oven in the last step, though. It could toughen. You need to soften the feta and heat the olives, but then folding in the sautéed squid would do it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: As much as I love cast iron, the acid in the tomatoes means that you can taste the iron in the food, so I have avoided cooking tomatoes in cast iron.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Make this with chicken tenderloins.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Since all the comments that worried about the shrimp being baked too long did not appear to be by people who had actually cooked the recipe, I made the recipe as written (15 minutes in the oven) and the shrimp turned out fine and was like you would expect in jambalaya or paella. I served it on a bed of baby spinach which wilted under the heat and bread.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: The word "saganaki" actually means "little frying pan" and refers to a type of pan for cooking on top of the stove, particularly for flaming cheese or other dishes This recipe is close to how it's made in Greece, but it is  not finished in the oven.  Oregano is used rather than thyme

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I have been making a different version of this recipe where green onions are sauteed in the olive oil and parsley is added to the tomato mixture along with dry white wine (1/2 cup) (which replaces the ouzo).  Raw shrimp are layered with the sauce and more parsley added on top before putting it in the oven with the feta cheese added on top.  Oven Temp is 500 degrees and it is seven with crusty bread, rather than toast. It is called "Garthes Yiouvetsi" in Greek.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: No need to bake this at all!  I lived in Athens years ago and always enjoyed this dish - both homemade and restaurant-made - as a stovetop preparation.  Sautéing and then baking shrimp for nearly 20 minutes will turn them into something unpleasant.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: As often happens w/ NYT Cooking, this recipe is underspiced. My go-to saganaki recipe also has: basil, red pepper flakes -- & as another reviewer commented, what I see as the key ingredient -- fennel. I pound a tablespoon of seeds w/ mortar & pestle; ground could work too...I also agree with making the sauce first and then nestling the shrimp in at the end for 3-5 minutes until they're cooked. Easier and just as good. Can even add the feta then, too, and cover til shrimp is cooked & feta melted.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Why cast iron? Wouldn't it react with the tomatoes?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly the first time just to see...except that I marinated the shrimp in garlic/oil/wine for a couple hours.  As other have noted, sautéing the shrimp then baking for 15 minutes is way too long.  Add the raw (marinated) Alaskan shrimp to the simmered sauce, cover with goat cheese (I find feta too strong) and bake for 8-10 minutes.  A bit of crushed red pepper flakes would add a kick to the sauce too.   Also note - ouzo is very flammable!  Keep a wet towel handy...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I dislike licorice flavors, so I shell the shrimp and cook the shells in white wine to make a simple stock for use in place of the ouzo. The result is delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody:
Great recipe! 
Used a 13"(5 quart) sauce pan and happy to find a way to use some of the abundance of cherry tomatoes on hand at the end of the season. Parred the shrimp with a little olive oil for 2 minutes on one side and nestled them in the remainder of the simmered veggie loveness. Chardonnay instead of ouzo, sliced black olives instead of Kalamata. Almost embarrassed by how much feta I sprinkled, but was not disappointed with the repercussions. Baked at 400 until "just looked right".  WOW!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody:
This would be a side for 4-6 and a main for three.  As we were only two, I had a generous portion left over.

I topped a 10" califlower pizza crust and covered with grated mozzarella.

Two killer meals from one recipe!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I live in a town where shrimp is our primary economic driver. Cooking shrimp for almost 20 minutes is criminal. Saute the shrimp as directed , then add them at the end just before serving.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I made this dish last night according to the recipe. Bay leaves, tyme, sambuca, cast iron skillet, oven.dill for garnish. It was incredible. It's not under seasoned, it doesn't taste of the iron skillet, and the shrimp are not tough and rubbery. It's a gorgeous recipe that I fell in love with.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Just cooked this per the instructions (ie: sauted the shrimp first).....and, Yes! The shrimps were overcooked! Next time I'll  simply nestle raw shrimp into the sauce before baking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions for sides?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I haven't made it yet - but I'm thinking of making it with oven roasted potatoes

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Rice or orzo and a nice mixed green salad would be my choice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Serve this with whatever you like... greens, pasta, rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: The ouzo makes all the difference. Buy a bottle, and it lasts a long, long time. A worthy investment.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I make this often (always with rose or white wine) but would never use my cast iron skillet, and instead use a large stainless steel sauté pan, and have used a wok before as well. Wouldn’t the tomatoes be too acidic for the cast iron?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Tomatoes for 15 or 20 minutes in a cast iron pan will not affect the seasoned pan. Plenty to read on the Internet about this, e.g.: https://www.southernliving.com/can-you-cook-tomatoes-in-cast-iron-7110401

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I cook tomatoes in my cast iron all the time and it's no problem. As long as you've got a good seasoning, and as long as you replace a very thin coat of oil after every use, your cast iron should be able to handle anything (except dish soap!)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: As Mike has suggested, if you make this dish using white wine rather than Ouzo you can add that hint of licorice flavor by adding a few pinches (to taste) of fresh ground fennel. I suggest grinding the fennel (use a mortar and pestle) before adding, but that step can be considered optional.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Did an Athens food tour with Carolina Doriti last fall. Visited spots we would have never found on our own. She is amazing as is her book - buy it now!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I've made similar on a sheetpan in recent days, using up the cherry tomatoes from my garden. I've also made it with chicken thighs. I marinate the shrimp or chicken in a lemon juice/olive oil/dried oregano mixture. I add sliced red onion, zucchini, peppers, a  whole head of garkic and a couple lemon halves to the tray, drizzle some reserved marinade over it. I add the olives and feta towards the end of the roasting time.  Spri kle with fresh herbs when it comes out of the oven....

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Per Mike, above, add some fennel if you don’t have Ouzo or another wine with an anise note.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Nice variation of flavor in tomato sauce:feta and kalamatas didn't overpowerthe thyme(5 inch sprig) and bay. Skipped saute of smaller shrimp and used wine. Day old baguette slices warmed in foil with olive oil went well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I did this on the stove top in a cast iron dutch oven.  I added red bell pepper and simmered the sauce on high for awhile to break down the pepper and onions which melt in the sauce making it thicker and very slightly chunky.  Used white wine and added 1/2 t of harissa.  It's super delicious!!  Next time I will add diced zucchini and a bit of quinoa to round it out.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I’m eating this now after making a few changes.  It was really good and could be better.  Today, four stars.  I used boneless chicken thighs cut into 1” sq.  I had no ouzo nor Pernod, so I added some diced fennel.  Next time, I’ll add the booze.  I’d also add tomato paste when sautéing the albums.  No need for that final addition of water.  Served on white rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Do NOT use Sambucc - too sweet. Use Pernod or something similar.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Followed the directions exactly, and it was very soupy.  It was good, just not quite what I expected.  Would definitely leave the water out next time and probably use less puree.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: This was very tasty. Made with a few minor modifications. Sautéed the shrimp and set them aside. Made the sauce as directed, using white wine, but substituted 1/4 cup marinara for the cherry tomatoes - probably could have used 1/2 cup. Baked the sauce as directed but left out the shrimp and feta. When the sauce was ready, I stirred in a few handfuls of baby spinach, then I laid the shrimp on top, sprinkled the feta over, and broiled for 2 minutes. Served over buttered orzo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Easy to make, well-liked. I was surprised the ouzo imbued only a whisper of anise; add fennel seed if you would prefer a shout. Crushed red pepper would be nice as well, and double the garlic. Shrimp came out great when prepared as described.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I don't bake it.  It just isn't necessary, in my opinion, to brown the feta.  I can't find my old Bon Appetite magazine that had this recipe, but it called for dry sherry rather than Ouzo, and that's what I use.  A bit of Oregano works well too.  It's really delicious with some crusty bread.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: A tasty base to riff. I used colossal shrimp and cooked for 6 mins before putting into the sauce, and with the 15 mins in the oven they turned out perfectly. I subbed sherry and a bulb of fennel for ouzo, doubled the feta and olives, and used a healthy quantity of thyme and parsley. As per an earlier reviewer's recommendation, I served it over a bed of spinach.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody:
Made this the other night and it was a huge hit
Definitely needed to spice it up a bit with red pepper flakes and even added a little cumin
Sauteed  the shrimp first for only about 2 minutes until just lightly pink but still mostly grey and finished it in the oven for the recommended 15 minutes
No need to worry about overcooking, they came out perfectly

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody:
I thought this was delicious! The ouzo definitely added an interesting (but surprisingly subtle) note to the sauce.
I think I did overcook the shrimp, but they were still tasty. I see a lot of comments saying not to bake it, or not to saute the shrimp first, but I think the juice from the shrimp in the pan also flavored the sauce. I wish I'd been able to get a bit of color on my feta, but that also did not happen.
Overall, highly recommend!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Made this as is last night. Yes - cooked shrimp first and then finished in oven and it was delicious! This will be going into the rotation. I added oregano at the end to mine. Someone else commented fennel would be a nice touch - I agree. 10/10!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe as instructed, and the shrimp were not overdone.  I think the key is a very light sauté.  They had some color, but were clearly not done   When initially removed from the pan.  If your cast iron is properly seasoned and aged, you will get no metallic flavor from the pan.  Be sure to clean the cast iron as soon as the meal is over to prevent damage to the seasoning.  I think this would be great served over pasta, and I may do that next time I cook it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I fell in love with this dish tonight

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: This is the 2nd time I am making this recipe, and the 2nd time I am following as written (pan and all). It is delightful. Thank you Carolina for the recipe, and Alex for the adaptation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody:
Made this pretty much as directed. Used 4 garlic cloves and both dried thyme and oregano along with the bay leaf.  Used stainless rather than cast iron. 
I didn’t find the shrimp tough following the recipe. I did use the broiler setting for the last 4 minutes in the oven to make sure the cheese was soft. 
So delicious with good sourdough toast. A good appetizer for 4-5, or dinner for 2-3.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Love this recipe. Shrimp was not overcooked. Sautéed on stove with cast iron pan for 3-4 minutes and set shrimp aside. Nestled the shrimp in and then added olives and feta, into the sauce and placed in oven for 15 minutes. Perfection. (I used Argentinian large shrimp).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious.  I didn’t have fresh tomatoes so I used half a can of diced tomatoes. Skipped the water. 
This was delicious. Shrimp was fine. Would have been fine without the baking but the sauce was thick and saucy - which we all liked. 
Would be fantastic with sea bass.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this twice now and it was well received both times. First time, as is, second I followed many of the suggestions here: added oregano with the thyme, added spinach with the feta before popping it in the oven - 10mins to melt the cheese, then added the shrimp back in. Also *barely* cooked the shrimp beforehand to avoid the overcooking issue a few have mentioned.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Take care not to over cook the shrimp as you saute them, they finish nicely in the oven!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody:
I entirely shelled and removed tails of the shrimp, and sauteed those in the oil to get some of the flavor from them before discarding. 
I put it in the oven without the shrimp, and only added them in the last 5 min or so. 
I used the scant 2/3 cup pernod instead of ouzo, and do recommend the dill. Loved every bit of this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: what are measurements of a "large cast-iron skillet?" looking to make this for 8. thanks.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Generally, 8" or less is considered small, 10" is medium, 12" is large. To serve 8 ppl you'll probably need a 14"+ or 2 skillets. Wouldn't advise working in batchess with shrimp since it turns rubbery so quickly. Hope the meal goes well!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: A paella pan or any large pan (roasting, skillet, sauté) that is presentation-worthy would work. There is no inherent need for cast iron in this recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone finished this on the cooktop instead of the oven? It’s still too hot in Texas to use the oven.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Yes, you can make this on the cooktop or on the grill. I'm basically lazy, so I make my tomato sauce first. Sauce should be simmering then add olives, and feta, give the sauce a stir then place raw shrimp on top. Do not cover. Depending on size of shrimp let cook 1-3 minutes, with a tongs turn shrimp and cook another 1-2 minutes. I have been making a recipe very similar to this for 30 years. During heat of the summer on the cooktop or grill and in cold months I use the oven.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Try using an outdoor grill to finish it. I've moved to baking sheet-pan dinners in my outdoor propane grill which has a thermometer on it during the dog days of summer.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Pernod could also be an appropriate substitute for the ouzo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: You could serve this over rice or pasta like orzo or even spaghetti.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody:
You can cook tomatoes in cast iron.
From America's Test Kitchen  "... our tasters could detect metallic flavors in the tomato sauce only after it had simmered for a full 30 minutes. So, while you can definitely cook with acidic ingredients in your cast-iron skillet, you have to be careful."
Make sure your cast iron is well seasoned, use as short a simmering time as possible and clean promptly after cooking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Would Pernod work as substitute for ouzo?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Yes; anise-flavored liquors are functionally interchangeable in cooking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Fresh goat cheese would do the trick

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Had something like this in a small community of Pilea when living near Salonica in the mid 70's, but not so much tomato, more olive oil and garlic, the feta and shrimp in shells were in equal proportions, all just cooked in the oven in sauce, which we sopped up with bread.  Heaven!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: What could be substituted for the feta? Or just leave it out?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: A soft goat cheese such as chèvre makes a good substitute for the feta—tangy but not so salty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I cannot imagine cooking shrimp for so long!  A good sear on one side for one to two minutes, then into the bowl where it will continue to cook, and it will finish in the oven.  Overcooked shrimp is like rubber, and as long as it ends up pink all the way through it is finished.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I havent cooked it but a restaurant i used to frwquent made it with Haloumi and shrimp as an appetizer and sonewhat less,ingrediants .i think ut mughrmt be good over pasta or for a shared app with mini pita.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Would Herbsaint work instead of Ouzo or be totally wrong

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: This was easy and delicious. I did not precook the shrimp because mine were smallish. I added hot pepper flakes to the onion mixture. I served it over orzo. Great weeknight dinner!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody:
We doubled up on the garlic and used white wine. 
We also added 1/2 cup of cooked Orzo to the pan just before baking. We would recommend reducing pan sauté time on the shrimp and allowing the bake to cook the shrimp. Fantastic recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight primarily because I had all the ingredients on hand, including the ouzo.  I was not expecting the flavor bomb this turned out to be.  It was so good my husband asked me to put in on our regular rotation of monthly favorites.  I made this exactly to instruction with 2 exceptions: 1) I omitted the extra half cup of water as it did not seem necessary given the tomatoes already contain a fair amount of their own liquid and 2) I reduced to baking time to 10 minutes. Delish!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Used dry white wine, garnished with both dill and parsley. Served with salted and roasted on olive oil bread. Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Used homemade tomato sauce I had in the freezer and it worked perfectly.  cooked the shrimp w fresh red chilis which added just the right amount of heat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly like the recipe said and my wife cleaned her plate --- practically licked it clean!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Would this go well with smashed potatoes?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Very nice, easy weeknight dinner.  Served with rice and broccoli rabe.  Added a bit of red pepper and some fresh oregano.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious but I changed a few things. I skipped sautéing the shrimp and did not add the ouzo. Instead, after sautéing the onions and garlic and adding the tomatoes, I added some red wine, a pinch of cinnamon, and a teaspoon of fennel seed. After thickening, I nestled the raw shrimp on top and put the olives and French feta on top. Fresh dill is a must on top!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: One more thing… using a cast iron pan does not make the dish taste like iron but is a great and natural way to give your family extra iron.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: See Mediterranean dish recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Tried this for the first time last night. Surprised at how sweet it tasted. Anyone know why?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic and so easy! I've been eating the leftover sauce with everything basically

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Made with skinless, boneless chicken thighs cut into 1" tenders.  Delicious and less pricey.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Excellent, i cooked all on stove top. I used farmed frozen shrimp (Monterey bay seafood watch recommended) came out fantastic

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Made this as is and then also with a white firm fish like cod or tilapia and it was delicious! Only addition might be some crushed red pepper when you add the tomatoes or a bit of lemon juice at the end. This also goes great with pasta and rice!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Made it like the instructions said.  Delicious.  Shrimp, feta and tomatoes are a perfect trinity.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: This is great, but the shrimps don’t need pre-cooking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Added fennel seeds and used white wine  (didnt have ouzo) and red pepper flakes.   Might cook shrimp a bit less- put in halfway through baking time.  Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody:
I put the raw shrimp into the sauce and it cooked perfectly in the 15 mins in the oven. 
We had a big bunch of basil so used that instead of other herbs. 
 I served it over quinoa,  the family enjoyed the meal.  Quick,  easy and tasty, it's a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I followed the instructions as given and thought the dish turned out great!   I think the key is a very light initial sauté of the shrimp.  Mine had some color, but were clearly not cooked through when I initially removed them from the pan.  As far as cast iron concerns go, if your cast iron is properly seasoned and aged you will not get any metallic flavor from the iron.  Make sure to clean the cast iron ASAP after the meal to prevent any damage to the seasoning of the pan.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Thinking of adding red pepper flakes, olive oil really good

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Great summer recipe and flexible.  I used 1.5 lbs jumbo shrimp and increased the cherry tomatoes only.  Cooked in a 14 inch stainless skillet and did not precook the shrimp.  Just nestled them in the sauce.  15 minutes was fine in the oven to cook the shrimp but the feta didn’t melt all the way, so I put it under the broiler for one minute and that got the job done.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Capers

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was delicious, and loved by my 92-year-old mother-in-law, my 23-year-old daughter and my husband and me. I didn’t have ouzo so I used Sambuca and it was excellent!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: A little bland - definitely could use red pepper flakes or something similar. Agree with others that oven did not seem necessary

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: @isabelle you might like the ottolenghi recipe for this dish on this site!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and the shrimp were decidedly NOT overcooked. My husband kept commenting how delicious it was. Easy, healthy, delicious - will add this to my rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: It took far longer than three minutes to reduce the wine, and I had to increase the heat to medium-high in order to speed up the reduction. The 1/2 cup of water wasn’t used, and I threw in about 2 tablespoons of Kirkland  Signature No Salt Seasoning instead of the last addition of salt and pepper as specified in the recipe. I also subbed capers for the Kalamata olives as one commenter suggested. So while I had a problem with the wine reduction, and while I didn’t follow the recipe as written, I gave it 5 stars. It was delicious. And that wasn’t just my opinion. I cook for myself and my sister, who rarely talks without being prompted. However, as I was clearing the table, she said, unprompted, “That was really good.”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Used 3 oz. leftover crushed tomatoes in lieu of water. Would not add water in any case. To avoid overcooking shrimp, I under-sautéed them. Next time I'll sauté at least a minute on each side. Fifteen minutes in the 400 degree oven did not overcook them, and Bulgarian feta had started to melt. Delicious w/ Kalamata olives & fresh & canned tomatoes simmered w/ bay leaves. Brandy & dry sherry flavor was lost; will try wine & fennel bulb next time. Great on baby spinach w/ crusty baguette.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent. No ouzo or pastis so  I pulverized fennel seed and mixed it with wine. Fresh fennel would have been great in the base for the sauce. Cooked everything on the stovetop. Put a layer of baby spinach on the tomato sauce, then shrimp, olives and feta. Extra crushed pepper, because that's what we do. Served with a fresh baguette (I live in France - it's almost required).
AND, only one pan to wash.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: So excellent! Made (nearly) exactly as written (we added an anchovy). It was good the first night but stellar the second night so our plan is to make the sauce ahead next time and then just throw the shrimp in with the sauce to cook as the sauce reheats (cooking the shrimp in the first step isn't worth the fuss to us). A definite keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: Quite a good recipe. Definitely did not use Ouzo as I don't like the flavor. White wine for me. Lots of thoughts from reviews about sautéing and then cooking the shrimp. I'll say if you are sauteing the shrimp longer than 90 seconds, then you're cooking them way too long. Lots of great flavors here and actually the time listed is extremely close to accurate, which is nice for a change.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
commentBody: followed recipe but kept skillet on stovetop instead of transferring to oven.  Next time I'll add the shrimp back into the sauce closer to the end of cooking since they don't need that much time.  I used wine instead of ouzo, which I didn't have.  Very flavorful with feta and olives.  Maybe increase them next time, possibly add chile flakes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pa Muchim (Scallion Salad) Recipe
commentBody: I have not made this, but I had the pleasure of eating this salad at Cote, and it is divine with the roasted meat - the perfect tang and spice contrast. I'm excited to make this at home.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pa Muchim (Scallion Salad) Recipe
commentBody: Made this for a friend's chuseok dinner this weekend, and everyone loved it! Don't let the lack of a scallion cutter scare you, a decently sharp paring knife and a fork got the job done for me!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pa Muchim (Scallion Salad) Recipe
commentBody: Instead of sugar, would an equivalent amount of honey work?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pa Muchim (Scallion Salad) Recipe
commentBody: Made this as a bed for Lemongrass Pork Chop.  It was great but 2 bunches, not enough.  I double the recipe and keep any extra for sandwich toppings or pair with almost anything.  BTW, use sugar not honey.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pa Muchim (Scallion Salad) Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely fantastic! Perfect when you have that giant bag of scallions from Costco or when it’s really hot outside and you want something super refreshing. I enjoyed this on its own, but it would be perfect with fish, grilled beef or skewers, or just about anything. Truly exceptional!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pa Muchim (Scallion Salad) Recipe
commentBody: Do you use the whole scallion?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pa Muchim (Scallion Salad) Recipe
commentBody: yes - both white and green parts

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pa Muchim (Scallion Salad) Recipe
commentBody: Would red pepper flakes be an ok sub?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Pa Muchim (Scallion Salad) Recipe
commentBody: I would be more inclined to use a 50/50 or so mix of dried chipotle and paprika ... and if you don't have dried chipotle, 25/75 cayenne and paprika.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: You can find the rice cakes frozen at Trader Joe’s.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Yes, I saw them there last week!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Where does one buy the rice cakes?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Costplus World Market has them, as well as asian grocery stores.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: One could certainly substitute pineapple for the Asian pears as a tenderizing catalyst.  Or, considering using kiwis for a more neutral flavor profile.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: @Ryan Barton I feel apple is a better substitute especially if you’re marinating for a long time (e.g. overnight). Kiwi or pineapple may turn the meat over tenderized (mush)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Any thoughts on how this would work in the oven or under the broiler?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: I live in a small apartment and do not have access to a outdoor grill. I adapt grill recipes to the broiler all of the time. Not identical, but such as life. I rest the skewers on either a 8x8 or 9x13 baking dish so the food broils rather than poaches in its own liquid. Rotate the skewers when halfway done and you may need to adjust the cooking time. Good luck!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: I would not substitute Pineapple or Kiwi if you want the original flavor intended by this recipe.  While those fruits could be tasty also, Korean pears are not sour at all.  They are much less dense, with a subtle floral note, plus the water content is much higher, so the marinade would not come close.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: You can substitute normal pear for Asian pear. I would be careful with pineapple. It breakdown meat much faster if you marinate too long.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: For those asking where to find tteok, they are readily available at most Asian grocery stores (definitely at Korean markets like H-Mart or Zion, sometimes at the Chinese or Japanese ones) but I’ve also seen them online! They have shelf-stable ones that they can deliver.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Thanks to those who gave recommendations for acquiring or substituting ingredients or alternate cooking methods. I appreciate your help so much!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Tteok also goes by the name "rice cake" and "nian gao."  It's available in many-to-most Asian grocery stores, certainly Chinese grocery stores, and is available (dried) online from Yami.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: I’m going to test out this recipe with dried tteok and next month I’ll make it with the frozen variety for my sons birthday party. My question is, with the dried ones, would you boil them first, according to the package preparation, before grilling? Or what would you do with the dried ones to make them ready for the recipe??

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. I didn't have orange juice so I used apple juice instead, and omitted ginger - just a personal preference. Also my local store didn't have boneless short rib so I used tri-tip and it worked well! My kids asked this to be added to the rotation. I'm not sure why rating is only 3 stars. This was relatively easy to make and had lots of flavor. We had this with a side of grilled broccoli. I will definitely make it again. I may just keep using apple juice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: My wife is vegetarian, so I substituted the pork meat with soybean curd knots previously pan-fried in butter and soy sauce. They held very well on the skewers, together with the rice cakes. I must confess I followed the rest of the recipe a bit loosely. The result was so good, though, that I will try again, this time being more faithful to the recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Sounds fantastic.  Any thoughts on finding tteok, substitutes, making from scratch...?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: They are sold in the frozen section at Trader Joe's, if you have one near you.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: You can buy cylindrical rice cakes at any Asian market in a variety of sizes.  They can even be found in the frozen section.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Found some tteok on amazon but not sure if that's what the recipe intends.  I regularly shop in Chinatown and I've never seen or even heard of these things.  Can you please give us some guidance for these recipes for hard to find items?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: You can find frozen rice cakes in any Asian grocery that has Korean food.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: It’s interesting that this recipe has a pretty low 3 star rating without anything really negative in the comments. I find galbi delicious in most forms. I like tteok as well,  it have really only had them in soups or stews.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Update - way more ratings and it’s up to 4 stars.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: We liked the combination of flavors and textures. Dipping the rice cakes in a little sesame oil was delicious, and we also tried these as lettuce wraps. This Korean American family recommends!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: I’m planning on cooking these at the end of a 7 hour road-trip. Can I prep the rice cakes before the road-trip ( goal is least amount of fuss at destination)? Ideas anyone? Thanks in advance!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe offering caramelized and crunchy bits, lots of chew and meaty umami. No need to spend money on asian pear, regular ones like bosc or anjou work fine. There is no way to use one tteok between the meet and use up one pound so I stacked up two and three and it worked well. The recipe is tasty but it needs edge so I would serve it with gochuchang or, even better, kimchi.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Any substitute ideas for the pear?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: I use applesauce instead of pear in bibimbap, but others have indicated you can use pineapple or kiwi.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: I just found the rice cakes at H Mart!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: In Los Angeles Fuget-su Bakery in DTLA is the place for sure! Historic as well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: If you have a local Korean or Asian market they'll probably have the rice cakes (bagged, frozen).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: I bought rice cakes online for another recipe - a soup, I think.   I did not like them at all, but I can see where they might be better if they were grilled or fried.  I am skeptical about how they would cook on skewers.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: The Trader Joe’s rice cakes are not good; super sweet and inauthentic. I do not recommend.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: You can buy tteok at any Korean market. Might be able to find it in a more general Asian market as well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: The marinade sounds delicious. Has anyone tried it with tofu or chicken?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: I can find all of the ingredients relatively easily except boneless short ribs! I can only find bone-in short rib. Short rib substitute suggestions are appreciated. I considered Chuck Roast but I think it will be too tough, even after overnight marinade. Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: The flavors were spot on excellent! I would like to try with a different cut of beef--not quite as fatty...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Any substitution idea for orange juice? Would lemon juice work?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: I used the frozen Korean rice cakes from Trader Joe’s, used whatever pear I found at Trader Joe’s, and cooked this in the air fryer at 375° for 12 minutes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Galbi and Tteok Skewers Recipe
commentBody: Used the marinade for beef skewers (didn’t have any tteok). It was so unbelievably good even with a short 1 hour marination time. Will definitely be making again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: The key to this dish is ingredients-all of which are mercurial, and flexibility. If you are rigid recipe follower looking for a 5 star result, back away and query another dish. Forgive the author if it doesn’t suit you and adjust. This is a quick-to-assemble, healthy weeknight dinner. Depending on the brand of hoisin sauce you choose, it may be sweeter or more savory, likewise with the kimchi it may be spicier, more or less acidic, or in need of an herbal note like cilantro. Be grateful.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Was a wonderful way to repurpose leftover chicken. I added toasted cashews and cilantro for color and crunch. Delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Used the recipe as written except I squeezed lime on the wrap. It was too sweet for me. Next time I will add onions and jalapeños to the chicken and use only 2 tablespoons of hoisin and keep the addition of lime juice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: @IreneM Yes! We did this because we didn’t have bib lettuce & it was very good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I am wondering if this couldn't be made into a salad. Cook the chicken as directed and combine with chopped kimchi. Toss it with a little rice and appropriate amount of lettuce. Less messy to eat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Messy can be good though:).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Pretty good for such a simple recipe. I used ground pork and threw in some sliced shallot before the pork and it worked great. My main observation is even with the shallot it’s missing a textural component. I think I’ll toss in some water chestnuts next time as well

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I made this with ground dark turkey, kimchi from Costco and hoisin sauce from Whole Foods. It was soooooo good!! Will be a regular at my house. And Anyone can make it— easy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This recipe harnesses some of my favorite elements: Simple, tasty, and quick. While there is a whole lot of room to improvise if you so choose, this dish is bang on as is.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I used crumbled Tofu and it worked out great (I'm not even vegetarian). 2TB hoisin   1 TB oyster sauce, and a little sesame oil. Added some fresh mint, sprouts, and cashews per others recommendations. It was delicious and will be going on the favorites list. So versatile and easy, you can use almost any protein.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I loved how fast and easy this was. I used chicken breast as this was all I had in the house, and it has way less fat than dark meat. The 2T of oil was too much, but added the fat the breast meat lacked. Homemade kimchi and lettuce from the garden made this especially delish. I did not use rice as I didn't want the empty carbs. This was really good, healthy, and filling.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This went straight to the “Recipes that SLAP” folder! So delicious, so easy and so fast. Doing low carb, so skipped the rice and added some sautéed shiitakes. Was requested to make this again before we even finished. If PF Changs lettuce wraps were good, they would be this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: added some roasted salted peanuts for a crunch

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Don’t let the simplicity fool you. This is as delicious to eat as it is easy to prepare. Serve with some chopped cucumber and peanuts for more crunch, and you have a mighty nice dinner with so little effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This simple recipe turned out to be a delightful surprise! I followed some user suggestions and added jalapeño peppers, onions, a squeeze of lime, and fresh cilantro before serving — it was absolutely delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This is easy, healthy and delicious! My family loves it. For people who don’t want a sweet dish, Whole Foods hoisin is not that sweet. Traders joes is thicker and sweeter. Both are good, just different.  We love and use the small towns cultures wild ramp kimchi, but it doesn’t give the lovely reddish brown color pictured.  I agree with the person who squeezed lime over it and also the addition of cilantro, but it’s delicious as written!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I was not impressed with the flavor overall.  I added green onions, cilantro and lime on the side and still it fell flat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I added scallions before adding the hoisin, and then after for garnish along with cilantro and a generous squeeze of lime. I made this into a salad bowl using mixed greens, quinoa, the meat, and some carrot and cucumber matchsticks!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: So tasty - I chopped a can of water chestnuts and added it to the chicken for crunch it was delightful!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Ok so no hoisin but used the Japanese bbq sauce Bachan’s aaaand it was a hit! Served with a daikon radish salad and rice. Came together really quick!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Where might I find ground dark meat chicken?  I asked at my local grocery store, and the guy looked at me like I was from Mars.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: @Joyce we have Amish meat markets where I live and they sell both light and dark ground turkey

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: A dinner staple for me. I tend to use ground pork instead of chicken just for personal preference and it wouldn't hurt to add some ginger or garlic where you see fit. Can't go wrong with these!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty. I used my homemade Tongbaechu Kimchi, which is an adaptation of the recipe found on NYT Cooking. (I swapped the fish sauce for Saeujeot among other things.)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Finished product needed more depth of flavor, but was a decent springboard for my meal. Bulked up the meat with some carrot and yellow summer squash shredded on a box grater. Added Red Boat fish sauce, toasted sesame oil, a heaping spoonful of minced garlic, onion powder, rice vinegar, soy sauce, ketchup, honey, brown sugar, sriracha and lime zest. Finished it with fresh lime juice. Incredible. Will add shredded cabbage next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This was so good!! I used 14 ounces of kimchee, and served it with chili crisp, chopped green onions, basil, mint, toasted sesame seed oil, limes, and shredded carrots. Hubby and I loved it, it's really quick and easy to make. Definitely a keeper!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Dead simple, easily adaptable and absolutely delicious. So pleased! Will be putting this into regular rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Was a little afraid of the dish being too sweet so I used half oyster sauce/half hoisin sauce (1.5 Tbs each), which worked really well!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Made this with crumbled high protein tofu and it tasted shockingly chicken-like - I think the key was tossing the crumbled tofu in a cornstarch/olive oil mixture and letting it “brown”. Followed all the other instructions and it came out great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This was a delight. Great flavors and easy to throw together. Used homemade kimchi and added some vegetables: sprouts, mushroom, bell pepper. Easiest and best tasting recipe I’ve done in a while.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Great easy weeknight dinner. Comes together quickly and uses ingredients I already typically have in my pantry.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and oh so easy. Tossed some roasted peanuts on top for a little bit of crunch!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: The ratio of ease to tastiness earns this one top marks.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I'm making this for the 3rd time tonight as written -- used ground turkey, lucky hoisin sauce from whole foods and spicy kimchi, good rice and butter lettuce. The kimchi adds the touch and the hoisin has no chemical aftertaste. Thank YOU!! sooooo good

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Double to have leftovers for lunches

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I liked it! Will probably do it as a salad next time as we're not so handy around here. May add jalapeno and cilantro next time

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Swapped the chicken with ground plant-based beef and served on top of the rice with torn up lettuce. Somewhere between a salad and a rice bowl. Super quick and absolutely delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Why does this have such a large carbohydrate count? It would be easier to determine whether this is diet-conscious if it were broken down into single servings.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I followed another commenter’s suggestion to eat this as a salad, and it’s a new meal prep staple! I toss the chicken and kimchi with brown rice, chopped bibb lettuce, chopped peanuts, and a drizzle of hoisin sauce. It’s delicious and needs less assembly for a quick meal!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This was great. Was a little sweet for my taste so next time I may substitute a tablespoon of oyster sauce for hoisin. And will also add a tablespoon of gochugaru. For sure a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Made this with firm tofu but first tossed the tofu with some oil, cornstarch, onion and garlic powder,oyster sauce, hoisin and put it in the air fryer until crunchy. Then stirfried it with the cut up kimchi. Assembled with rice, chopped toasted peanuts, chopped sugarsnaps for crunch and cilantro.. served with a dab of siracha…

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Easy and adaptable.  Added chopped cashews and cilantro.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody:
I chop 1 lb chicken thighs into 1/2inch pieces and use short grain brown rice.
Using a sturdy lettuce like oak leaf is useful. Have a big napkin and a glass of water
near by. I spend on a hoisin sauce made by TanTan from Oregon. Make every month.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody:
anyone have a Kimchi rec.?  ....prefer spicy over acidic
I have tried one branch so far- (just ok)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Cleveland Kitchen's Kimchi is spicy and delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Commercial ground chicken grosses me out, so when I make chicken-lettuce wraps, I chop chicken thighs in my food processor. Just trim and quarter them and pulse a few times. Makes for a better texture, too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: @RBCinNYC I don’t know why I’ve never thought to do this!  Thanks for the tip!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Based on a comment I read saying it was too sweet and my general feelings about hoisin being sweet, I didn’t use hoisin, I used oyster sauce, gochujang, Doenjang, sesame oil, and Korea red pepper flakes. I opted for chicken thighs and sliced them in cm strips. I did add the kimchi to the chicken. I wonder if nytimes cooking recipe writers are asked to keep ingredients as minimal and common as possible. Sorry for not following the recipe, but it was really yummy with the mods. Thx for the inspo!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This is a great base recipe. I don’t usually have hoisin sauce. So instead i mixed soy sauce, sugar and toasted sesame oil. Added green onions and cilantro. And, in the words of others, bam! Accompanied by sticky rice and topped with gochujang sauce. Delish! Thanks!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This is a great weeknight recipe. I added shiitake mushrooms, water chestnuts and scallions. This recipe is super versatile and a ton of different vegetables could be added.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This was a great recipe! I used my homemade Kimchi which gave it the right amount of bite!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I get frustrated with NYT recipes because they often have a bunch of ingredients and feel overly complicated…this recipe was the opposite! I didn’t have hoisin but I mixed ponzu, soy sauce, and bbq sauce and it was so. good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Add a bit of chow mein for crunch! Delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody:
Amazing flavor, quick to make, and lots of room for improvisation/customization--as Josh in the comments stated. 
My improvisation:  diced 2 portobello mushroom caps and sautéed them first with some garlic and onion [powders since I was in a rush]. Then removed from pan and added back in when the chicken/hoisin/kimchi part of the recipe was done. Also chopped some green onions for garnish. 
I am happily adding this to my quick-delicious menu options.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: So quick, easy, and tasty! 11yo and 7yo gobbled it up.  Perfect weeknight dinner

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Used ground pork instead. Also diced 1/3 of an onion and added lime and cilantro at the end.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This was the fastest, most popular and tasty meal I’ve made in longer than I can remember. I only used half the recommended kimchi, but everyone added additional kimchi to their taste. The amount of hoisin recommended is perfect. I used two pounds of ground turkey and doubled the recipe, and it barely fed 5 hungry people—make a big batch!!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and so adaptable! I had leftover rice and poached chicken breast - so minced the chicken breast and used that. My lettuce leaves were from the garden and too small to wrap—so tore them up and topped with the rice and the chicken mixture. Extra special with homemade hoisin sauce, slivered basil, and chopped peanuts

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: A perfectly solid recipe that gets much better if you add another topping or two, especially something spicy like chili crisp or minced Thai chilies. It would work very well as a salad bowl or rice bowl, but isn't the point that lettuce wraps are fun to eat?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Quick and easy. Use romaine lettuce leaves. Butter lettuce is too soft, not enough.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Simple, but pretty underwhelming.  Could do with a lot more depth of flavor and more heat.  I agree on the jalapeno that someone mentioned or red pepper flakes.  And perhaps some pickled cucumbers too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I made this as a bowl. Add a bed of rice, heap on a scoop of the cooked chicken and kimchi, add a handful of shredded lettuce, top with fresh cilantro, diced green onions, more kimchi, and cashews. You could even add diced avocado cubes for more of a lunch bowl feel.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I discovered too late that I was out of Hoisin. A quick substitute of soy sauce, sriracha, and sesame oil came to the rescue and a delicious “mom you need to make this again” meal that I can feel good about was on the table in under 20 minutes. WINNER WINNER CHICKEN DINNER!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Great vegan dish with these changes: dice 6 oz. portobello mush. caps and mince clove of garlic, sauté them together; add Garden ground be’f instead of meat;  add 2T hoisin (3 is too sweet); sprinkle onion powder and garlic powder and 1-2T unseasoned rice vinegar; turn off heat, add squeezed lime and some toasted sesame oil; serve with a lot of chopped cilantro (great addition). Also we used only 2T chopped kimchi so not too spicy for certain family members (with more on the side for others).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic recipe. Replaced the chicken with 1/2 veal and 1/2 pork and it really upped the flavor.  The kimchi both in the chicken and then cold on top is a delightful finisher.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: For a vegan version I used quorn and chopped mushrooms in place of chicken

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: So easy and so flavorful and healthy. Great go-to easy weeknight dinner!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Cooked as directed.  It was super simple, tasty, and my son asked for seconds so it’s definitely a keeper!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I prefer to make my own ground chicken--just quarter some boneless skinless chicken breasts and pulse in the food processor until chopped. Much better flavor and texture than the pre-gound pasty stuff!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: delicious! I’ve made this many times now, for myself as a quick lunch on a lettuce base, or for dinner for me and my boyfriend in the lettuce wrap form. healthy & fast. this will be in heavy rotation in our house for a long time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Yum, yum, yum! Perfect for a quick dinner. I added a little coriander and sesame to make her pretty but she didn't need the extra embellishment - she was gorgeous as written!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: @Teddi that’s what I did to serve my toddler. Mixed the chicken and rice without additional kimchi and with shredded lettuce. But I think making a ssam (wrap) is part of the fun of this dish so adults did just that. Loved by all!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Use Larb recipe instead

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Really nice and fast. Sauteed an onion and pepper before adding ground chicken. Good flavor with nice contrast between sweet hoisin and fiery kimchi.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody:
Delightful and a huge hit with my family!  

As an additional side, I added the ginger-scallion sauce from Momufuku’s Bo Ssam (another recipe in NYT cooking. If you have not tried it, go to the recipe and then run to the store!)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Family LOVED it with these additions: chop up water chestnuts and cook some with meat, leave rest for garnish. Add Sriracha sauce when cooking and as garnish. Thinly slice 2 inch long pieces of cucumber, shred carrot, and chop cilantro for garnish. DELICIOUS (and can have leftoverrs as rice bowls with kimchi. Gotta get fresh kimchi at the Asian market.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: So delicious, such fun to eat and so messy!  The presentation at the table definitely whet our appetites. The lettuce didn't hold up to being rolled around the filling, but we had wet napkins at the ready. I used a combo of 1 pound each of ground turkey and chicken.  I used mild kimchi and next time I make it, I will use the spicy kimchi.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: More recipes like this, NYT! I really appreciated the depth of flavors and the ability to build your own depending how much kimchi you like. This was a perfect weeknight meal that is a far cry from the same routine meal traps we can fall into. My kids loved it, and I’ll definitely make it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I decided to roll everything up in spring roll wrappers and they turned out great! Much more structural integrity. Next time I’d like to add some more ingredients like sprouts or cilantro as others have mentioned to add some more crunch and freshness.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I made a version with Borlotti beans instead of chicken for my vegetarian nephew and it was as good and as flavorful as the one we had with chicken.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This was lovely- I added half a can of mixed bean shoots/water chestnuts and baby corn, which I had left over and didn’t bother with the rice. Will definitely be in regular rotation now!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: So easy and so good. This is one is included in our monthly rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I liked it but family was not a huge fan. Probably will not make again. Ingredients were expensive for medium result for the family. They normally love lettuce wraps. Will go back to my old recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: @A R Did you add the peanuts to the skillet or uncooked in a serving bowl?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Easy and quick!! I used oyster sauce instead of hoisin sauce bc I didn’t have any. It came out a lil too salty , delicious still though !!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Made tonight. Delicious and more than the sum of its few parts. Added all the toppings (cuke, cilantro, peanuts, more kimchi, lime, etc) and we scarfed it down. Super easy. I think the quinoa that someone suggested would be a good swap for the rice - maybe next time. There will be a next time!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: We make this with ground beef instead of chicken and add anything pickled in the fridge. It's a big hit every time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! The perfect weeknight dinner. Not only did it taste great, it came together in the time it took to cook the rice. I finished work at 5:45 and I was sitting down to eat before 6:15. I added a bit of lime juice to the chicken mixture, and some scallions + chopped peanuts to the finished wraps. Filling, satisfying, and TASTY.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Somewhat bland.  I used white meat chicken...maybe dark meat would have been better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Sounds very much like chicken larb. Better yet, make it a more traditional larb: add pulverized toasted rice to the mix for texture. Excellent!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Tried it with a flour wrap and it was excellent!  Not as healthy as a lettuce wrap, but pretty good.  Added some shredded romaine for some texture and crunch

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody:
I did this last night with what I had (didn’t want to make a trip to the market). 
Used 1 lb ground sausage (it came lightly seasoned with no sage) Pan fried the sausage, cut up a can of kimchi with scissors right into the sausage. Added 4 large green onions trimmed and sliced. Served with Little Gem lettuce leaves, peanuts, fresh lime juice and drizzled with Trader Joe’s peanut sauce. Two of us ate every bit of it. Extremely delicious and took no more than 15 minutes to prepare.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Follow the recipe except didn't have hoisin so used Tonkatsu sauce instead. Awesome and fast for a weeknight. I'm grateful and so was my partner :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Really easy, nutritious and flavorful-enough dish. I followed the recipe to a T but at the end I added chopped scallions in addition to the uncooked kimchi. This is good and spicy cuz the kimchi I bought is spicy.  Will definitely make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This was really, really good! I didn’t have Bibb lettuce, so I used iceberg instead. I also didn’t use rice, but used some diced cucumber which added a nice crunch and a cooling foil to the spice of the kimchee. I’ll definitely make this again. It is an easy, fast and yummy addition to my cooking repertoire.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Delish and super easy.  Probably adding chopped peanuts or cashews next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This looks delicious but I’m confused by the calories. How could this have 889/serving? Seems a lot closer to about 500, including the rice…

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: @Cari Yes, I have the same question

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I have calculated it at 330 per serving using avocado spray instead of oil and 1/2 cup of rice. I just added the ingredients separately in my fitness pal app. Hope this helps!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Between the kimchi and the hoisin sauce, there is plenty of saltiness. I added none. Also used sauteed chicken thigh meat and brown rice and got thumbs up from the fam

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: For those wanting a meat-free option, try adding soy-curls as a sub for chicken. It's great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Felt compelled to comment! This was delicious and turned my day right around. I used Whole Foods brand hoisin and the Cleveland kitchen brand kimchi. I cooked green onions into the cooked mixture and added cucumbers as a topping. Served with eggplant on the side. The cooked kimchi has this nice complexity that tastes like a roasted salsa. Made the chicken really juicy, not dry.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I added some toasted sesame oil at the very end and felt like that elevated the dish! This is the perfect weeknight meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This was an excellent and FAST dinner! As others suggested, I chopped up the lettuce (it wasn't firm or big enough to make lettuce cups), topped with rice, then the chicken, loads of cilantro and cashews. Will try crumbled tofu next time. Used Whole Foods 365 hoisin, probably twice as much with a little water at the end to make it saucier for the rice, and Mother in Law's Kimchi.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic! Easy to assemble and a quick weeknight dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I made this with Turkey and it came out really well except for a couple of additions.  Adding scallions, jalapenos, lime and cilantro like so many others suggested is a must for a great kick in flavor.  More Kimchi and Hoisin sauce than suggested should be added to the meat, plus the meat should be drained prior to adding the Kimchi and Hoisin, otherwise you overcook the meat trying to reduce the liquid and it can also help with coating the meat.  Butter Lettuce works well with this too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Such a great recipe to tweak and personalize to suit your taste. I added garlic, cilantro, and a squeeze of lime. Served in lettuce cups with a little bit of cold brown rice Maifun noodles that I dressed with a splash of soy and sesame oil. Next time I might sauté the cooked noodles with the meat mixture right at the end for ease of eating and assembly. Eating out of lettuce cups is always messy but lots of fun and tasty. I used kimchi from Trader Joe’s, and hoisin from Whole Foods. Not too sweet at all. Will be making this again for sure!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Easy and delish!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: So yummy i made without rice and was quick and delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody:
Upon reading this recipe one might conclude that this should not work. 
One would be wrong, however. This has been added to our current rotation! (no, it's not going to impress your foodie friends but good  food is good food!)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Really light and healthy, yet felt satisfying and flavoursome. A lot of ways this can be adapted and played with. I agree with other commenters that it was missing something textural, some additional bite...I added crushed honey-salted peanuts on top of each wrap which gave it some needed crunch. Will definitely be making again, a great mid week dinner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: What would you recommend using instead of hoisin sauce if I don't like sweet with savory?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Used grated tofu, added finely chopped mushrooms and cashews and cilantro at the end.  Increased the hoisin sauce by a bit.  Perfect easy meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I made chicken burgers using all the ingredients except the rice and I added Panko to the mix. Topped them with pickled veggies.  We loved them

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: simple, tasty, easy dinner. like most recipes, a good jump off place. take this and do you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: To address complaints from other reviewers that it is too sweet, I cut the hoisin sauce down to 1 T and added a little soy sauce and sambal oolek.  For crunch I added a lot of peanuts, and to boost the flavor some sichuan chile crisp.  Was delicious and will make again with similar mods if I can remember them :).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: This was so easy (doesn't require a lot of ingredients), fast (the rice takes the most time), and tasty, while also little touches like green onions, cilantro or other add-ins of your choice can really elevate the meal. I'll double the amount next time and will consider adding lime, hot sauce, and maybe some sort of toasted nuts on top. Definitely a new staple!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: I subbed farro for the rice and we loved the nutty crunchy elements it added. Yummy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Kimchi Chicken Lettuce Wraps Recipe
commentBody: Perfect as is!  Can’t believe how easy it was to make.  Will definitely add some water chestnuts for crunch next time as others have suggested and/or serve with chopped peanuts on top.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, fresh salad that tastes like a distillation of summer. I used bufala mozzarella bc that’s what I had on hand, but the suggested ricotta salata might have been even better. The dressing is delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Made this salad for lunch and it was amazing! I did have to sub a few things: Used peaches instead of nectarines and pre-shredded parm instead of the ricotta salata. I looked up ricotta salata and it seems similar to feta but while I think feta would be okay on this, I prefer parmesan.

If you want to serve it as a main course, adding seafood or steak would be a great option! This could also really easily be turned into an awesome ceviche recipe by adding fish or shrimp with the dressing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Used peaches, plums and fresh from the garden basil and cherry tomatoes. Ricotta Salata  can be found in the cheese section of most supermarkets and was delicious here. tip: I freeze ginger root and grate  as needed in recipes like this. No need to peel and you always have it on hand when you need it

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: why separate the nectarines from the tomatoes when dressing? why not make it a one bowl meal??

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: We used fresh peaches instead of nectarines. Was delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Serve this over a small bed of arugula as there is more than enough dressing for the fruit alone. Sub in Mike’s Hot Honey for those who like a bit more heat. Add some chopped roasted cashews or pistachios for some texture and depth of flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made this quickly in one bowl; used TJ feta because that’s all I had to go with our homegrown cherry tomatoes and some great, great peaches from Palisades, CO.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Very nice.  I eliminated the jalapeno and substituted a mango habenero gouda for the cheese, added smoked almonds for crunch, and cilantro for the herb.  Served with jerk chicken on the side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely amazing & unexpected flavor combination. Served on top of a bed of arugula to make it a leafier salad and it was amazing. Didn’t find ricotta salata so used shavings of parmesan instead and complemented the flavors well

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this as written except I subbed in feta as that was what I had in the fridge. It makes a great side dish at brunch instead of the usual fruit salad. Next time I won’t cut the nectarines so thin or quarter the baby tomatoes. It became too soupy and while it tasted good it also looked less appetizing. I’d also add more jalapeño or use less oil. The pickling step really tamed the jalapeño and then the oil tamped it down further. I guess we just wanted a sharper flavor in the dressing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night, followed all the steps, except for the layering… I just piled all the elements on a platter at the end, and just gently tossed lightly with my fingers. And I did take another Raiders advice to make the fruit slices a little thicker. Absolutely out of this world.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: I loved this salad! It was tangy, spicy and really robust. I used peaches instead of nectarines since I the farmer's market had them. I used Manchego cheese because my partner doesn't like really soft cheeses. And I doubled the jalapeno (I used a full one). It was delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: I make the dressing now just to have on hand in the fridge for any salad, it’s that good! But this salad was really tasty too- used peaches instead of nectarines sine they were ripe - it was delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Decided to make a smaller serving size because I had a feeling I wouldn’t care for it that much. It’s okay - I see what the recipe is going for but it probably needs a softer, tangier cheese. Any suggestions here would be most appreciated.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Couldn’t find ricotta salata so used pecorino romano instead. Maybe next time use fresh mozzarella. White peaches weren’t sweet enough.
