author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was mind blowing delicious. I made this with nectarines as instructed and the only variation I made was the use of freshly shaved pecorino romano. I think it’s the best salad I have ever made. Beautiful, unique and so delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: This took waaaay more than 20 mins prep.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: what's the best vegan sub for the honey?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty!  Great way to use those summer heirloom tomatoes and peaches from the farmer's market.  The ricotta salata was a nice add as well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Fanstastic!! I had a super juicy nectarine and peak heirloom tomatoes. Added spinach because I knew it’d be… well, juicy. Amazing! Should’ve added fish or shrimp! Used Asiago because that’s all I had. Mint and basil were a must. I could see this being just okay if not for peak produce season.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad was so refreshing and the flavors really popped.  I served it with the Cumin-Baked Pork Chops.  Very nice dinner but think I’ll do a chicken breast next time.  I’ll also add a crusty bread to capture any extra dressing.  Can’t wait to make this one again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad was delicious.  I have been looking for creative recipes to tackle all the tomatoes from my garden.  I put half a jalapeño in the salad but next time will add the whole thing.  It could use a little more heat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Excellent salad.  Dressing a bit too sweet for our palette, would cut honey in half.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious.  I used a mix of plum, yellow and white nectarines and peaches and a cherry tomato mix of red, orange and dark green.  I could not find ricotta salata so I used myzithra greek cheese. Took to a party everyone & enjoyed

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Great summer lunch. I had a side of fresh corn on the cob.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad was delicious. The spiciness is a  surprise and welcome balance to the sweet fruit. Used nectarines and plums. Could not find Ricotta Salata so used a teardrop shaped dry Greek cheese.  Praised by guests at a family dinner

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was good, but with 5/5 stars for so many reviews expected perfection. In search of that for our palates, next time will cut the salt way back - enough to bring out the juices but not need to drink a gallon of water over the next few hours to recover. Will also add some mint to the basil, consider a different cheese like buffalo mozzarella instead of the dry and salty ricotta salata shavings, and try Mike’s Hot Honey for all of the honey.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made this with fresh peaches from my tree and it is absolutely outstanding. I removed some of the seeds from the jalapenos as to keep the heat from overshadowing the stars of the show, the peaches and tomatoes. I added mint along with basil which was nice too. I debated using the ricotta salata as I never cook with it but I'm glad I stuck to the recipe there! Perfect end of summer dish and easy as can be. Definitely make this!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: so good! used feta cuz thats what we had but mannnn I bet ricotta is amaIng

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: prefer the flavor of Serrano vs jalapeño so made that swap - this is a great, simple and fresh summer side/salad.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Spicy Tomato and Nectarine Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night, using nectarines and a handful of halved sweet cherries, compari tomato wedges, feta (as that is what I had), and basil from my garden.  The dressing is delicious with the fruit, but my husband and I did not enjoy the tomatoes in this salad.  Perhaps cherry tomatoes would be a better choice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: At least 15 minutes in the refrigerator before frying really firms up the breading so you don’t loose any in the pan.  My mother’s hint.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Came here to say this!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Brushing the cut chicken breasts with vodka before dipping will make for a crispier cutlet when frying.  The vodka will dissipate and not leave any taste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: @David Stone vodka instead of egg mixture or both?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I don't find it necessary to pound the split and butterflied breasts in recipes that use cutlets.  The thicker meat stays juicer and is more forgiving.  Thin cutlets cook too quickly often resulting in over cooked meat and/or undercooked breading.  I also skip the flour step which leads to a thicker, gluey texture breading.  The meat goes into the beaten eggs and straight into the breadcrumb.  Who doesn't like to save a step and a dish and produce a better result!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: @Paul ID you might not pound the cutlets thin but thinner cutlets make for a faster fry and, in my honest and humble opinion, make for tastier cutlets.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: When I make anything that needs to be pounded out, I put the cutlets or breasts or whatever into a clean cereal box liner. Fold the opening over, make sure the meat is flat on the counter and pound away. The bags are pretty hearty and won't break, and they prevent the meat from spraying "juices" all over you and your kitchen.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Cooked exactly according to directions. My Italian wife said it was "perfect". She wouldn't even give our spoiled cat a taste!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Chicken Milanese (or schnitzel - basically the same, I think) is versatile -basis for Chicken Parm, or a sandwich with Dijon/lettuce/tomato/bacon/avocado, or a simple entree with a side of potatoes and a green vegetable.  Always tasty.  I spray both sides of the breaded cutlet with cooking spray or misted olive oil, then bake (on a foil- or parchment-lined baking sheet) at 375-400 for about 8-10 minutes, then flip and finish cooking for about another 6-8 minutes.   No mess, and always crispy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I did something similar a few days ago using the ATK method for chicken parmigiana from their air fryer cookbook. Perfect! The bread crumbs are toasted in the microwave then mixed with grated parm and the egg is mixed with a bit of flour and spices. Dip the breast in the egg mix then the bread/parm mix. I always let the breaded chicken sit on a baking rack in the fridge for about 30 minutes. Then cook in air fryer. Crunchy outside juicy inside. Endless variations possible.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I usually use a mixture of approximately 2/3 panko and 1/3 "traditional" (unseasoned) breadcrumbs; I still get the added crunch of the panko but with better coverage overall. Also, resting the breaded cutlets on a cooling rack (uncovered) in the refrigerator for a few hours ensures a drier coating less susceptible to being "blown off" by steam generated during the cooking process.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: In many traditional Milanese or Schnitzel recipes the meat is not seasoned first because salting makes the meat weep and often the crispy coating doesn't stick properly.    I have found early salting and time in the fridge before breading allows for the meat to be seasoned and patted dry prior to coating.  Once breaded you can put back in the fridge and relax until time to fry making for a stress free evening

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: One of the best tips I’ve ever gotten for a dish like this is to have a low oven on the go and when finish frying; put the cutlets on a wire rack on a tray and pop that in. You can hold there without sogginess for about 15-20 mins. Helps a ton.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I have been using a similar recipe for years from the former Berkeley CA restaurant Omnivore. The slight differences are in the coating: half grated parmesan and half seasoned croutons make the flavor profile instant. After dipping they go in the fridge for at least 1/2 hour for the coating to firm up, then sautéed in a little butter (Julia's orders) to reduce the oiliness. The same coatings/prep on oysters is magnificent.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Living in Argentina, Milanesas were a staple.  Way before Panko, I just used regular bread crumbs plus the seasoning.  With Chicken I didn't even do the flour and egg, just passed the thin cutlets through milk and the bread crumbs.  when done, squeeze lemon and your good to go. With meat, I used egg mixed with garlic and parsley and then the bread.  There are many ways to fancy them up, but the best is with a squeeze of lemon and a salad with vinegrette.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: The NYTCooking daily email had an excellent recommendation from Margaux Laskey: Pound the cutlets between sheets of parchment or plastic wrap. And while you're at it, make extra and then roll them up between the parchment & freeze. _That's a useful tip. And if you're interested in this recipe, you should also look at Ali Slagle's option with a sour cream & onion topping: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1021131-crispy-sour-cream-and-onion-chicken

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I don't bother making such a small amount as there are so many great ways to use the leftovers.  Chicken cutlets are so good for a lunch sandwich, sliced on top of a salad, making Parmigiano. I use home made toasted bread crumbs instead of flour.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: They freeze really well, too — reheat in the oven for a quick future meal!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: @AM did you freeze them after cooking? How did you reheat? Just as good?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
A Tip for Less-Mess Cutlets
When pounding boneless meat to a particular thinness, sandwich it between two sheets of parchment paper or plastic wrap before whacking away. Not only is it less messy, but I find it’s easier to get an even thickness when the slippery meat is neatly contained. You can do this in advance, then roll or fold up the sheets — meat included — and store in the fridge until you’re ready to bread and fry. By Margaux Laskey 5JUN23

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: For a little more interest i use part of Julia Child's recipe for gougers. Marinate the cutlets in a mixture of lemon juice and garlic for about 15 minutes.  Dry them off and proceed with the breading.  I do this with chicken tenders too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night per the recipe except I used boneless/skinless chicken thighs pounded out instead of breasts.  It came out great!  I made a couple extras and had one for lunch in a sandwich with tomato slices, red onion and lettuce with siracha mayo.  Really good too!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, plus heeded advice to chill the breaded cutlets in the fridge prior to frying (45 minutes in our case). Perfect, tender and crispy chicken. Being November, I roasted the cherry tomatoes for 15 minutes in 350 degree oven to bring out their sweetness. Rave reviews all around.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I’ve been making cutlets like this for years and they’re always terrific. Last night I tried making them in the air fryer, just because…My advice? Don’t do it!! They were awful and not even close to the real stovetop prep. Take the time and do it right. I also add a drop of white wine and a bit of lemon juice to the egg mixture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
Halve, then pound breasts very thin.
Air fry @370 for 7’

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Had breadcrumbs and not panko. Put one cutlet into the air fryer after a quick spray with oil. 400°F for 8 minutes, turning halfway through. Served with Chinese cucumbers with sesame oil from the NYT. A great pairing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I always add a little shaved fennel & artichoke to the salad.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I like to add a dollop of mayo mixed with sriracha for dipping the cutlets.  Nice spicy touch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: A weekly staple. Easy and tasty. We enjoy baked sweet potato as a side. Would recommend leaving the battered chicken (uncooked) in the fridge for at least 20 minutes before frying. Helps keep the breading on. I don't pound the breasts because I already buy them halved.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: No notes. A perfect, delicious winner winner

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: For those who want to avoid the frying…Melissa Clark has a great recipe for fish and chips (on the NYT site) which involves a similar flour, egg and breadcrumb coating that crisps up beautifully in the oven - no frying needed! The trick is to add a liberal amount of Dijon to the egg mixture and to toast the breadcrumbs prior to breading with whatever seasoning you like. Works like a dream. Check her recipe for a full explanation of the technique!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: You can also not fry them at all --- and instead, prepare a flat baking sheet by placing a sheet of parchment or foil, and spraying the foil with olive oil or the cooking spray of your choice  Lay the prepped chicken (all dipped and pankoed) on the sheet, and spray the surface with the oil.  Bake at 375 till golden.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This method for making crispy chicken at home is absolute perfection. I've made it over a dozen times now, and served it to many guests, one of whom commented that this tasted like "restaurant quality chicken." The only thing I do differently is that I usually add some additional seasoning to the breadcrumbs depending on how I'll serve the chicken (sometimes I throw in generic "italian" seasoning, sometimes cajun, etc.). Whole Foods also sells thin-sliced chicken, which is a nice shortcut.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: My recent twist on this recipe is to coat the cutlets in mayo that has been seasoned with salt and onion powder.  I then dip the chicken directly into panko mix, where I've added a bit of lemon zest and thyme, in addition to the onion powder, garlic powder and parmesan.  No flour or egg needed.  After many years of making "panko chicken", this latest mayo variation feels (and tastes) like the one!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I cook these in the air fryer instead of frying in a pan. Just give them a spritz of olive oil spray. Makes for a lower fat dinner. And less to clean up.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This was lovely. We added the Parmesan on top instead of in the breading since we’re in the middle of a move and didn’t have finely grated and it was still lovely. Frying in butter versus olive oil added a richer depth imo. Will be making again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Got tired of pounding chicken breasts.  Now I buy thin-cut.  Harder to find but not impossible.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: use less salt and fry less time, 2.5 min per side

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: @Connie—yes I would say decrease the salt by half.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
Francese flour based batter no breadcrumbs. 
Easier slightly different option smear cutlets with mayo dip in crumbs then proceed. 
Corn flake crumbs make a decent gf option.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Does anyone have guidance on baking this instead of frying it -time & temp?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I don't want to fry - can this be made in air fryer/oven?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Yes, depending on the thickness 5 - 8 minutes (total, not each side) at 400.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Why oh why do you use garlic and onion powder which essentially ruin the flavor of any dish. Either use the real thing or leave it out.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
Interesting comment.  Many world-renowned chefs have both ingredients in some of their recipes.

If it's good enough for them, it's good enough for me!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Powders ensure that the flavors are distributed evenly, especially when combined with dry ingredients. Powers are dried and ground onions/garlic. The salt versions have other additives.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: My friend is from Uruguay and his family makes this dish, but before breading they add chimichurri sauce. The mayonnaise according to him is required. This one of my favorite dishes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: A touch of bottled horseradish in the mayo (e.g. horseradish mayo) also special.  Rounding out the dinner with classic starch and veg and still be tasteful always difficult.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I advise using ghee for frying milanese. Oh, and skip that onion and garlic powders nonsense as they both do not even exist in Italy. For a video tutorial on the prepping, just watch Delphine Seyrig in "Jeanne Dielman, 23, quai du commerce, 1080 Bruxelles".

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I hate the splattery mess of frying so I cook these in the oven instead. I mix a couple tablespoons of EVO with the panko/parm mixture, place them on a rack on top of a foil-lined roasting pan and roast at 400 degrees for about 12-15 minutes. I also add some slivered red onion to the arugula salad. Soaking the onion in ice water for a few minutes first takes away some of the rawness. So good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Pyrex pie dishes are perfect for schnitzel prep. And peanut oil fries hotter and makes for extra crispiness.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
I read a great tip on slicing cutlets which I always use now. Put the chicken breasts in the freezer for 15-20 minutes. This will firm them up and make the horizontal slicing a lot easier and more precise.

Separately, I have used a stale baguette for the crumbs. Just slice into smallish pieces and run through a food processor until you have your desired "crumbiness".

Milanesa has always been one of my favorites from spending time at my grandmother's in Argentina.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Can these be "oven fried" using a heated cast iron skillet with 1 TBLSP of olive oil at 400F for 4 or 5 minutes per side?  I oven fry panko encrusted fish in that manner and it comes out perfectly ever time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: For those of us who *really* don't like to fry, is there an adjustment to this recipe such that it could be over-baked?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Forty-six years ago, I was a  young bride, and anxious to learn my husband's favorite recipes from his mother. She taught me this method: pound the breast pieces, dip them in melted butter, then in seasoned bread crumbs and parmesan, and bake on a hot cookie sheet in a 400 degree oven, flipping once. So perfect I haven't found any other method over the years that can improve on it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Often use "the mayo trick," rubbing cool cutlets with mayonnaise, then press them in a plate of crushed, seasoned panko, and last, fry -- in much less oil than suggested, maybe with a bit of butter. (I slice a breast as recipe says then pound lightly with a vintage wooden kitchen mallet, not too thin.) Everyone has their way.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! The time in the fridge seems to really help out. Made for excellent sandwiches the next day too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I never bother with pounding.  I just slice the breast filets in half lengthwise. Turn the heat down after you have the first sear and they will turn golden and never dry out.  Agreed that fridge time really helps.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is the bee's knees! It is unbelievably delicious. I used the hacks suggested by other cooks, including eliminating the flouring, and let it rest in the fridge after breading .  I added toasted pine nuts to the salad and doubled the seasonings in the breading.  And, I did the thing my Depression-era mom always did with breaded cutlety things: I made the leftover egg and breading into a special pancake, served with the cutlets. It was a truly delicious meal!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: For even cutets, pound each serving in a sandwich bag. The cutlet will form a uniform shape.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This is such a great, classic recipe: a thin filet of something, dipped in flour, egg, well-seasoned bread crumbs. Then nicely fried (you can use less oil.) The flavor and crunchiness here are perfect. In Texas, in the 1950s-60s, this was “chicken-fried steak,” and we made it countless times with tenderized veal cutlets.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Followed this recipe to a T except gluten free Panko. Really excellent! You do have to get the cutlets very thin and watch the heat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: My whole family loves this recipe and I make it often. I usually substitute with Italian bread crumbs and use grape seed oil for cooking. I also suggest pulling the chicken out of the fridge about an hour before cooking if you think of it. Instead of arugula, we serve with romaine and tomatoes dressed with Brianna’s French vinaigrette. Yum!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: A few years ago I stayed at the Four Seasons in Milan.  I asked how the Hotel made their fried sage leafs that they served in the lounge.  I was taken to the kitchen and given a lesson in "Milanese" breading.  They put plain grissini (store bought bread sticks) in a food processor, and then sift them  through a large holed kitchen strainer (like a vegetable strainer.) Then egg, then squeeze the excess egg off, then dredged in the grissini crumbs.  It is an excellent technique for breading.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This can easily be baked in the air fryer and turns out really well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: The best I’ve ever had.  I did brined my chicken overnight. So good

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
I learned from a Viennese chef, when frying make sure the oil is constantly on the cutlets. He did this by shaking the pan down and over. I'm not that talented I use a large spoon to lift out some of the oil and spread over while frying. I usually use my Bronx made Farberware 12" precious fry pan, but I'll try a smaller one as Alexa suggested. 
FYI, there is a noticeable difference in taste between small organic farm chicken and industrial. You can taste it even in a bland chicken breast.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: When we made this in the restaurant, we would put the parm in the egg mixture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I made as written except that I used avocado oil instead of olive oil for the chicken to avoid burning. I did use olive oil in the salad and I added some thin sliced of red onion to the tomatoes and arugula. Excellent and easy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: My family told me this is the best chicken I’ve ever cooked. I used thighs and pounded them out without slicing. I also had no arugula in the house, so I just paired it with a squeeze of lemon and a tomato salad this time. A definite hit!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I make a similar recipe regularly, and wanted to suggest a summery version I’ve made recently. I put the cooked cutlets on a brioche roll and top with homemade coleslaw (though good deli coleslaw would work too). Really delicious and a nice change from my usual salad accompaniment.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Definitely in the "gilding the lily" compartment.  To this wonderful recipe, I like to add toasted pignoli to the salad, a gilding I learned in Milano, of all places!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: The chicken was certainly crunchy from the panko coating, but I and everyone in my family failed to taste any parmesan cheese which was disappointing since the recipe calls for adding a 1/2 cup to the breading mixture.  It's also a tremendously wasteful recipe, using a full cup of olive oil to fry the chicken and then discarding it.  With a large bottle of olive oil now costing about $30, it also adds another expense to the recipe.  Messy cleanup as well and would not recommend.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Use a brioche bun, same Arugula and tomato salad, a little lemon aioli, and man, oh man, best chicken sandwich in the tri-state area.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I learned from an exceptional Japanese tonkatsu (pork cutlet) chef in Tokyo a key to cooking fried cutlets perfectly. Heat the oil and maintain it at 320 deg F. Use an instant read thermometer. Cook the cutlet until it is a nice golden in color - just before it starts to turn brown. Check the oil temperature regularly and adjust heat as necessary to stay as close to 320F as reasonably possible. This removes the guesswork from frying a perfect cutlet.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I don't see the difference between "Milanese" and "Francese"? Anyone care to elaborate?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Milanese and schnitzel are the same dish. Francese is Milanese with a specific sauce (much like marsala).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Francese incorporates only flour, not flour and breadcrumbs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: from my understanding- chicken milanese are basically fried chicken cutlets and franchase is made with flour and eggs

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Try topping with a strip of prosciutto and melt some mozzarella on it, cover with some tomato sauce and you have a Bolognese cutlet. Excellent!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Do you have a GF version of this? What would be best to replace the flour with?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Can you bake that also instead of frying?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Question… Do any of you cooks have a good suggestion for eliminating/reducing the splatter from cooking the chicken? I love this kind of dish, but hate spending the extra 10 minutes cleaning the range top when done.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: The best way to avoid the mess from frying is to not fry. Try baking instead. If you aren't concerned with the added fat of frying anyway, you could just LIBERALLY spray with oil (both sides) or you can even mix your fat with the coating before breading. Then just flip them once or twice while cooking. I use non-stick foil for easy clean up. They won't be exactly the same as fried but close enough to make less clean up worth it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I use a "Frywall" splatter guard all the time. Buy on Amazon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Get yourself a splatter screen.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: For Heather … use any all purpose GF flour and GF panko and/or breadcrumbs. I’ve doing this for many years, it works just fine. When my kids were little, they called this, “Daddy’s Famous Chicken.”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: i dont know what i do, but i cant get the crumbs to stick some stick but not beautifully and they fall off immediately when you cut into it. what am i doing wrong

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: My only adjustment to this recipe is that i dip the cutlets into a mixture of lemon and olive oil and red chili pepper flakes and grill them in a grill pan on my stove without breading.  I also make a heartier salad by adding cucumbers, and parmigiana cheese.  i personally do not need the extra carb and fat calories of the breading.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This might be sacrareligious but can this be made in an airfryer? What steps would you change or leave out, if so?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Make extras, because this works great as a sandwich filling, or in a pastry, too.  Look up Torte (or Tourte) Milanese, Cheese, Spinach, Peppers and the Milanese instead of the eggs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: The recipe is perfect! Most of the time I forego the cheese and mix crushed cheddar Goldfish (1:1 with panko) for the breading instead. Seasoning is excellent with chicken, pork, beef. This is also how I make pork schnitzel and sometimes country fried steak. Maldon Salt is my favorite finishing touch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Why does every recipe for chicken breasts eliminate the skin? That's where all the flavor is.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Use the skin to make schmaltz, and fry in schmaltz. (Schmaltz = chicken fat.) Basically, this is schnitzel (minus the cheese).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Whenever a recipe appears that calls for skin-on chicken, many commenters will post that they used boneless-skinless chicken pieces because "that's what I had." I believe they mean "that's the only way I buy chicken", but that ain't no nevermind: people don't like the skin. Beef, pork and veal schnitzels don't involve skin. Why should chicken?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I learned a great new trick. Beat the eggs & flour together & then dip the cutlets in that batter & then dredge the cutlets in the panko mix. You eliminate 1 bowl to clean & it works just fine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I toast the panko before breading. Very crispy without overcooking the thin chicken breadts.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I don't understand how this isn't fried chicken. I make this all the time and thankfully I have an induction stovetop for easy cleaning because the oil splatters everywhere.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Can’t say I’m fond of the idea of onion and garlic powders. Also, I fry them in safflower oil. You can heat it hotter than olive oil producing a crispier coating.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: At the end of cooking the chicken, add a few spoonfuls of butter to the pan, which will then separate the breading from the chicken (old Viennese trick for perfect schnitzel)!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: An old recipe that I've used for over 40 years. Nothing new here. Forget the Panko and use regular bread crumbs. Panko is so 80s.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: While I do like panko crumbs, you are spot on there is nothing new. It is a twist on schnitzel. Schnitzel doesn't need a twist.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I've been making these for years--but use boneless chicken thighs...it may take a little longer to prep them, but they are much tastier than a chicken breast!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Nice recipe here. If you are looking for a lower-oil recipe, you can shallow fry the pounded cutlets in the amount of oil stated in the recipe, much or less. Baste the top surface continuously with the hot oil while cooking. But 1 cup of oil may not provide 1/2 inch of oil in a medium-sized sauté pan that will accommodate a thinly-pounded 5oz chicken breast. Unless you add in the chicken breast into a small sauté pan with the hot oil and then measure, at least for me. But I loved this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: 3 minutes per side for the chicken is way too long!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: What is seasoned salt?  What is in it?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: You can find it in a jar at your grocer. Each brand is different. It might included Italian seasonings, Greek seasonings... lemon, etc. Read the label for details.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: OK, forget the whole separate eggs and flour thing. Step 1:  Take the eggs and mix with flour and if the mixture is too thick, add a little water.  Also throw in the seasoned salt.  Step 2: dredge chicken in mixture, drain off a little liquid and then put in panko mixture. Step 3: Press the chicken into the panko as this will prevent crumb falling off in the pan.    Pro tip:  never crowd the pan as this lowers the heat of the oil as this makes it more oily and more prone to uneven frying.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This method works better the more you have to crumb, I like to add a dash of Kikkomon soy sauce as well as freshly ground pepper to the egg flour water mix

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: If you have an egg allergy, I substitute melted butter and dip the chicken thoroughly, then bread it. It will allow the breadcrumbs or panko to adhere.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight with zero expectation of HOW it should be, used our fave TJ dressing on the salad, topped the chicken with a lemon herb aioli from Stonewall kitchen because I’m a busy parent. Took a bit of prep but not overwhelming. It was a hit with the fam.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This is a go-to meal at our house. I have a small sink and a small kitchen so do all the breading in advance and place cutlets on a wire rack over a parchment-lined sheet pan in the fridge. When you are ready to fry, everything comes together quickly with little mess. Delicious. Amazing with the salad on top.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This seems perfectly fine. It occurs to me that the only thing new about the recipe is the addition of the word “new”. Otherwise it’s the breaded meat recipe that my Mother learned from her Grandmother. Of course she would have used proper spices, but I suppose we live in a powdery world.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Nice simple recipe.  Try seasoning the eggs used between the flour and panko layers with a decent dry white vermouth.  Staging the cutlets un the fridge for at least an hour before cooking will make the bread crumbs stick firmly and stay nice and crisp.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Adding a dab of coarse mustard to the olive oil and lemon juice salad dressing will help keep it emulsified.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous receipt simple instructions classic

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Frying with lard is far more consistenly crispier than with olive oil. An egg or an omelet, fine, but not anything breaded.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: And I always have a supply of lard. Not!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: What temperature and how long do you cook the chicken after its fried?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Once the chicken is fried, it's cooked. Nothing more to do.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: this was perfect the way it was !!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: For a more delicious crust try matzo meal (fine grind)instead of panko it will adhere better and add much more flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I thought this recipe worked beautifully exactly as written! I didn't change a thing, other than to make my salad with a lemony ranch dressing (which was also excellent drizzled on the chicken!) Next time, I'll use half the amount of panko/cheese bc I had so much left-over and it had to go in the trash once I'd dipped raw chicken into it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Rather than cheese, I use dried and/or fresh herbs in the flour and breadcrumbs. Personally, I prefer not to use onion or garlic powder in anything, so I'll include chives instead.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: "Invented" this for our son's birthday dinners decades ago, but called it Mock Wienerschitzel. Served with lemon wedges and watercress.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: We made this exactly according to the recipe but added smoked paprika to the breadcrumbs. It was heavenly with an astringent salad.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Mine was not crispy and followed directions.  Not worth the trouble of doing again.  I'd find a better chicken farm recipe and make adjustments with the salad and omit red sauce.  left in refrigerator 15 min. plus for crispness and wasn't that great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
Perhaps the oil was not hot enough, or there wasn't enough oil in the pan ? That is the key to crispy Milanese, or Schnitzel as it is in my house.  Put the cutlet into the hot oil, shake the pan a few times to keep the cutlet from sticking and also to get some of the hot oil on top of the cutlet.  Don't move it until it is time to flip.  Flip, shake the pan again, and when done, place on a wire rack to drain any excess oil. 
Don't use paper towels.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: For a gluten free coating, cornflake crumbs are a good substitute.  I like the air fryer and baking methods over deep oil frying to avoid having a lot of hot oil to mess with.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Olive oil does not seem appropriate for pan frying. I would rather use canola oil as it is more heat resistant.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Olive oil is perfectly appropriate for frying. No need to use the fancy stuff. See this from the North American Olive Oil Association: https://www.aboutoliveoil.org/why-olive-oil-is-the-best-oil-for-frying

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Delightfully delicious for summer evenings. Add a slice of lemon to squeeze over the meat before enjoying the brightness.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: The key instruction, and most appreciated, is to make the oil (go neutral btw) 1/2 inch deep in the pan. Don’t skimp on oil and your filets will turn out just like they do in the photo. I served this on buttered, skillet-toasted rustic white bread with (gasp) Chick-fil-A sauce and the family loved it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and added fresh parsley to the panko. It came out restaurant quality. I’ll make it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Im originally from Pa dutch country and minus the parm this is schnitzel, a favorite.  I love it with the arugula salad or spatzel.  Arugula… its a veg-a-ta-ble (My Blue Heaven)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I made it last night for an easy "company" dish. Out of necessity, I took the suggestion of 1/2 panko, 1/2 breadcrumb. Also the suggestions to: chill in the frig for 15 minutes after breading, and use chicken tenderloins to save an extra minute. They were perfect. A light salad and pickled veggies ...and I was done.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding. I'm putting this on repeat. When I serve it to guests it'll nudge it just over the top with a shower of parsley and a dollop of homemade mayo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
This was the perfect simple and light meal on a warm day! Cheated and used pre-cut cutlets but prepared the chicken exactly as the recipe stated. The accompanying salad was perfection - only change I would make next time would be to add shaved fennel and thinly sliced cucumbers to the salad to add
more crunch. Would also consider some aioli on the side and topping the cutlets with fresh herbs (parsley and chives).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Used many helpful tips here. Skipped the flour and placed breaded chicken in fridge for an hour. Empire chicken breasts, not sliced thin, cooked 4 & 4 minutes on each side, then about 2-3 on each side -- chicken was moist but could use more flavor. Will try a couple of garlics with the egg dip next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Used this recipe to make chicken strips.  Big hit with my six-year-old!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Is it ok to make this recipe with pork tenderloin instead of the chicken!,,

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
So very good and easy..
I admit to a short cut: I bought chicken tenders which worked out beautifully. 
Also, took the advise of others, and placed the bread meat in fridge before frying. Good move.
The salad was a perfect accompaniment.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I prefer regular Progresso bread crumbs over Panko. Panko crumbs absorb much more oil in the frying. I also put grated parm in the crumbs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
Good recipe.  I make it basically the same way.  Two suggested tweaks:

1.  I would add just a touch of cayenne to the  breading.  It’s not traditional but it works really well.  

2.  Don’t fry with olive oil, which will smoke and leave burnt flavors.  I typically use half ghee and half vegetable oil, but anything with a high smoke point will work.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: My new wife and I prepared this as is. Very simple and delicious. No modification necessary. Even my teen sons loved it, will now be an easy go to meal during the week.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Cooked exactly as directed and it was delicious, plus so pretty on the plate with the salad! I appreciated some of the tips from others about resting in the fridge after breading and before cooking. I'll try that next time!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This is a truly wonderful recipe.  I slice the chicken breasts horizontally and as suggested, don't pound them or dredge them in flour before coating.  I do refrigerate after coating, and have also found that the breaded, uncooked breasts can be frozen, thawed, and successfully fried.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight with a lemon arugula salad and butter garlic pasta. It was so good! The only thing I modified was the cook time. I had avocado oil in my skillet at about level 6 heat and it only took 2 minutes per side to cook through. I checked the temp as the crust was getting very brown and it was 175/180 degrees so slightly over cooked but glad I didn’t continue cooking 1 extra minute per side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Prepared this following notes from others to refrigerate prior to sautéing.  Also, I didn’t have panko so used crushed ritz.  This was amazingly delicious. Salad was perfection and squeeze of lemon over the whole plate was the final touch. So yum. Thanks NYT cooking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Way too much salt. If one doesn’t have onion and garlic powder, could adobo be substituted?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Why not?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  I love breaded chicken breasts and the seasonings made it even better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Simple and very delish!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I actually like reading these and other recipe comments. "Well my mother used to do this... My grandmother used to do this... Instead of this, use that..." Well, that would be almost another recipe entirely folks! Here's my comment, "Cooked this with my new wife over the summer, very tasty.. oh and the fish was too!"

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: To air fry instead, preheat air fryer to 400°. Generously spray breaded chicken with olive oil to coat. Cook each breast for 7 minutes turning once halfway.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Add cayenne pepper to onion Powder mixture and to panko and flour mixture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, but next time I’m going to halve the salt just to experiment a bit. The olive oil and lemon salad dressing was outstanding.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: You can cook this in a lot less oil and it still comes out crispy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: delicious! used canola oil for frying and slap ya mama seasoning in place of the garlic and onion powder.  3 mins per side was crispy/juicy perfection!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Even the finicky eaters at the table enjoyed this recipe. I didn't change a thing in the cooking, and even did a vegan patty with the same breading and it came out nicely. We paired with a Russian River Valley Chardonnay and that was lovely.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Another keeper. A far cry from the Aussie pub staple, snitty “(we can’t manage the ‘sch’ combo).  Really liked the garlic/onion powder mix and the “parmie “ in the crumbs. Accompanying salad delicious, light, astringent and a perfect foil for the rich crust on the chicken.  Not much for leftovers.  Make more next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I added some minced parsley to the breading. Used 3 eggs to make sure I did not run out. Very flavorful!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Wow.  This was fantastic.  I followed the recipe precisely.  My only comment is that I don’t believe the nutritional are correct.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Absolute perfection. Now a household favorite. The only dish I cook that elicits a “yessss” from every member of the family.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Realized I was out of egg so I used mayonnaise instead before breading. Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I did panko, potato starch, and egg, and it fried quickly and lightly and everyone was very pleased.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: When using the air fryer: at what temperature and for how long? Thanks

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I double breaded the cutlets and doubled the recipe chill on a rack in fridge until it's dinner time - prepping them in the morning makes cooking dinner a breeze.. The leftovers make great sandwiches. They also find their way into salads of all sorts.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Big thumbs-up, even from the picky SO. I'm gluten-free so I used smashed-up Rice Chex (Trader Joe's version) and all-purpose gluten-free flour for the breading. Worked very well and was quite crispy. Also, added quite a lot of white pepper to the spice mix. The SO suggested next time putting a bay leaf in the oil while frying. Thought the recipe called for way too much olive oil for frying – about half that was just fine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: With the fresh arugula from the garden, a wonderful bite.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: The flour isn’t necessary. I just used the panko and Parmesan mixture and they turned out great!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Obsessed with this easy and quick dish that’s delicious exactly as written. Ive experimented with various salads and as long as it is bright and not too heavy, it works.  Sometimes I dress it up with lemon juice and capers but not necessary.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
Way too salty for me. I ditched the flour and chilled the coated cutlets and they came
Out perfect but I would half the salt in the seasoning

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: @Jessica Lanier it’s a shame you ditched the flour because that makes for a faster fry

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Can you use boneless, skinless chicken thighs for this? Pound them out and do the same breading and cooking? I do not like breast meat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I make this for one (one breast does 2 meals!).  Instead of wasting half an egg, I use heavy cream as the "wet" base.  I also prefer cornstarch to flour- not sure why, I just tried it and it worked.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I added sumac to the spice rub and it was delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This is a go-to recipe in our kitchen. Thank you! I recently swapped out the pan for the air fryer and it comes out delicious and crispy without all of the mess on the stove. Be sure to give the chicken a spray of oil on both sides (I use avocado or olive oil) before air frying for 18 minutes. Perfect!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Not a tip but taking this to the Hollywood Bowl this Friday. We are leaving out home at 5 pm and it’s about an hour trip with driving and parking, checking in etc. we have something to keep them warm. I’m hoping it will still be crispy. If it’s lukewarm, that’s okay. What do you experts think?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: These turned out beautifully. I had pretty large cuts, so I cut them in half down the middle before slicing horizontally. I also let them rest in a square baking dish in the fridge for a couple of hours before frying. Didn't have any trouble with breading coming off during frying. They were delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
Incredible recipe. 
We sometimes add in fennel to the salad, sliced thin with a mandolin.
When slicing the chicken breast, I only slice 2/3's of the length and then cut off the top portion.
I do this to prevent the last 1/3 from being too thin.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: So delicious! I skipped the flour step because I was out of it and it came out fine. Followed recipe except for adding a bit of dried oregano, chopped fresh basil and the zest of a small lemon to the breading. Did not refrigerate after coating, just let them sit for 5 minutes and in to the frying pan. I lost very little breading and the crisped up beautifully. Everyone raved!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
Oh my heavens!
It is French Fried Chicken, and it is devastating! 
Make It!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
While I am certain this method is delicious I am married to an Italian whose Grandmother taught me all of their favorites. Italian households prepare the same meal differently, which means there are many variations and all of them good. 
We take our breasts and split each horizontally; therefore no need to pound flat. We then run through beaten egg with a hint of cream in the mixture. Then we dredge through seasoned bread crumbs. Keep in mind each step is seasoned well with salt and pepper. They are then fried in olive oil until golden. We make a salad of only mixed baby greens with a vinaigrette of olive oil, salt, pepper, garlic and a little dijon mustard and a splash of champagne vinegar. Dress the greens, and plate along side chicken with a lemon wedge. Sometimes we also add a small mound of pasta dressed with aioli.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Didn't have panko, so used regular bread crumbs. Followed the advice of another commenter to put the breaded chicken in the ridge for 15 mins before frying. Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: I added 1 tsp Aleppo pepper to spice blend. Adds a hint of hint without sharpness.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: My kids gave this a unanimous 4.9 out of 5, which was generous considering I forgot to buy panko and compensated by double dredging the chicken in flour (mixed with the seasonings and parmesan). They both felt it needed some kind of sauce, hence the .1 point deduction. Tough crowd! I felt proud that I fried something without burning down the house.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This was soooo good... Made April 2026- Salad good... put the onion/ garlic into the flour mixture. I also used chinese panko and seasoned and did crush in mini chopper. Also put breaded cutlets in fridge ... used a combo of avocado and olive oil - it was honest the best and served w/ orzo on the side... buttered with starchy water and some parm ... very tasty meal

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: @Jessica Lanier I had same experience and am surprised others didnt mention. I knew it was a lot but figured I'd go as written the 1st time making it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: This dish is bonkers delicious. I’ve made it a few times, in my favorite version I skip the flour, add some dried oregano and lemon zest to the breading and a glug of balsamic to the dressing. Chilling the breaded cutlets for half an hour really helps hold it all together.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous!  I make this all the time because it's easy and always delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Chicken Milanese Recipe
commentBody:
Wow! So very good just as the recipe is. I bought chicken breast cutlets from the store, so no need to pound the meat. I added Italian herbs. 
Will make this again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: This is even easier if you poach the eggs in the oven.  Add a Tablespoon of water to each well in a muffin pan—even the ones that won’t get an egg.  Crack an egg in to a muffin cup. Bake at 350 for 11-13 minutes (depending on your oven and how runny you like your yolks.    They all finish at the same time and require no minding while they cook.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Very similar to a recipe I've been making for years. Instead of the pepper flakes, make a spoonful of the butter-olive oil sauce with smoked paprika and mint that gets drizzled on top of the garlic-yogurt and egg. I also use garlic paste from a tube, which is easy in the morning and not quite as pungent. A favorite breakfast!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Stop fiddling around with vinegar and swirling. Follow Kenji’s advice and drain the excess watery whites from the eggs in a fine strainer, then slide the drained eggs into the simmering water. Perfect results every single time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
nobody wants to wash a strainer after cooking breakfast for themselves

just get fresher eggs

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Sam Sifton shows a Julia Child tip in his poach an egg video - a 10 sec bath in the shell in simmering water before cracking the egg - that is much less trouble than straining an egg. Super easy, no clean up, perfect poached egg.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I make this easier by making the pepper butter (or oil), then gently frying the eggs in that fat rather than poaching them. The eggs and the spiced butter all in one step. Life is easier without worrying about poaching!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Saw this recently on America’s Test Kitchen (ATK) where I learned that palace chefs in the Ottoman Empire auditioned for the royal kitchens by making an egg dish such as this one. Poached eggs can go so wrong, so making a perfect one could get you high rank in the palace kitchen. The poaching method ATK used: strain the egg then slide it into just-boiled water with salt and vinegar for about three minutes with the lid on and heat turned off. Best-looking poached egg ever!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: After mincing/grating your garlic you can squeeze in a little lemon juice and let it set for a few minutes before adding it to the yogurt; this mellows the garlic.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
Try mincing the garlic finely, then add some
Salt to mince it finer. Then smash with the side of your chefs knife to form into a paste. Add TT (to taste)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
@JAK

What is TT???

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: @a canuck cooks TT = to taste, but it took me a while to figure that out!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. I would reduce garlic by half. Also , easier and cleaner to melt butter in microwave glass cup vs stove top

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Found the grated garlic a bit overwhelming as the juices are releases.  A little goes a long way.  Otherwise this was fabulous.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Saw this recipe just before lunchtime and I had to have it. Not the neatest poached eggs I've ever made but I would put the butter-aleppo pepper mix on anything. I used labne and did not find the garlic overwhelming. Absolutely delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: @Es Totally! I had it with TJ's tomato labneh dip and their simit (bread). When the bread was warming up I added few sweet peppers and tomatoes to the same pan. I made the poached eggs in the microwave. Butter-aleppo pepper-mint leaves dressing was FIRE!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: The flavor is outstanding

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: This was scrumptious - and quick.  In the future - I know I'll be making it again! - I would change just one thing: I wish I had salted the yogurt a bit, as thee dish needed a bit more oomph.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: @LW I agree! I add a bit of everything but the bagel seasoning to my yogurt as it comes to room temp and it really adds great flavor here.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Using Black Urfa Chili instead of Aleppo or red chili flakes, is fabulous!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I have a lazy version of this that I absolutely love. Greek yogurt.  Light shake of Penzey's granulated garlic, let sit.  (I'm not mincing garlic in the morning, full stop.)  10 minutes is enough to infuse flavor.  1-2tsp grapeseed oil in skillet on medium-- shake in Aleppo pepper and bloom it in oil; fry egg in the oil, taking care not to break the yolk. (I add a little water to create steam and clamp a lid on it to avoid needing to flip the runny egg.  Dump egg on yogurt, serve with bread.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: @Erica A crispy egg fried in that spiced butter was amazing!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: For a smoky flavor, substitute Urfa for Aleppo pepper.  It goes nicely with roasted eggplant on the side too

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
Works best with what we call in Europe, natural yogurt, or real yogurt.
Better for the balance of flavours; acid/alkaline ratio; digestion; gut-health, etc. 
The newly invented American yogurt is almost double cream, or clotted cream!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: great for brunch, but we found it lacked a little flavor-wise. adding a bit of salt and maybe a bit of lemon (depending on the acidity of your yoghurt) to the yoghurt is one step. The other is to use sumac on top, after you drizzle the oil, plus fresh mint along with the dill if you really wanna be fancy

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: This is even better if you add ground cumin to the melted butter sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I have been making this dish for years,  except--confession here--I fry the egg.  Keep the yolk runny and it tastes great, and I find fried egg easier to handle than poached.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: spoonful of chile crisp in ghee was excellent here!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Super delicious. Made with fried egg with soft yolk. I have a Lebanese restaurant around the corner from me that makes absolutely amazing hot sauce. I have used that in place of the Aleppo butter. Going to try it with chimichuri next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I made chili crisp in the past that I keep in the fridge with TVP, mushroom powder, crunchy garlic, several kinds of chili peppers in avocado oil. It’s so tasty and easy to spread a little on my crusty bread and swirl the rest over the poached eggs and yogurt. So much savory spicy flavor

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: What is TVP?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: This was great! As others have mentioned, I recommend adding a little lemon and salt to the yogurt after mixing with the garlic.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: A squeeze of lemon over the herbs, eggs and yogurt is sublime.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I love these eggs. But sometimes I just use a gentle fried egg (with runny yolks intact!) when I don’t feel like poaching. Works really well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: used some toum we had on hand and labneh as a shortcut for the yogurt. we're unlucky with poached eggs, works just as well with fried. served with potatoes in lieu of bread

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Dead easy and so delicious. try to use farm fresh eggs and if you enjoy garlic add more. Add dry mint for sure and a pinch of sumac...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Superb results and gorgeously creamy

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: All that fuss for shaping poached eggs? The vinegar does the trick.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: but eggs taste like vinegar!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I made this for one so halved the recipe but i doubled the garlic. I didnt have herbs but topped with pepitas (shelled pumpkin seeds). Loved it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the garlic and loved it. Added salt to the yogurt. Used chili crisp from a jar - so easy and so good! Crusty bread is necessary.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I made my portion extra garlicky.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: The garlic is a bit much. I’d decrease it or just delete it. I make this dish with over easy eggs rather than the pouched. Chopped mint sprinkled over the yogurt is quite swell. I use my own homemade probiotic yogurt, the finishing taste is more intense.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I made two minor adjustments.  No Aleppo so I used a combination of smoked paprika and crushed red pepper flakes.  I served it with Greek pita, some quick sautéed spinach, and a light sprinkle of finely chopped feta. The garlic was a little potent, but the addition of the spinach helped it not be TOO overpowering.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious.  Will make often.  Made for just one person.  Less garlic (per notes), added some lemon and salt to yogurt also bc why not.  Used mint and dill on top, but almost anything would work here (cilantro, scallions, basil even?).  I used butter not oil and browned it a bit.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Instead of blooming the Aleppo pepper, I used chili crisp from a jar. Delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
Great taste. Pretty straight forward recipe. I even forgot to include the vinegar and it still turned out well. The red pepper flakes worked. I think it would be fine with 2 tablespoons of oil. I'd say it could also have a little more garlic in it.
Well received by all.
Tim

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Instead of adding the raw garlic to the yogurt, try sauteing it with the aleppo peppers in oil. Takes the pungence off and gives a nice blended finish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, as is!  Note - no need to add vinegar to the poaching water. Egg whites become more watery w time (even a week or so old), and store-bought eggs can be a few weeks old. Strain your eggs in a fine mesh strainer to remove the watery bits that make your poached eggs look unkempt.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: This also works well with fried eggs. Just enjoyed it immensely with a fried duck egg, asparagus and avocado. So delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! I added a little olive oil and a tiny pinch of salt to the yogurt and used a combination of parsley and dill because I had both on hand. WOW!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Try warming the Yoghurt mixture in the microwave.  5-10 seconds, taste test as you time it.  Then place cooked eggs onto the Yoghurt.  Also fried eggs can be placed onto the Yoghurt bed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Nice recipe and so easy. I put the bread into the skillet of red pepper and butter to coat on both sides, then toasted it in the oven. Perfection.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: After a trip to Israel I came back with a resolve to never eat sweetened yogurt again.  I fell in love with savory toppings on yogurt.   I love this recipe for breakfast, the only difference I made is to lightly fry eggs sunny-side up and slip them on to the yogurt.   Also makes a quick dinner when cooking for one.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I'm broke so that's all I had at home and it was a very nice and easy dinner :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
This is a quick and rewarding dish with a nice kick. 

I microwaved the butter mixture with some chilli flakes and it turned out great. 

Next time I will do some fried potato and a rocket side salad to round out this meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. I had it with sautéed spinach and it was perfection.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I liked this! Had a bunch of leftover yogurt and put it to good use :) I didn't have Aleppo so I made a spice mix with paprika, cayenne, and cumin; and I just fried my eggs in the pan after I was done with the spice-butter. Loved the garlic-yogurt as-is! Turned out nice!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: We also add turmeric to the butter, and sometimes garnish the bowl with thin avocado slices. As for the garlic, we alternate between mixing fresh garlic into the yogurt before or topping the dish with fried garlic.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: One portion of this can last for a weekend, used 3 eggs. Add mint and cumin to the butter. Had leftover herbs, added to tougher mixture. Don’t put naan too early to toast. Can use more water in the skillet to poach eggs. May try to heat butter and spices in the skillet (make brown butter!) and then fry eggs in that same skillet.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. The garlic really made it. I had a bunch of backyard tomatoes, so I grated a small one in the oil and included another small chopped tomato on top. Delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: really delicious! did everything in the microwave (eggs & butter), and it took about 5 minutes. only used about 3/4 of a garlic clove and it was still pungent! Would cut down on the garlic next time, especially if there’s anywhere I need to be afterwards.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I love this dish! I always add lots of dill and sometimes substitute the poached eggs for soft boiled ones.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Didn't have anything in the house but some eggs and a little leftover yogurt. This made a fantastic last minute dinner for one. Didn't have Aleppo pepper or red pepper flakes either so sprinkled it with Tajin at the end and it was FANTASTIC! Can't wait to have it again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
I didn't have Aleppo pepper, so I put crushed chili flakes in the butter, plus a little smoked paprika and harissa. Oh, and definitely salt the yogurt like another commenter said.

Honestly, I don't think I'd bother with the eggs in this dish if I made it again. the yogurt with the browned butter and parsley was tasty enough.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Reduce the hassle.  You can poach multiple eggs simply in a minute or two in an instant pot.  (Bonus hint: IP makes great hardboiled eggs too.)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
I LOVE this recipe!! A couple of hints: when poaching eggs, crack each egg separately into a sieve (not too fine), let the thin white part run out, then pour the eggs into a small separate  bowls. This way no need for vinegar in the water and you get a perfect poached egg every time.

Also, I heated a little butter, a little olive oil, and instead of using Aleppo chili, added a pinch of smoked paprika and a spoonful of Chili crunch...stirring for 30 seconds. Dee-licious!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Good gracious, four bowls for eggs? No, crack one in a bowl, add it to the water, then crack then next egg, etc. I have not yet tried this recipe, but I have poached hundreds of eggs, maybe thousands. No reason to dirty so many bowls. But if you must, try custard cups, since they are small.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Am I the only one who uses an egg-poaching pan insert? Flawless eggs every time, no fiddling about. Also, this recipe made me drool. I need to actually try MAKING it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: If you're lazier/ can't be bothered to poach eggs-- just cook them in the oil. Put the lid on your pan after you crak the eggs in and keep the temps really it low so the whites are set but the yolks are still runny. This is how I do it and it's great everytime :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: It's not traditional, but this is also fantastic with smoked paprika instead of Aleppo pepper

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I didn't need all the Aleppo pepper butter, otherwise truly great! I will definitely make this again, next time for guests.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Labneh is the way to go!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Yummy, maybe a hint of lemon might make it even better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
Made this and used a medium clove of garlic and it was a delicious revelation. 
Also made a Turkish Shepherd’s Salad and Turkish Flower Bread. The best brunch we have made in quite a while. Will be making again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: again too much hot  pepper. What gives?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic! For those who can't find Aleppo pepper, I used an equal amount of Sambal Oelek and it worked great (although watch for splattering from the water in the sambal hitting the hot oil).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I've been making this for a few years now as a high protein breakfast. The garlic amount is up to your preference. I'm a garlic girl so I use one clove but some days I use garlic powder to give a subtle garlic flavour. I add a small squeeze of lemon to brighten the yogurt. Some days when I am lazy I use 6-7 minutes boiled eggs instead of poaching. But overall great dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Cries for Chili Crisp

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
