commentBody: I found this delicious. Added some of my homemade preserved lemon chopped finely on top of the garlicky yogurt. Didn't have Aleppo pepper, so I used gochujang.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious soup. I replaced the barley with white beans and it turned out great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I served this recipe with a sauteed red onion, deep green pepper, spinach and garlic along with chopped radish and sliced Roma tomato.  Beautifully finished the look of the dish and complimented the creamy poached eggs over yogurt base.  Dotted dish with capers for finishing touch of flavor.  Fantastic!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: The garlic was a bit much for me. Next time maybe powder or a little less. The yogurt and pepper butter drizzle was a nice addition. Overall a great start to the day!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Never liked poached eggs so fried them. And, not having Aleppo pepper I used some crispy chili oil so I only kind of made this but it was tasty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, fast and easy! Definitely going into my steady rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: This was soooo good, even with adjustments I had to make because of what I had on hand: garlic powder instead of fresh, dried dill instead of fresh, Chinese red pepper flakes instead of Aleppo, and poaching the eggs in the microwave because my stove was broken...and I added fresh parsley 'cause I did have that....I look forward to trying it with the correct ingredients, tho I'll probably stick with using the microwave to poach the eggs...This recipe really makes a simple breakfast seem special

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Served with garlic yogurt over toast, then eggs on top. Great breakfast dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Being lazy, I just kinda sauted the eggs in the butter sauce. Added a layer of pea shoots between the yogurt and the eggs: worked just fine. Thanks for this inspiration!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I made this in 15min on a Sunday morning. It had depth of flavor and subtle heat. It definite needs the crunchy bread! Highly recommend.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I often make poached eggs on Sunday, so I was delighted to find this tasty version. But we only eat one extra-large egg apiece. I reduced the amount of Greek yogurt to a heaping half-cup, and the garlic to a very small clove. Thanks to the reader who suggested smoked paprika & mint instead of chili pepper/red pepper flakes. I used about a T of oil and a few shakes of paprika. Totally yummy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I make Turkish eggs all time and I have some recommendations. Sub w cayenne paprika for pantry simplicity. Mint is my preference over dill. And most importantly, add fresh lemon juice to the pepper butter sauce. Serve w toasted sourdough. Chefs kiss.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Sensational! The only variations I made were using a 50/50 blend of olive oil and clarified butter. I also used a finishing Aleppo/Sumac/Paprika/Salt mixture sprinkled on the eggs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Ozlem's recipes are all amazing FYI here blog is such a great resource, highly recommend getting her book.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I would add purslane or cucumber to the garlic yogurt, plus some salt for a bit of a crunch and flavor. This makes it more like cacik.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: If your Greek yogurt seems too soupy for this dish, use labneh instead - same flavor, and luxuriously thick.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I followed the tip of using smoked paprika and also added some of Trader Joe's hot and smoky red pepper powder. I also used leftover tzatziki as the yogurt and topped with fresh parsley and cilantro. Absolutely such a gorgeous dish!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: It was just okayl The spice butter turned out burnt really fast.... and the whole dish just just had strange flavors

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Good recipe, consider salting the yoghurt as well

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I like to put the garlic in the butter, as that makes it less pungent since I‘m not the biggest fan of garlic breath in the morning

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
After seeing a variation of this dish on Beryl Shereshewsky’s YouTube channel quite some time ago, I attempted it (from memory) for dinner tonight. It was great, and I look forward to trying this official recipe! 

I’m also contemplating adding a layer of thinly sliced sautéed eggplant between the yogurt and the eggs to make it a bit more of a substantial meal. We’ll see how that goes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: If you pour 2 cups of boiling water over the garlic, the flavor mellows.  This was excellent with fresh baked Turkish flatbread.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Way easier to use ATK's sous vide poached eggs method: 167 degrees for 12 minutes. Excellent flavor for a weekend breakfast!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
Add ginger and green onions to chili oil 
Mix the dill into the yogurt

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I make this 2-3 times per week. I do not think the garlic is overwhelming, but I would suggest letting it sit for a little while to mellow out. There’s an Italian butter made from Parma cows that’s a bit pricey but has a light grassy taste to it that I think really compliments the pepper! Favorite herbs I’ve used: chives/scallions, dill, parsley, and mint. Ideally a combo of all of them is just an amazing burst of freshness.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious. Used Dahi yogurt. Poached eggs in the microwave. Used flat iron chiles smoky blend instead of Aleppo. Ate with some toasted Italian bread I need to use up.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody:
I love poached eggs and I love yogurt, but for the life of me I could not figure out how this could make sense. 
Let me tell you, this is so cosy and fulfilling, after a long day at work it will make you feel your soul again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: This was good! Would add lemon and more salt to the yogurt base next time

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Incredibly tasty, more than the sum of its parts. I accidentally browned the butter, but it was a happy accident—tasted so good I could have easily eaten twice as much as I did. I topped it with dried dill and mint, black pepper, and flaky sea salt. I didn’t think the garlic was too much—ended up frying the last little heel of it that was too hard to grate in the butter with the Aleppo pepper. Can’t wait to try this again with fresh herbs to top it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: This was so delicious. I did substitute the aleppo pepper for hot paprika, tho.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I add a little dill to the yogurt and cumin to the butter. If she's making it she poaches as it says, I soft boil instead

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I made only the garlic yogurt and served with keema (also from NYT Cooking) on tortillas (fusion is a go) and found that they went together beautifully and made a whole meal. The keema provides the spice and flavour that the butter-aleppo + garnish does here.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Use the herbs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: How does the cold yogurt taste with the hot eggs. I’m concerned about the coldness.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: @Kat Nigella Lawson’s version calls for warmed yogurt https://www.nigella.com/recipes/turkish-eggs

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: @Lisa K yep, I first saw Turkish eggs done by Nigella and always warm up the yogurt a bit like she does.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I use smoked paprika for the butter and a little Aleppo

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I believe the recipe calls for room temperature yogurt@Lin

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Instead of poaching I usually make a soft-boiled egg and gently peel your wobbly egg under running water.  Steaming is my preferred method.  For this, for oeuf en meurette, benedicte, anything rather than the mess and uncertainty of poaching.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Sous vide the eggs. Esy and foolproof.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Really tasty if you like garlic infused yogurt. Never poached before so just cracked open egg and dropped into boiling water. Pulled out 3 min later with plastic slotted spoon and added to yogurt. Used a mix of butter and olive oil for butter Aleppo pepper sauce. Will try sauce next time with smoked paprika as someone else suggested. Just lightly seasoned with salt as I have to watch that in my diet but a little salt still brought out the flavors.  Not hard once you’ve tried the process once. Ate it with multigrain toast.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Loved this dish!  Simple to make and satisfying.  I cut this recipe in half as there were only two of us to feed.  Will keep this in my lineup as I always have these ingredients on hand.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Didn’t have the right pepper so I made the butter sauce with 1.5tbs salted butter, 1tsp chili oil, and 1tbs olive oil, topped with some chili onion crunch and it was really tasty. Highly recommend!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I added smoked paprika and a dash of chili powder and it was amazing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: delish!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: The first time, made as written except for Aleppo pepper, Kashmiri Red Chili instead, and melted the butter with the chili in the microwave. The second time used sour cream. Fried eggs gently in a nonstick pan, lid on for a few minutes at the end - worked great. Tried freeze-dried chives as a garnish, a bit crunchy. Ate it with homemade bread, toasted. Made a single serving. It's a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: Used fresh oregano on top, and Penzey’s Turkish Spice Blend + cayenne + paprika in lieu of Aleppo. It is so so so necessary to have a fresh herb to complete this dish, and I can’t imagine any that wouldn’t shine here. Came together in perhaps 10 minutes, was delightful.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Çilbir (Turkish Eggs With Yogurt) Recipe
commentBody: I decided to fry the eggs in the bloomed pul biber butter and put arugula on top of the garlicky yogurt and it was delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Lotus Root Recipe
commentBody:
...you will like lotus root. It is very similar in texture and flavor. It's not as juicy.

The local Asian grocery carries precooked vacuum packed refrigerated lotus root. I boiled it for 2 minutes to leech out some of the salt and warm it up, and them cooked it for 2 minutes for stir-fry. Did great as a substitute.

It was my first time trying lotus root. This is a very MILD dish with no punchy flavors. It's great if you want something easy on the stomach. It struck me as a comfort food.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Lotus Root Recipe
commentBody: I had lotus root at a two restaurants in Kyoto: Korean and then a Japanese sake bar with bar food.  Both were heavenly. I’ll try this one if I can find fresh lotus root.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Lotus Root Recipe
commentBody: Would this be served as a main o ra side dish?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Lotus Root Recipe
commentBody: YOU get to decide that!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Lotus Root Recipe
commentBody: My grocery store doesn't have fresh lotus root until the end of August. I thought it was a fall vegetable.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and versatile, but overly complicated. Made twice now, with different greens each time. Blanching the kale and straining the leeks and thyme out of the cream are both unnecessary steps, just use remove the thyme leaves from stems, and toss the fresh greens directly into the hot cream and leek sauce to cook. Streamlining the recipe this way and cooking the protein simultaneously gets this on the table in 30 minutes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe. Very tasty! I followed another reviewer's advice (thanks, Brenda!) and skipped the blanching of the kale and the straining of the sauce and just cooked my kale directly in the cream with all of the leek and garlic goodness still in. Used 1/2 tsp dried thyme because we were out of fresh. It turned out great and much less mess, clean-up, and effort. This will be in heavy rotation in our house!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody:
The flavors of this dish where very well paired. That leek cream sauce tied everything together. To cut down on dishes I started by blanching the kale in the same large skillet I made the sauce and seared the salmon. 

That creamed kale is going into the rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: This was yummy! I made it with coconut cream and bit extra mustard and it was still good: even my husband that dislikes coconut liked it as the mustard hides it. Nice dairy free even! I didn’t have a buillion cube so let some stock boil down a bit while I was doing the other ingredients: will be a new go to

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Used spinach because I didn’t have Kale, Scallions-green onions because I didn’t have leeks.  Didn’t strain sauce because I took leaves off of thyme sprigs and thinly sliced scallions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: The kale and rice were great! I split the leek solids between the rice and kale, with most of the liquid going to the kale - would definitely make these sides again. The preparation of the fish was... remarkably boring. I used salmon, and thought that pretty much any other prep/recipe would have been better. Grilling, roasting (5 spice recipe, or brown sugar/mustard glaze), even pan frying in butter with another thyme spring would have been an improvement.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Hopefully people making this know that leeks are usually sandy and need good washing even more than kale. It was delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: I haven’t read any of the notes yet so I don’t know if others have said this - but this recipe says midweek meal.  Luckily it’s Sunday. This recipe has used 4 different pans/skillets, 2 covers, multiple strainers and sieves.  It’s delicious, the sauce in the kale is superb, I love using the leeks in the rice.  But it took me forever. It better be good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Blanching the kale reduces significantly the burden of oxalates on your kidneys.  It is always wise to cook kale and other oxalate-containing foods at least briefly in water.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Really tasty, but definitely takes way longer than 30 mins to make, even with 2 people in the kitchen.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody:
This was delish. Relatively easy to throw together simultaneously. I’ll do less mustard next time and hopefully the creamed kale will be even better, but with the salmon it was really good as prescribed. 
I’m a new chef as of these last few months and have been cooking 3-5 nights a week from NYT and a few solid cookbooks - my partner said this was by far his fave so far. So the review had to go up :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious and super fast to make. We made it with farro and I would highly recommend that. The chewiness of it works so well with the cream sauce without getting gummy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: The crispy bacon-y salmon skin was a total hit with all. Used dried thyme instead of sprigs, so no need to strain sauce, just left the leeks in with the creamed kale.  That way pearl cous cous was untainted by vegs for picky daughter.  Very excellent meal, though not quick; about an hour if you count the time to prep the kale.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody:
This took a long time and made a huge mess of the kitchen but was really excellent. The kale was phenomenal. The two of us ate all of it. 

Made the following changes based on what I had available: used 1/2 tsp dried thyme instead of fresh, and instead of stock concentrate used 1/2 tsp soy sauce plus 1/2 tsp nutritional yeast. I didn’t strain the cream. My fish was cod. I served it with plain brown rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody:
Really was inspired by this meal. It lent itself to all sorts of modifications and was still scrumptious...even for a weeknight. I clocked in at a little over an hour. Here goes:

1/2 & 1/2 instead of heavy cream
Dried thyme
Lacinato kale
Trader Joe’s Harvest Grains Blend (couscous, orzo, quinoa, baby garbanzo beans)
Fresh Pacific cod with blackened spice on both sides (Nice sous vide candidate, but it was after all a weeknight. Still, fresh, it held up well. Don’t overcook!)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: def took an hour - mix leeks with rice per recipe - steamed kale - serve with rice on bottom, salmon middle, then kale and sauce on top

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: This uses way too many dishes but tastes great. Wording was confusing, as it wasn't clear if the "sauce" was for the fish or was the "cream" for the kale (it was the cream for the kale). But only do for a weeknight dish if you have someone else doing the dishes

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: This definitely takes an hour and many pans.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: We put the leek mixture on top of toast for a leek bruschetta rather than mix in rice (due to poor time planning on our part). It turned out fabulous! Would absolutely do it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, I more or less followed the ingredient amounts, probably a more leeks and garlic and used Latino kale. Added some grated nutmeg to the creamed kale before serving. There were no left overs, definitely a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Why would one discard leeks and garlic? It's wasteful. More importantly, they feed the gut microbiome, not to mention enhance flavour and texture. Maybe in a haute cuisine setting but not in my kitchen.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: I am entering the second hour of trying to cook this dish! I think it will be delish but be warned of the deceptive time estimate, other novice chefs

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!! Inane amount of dishes!! Took over an hour. I made with couscous. The leek cream sauce with the couscous was stellar. The kale and cream sauce was even better with the Dijon and chicken concentrate. I do recommend… I also warn you of the clean up.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody:
I wasn't anticipating liking this as much as I did! Took some shortcuts to cut down on time -- followed the suggestion to skip the straining + mix the kale right into the cream sauce, then mixed the cooked rice in there as well. 

The Atlantic salmon I had was a much thicker cut, so I cooked it in my go-to Sohla El-Waylly way -- skin up in the oven for 12 mins @ 425, then the broiler for 6 mins. The crispy skin was perfect with the creamy greens + rice mixture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Made this to guidance but instead of rice made farro. Teenager who is hard to please said it was “ok” which is high praise and took seconds of the kale. Will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Made This tonight and it was fantastic ! Definitely putting this one into the monthly rotation! The sauce was heavenly… Making you want for more and more! Made four healthy ( not American sized) portions… Two for dinner tonight two for lunch tomorrow! my method was a little bit different I did the salmon en papilotte - A definite winner! Would be lovely for a romantic Valentine’s Day dinner - open the white burgundy for a wonderful experience - with time to enjoy the evening - Enjoy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: But the leeks are very good; made with cous cous

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: I personally liked it especially the creamed kale but Chris didn’t care for the creamed kale.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Wouldn't call this a weeknight meal, a lot of steps. But what I would call it is delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: I pared the recipe down for just my husband (I'm vegetarian) but I could have eaten the entire pan of creamed kale myself, it's SO good. I used salmon, and hubby is very impressed with how crispy the skin turned out. I prepped as much as I could ahead of time and it still took a lot of multitasking, pans, and time, so I would stick with making this on a weekend or a special occasion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe except I used sockeye salmon, since Arctic char is unobtanium in the PNW. It’s delicious — and the first kale dish I’ll make again. The sauce is a bit fiddly for weeknight cooking, though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody:
This is a great dish and fun to cook ----but I put in too much salt.
Will make it again.
Not sure why so much fuss is put into the kale---I chopped it up and put in the microwave for a few minutes--was perfect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Made for a good, simple dinner. Like a lot of the other commenters, I didn't strain the sauce. I blanched the kale and then just threw it in the pan with the leeks and cream. I cooked the rice in bouillon, too, to add extra flavor. Served with lemon wedges to cut the richness.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody:
Super tasty dish, but the preparation was overly complicated. It was too much kale in relation to the other ingredients, I had to add some cream to cover all the kale. Before I tried it, I said “this is too much trouble.” But once I ate it, I thought “maybe I can just simplify the prep.” Next time I won’t strain and I’ll start with half the kale. 
I did this over quinoa (for Passover) which worked well, but I could also see ir over rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Very bland cafeteria food. I also don't understand flipping fish in this way, strange 'recipe'.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody:
This was the BEST KALE we've ever had, no restaurant compares.
Mostly followed the recipe. Only used coconut creme, dino kale, skipped the stock concentrate as I didn't have any. Quinoa instead of rice.
We had left over chicken for the protein.
Followed all the steps. This turned out so delicious!
Easily could see this with spinach too, and will try buckwheat kasha next time instead of rice, quinoa was great though. Those strained leeks will elevate anything!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Love this recipe. The saved leeks were great in the rice so its worth the extra step of staining them out of the cream sauce.  I'm making it again this weekend for friends.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Love love love this. Absolutely delicious. Agree with other notes to not bother blanching kale, simply add it with the cream and don’t bother with any straining!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Loved it! I used icelandic cod instead of salmon/arctic char, and next time would maybe use halibut if it's available.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Used lacinto kale instead of curly, cooked right in the unstrained cream sauce as noted by others. Didn’t have leeks so used spring onions; didn’t have a bouillon cube so used a few splashes of coconut aminos. Used sockeye salmon for the fish. Very good & quick recipe, will make again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: This was great! Made almost as written. I used turnip greens instead of kale, and added a spoonful of Calabrian chile paste- my husband and I like a little kick. As several comments suggested, I didn't bother blanching the greens, but I did strain the leeks out of the cream sauce to mix with wild rice blend. Also had my husband grill the salmon to avoid the mild hassle of pan searing.  Will definitely make again, and try different fish/greens/grain combos!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: The flavors are so great! I skipped the fish and also didn't separate the cream from the leeks - just threw the blanched Kale in with them, and added cubed firm tofu at the end. Will be making on repeat!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: We made this with Hake, superb! Recommend cooking the couscous in chicken broth.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as written and my husband and I both thought it was delicious! Definitely will be making this one again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: My husband described this dinner as, "Super yummy!" The recipe halved perfectly. I followed the streamlined advice in the comments, worked great. I used the lobster based Better Than Bouillon - I really think it added additional depth to the flavors. I served it with warmed basmati brown rice on the side. Drying and salting the skin - finally, I achieved crispy skin!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Both over-thought and under-thought. If you like the ingredients, make your own choices about proportions and timing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Used Spanish mackerel as that was the fish we had on hand; it partnered very well with the rice and kale. Roasted butternut pumpkin/squash as an accompaniment — EVOO, pepper and salt. Kiddo enjoyed this too. Will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: This is my favorite recipe on NYT cooking. I’ve made it several times and even ask my partner to make it for me on my birthday. I make sure to mix the aromatics solids in with the rice and skip blanching to cook the greens down right in the sauce. The shatteringly crisp skin on the fish is the best part!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Seared Fish With Creamed Kale and Leeks Recipe
commentBody: This is excellent but 100% agree on how to simplify the process. Next time I’m doubling kale + spinach combo. It’s sooooo good! Not a soupy creamed spinach like at the steakhouses. Feels light and yummy but also comfort food. In the rotation!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This recipe tastes fantastic, and is not difficult to make. However, the only way this recipe takes 30 minutes is if you have an entire kitchen crew working to make it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Adding this to my "meals to impress a date with" list. For protein I added a drained can of cannellini beans at the same time as the rice; it worked remarkably well! They melted right into the risotto and added to the creaminess.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: @Anna this was a great tip! I added two cans and they still completely blended in

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This sounded interesting to me yet easy and it lived up to my expectations. It was delicious. I incorporated some grated turmeric root into the grated carrot and used a ginger and turmeric broth made by Kettle and Fire. The flavors all melded together and the roasted carrots were delicious. A winner!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I'm 59 yo and have been cooking for myself and my family for over 40 years. I have never made risotto because I thought it was beyond my skill set. I don't know where I got that idea. I had to overcome similar trepidation to make polenta, but I did that many years ago. Now I know that homemade risotto is as doable and delicious as polenta. Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: @Sue B oh no! risotto was literally the first thing i taught myself how to cook (other than family staples) in my early 20s… which btw were 30 years ago, lol. glad you rid yourself of the idea that it’s hard!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe, easy and delicious! To cut down on prep I used baby carrots and because I've recently tried a few no-stir risotto recipes, I decided to add all of the broth at once and then put it in the oven (see NYT Baked Risotto with Green and Peas). It took about 40 minutes at 350 and came out just as I could expect a stirred risotto. This recipe will be added to our regular rotation!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: @Erica I was wondering about this having just made the oven-baked risotto for the third time. We like that one a lot and it is certainly a breeze to make, so I will try amending this recipe. I also successfully use my instant pot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Wow, this was stunning! We thought about omitting the parmesan but so glad we didn't-- it made the dish. To up the nutrition, we used farro instead of rice and wilted in ribbons of baby spinach near the end. I couldn't really taste the spinach among the bold flavors, but it did add a nice color contrast. The farro worked brilliantly. If you haven't tried using farro in risotto, this is the perfect recipe to start with.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I really loved this recipe. Just be aware, I have no idea how someone could make this is 30 minutes. It took over 30 just to cook the risotto, not including the prep. Definitely worth making, just know it's going to take longer than 30 minutes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed, this was excellent. The risotto on it's own is so good. Topped with the roasted carrots and a little bit of extra chili crisp I thought was great. My husband thought the additional chili crisp on top was a little too oily, maybe consider topping with just the chunky crispy bits if you think the extra oil will be too rich for you.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Adapted to work my tried-and-true Instant Pot risotto method. 2 c abrorio, 4 c broth, increase other ingredients just a bit. After sauteeing the aromatics, add rice and toast it for a few minutes before adding the wine. 6 minutes at high pressure and a quick release. (Still roast the sliced carrots in the oven, obviously.) SO GOOD.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Easy to make vegan using vegan butter and nutritional yeast instead of parmesan. Since my broth was not low sodium, I did not put any additional salt. Very tasty with a unique flavor from the chili crisp!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Thank you, exactly what I needed to know - does it still work with vegan substitutes. To me (a vegetarian, not vegan) the butter and parmesan can a be pretty big part of the flavor. If the vegan version is going to be meh, I'd rather make something else, as half of my family is vegan.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: @N - My campaign slogan: Nutritional yeast with everything! It is an amazing undiscovered secret weapon to zap up the savoriness of almost everything. Just a sprinkle goes a long way.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: @MerfromMD Do be careful about going overboard with the nutritional yeast if it’s fortified with niacin. Very few nutrition labels are accurate about the amounts of their additives, and niacin is something you don’t want to overload your body on. Two tablespoons of nutritional yeast can put you at the safe daily limit.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t love this as much as other commenters did. The 2 tbs of chili crisp (maybe chili crisps vary in their heat level?) overwhelmed the more subtle savoriness I associate with risotto. So next time I’d try one tbs. I also thought the ingredient suggestion for 2.25 lbs of carrots was a little odd, seeing as how carrots often come in 2 lb bags. I used 2 lbs and found the recipe carroty enough.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: So good! This is our new go to risotto. Served with an arugula salad.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This is a delightful risotto! The only reason I tried this recipe was to use up my CSA carrots, and now I'm a fan, especially with the chile crisp! Made this in the instant pot with 1/4 cup white wine and 4 cups stock. Highly recommend.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: @coffeespoon can you explain a bit more how you did this with the instant pot? Mom of young child here who relies on InstantPot when I often need to “set it and forget it” with recipes. Thank you if you can!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! The contrast between the smoky spicy chili crisp and the sweet carrots makes this very different than other risottos I’ve cooked. Next time I’ll prep the carrots in the morning — I found that the most time consuming part.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This is a wonderful recipe, and I only made one adjustment. Made as directed, I found the dish a little sweet, so I finished with lemon juice. YMMV depending on how sweet the carrots are.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Risotto, carrots, chili crisp. In what world do these go together? This world, apparently. This was delicious! The only change I made was with the wine. I forgot to get some and was too lazy to go back to the store so I used a scant cup of broth and a splash of rice vinegar. Worked like a charm.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I went Mexican-inspired on this with two easy substitutes, used salsa macha instead of chili crisp and subbed cumin for the coriander. 10/10 would repeat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I don’t know about you, but I always have leftover risotto and leftover risotto is never as good as the day you make it. So I turned my leftover risotto into chicken and rice soup. I chopped up some celery and onions. Sautéd that in some olive oil with some thyme then I added two frozen chicken thighs and let it simmer for about an hour. I took the meat off the bone, put the broth and the chicken meat into the leftover risotto pot, and it was probably the best chicken and rice soup I’ve ever had!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was a unique dish that was delicious, and as a semi-novice cook I found it easy to follow. I’m proud of myself for making my first risotto! I didn’t have that many carrots on hand and that was fine. I had baby carrots and shredded carrots and used those up-great way to use a lot of carrots before they go bad! We added grilled chicken for the meat eaters in our family, and it was a hit for all of us.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This is the best recipe I’ve ever made. It saw directly into my soul. If I remade The Hours, but about my life it would have Miss Dalloway filling her vases with carrot risotto

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have enough carrots to make the roasted carrots, so I just made the risotto and topped it with chili crisp. SO GOOD! I used 1 lb of carrots rather than 3/4, 2 shallots, and 4 cloves of garlic. I’m so glad to have this in my pantry-cooking arsenal moving forward.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night pretty much as written (except I used about a cup less broth and made it in my  instapot). My husband and I loved it.  I had never used chile crisp before and although the jar I purchased was pricey (momofuku 11$) it added the right amount of spice and unique flair to go with the sweetness of the carrot and now that I know about this condiment it might become a staple in my kitchen.  Thanks NYT.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Made to recipe instructions as an entree - very good! Side note - the roasted carrots w/chili crisp could be used as a side for anything, they’re so good!  This does make a lot of risotto though, so maybe try to halve for two? Or just get ready for leftovers :). Used purchased Momofuko Chili Crunch.  Would like to try making my own chili crisp at some point. Make this again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Same technique, but with sweet potatoes!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: NYT cooking hits EVERY TIME. If I could offer one suggestion: please be more realistic about the estimated time to make. I spent almost 1/4 of the time grating and prepping the carrots.  Worth every moment - but a heads up would be great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Chili crisps vary wildly by brand/recipe in terms of the proportion oil. Those who found the dish too oily might want to use a less oily chile crisp. ALSO note: the link in the recipe for "homemade chile crisp" is actually for chile oil-- quite different.  I'm looking forward to making this, and will probably use the pressure cooker which is for sure the way to go with risotto, and am guessing it would be great with Hot Pot Queen's Wild Mushroom Chunky Chile Sauce

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Great easy recipe! Didn't need the extra 2tb of butter at the end. I used the carrot roasting pan to roast up a can of chickpeas with chilli crisp, salt and pepper at 425 for 10min to add a little extra protein and it worked great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Well, I don’t  normally do this, but I had to make an ingredient switch. Midway through my prep, I went to my cabinet to get my box of Arborio rice that I was sure I had, and apparently I had used it up. Too far into the recipe to pivot, I subbed farro and am pleased to report that it was delicious. I will make this again with rice, but if you’re in a bind- the farro worked!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Super tasty. I minced celery and swiss chard stems to cook with the shallot and carrot mixture to sneak more veggies in, and threw some swiss chard leaves in at the end as well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I had high hopes for this recipe. The roasted carrots in the chili crisp were delicious but I felt the flavor notes of the risotto were sort of…ambiguous? I would potentially make it again with more Asian elements (soy sauce, ginger, maybe some snow peas, etc.).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I make this at least once (if not twice) monthly. It does take longer than 30 minutes but it is well worth it. I have swapped the carrots for tofu for a bit of protein and it’s gone over really well in our vegetarian household. 10/10 recommend you try it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I loved this recipe! I adapted it to the pressure cooker like other reviewers mentioned, and it made a stress free way to serve risotto for dinner guests! I did all the steps - short of adding the arborio, broth, and cheese to the pressure cooker and putting the chili crisp coated carrot slices in the oven - a few hours before guests arrived. Then just before we sat down and started serving salad, I added the arborio and broth to the instapot, started it, and stuck the carrots in the oven to roast. I used 3 and 1/2 cups of chicken stock and cooked at pressure for 8 minutes, and then stirred in another 1/4 cup broth after quick release to get to the right creaminess, then added the cheese.  I really like the way it turned out - this recipe is now on my favorites list!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Ended up with a bunch of extra carrots from the farmers market and decided to make this recipe. Outstanding, A++, and no real changes to the recipe- as written turned out great

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I'm a novice cook but managed this easily and it was terrific.  In fact, good enough that I'm going to make it again and invite friends over that know how to cook.  That ought to give me cred!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: We enjoyed this dish.  I did it in a pressure cooker, as I do all of my risotto.  So much easier.  I used the citrus chile crisp recipe from the "Roasted Winter Squash with Citrus Chile Crisp" by Andy Baraghani, which is so worth the time.  One thing I think is a mistake in this recipe is calling for mixing chile crisp into the carrots that are going into the oven.  It just burns, of course.  Next time, I might toss it with the carrots after they come out of the oven.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Echoing what everyone else said, this will definitely take more than 30min. It's worth it though. Highly recommend!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Truly a uniquely delicious dish, not a combination you come across every day but unexpectedly amazing.  To keep it vegan I used Earth Balance for the butter, and omitted the cheese but stirred in a tablespoon or two of miso near the end.  Highly recommend!  My partner doesn't even like carrots that much, but was 100% converted by the roasting/chili crisp combo.  Who knew a weeknight risotto could still be new and interesting.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Didn't make the topping but it was a hit anyway. My family isn't into spicy fare.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: @N Of SeattleThe chili crisp isn’t spicy, despite how it sounds! A simply delicious addition to the top!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic as written. The second time I made it for a vegan crowd by substituting coconut oil for the butter and finished it with coconut milk instead of cheese. The coconut complimented the carrots beautifully.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I absolutely love this recipe! I accidentally over-salted it because I didn’t realize the broth I used was incredibly salty. Adding Parmesan cheese would have made it inedible, so I decided to add a couple of dollops of cream cheese instead, and it saved the day. I can’t wait to make this recipe again as intended.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was really quite delicious. The prep time is perhaps understated but certainly worth the effort. The seasoning and chilli-crisp are essential for flavour. Would  make this again in an instant.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Joining the chorus here. Lovely recipe. I would highly recommend making all risottos in instant pot, if you already have one. Cook as directed in the first 5 steps. Then add 1:2 broth, cook on high pressure for 6 minutes. Release the pressure manually after waiting 5-10 mins. I have made risotto both the traditional way and in pressure cooker(IP). Pressure cooked risotto is equally delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I've been making risottos for years and this is a really good one: lovely balance of flavors and texture, with the roast carrots and chile. My only caveat is the quantity of liquid here, which is insufficient to cook the rice right through and create a creamy texture. I made a half quantity and added around half a cup more (hot water), which brings it into line with my standard risotto recipe. I would also add some chopped green herbs--cilantro or mint, say--for visual interest and extra zing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: @Amanda Beresfor agree that needed more liquid

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: @Amanda Beresford agreed on the liquid proportions. I added another 1.5 cups of water t

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
@Sue B 
Try making risotto or polenta in an electric pressure cooker, like Instant pot. Cuts down the cooking time and all the stirring and babying. 1:2 rice to broth, on high pressure for 6 minutes, quick release of pressure, after 5 minutes. Stir in cheese, butter at the end, and additional broth if needed.  I have stopped making stove top risotto or polenta. Give it a go.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This is a great take on risotto. So easy, and the color is just beautiful. My wife proclaimed this to be a “10” and cannot stop talking about it. The only change I made was to make the risotto in my instant pot, following instructions from https://www.pressurecookrecipes.com/instant-pot-risotto/ regarding cooking times. There is no need to stand and endlessly stir the broth into the rice. This is so much quicker and easier. I love this recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I love risotto and I love Chile crisp, but I was skeptical about the combination. I was wrong. This is absolutely amazing and I will make it again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
Loved this! Carrots in risotto is an inspired idea. Used box grater to avoid cleaning a food processor, and added some champagne vinegar to the pan with my not-quite-dry wine to give it more acidity. Tossed the carrots in Fly by Jing’s Zhong Sauce before roasting (thought I had chili crisp, whoops) and it was delicious - no crunch, but more sweet and Szechuan spice, beautiful complement to the slightly-sweet risotto. 
Paired nicely with a chilled bottle of pecorino collide pescaresi.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was tasty, but the roasted carrot slices didn't work for me - they dried out before they had a chance to soften.  Next time, I'll use thicker slices and roast for longer.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe made as is with homemade chicken stock. The combo of roasted, sweet carrot/hot oil with the totally different carrot flavor of the 'riced' carrot is surprisingly effective. We will definitely make this again with our garden carrots this fall

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Liked it more than I expected! Served with arugala on the side. But I will say that this recipe takes waaaaay longer than the suggested 30 mins, so if you’re planning on making it, set aside at least 1.5 hours.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I am not a huge carrot fan so had low expectations, but the chili crisp drew me in and I am glad for it. I followed the recipe almost exactly but thought the addition of the wine should have been after adding the rice, not before, so I did both... adding 1/2 cup of sauvignon blanc to the carrot/shallot/coriander mixture before the rice and a half cup after. I've seen my uncle pour an entire bottle of wine into risotto! Will definitely make again and maybe add turmeric, but it's delicious as is.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Really tasty! I added ground turmeric along with the coriander and served with some fresh cilantro for a pop of green. Next time I might double the roasted carrots.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Wow.  Made as directed and it was fabulous. Can I make it again tomorrow?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Just amazing! Make it. The flavor combination is just spectacular. Really enjoyed this dish

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Went for something a little different and vegetarian. It was absolutely delicious. 10/10.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Amazing recipe but WAY longer than 30 min to cook!! I added some turmeric at the advice of another commenter, totally delicious. The cheese and butter at the end are essential and the perfect finishing touch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This has become one of my favorite dishes. Unexpectedly wonderful. I’m trying a cheatie lazy version in a combi-steam oven today and will report back. (Btw I now am in a serious chili crisp affair. Dukes and chili crisp on good foods is wonderful, and it can rescue almost every cooking mistake. They both are in my “Need two backup bottles in the pantry” list).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This is a bit time consuming but really delicious. I put some toasted pumpkin seeds on top.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was fantastic. No modifications. I'm at almost 8,000' in altitude and the absorption takes much longer. A truly delicious recipe that I will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Carrots took about twice the time to cook.  Needed a bit more broth at the end.  Also need much more than 30 min for prep and cooking but well worth the wait.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Risotto was perfect. The roasted carrots were a touch spicy. Would use only 1 tablespoon of chili crisp next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed, just added a bit of oil to the roasted carrots.  Delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This is absolutely delicious and I will be making again. I didn’t have a full 1 1/2 cups parm, and I added orange zest / juice as well as some cilantro, but otherwise made as is. It would be great served with fish, but makes a great vegetarian main on its own.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Made this as written and it was positively delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: The idea is there but it needs more garlic! The coriander also felt like it did not quite belong.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Delish! I used cranberry orange chile crisp from Trader Joe’s

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I used about 250g of Pearl barley. Not enough for ravening hordes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely amazing. I used a seaweed chilli crisp and it was perfectly umami. I had never made risotto and it came out flawless, I absolutely will make this again. It did take a close attention for a period of time, but it was not impossibly complex, the most important thing was having all the ingredients measured out and nearby as I was making the risotto so I didn't have to stray far from the stove once I started it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Had no idea what it took to make a risotto — had to look it up and it was more rigorous than the recipe said. Despite that, it’s amazing. Would I do it again knowing how much work it takes? It’s likely.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Added some black beans, thinly chopped dates, and parsley and it was DELISH!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Meh. A lot of work for not a lot of flavor when I really wanted to love this. Love roasted carrots and chili crisp, but this just didn’t work.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Love this recipe! Such a good combination of sweet and savory. I add rinsed and dried chickpeas to the tray with roasted carrots for a little added protein. I’ve made it three times and flavor is always great, but it has never taken 30 mins… more like an hour.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Top 5 risottos of all time. Added a dash of lemon juice at the end. Plan on more than 30 min.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was delicious but that, for my tastes, it could do with a little acid/tanginess - maybe a squeeze of lemon at the end? Or served with a side salad with vinaigrette?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: was hesitant about the chile crisp, but it was fantastic on this dish...highly recommend

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: For a vegan approach, has anyone used any of the vegan parm cheese equivalents you can get in stores now?  Or specifically, do the vegan cheeses mix well in the risotto, melt properly and overall taste good?  I saw another comment that mentioned subbing vegan butter & brewer’s yeast. I just like the idea of sprinkling a cheese into the pot, and am hoping a vegan parm cheese would work well in this situation. Thanks

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I used Violife vegan parmesan and Miyokos vegan butter, and the dish was creamy and delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: While delicious, this dish took me well over an hour to prepare. To save time don't start peeling/ slicing the larger amount of carrots until after you have started the rice. The risotto took at least 30 minutes to cook. You could do the whole carrot roasting prep/ cook between steps 4/5 and save a lot of time. No need to do it up front.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: As others have said- this took me a good hour   to make, definitely not the quick & snappy weekday meal I was expecting. Perfect unexpected use of fall veggies. I found it to taste a little thin, so added a large spoon full of white miso paste and it was great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This is so delicious. Absolute perfection. Some fresh coriander (cilantro) and a pinch of lemon zest stirred through at the end takes it to another level. It's like a spicy riff on carrot and coriander soup. Delish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This is a fantastic and delicious meal that comes together with consistent attention required for more than 30 minutes, to be sure. Instead of topping with carrots, we used roasted tofu for some protein and it was spectacular. We omitted the Parmesan (used nutritional yeast) and used vegan butter and the flavors was not lacking one bit.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Made exactly as written. Was more than 4 servings.  Took this home cook about 75 min.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
Made this tonight and I’m never one to follow recipes to the letter unless it’s for a cake.  Volumes were eye-balled according to how carroty I felt the risotto should be.  
Had no white wine as my hubby wanted to drink it outwith the risotto and I didn’t add Parmesan to the risotto as our daughter is lactose intolerant.   But the result was quite delicious.  
We ate it with arugula and pine nut in addition to roasted carrot topping, and of course, hubby had his wine

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
I added or changed:

1. Caramelized the shallot and garlic in avocado oil
2. Added some fresh parsley because my kitchen table plant was getting a little too big for his britches. Also grated in some ginger root with the coriander since they complement each other, maybe 1 tsp.
3. Used a box grater on the carrots. I don’t own a food processor or the like. It took seven minutes. Not worth owning a food processor. 
4. Cream! What is risotto with no cream? I added probably 1/4 cup. 
5. Crumbled some dried shiitake into the stock because it’s super umami and went well with my parmesan I grated
6. Frankly the chili crisp, but will probably add it on the next serving
7. Put some jammy eggs on top

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Did everything as directed except I roasted rhe carrots a little longer for more caramelization goodness. Everyone in the family loved it and they all cleaned their plates.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I subbed olive oil for butter and nutritional yeast for Parmesan to make it vegan. Very delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely loved this recipe! Perfect balance of creamy and spicy. No idea how anyone could make this in 30 minutes. If you have to grate and chop the carrots, expect 1 hour 30 minutes, as that's how long it took me. Regardless, it is a delicious recipe that makes a large batch. I would also recommend salting a few times throughout adding the broth.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Tried subbing the cheese using coconut cream, still delicious but the coconut flavor overwhelmed the carrot flavor underneath, which is mild. I will try nutritional yeast next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I don't enjoy cooked carrots, but I love this, and so does my family!  I've been adding cauliflower florets and oyster mushrooms to the roasted carrot topping mixture, and using barley or farro instead of rice.  The barley needs extra stock and cooking time.  Even without the changes the recipe takes more than 30 minutes, but it's worth it.  This dish has become a family favorite!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: absolutely a 60 minute meal. took me 10 minutes to find my grater! @Tophkissane

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Way way too much work for a risotto, which is usually such a calming dish for me to make. Don't know in what universe this takes the NYT recipe developer 30 minutes. Took an hour and a half to get to the table, even with a food-processor for chopping. Good but not great and really not worth that much effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Liked this a lot--interesting and delicious, feels sophisticated. Note that the chili crisp was fairly spicy at this quantity. I was left thinking this needed one other ingredient to make it really shine... not sure what, though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Carrots needed to be roasted for much longer than the recipe indicated. The Risotto was excellent though. I added a bit more wine at the end than the recipe called for which I liked.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Surprisingly delicious recipe! Will definitely make it again, especially for veggie friends! It is not a complicated recipe and I think would be easy for anyone. However, like others comments, I have no idea how this could possibly only take 30 minutes. We have a slow heating oven, so I was delayed a bit there but it too about an hour and a half. Maybe someone forgot to put a 1 in front of the 30 minutes for the time? Will absolutely make again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This is truly one of the most delicious dinners I’ve ever made. Nothing cozier than pouring some wine for me, some for the risotto, and stirring away while knowing I have this meal ahead of me. It’s a must-try.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: @Ginny Wine! Half cup for the recipe full cup for the chef. It’s a rule.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Super good but took forever to make. Makes me wish I had some house elves (to enjoy it with)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Can I use regular rice? I am determined to NOT buy anything more for the pantry till I use some of what's already there. Thanks-

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Yes, but it won't come out as creamy as arborio would. I'm with you on using up what's already in the pantry!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Risotto rice is special, higher starch rice. You won't get the velvety texture that great risotto has without the risotto rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
If this is a weeknight dish, there’s no reason you should take out your food processor just for the carrots but do the garlic and shallot by hand. Throw it all in there. 
Next time I’m going to add some mushroom, too. I added powdered mushroom midway through, since the dish lacked robustness, to my tastes. 
If anyone’s shopping for chili crisp and needs a suggestion, Laoganma is the original authentic stuff.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Added two slices of fixed bacon to the risotto base but what a winner this recipe is. The chili crisp toasted carrots totally elevate the dish. Thanks very much!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
I loved this! It was different and still felt like a comfort food, and I can’t wait to make it again. Feels like something that would be a hit at a dinner party. 
However as other commenters said this is not a 30 min meal. The risotto itself took more than 30 minutes to cook and the prep was done with 2 people with decent chopping skills. I would say it was over an hour of active kitchen time. 
The other note I’d like to make is we scaled it down to .5 and the amount of liquid required was probably 1 cup more than the recipes called for.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is a nice twist with the carrots and chile crisp. I've made a lot of risottos, and this one is a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Just made this with a bit less of everything as had just one cup of rice in pantry. Still absorbed all 5 cups of broth (stove top). Delicious! Used TJ onion chili crisp. Anxious to try with garlic chili crisp and oven method.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried doubling recipe? Made as written and it’s a keeper! Double the chili crisp carrots! 5 star!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! I made it as described in the recipe but with slightly less carrots as it was just for two. It definitely takes longer than 30 minutes, though… closer to 45-60 mins, but that includes prep time. Definitely making it again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: mushroom powder, turmeric, poached egg

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I forgot to add the wine, but it was still very nice, probably just a bit heavier than otherwise.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Super easy and delicious!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Looks wonderful!  Tastes wonderful!  I had some homemade chile crisp that was "ok", so added some store-bought "garlic crisp" that we like as a final topping.  That was good too!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
I’m trying to get more meatless dishes into our rotation and this one is a keeper and cheap to boot.  

My chili crisp has little oil - I poured that off to air fry the carrots and added tumeric.  I wish I’d made extra for snacking and adding to a sandwich.  

Went lighter on the butter and put grated ginger and white pepper in with the aromatics, and added a squeeze of lemon juice at the end.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding!  Made as written.  Of note is that it takes way more than 30 minutes to prepare!  Wait to begin roasting carrots until about half of the stock has been added to the rice

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Loved this! It does make a lot. I needed to add quite a bit more liquid, and it took quite a bit more time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I made this according to the recipe and it turned out well.  I would put zip into it, as I think someone mentioned already, by adding or orange or lemon zest.  I did not add the chile crsip as eating anything hot causes me congestion, like having a cold.  So nix on the chile.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as directed and couldn’t be more delighted. Served with some roasted broccolini, which balanced it out perfectly.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was a wonderful risotto - everyone enjoyed it thoroughly. Served it with baby back ribs and it was a great-looking, great-tasting meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent, but careful with the salt—mine was a tad over-seasoned after adding stock & cheese. Carrots roasted with chili crisp were a revelation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe and it was delicious. The chili crisp roasted carrots 🥕 added a nice demision. Will definitely make again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Wow! A new classic & go-to. Followed exactly as instructed, 10/10

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Perfect.  No changes.  Will try in IP per Wendy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: 1 T chili oil is PLENTY. Use sparingly

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: 1-2 more pots and dishes for prep than I’d like but the risotto was delicious. I used rainbow carrots for color.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Followed recipe, but used a bit more coriander and pepper. Food processor was incredibly useful. Make sure to cook by the weight rather than measurements, found it far better for this dish. You can add more cheese as well

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Really easy tasty recipe that uses a lot of carrots. The risotto is extremely good, we also thought it would be a good side to roast salmon or other proteins on top. We used Lao Gan Ma chili crisp with great results. Looking forward to trying this with other chili crisps we have. Adding this to our vegetarian (or just vegetable heavy) nights. A keeper!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: We really liked the spicy/sweet/creamy/cheesy contrasts.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  Needed a quick side for grilled chicken and made this with ingredients on hand—fantastic.  Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
Love love love this recipe.  I’m
not even a huge risotto fan but this one is special.  Prepping all carrots definitely takes some time but it’s worth it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
Fantastic recipe
Added some fresh tumeric as others have suggested to the carrot mix
The chili crisp is key to adding a depth of flavor to the dish

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: The 30 minutes for preparation mentioned in the recipe are not realistic, but I’ll happily spend an hour again soon to prepare this dish. The flavor exceeded my expectations! Had to leave out the wine for health reasons, added more stock and some lemon juice to substitute.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and used a ton of CSA carrots that had been languishing in the fridge.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This dish far exceeded my already high expectations!  The risotto itself is just delicious,  it the Chili Crisp-roasted carrots took it over the top!  I chopped the roasted leftover carrots and mixed them into the leftover risotto to make mozzarella-stuffed arancini!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
Kinda lacks a punch for a tamale. Id mix and match some other spices, or boil some dried peppers and process them and add those in. I also cooked down my peppers and onions after cooking/removing the beef. Then added the spices after cooking the peppers/onions for 1-2 minutes before adding all the watery elements.

Also be wary of how much cornbread you need! I started pouring before I realized I needed more.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Less chili oil on carrots

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
This is an example where the nuances of every part of this are lost when put together. Perhaps it is because I make a lot of risotto. Perhaps it's because I've made my own ma la chile oil. Perhaps it's because I've made my own Sichuan hot pot broth with beef tallow and roasted rapeseed oil....

All of these things are good. Together, it's muddled and a lot of work for nothing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I like spicy but this was very spicy with the chili crisp I used. Chili crisp varies in taste but the taste also changes as you cook. Easy & cozy meal that makes great leftovers too!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Surprise hit! Was looking for carrot recipes after a glut of carrots in last week's winter share. Made the risotto as written, and eyeballed a version of the chili crisp ingredients from the linked recipe for the roasted carrots (I'm sure those would be better with the real thing). The quantity of stock was accurate for my taste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Was okay, not my favorite risotto for the effort. Maybe with asparagus or another green veg on top and just carrots in the rice itself.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Be careful with saltiness levels

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: absolutely delicious, but definitely takes longer than 30 min. don’t rush tha risotto!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: easier method -bake the risotto, add all the stock, bring to a boil, add cover, bake for about 20 minutes.  Add remaining ingredients.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
Really amazingly yummy and surprising:) 
I added probably twice the amount of the chili crisp to the carrots, was nice and borderline spicy.
Who would have thought that the humble carrot can create such a festive risotto

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Take this to the next level by adding a squeeze of lime to the carrots. Garnish with cilantro.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Yum! I've made risotto before and found it labor intensive. This one is pretty easy, and really good. Since I was making for kids, I roasted half my carrots with only olive oil, smoked paprika, and salt & pepper... the other half with the chili crisp for the adults. I prepped all of my veggies in the morning, that way putting it together at dinner time on a weeknight wasn't overwhelming.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! It took way longer than 30 minutes (mostly because of the carrot prep) but the flavors were all really good! When I make it again I'll probably experiment with a different flavor profile for the roasted carrots since the chili crisp became a bit overpowering.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: where is the chile crisp recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
