commentBody: Delicious, loved the unexpected combination of chilli crisp and parmesany risotto. Definitely takes much much longer than 30 minutes though. I didn't have enough carrots so used some sweet potatoes as well. And not enough parmesan so some nutritional yeast, but it was still very parmesany flavoured. Homemade chilli crisp made it even yummier.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed! I did a half recipe and at first I thought I had too much grated carrot, but the final result was *chef's kiss*.  This will become a regular rotation in your kitchen if you decide to make it!  I served it alongside Melissa's crispy mustard chicken with bread crumbs recipe- great combo!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: YUM a favorite. Prep takes ages but it’s worth the ride

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Made with some Parmesan broth. Rich enough it didn’t need more butter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. A bit more work and time preparation as marked. Worth it.!!. Served on its own as a secondi piatti . The roasted carrots are absolutely necessary.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Love this recipe, however I always need more stock when I make it. I use closer to 7 cups instead of 5.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I found this carrot risotto to be too sweet for my taste—I guess I prefer risottos with a more savory and umami bent. But if I was going to make a carrot risotto again, the chile crisp does cut the sweetness and make it immeasurably better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was very nice! Just rich enough, and the chili crisp was the best part.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Made several times already and will continue to do so. Great recipe, easy to make, and goes with so many things, from a simple green salad to meatballs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Very nice. Did it again. Outstanding.!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Would Momofuku Chili Crunch substitute for Chile Crisp? I bought a jar and don't know how to use it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Yes, that product is one brand of chili crisp.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I usually make the NYT chili crisp recipe, but I didn't have all of the ingredients. I bought a jar of Momofuku chili crunch and it was great on this risotto dish and ramen. I'll go back to making the chili crisp because the pre-made options are really expensive.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I have never used Chile crisp before because I didn't know what was used - the crispy flakes or the oil?  And if both, in what proportions?  In this recipe would each tablespoon be half crisps and half oil?  And any advice of which brand to buy?  Thanks for any help

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Check for online reviews of chili crisp brands--some are very spicy, some only mildly so. Some have relatively little oil, others are more like a third chili flavored oil.  Depending on your own preferences and what you use them for, you'll find a brand that hits your sweet spot. And if you don't, or even if you do, you can try one of the many online recipes to make your own!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was great!  Chopped up the carrots and then the shallots in the food processor.  Used the less-stirring method and didn't heat the stock ahead of time.  Added 4c. room temp stock, brought to boil, covered and simmered and stirred occasionally until rice was tender, then stirred in the other 1c of stock, then parm and butter.  Topped with the roasted carrots and some slivers of fresh sage.  Used Mr. Bing chili crisp.  Would be a great meal for having people over.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
I needed to use up the last few pounds of carrots before bringing in this year's crop, and was getting bored with the recipes we already had.  This one was great!  I added a bit more wine than called for to add some acidity to the sweet carrots, and went easy on the chili crisp's oil.  And, in the end, sprinkled on some fresh lime juice for a contrast to the rick, spicy chili crisp. 

Not only did I get a great new risotto recipe, but a bonus carrot vegetable dish to have as well!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Made a 1/2 batch for 2 people and it was exceptional. As with all NYT recipes, the cook time is only for the steps as listed, and does not include prep time. I knocked this out in an hour flat, and that includes all the prep and cook time for Lidey Heuck’s Parmesan-Crusted Chicken which was a perfect pairing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Really really good, chili crisp carrots on their own would be a killer side-dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have quite enough carrots, so I improvised the proportions.  Next time, I might double the chili crisp carrots -- yum-o!  I have not made a risotto in a very long time, but this was very rewarding.  To give the meal some protein, I put some canned garbanzo beans (rinsed and dried) onto the tray with the carrots for the last 5-odd minutes of cooking: they added a nice texture to the plate.  Will definitely make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Very easy to make and it’s beautiful to look at. The Bought good, sweet carrots at local farmer’s market and followed the recipe to a “T.” The first time we had it, we found it pretty bland … even with the chili crisp roasted carrots in top. The second time, we chopped the chili crisp roasted carrots and added them into the risotto before serving. Ta da! Much improved at least to our taste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
OMG. The best recipe ever. Followed it to a tee minus coriander. Had none. Chili crisp readily available at Publix. And of course Amazon has it. 
This is a keeper

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was great as written.  Since there are two of us, I used the leftover risotto and toppings to make a really funky arancini with smoked mozzarella at the center.  A total winner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: We loved this recipe. We used vegan butter and cheese, and I had to substitute curry powder for the coriander. We will be a little more careful with the salt next time. Generously seasoning the roasted carrots and the risotto base is too much unless the stock is low sodium.  It's a creamy, carroty, spicy dish. We will keep it in our rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: We don't care for cooked carrots much, but this was excellent, and I'll make it again soon!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Oh. My. God. So good! Cumin added as well, and finished off with some lemon juice for added kick.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent! I made several substitutions based on what I had on hand, so I used the recipe more as a guide. I swapped Arborio rice for farro, feta for Parmesan, and added zucchini and a little miso to the broth.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was a little underwhelming. Made exactly according to the recipe and it missed the complexity of flavor that I was hoping for with the spicy, sweet and savory flavors.  And the carrots that were incorporated ended up having some crunchy chewy bits that distracted from the risotto for me. On a positive note my teenage daughter who doesn’t like carrots ate a moderate amount and didn’t realize that they were in the risotto, so would be a good option for picky eater kids.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I used purple carrots.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
So good! So fall. I will say, I had some leftover fennel bulb and so used that along with a little white onion instead of the shallot. I think it enhanced the carrot flavor. Would not go out of my way to always use fennel - but it was a nice way to use up small bits. 

Thinking about bringing this as a Thanksgiving side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was very good. Made as written except we added some chopped pistachios and parsley for serving, which was a nice touch. I agree with some of the other commenters about the time- this took over an hour from start to finish, even using a V slicer for the carrots. Also thought the 2 1/4 lbs was an odd figure - I happened to have a leftover bag plus a full 2 lb bag, but 2 would have been plenty of carrots. We ended up with extra sliced roasted pieces anyway.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: We made the recipe exactly as written.  It took 4 cups of stock.  I think you could easily double the shallots and a sprinkling of cilantro and maybe a squeeze of lemon for serving.  Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Really yummy and fairly easy to make! The chili crisp made this dish warm and comforting with the extra depth of flavor it adds. I roasted the carrots a wee bit longer and left out the wine I place for extra broth since I didn’t have any.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This didn’t do it for me as written, but I will try again.  Next time, harder cook on the sliced carrots, and none in the risotto itself.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Loved it, but as many others have noted it took much longer than 30 minutes to make. More like almost an hour for me.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Y'all are out of your mind with the 30 minute cook time. With three people, maybe...  But otherwise, tasty!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: The risotto itself is quite good.  I guess my chili crisp was hotter than normal, because the carrots were very spicy!  I would suggest starting with half as much, and only adding more at the end.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Wow! This was excellent. I was going to make Champagne risotto, but my asparagus had to be tossed, so decided to make this. Glad I did. Halved the recipe for 2, but kept the same amount of shallots and garlic and boosted coriander to 1 teaspoon and added a teaspoon of turmeric, per others comments.  Had the small bottle of Champagne out, so used that instead of the white wine. My husband was not initially excited about carrot risotto, but he loved it! Adding this to the rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Sadly this didn’t work for us. I thought I was a big fan of carrots but this was too many. There was very little flavor apart from CARROT.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Exceptional with vegan Parm.... this recipe is a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe- perfect satisfying and filling food for the “lion” phase of March when you’re still cold and miserable but there’s a hint of spring in the sunlight. I added a can of rinsed cannellini beans to the risotto for protein. I also had a few spears of broccoli rabe that needed to get used up so I threw them in the roasting pan w the carrots. The contrast of colors and the slight bitterness of the rabe was a great compliment to the whole dish. Will definitely be making this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent and delicious recipe! I highly recommend it, it's truly out of this world. A few notes:
- there is no way this takes 30 minutes
- step 1 should be at least 5 different steps, I'm surprised no one else commented on this!
- adding cannellini beans, as suggested by a comment, is a great call!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was ok.  I did not add Parmesan because I am vegan.   The chili crisp was way too hot for one of us.  Total time was closer to one hour.   I won’t make it again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Really, really liked this. I also think a food chopper/processor is necessary for your sanity.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: My husband is a carrot fanatic, so I knew I had to make this for him. He actually preferred the risotto without the roasted carrots, but I disagree and think it adds a much needed textural contrast. I served this with seared scallops for protein, and the scallops match the sweetness of the carrots very nicely. I would for sure make this again. And like others have said, in no way is this a 30 minute recipe, even using the food processor for all of the dicing work.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I used all my gadgets, so it was not too much work. Food processor, fancy mandolin for slicing carrots and instapot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic!! I made the risotto in the Instant Pot as others have previously indicated. I added ginger and cannellini beans as others did. Very helpful comments! Will definitely make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, a different take on risotto. I didn’t find the risotto bland, as others have said, but I did find the carrots quite spicy. I think it was the chili crisp that I used, it’s spicier than most. Served it either way a honey chili crisp, whichever delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe.   However, be careful where you source your carrots.   Many supermarket carrots are bitter.  I'm a huge Trader Joe's patron, and I find their carrots to be bitter.    I don't do  Sprouts so much, but their carrots are sweeter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this without the chili crisp? I would like to modify this dish for a low fodmap diet

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: The Parmesan seems like it wouldn't be the right cheese for this. Did anyone find that to be true? Did anyone try another cheese? I see that one person tried cream cheese, but while that would add great creamy texture, not sure that would add much flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: @Jude Parm is standard for risotto. Also don’t clutch on the quantity. I grate it with a cheese grinder into a one cup bowl, not worrying much about overfilling it a little instead of packing it down. Romano is second choice but gives a sharper taste profile. But why not try it with some other cheese? Lots of comments for every NYT recipe on variations. That’s the joy of cooking! I love making risotto for the tending. But just like the advice for grits in the movie My Cousin Vinnie, you can’t walk away. Enjoy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
I also found this very tasty, but found it took much more than 30 mins to make.
I appreciate all the ideas in other reviews.
I made it verbatim the first time, but I will probably only use 2 pounds of carrots and add spinach. But also less chili crisp.
Using Farro instead of Arborio is another great idea I will try.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: How can NYT recommend a recipe with such extraordinarily high amounts of sodium?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Because they are not the sodium police :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: A pretty sexy dish to make with your partner. Definitely takes an hour. Chili crisp roasted carrots is such a hack, but would love to make them softer next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This was phenomenal and id never even heard of chilli crisp,  so had  to make that up from scratch using a Great British Chefs mash-up.  Subbed a handful of orzo to the half bag of risotto and used a ham stock cube (all I had). Made it all within an hour including chilli crisp.  Ace. On repeat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This looks awesome.  I will dedicate more time than 30 minutes however.  Why, oh why, do so many recipes have to end with parmesan cheese?  I feel like it masks any flavor you have developed in a recipe.  I would leave it out

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I don't do well with chili crisp kind of stuff. Any other suggestions??

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and easy. I was out of chili crisp and didn't feel like making it, but it was still delicious without. Otherwise I followed the recipe to the letter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
Try whole coriander seeds. Toast it first in the butter, before adding other ingredients. 
If you have time, leave the grated/chopped carrots for a few hours to wilt/ lose the moisture. It amps up the flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Saw the recipe this morning, grabbed a few ingredients we didn’t have, and made it tonight. A little more work prep time than described, but the outcome was beyond beyond. Will be making this into the future!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: The time listed for this recipe is clearly wrong, but this is a family favorite that’s pretty inexpensive but feels totally fancy. I’ve never made it and not had someone love it (and that includes my three year old, who has VERY strong food opinions).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
I adapted this to use our Instant Pot (everything basically as the recipe is written, but 2 cups of rice and 4 cups of liquid, 6 minutes on pressure cook). It was so easy to throw together. And incredibly flavorful.
It has been decreed that this is our "house risotto" going forward.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous risotto recipe.  Easy ingredients.  Very flavorful.  Use good carrots from your farmers market if possible.  Risotto always takes longer than we think!  Worth it tho!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I didn't much care for this, and neither did my husband. I make risotto reasonably regularly and this was just meh. Adding the shredded carrots before the rice meant that the rice didn't get coated with fat and crackle the way it should, and the result was inferior texture and flavor. The carrots added way to much volume and moisture. The roasted carrots were ok, although I have a better recipes, but the shredded carrots in the rice were just a bad idea.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Truly delicious. Two things: 1. A little mistake I made, grating ALL the carrots instead of slicing some of them, IMO turned into a plus. I took the grated carrots that weren't mixed in with the rice, spread them out on a baking sheet (parchment paper!) mixed with the chili crisp and kept roasting, stirring, until caramelized and crisp. Grated crisp carrot on top was a pleasing addition to the texture and enjoyment. 2. We thought this was even better the second day. BTW, NOT a 30-min recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Just made this (vegan) and it was incredibly delicious and satisfying. Super easy, but definitely takes closer to 1-1.5 hours total. Used margarine and vegan parmesan and it was wildly tasty. Can't wait for leftover tomorrow!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: This is my favourite recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I make risotto regularly and I never would have come up with this combo!  It was delish and will make it again for sure.  Yummy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Love this recipe, make it all the times the kids love it too (if I leave the spicy out and just too it with chili crisp when I serve. However, this takes much longer than 30 minutes and usually double the broth.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Would this be an OK dish to serve for Easter? I’m trying to add a carrot dish and this looks amazing for my non meat eaters.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Really good and as an amateur that's trying to be better in the kitchen it was an impressive dish to make. Took a LOT more work than anticipated like others have said. I would cook it again as a side dish for a holiday.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody:
It's been 3 days and 3 nights since I made the risotto (It's spectacular by the way). The oven is still on.

I'm looking at it and it looks back. Neither of us knows what is our purpose in this reccepy or this world.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this twice (adding extra shallot because I’m obsessed with it) and I am blown away with this meal. It’s so good, so easy to perfect, and would impress even your fanciest eaters. Don’t sleep on this!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Carrot Risotto With Chile Crisp Recipe
commentBody: Such a delicious recipe. I added all the stock at once as cooking with a toddler, it worked out fine. I also had not enough carrots and some sunchokes that needed using up, so added them to the roasting pan. In the unlikely event this is also your situation, I can tell you they worked great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is a delicious recipe (I cooked it as written) hidden under bad planning. No reason it needed to arrive on the table lukewarm with a murder scene of dirty dishes in the kitchen. Bake the beans on a sheet pan as written - a quarter size is perfect. Bake tomato mixture in a large oven-proof skillet. Transfer skillet to cook top, add beans, then just scoop the pasta directly into the skillet with water clinging, adding more pasta water if necessary. 100% of sucs preserved with way less mess.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Rebecca thanks for the warning about the murder scene of dirty dishes! 😁 That’s sometimes an issue of contention when I make a NYT supposedly Easy Weeknight Meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Rebecca thank you for a great tip! You can also save dirtying a bowl by mixing the shallot marinade in the ovenproof skillet before adding the tomatoes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Thanks! A lovely dish, pasta e fagioli always a winner - the roasting is a nice touch.  But one suggestion, please, made with respect. I have lived in Italy, am married to an Italian, and have never understood why Americans add sugar to a tomato sauce - the acidity of the fruit is quickly cooked away.  Don't believe me - check out Antonio Carluccio, etc.   It's a terrible habit, sadly passed on by cookbook writers.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I think sometimes it's done to compensate for the relatively poor quality tomatoes available from most American supermarkets, where most people in the US buy their tomatoes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Brian my first generation Italian-American parents never added sugar. My Abruzzese mom took umbrage at the suggestion that her Sunday sauce caused agita (which it did not).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Brian. In my case, it was passed on by my FOB mother & grandmother, who immigrated from Italy in the 30s.  One of my earliest memories was of them commiserating about the quality of American vegetables, both fresh and canned, with my mother trying to insist that her mother speak English to do so.  They both insisted that American vegetables needed seasoning, lots of seasoning, while Italian vegetables did not.  The discussion only leveled off when we had enough property for my grandfather to grow his own vegetables (he never did get the idea of grass—he thought we should keep sheep or pigs), which my mother and grandmother canned.  Mom regularly salted, sugared, and vinegar’d tomatoes while she yelled—Taste!  You don’t know until you taste it!  Maybe American vegetables have gotten better in the last 90 years.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The recipe calls for two sheet pans because the beans won’t crisp if they’re crowded on one sheet pan with too much other stuff, especially if the tomatoes burst while roasting and the juices run.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: What you say is true only if you're making the full recipe.  Those of us who make 1/2 or 1/3 or 1/4 the recipe can easily use just one sheet pan for the beans and tomatoes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I put tomatoes and beans on separate sides of the same dish and it worked perfectly. This is an exceptional recipe. Add an anchovy to the tomato paste blend before tossing the cherry tomatoes in there and you will think you’ve died and gone to heaven.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Laura thank you for the suggestion! This was a great idea. I was out of anchovies so used a few glug a of fish sauce and it added a wonderful umami note.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with less than 3/4 cup of olive oil?  That sounds like a lot for 4 servings.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Lisa yes I did add any extra at the end and it tasted great for me

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Lisa I did not add the extra olive oil at the end and it was delicious. I found the oil in the dressing to be plenty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I really liked this. I suspect anyone saying they didn’t find it had enough flavour is either used to a lot of seasoning and/or didn’t season it enough. PS, I halved the oil and it was still great. Love the crunchy beans and the hint of heat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Two baking sheets is one too many. Why not put the tomatoes on one half and the beans on the other half?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Maybe the beans wouldn't have enough space between them to get crispy?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
I used 2 glass baking dishes for the beans and the tomatoes. Beans crisped and  tomatoes cooked up nicely as well.  
I use a Stainless Steel Steamer that I place in my Dutch oven to cook the pasta. This help’s strain the pasta, leaving plenty of pasta water. 
Used an immersion blender to cream part of the tomato/bean mix in the Dutch oven and added the pasta water as needed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
I followed the advice of another comment and cooked the tomatoes in a oven proof skillet and lifted the (wet) pasta straight out of its cooking pot to mix in the skillet. This saved the additional awkward step of pouring pasta water over a sheet pan and tipping it back in with pasta and tomatoes. 
Otherwise I made this as read - delicious,

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was very easy and absolutely delicious.  I'm in the two sheet pan camp.  My beans came out nice and crispy.  I think putting the beans and the tomatoes on the same sheet would have made the beans soggy from the tomato juice.  Also, the beans need the space to crisp up.  The slight heat was just perfect.  Only edit I'll make next time is to augment with more tomatoes.  They're delicious but I wanted more in the sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made just as written--delicious!  I especially like the complex textures that come from roasting the shallot, garlic and tomatoes...a little crunch, a little creamy, a little glossy.  Probably didn't use all of the oil, but close.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This came out delicious. I didn't have rosemary, so I used an Italian herb blend, but otherwise followed the recipe as written. I wouldn't describe the beans as "crispy," but I might have pulled them out a little too soon because they had gotten so brown. However, I enjoyed the chang's they developed from roasting, and the sauce is incredibly good: creamy, with a little bit of heat. This is definitely going into the rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I almost wish I could cuss on this page because I would say holy $&+# is this good!!! Made it mostly as written, skipped the sugar and roasted the tomatoes and beans in the same pan. I also only uses 8 oz of pasta cuz it's just me, otherwise followed the recipe. This is an absolute keeper. I will made this again and again. Can't wait to serve this to friends.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
I’d agree with others on three things:
1. Skip sugar
2. Use less pasta so you have more sauce
3. Don’t follow recipe if beans and tomatoes not brown and crispy. Leave them in a bit longer than 25 minutes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Delish. I should have the beans in a bit longer. I also think I could have added more garlic, more rosemary, and more shallot. It was flavorful for sure, but I think more of the above would pack a better punch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: No greens? Escarole or raab

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Worked great for me! Used substitutions I had on hand, like a small onion for shallot and gochugaru for red pepper flakes, and it turned out lovely. Nice and filling and a total no-brainer. Thought I would reduce the oil, but it really helped in the last step to keep the texture nice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I get why folks don't add sugar, but I challenge you to make two sauces, one with and without sugar, and blind taste them side by side. Any sauce will do, including mayo. We call sugar our secret ingredient; very much like MSG, it just waked up your tastebuds and brings flavors fully forward. Enlivening the sweet receptors on your tongue is a trick I have used for years. It doesn't take much, but a smidge in almost anything will make the flavors dance. Also, recipe is awesome as written.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Did anyone else enjoy this but also make immediate plans to make it again but with some pancetta?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @LBrennan I crisped some chorizo cubes alongside the beans and added this along with some feta in the tomato dish and was 10/10!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
This is excellent! Just 2 notes:

Make dressing in medium bowl and toss the sliced tomatoes in it before putting them on the sheet pan.

At the end, add the olive oil gradually. You probably don’t need the full half cup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe!  I have made it many times, and now make with the following modifications. 1) use 2 cans of beans vs one, 2) omit the pasta (while good, really don’t feel it’s needed), and 3) add arugula (or spinach) and allow to wilt from heat of beans and tomatoes. It’s delicious and a more complete dish in my opinion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I subbed polenta for the pasta.  The roasted tomatoes melted into the polenta.  The beans were home cooked cassoulet beans.  I will definitely make this again.  So easy!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was an OK pasta dish, but I didn't think it was better than a simple marinara would have been, which would have taken less time and would have been easier to taste-test along the way to adjust for flavor.  If you make this, I recommend reserving at least 1.5 cups of the pasta water before draining pasta because there did not seem to be enough sauce for this amount of pasta. We ended up leaving some pasta bare and saved it for another meal. I did like that this dish was vegan and used beans.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
To Brian

I'm guessing your time in Italy, and your Italian partner, are not from the south of Italy where the addition of sugar to tomato sauce is very common

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, but NOT a 30-minute, weeknight meal. It took 2 hours with all the chopping, roasting and cooking the pasta—and then lots of pans to clean up!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this dish exactly as written and it was absolutely wonderful. The olive oil provides luscious richness that I hope would keep guests from turning up noses and saying "She just fed us beans and tomatoes!" Just tell them you used the very best olive oil available! For those concerned about the pasta water--I just used a large pyrex 4-cup measuring cup and dipped out a bit more than a cup before draining. Problem solved.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I did not enjoy this dish at all. I h ave made much more flavorful pasta & bean recipes on the stove-top.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made this several times. Like many others here, I skip the second sheet pan for the tomatoes and also cut the amount of oil. While the beans are roasting and the pasta is cooking, I sauté the shallots in 2 tbsp of oil, then add garlic, chili and tomato paste, deglaze, then add tomatoes and rosemary. By the time the beans are done, the tomatoes have turned into sauce. Then just add pasta, beans, and pasta water as needed to make it all come together. Delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I agree wholeheartedly with Brian. Why do so many recipes--as well as any number of, really most, packaged products in the grocery--contain sugar? It's wholly unnecessary and, in most instances in my opinion, kills the dish. You don't need it and you'd be amazed at how good the dish tastes without it. Just ignore that specific line item on the recipe, and you'll be far better off.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: As is typical of New York Times "Italian" recipes, the amount of garlic -- 5 cloves!--is overkill. Garlic is meant to be a note, not a dominant flavor. I grew up in a southern Italian family. I've eaten in restaurants and homes in Italy and cooked for friends there. I've never experienced the kind of garlic overload as in this and other recipes in the Times. Beatrice Ughi of Gustiamo, the Italian food importing company, remarks that "garlicky is not an Italian flavor."  She's right.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I used basil as that is what I had, would use half the pasta next time...or perhaps double the tomatoes

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Canned beans dry out in the oven, using two cookie sheets is unnecessary and adds to dishes which defeats the “weeknight” menu suggestion

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was a hit with a few modifications for kick: two anchovies and two tablespoons gochujang to the sauce, plus plenty of that pasta water and a bit of extra low heat cooking to bring it all together.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Couldn’t agree more about the lamentable overuse of garlic in this and many other recipes. My parents were from northern Italy, where garlic is used sparingly, if at all; in fact, my father could t abide it so my mother never used it in her excellent authentic Italian recipes. While I know people that they automatically double garlic in recipes, no matter what’s called for, I usually start with a single clove, often only using half. Try it; you’ll be amazed at the subtle flavours that emerge.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I agree with you. So many people think that "double (or triple) the garlic" is the best way to show that they are knowledgeable about food.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I roasted everything in a 9 x 13 casserole dish and just poured the pasta water, and then the pasta directly in there. Less to clean up and it came out excellent!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I didn't add the extra oil at the end because the amount that was on the sheet pan with the tomatoes seemed like plenty. I didn't miss it. Also, I roasted the tomatoes and beans side by side on a single baking sheet and found it worked fine - the beans got crisp and the tomato juices stayed on their side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I thought the recipe was a bit olive oil heavy. I used 1/4 cup of olive oil for the tomato mixture as directed, but halved the rest of the olive oil for the remaining steps that call for it. The end product was just as delicious, velvety and flavorful as the recipe describes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
A rich, creamy, filling dinner that isn’t necessarily quicker than other pasta dishes but takes less babysitting due to so much work being done in the oven. 

I only added 1/4 of a cup of olive oil at the end instead of 1/2 and I think that was a good choice—there’s plenty of oil already and the sauce held together well thanks to all the pasta water.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was good, but the next day’s leftovers were way more flavorful. The beans had way more to say. Only time can do this. Make enough for planned “leftovers”.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Adding sliced olives at the end really elevates this dish. I also make it often on the stovetop rather than the oven: Works out fine!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Fabulous!

I roasted the tomato mixture and beans on opposite ends of the same large sheet pan. Worked like a charm. I left out the sugar as the cherry tomatoes are super sweet, and I like a little acidity, anyway, plus I used about half the pasta as we like more sauce rather than less. 

It was delicious!  EASY and FAST.  Perfect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
This was easy, delicious and much better than the sum of its parts. I used butter beans which exploded when roasting, but actually this created a lot of jagged edges which got nice and crispy so added to the texture. 
I used more tomatoes relative to the pasta which I would recommend. 
I cooked the tomatoes in a Dutch oven, then transferred pasta directly from cooking water into the tomatoes which simplified the final stages. 
Will add to my usual dinner rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Yum. Don’t like to add sugar to tomatoes so I just added about a cup of extra stewed tomatoes I had in the fridge. They were sweeter than the cherry tomatoes so it balanced nicely, and the pasta wanted more tomato anyway. That was a superior liquid to the pasta water, of which I only used 1/2 c. Also used less olive oil than called for- add slowly. Who cares if the beans are crispy, they are going to get mushed anyway at the end. Use one sheet pan.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was ok but not great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I wouldn't make this again since it was only 'meh' after increasing the tomatoes (as some commenters advise), the tomato paste concoction, rosemary, and garlic. Might be better during summertime tomato season, but otherwise too expensive, time-consuming, and bland.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, I used a little wine rather than sugar, and topped with some fresh vegan parmigiana!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is an amazing dish with rich, deep flavors. A new favorite! I used baby eggplant instead of beans--mixed with 1/4 cup olive oil and crushed garlic--and roasted them on a separate sheet. I also omitted the extra olive oil when mixing. A good quality tomato paste is key. This recipe seems easily adaptable. Next time I'll try it with artichoke hearts. I'm also going to try roasting the tomatoes and freezing so I can have summer fresh tomato sauce this winter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: @Carol yes to the roasting and freezing of backup tomatoes. Are you familiar with NigellaLawson’s Moonblush Tomatoes? Overnight residual heat roast on turned off oven after cooking something else on high. EVO, S &P, herb, garlic, tiny bit Sugar. Caramelizing, no hands.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made a few changes.  First, I prepared the white beans first, adding olive oil, salt and pepper to a bowl and then the drained rinsed beans.  Onto one end of a baking sheet.  Then I used the same bowl to mix the tomato mixture.  Spread that on the other end of the baking sheet.  It fit easily on one baking sheet.  Mine has a nonstick surface so the deglazing step was unnecessary.  I did only cook a half pound of pasta as I didn't have a big enough pot.  So my pasta was more sauce, less pasta.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Gee, almost a cup of oil? Kind of defeats the purpose of eating less meat for health.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Tasty and satisfying! I omitted the sugar. The proportions were right, but I think this dish could also handle less pasta and more beans, if you're aiming for protein

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Thank you. I was thinking of using less pasta. Maybe 1/2 pound to 3/4.Glad to know someone who made the recipe thinks less pasta will still work.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was just okay, IMO. I didn't find the sauce to be as luscious as described. The flavor was a little bland. After several bites, the texture with the beans and pasta felt mushy.  It needed some sort of "pop" to bring it all together. I served it with an arugula salad with parmesan and eventually combined the two in one bowl which made it a little better. I know this is a vegan/veggie dish, but if I made this again, I think I would add some spicy Italian sausage and maybe spinach.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Okay, so, this used so many freaking pots and pans. And there’s no way it’s a weeknight pasta dish unless you get off work at 4 p.m. HOWEVER, it was truly wonderful. Use all the olive oil it calls for. Use all the garlic it calls for. If you’re not vegan, parmesan is a must. I’ll make this dish again and again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made this for the first time in a so-so stocked air bnb kitchen and it was divine. Sure if I were at home I might have used the oven proof skillet et al. But a recipe that is mostly hands off, has easy to access ingredients, needs only two sheet pans and a pasta pot, and comes out delicious? Vacation dinner winner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I kept the beans and tomatoes separate on the same pan because I didn't want to wash two pans. Came out really good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Surprisingly delicious! Subtle, homey taste -- great for a rainy night. Made as written. Don't be shy with the salt -- in the pasta water, on the tomatoes and beans before roasting, and again as you stir it all together. Thinking how much my father-in-law will like this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This sauce is so good but too much pasta.  Make the same amount of sauce but half the pasta for 2-3 really nice size portions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I recently decided to try and cut out any type of animal product unless I know exactly where it comes from, which means trying to find a lot of plant based meals. This recipe is a win and because it was so flavorful I didn’t even miss the Parmesan! Made the recipe as-is and loved it. I may try skipping the sugar next time and possibly a little less oil. I think I could add more beans as well, just to make the meal go a little further.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I added bacon.  As one does.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: We found this only 'meh,' even after increasing the tomato sauce and adding anchovies, as some advise.  It might be better with summer tomatoes but I'll spend my money on another recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
It was OK and we'll make it again but...
1. Saute onions and garlic
2. Add tomatoes and beans to saute pan and bake. The bean won't get crispy but they also won't dry and will absorb flavors
3. Add some greens or zucchini to pan with tomatoes and beans.
4. Remove pan from oven and add pasta directly to saute pan, probably won't need additional water or oil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is an awesome and easy pasta recipe that I’m definitely adding into my rotation. I did change a couple things - I roasted the beans and tomatoes together because I didn’t want the beans to by crispy, and I used butter at the end to emulsify the sauce before putting the parm in.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I wish I had left out the sugar.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I don’t understand how the crispy beans work in this dish. To rescue it, I added another can of beans (not roasted) and it was greatly improved.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I 2xed the white beans, 1.5xed the tomato mixture, and reduced the evoo. I also took one commenter’s advice and roasted the tomato mixture in the same pot I mixed the pasta in. Brilliant! A repeat!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: If you wanted a little extra protein and you are not vegan, you could roast a couple of boneless, skinless chicken thighs at the same time you are roasting the beans and tomatoes. Just shred the chicken before you mix it into the pot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: What looks like a simple quick recipe was not. It took twice as long (1 hour) and left the kitchen packed with dirty pots, sheet pans, etc. That might have been fine if the taste were great, but we felt it was only slightly above OK. Sadly, tomato flavor was muted (despite using primo home-grown cherry tomatoes at peak season); cannellini beans added little overall; dish cried out for anchovies or capers to add lively depth. Plus, recipe yielded enough leftovers to feed an army (we're a couple).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Top of recipe: "Yield: 4 to 6 servings."

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Used Jovial GF mafalda - perfect shape.  I used less oil and less pasta water - I shouldn’t have , was a little dry.  Excellent combo and easy

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Excellent, but way too much oil in the recipe.  I used the liquid from the bean can for part of it, and cut other amounts down. I also cut the amount of pasta in half.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I’m not sure where this went wrong but the sauce was flavorless and the pasta too gummy. I even followed suggestions and halved the pasta to have a better sauce to pasta ratio. This is a no for me. I might revisit to see if I messed up somewhere and it just needs a second chance.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was so great!! If you, like myself, were wondering if great northern beans would work here (since that's all I had), they do! Also I made handmade orichette (since I had seminola but none of the dried stuff on hand) and the pasta water was starchy enough for the sauce to cut down the olive oil at the end for me!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This is an interesting recipe. It is similar to Pasta en Ceci but using white beans instead of garbanzos and deconstructing it with the step of roasting the beans. It works okay. I like the crunchiness of the roasted beans and it’d work great with garbanzos as well but I think it really needs anchovy, about five of them, mushed up with the tomato paste in the beginning. The squemish won’t even know they’re there, but they’ll know this tastes more yummy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: yummy.  easy to make and quick--looking forward to making again--maybe adding diced ham or chicken bits.  throw in some blanched broccoli or spinach.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
I was underwhelmed overall:
1. The sauce was very tasty on its own, and I loved the roasted beans addition to an otherwise straightforward tomato sauce. This part of the recipe is a keeper. 
2. As others suggested I oven-roasted the tomatoes, onion, garlic, etc in a Dutch oven, then added the pasta water to ensure I scraped up all the good bits. Then I added the pasta and olive oil to the Dutch oven to finish the dish. Saved some dish washing with this method. 
3. Overall result was not saucy enough when 1lb pasta used. I will take the blame for not adding the full ½ cup of olive oil at the end. That seemed like a lot so I held back. 
4. To give the leftover pasta a lift I will make more sauce and beans and add to leftover pasta. I’m thinking this might provide the sauciness I’m looking for.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I liked this a lot! The only this is the beans didn't stay crispy for long. After mixed everything together it seemed to have lost their crisp.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Flavor was very good. I added some extra garlic and tomatoes. I’ll make this again. But it took an hour to prepare the recipe. Given that the roasting time is 25 minutes and there’s preparation before and after that, a preparation time of 30 minutes is not possible.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
This is just ok. I’d recommend doubling the cherry tomato recommendation, or just making less pasta. The tomato flavor wasn’t as strong as I hoped..
Also the beans! Not sure what I did wrong but the skins of the means separated a bit and got kind of crispy / but hard to chew so I kept having to spit them out. The beans just got really dry. I liked the flavor but I’d probably just warm the up next time, and skip the crisping.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Reread the recipe to make sure I didn’t miss anything cause this recipe didn’t hit the mark. It wasn’t bad just wouldn’t be something I would make again, agree with other commenters that maybe some anchovies could add to the flavour.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Gross. I made the mistake of following one of the suggestions from another person m, specifically to roast the tomatoes in a 12in skillet. Instead of roasting, the tomatoes slumped into a stew and the shallots were uncooked. Instead of browned sucs I had an overwhelming raw shallot flavor. The canned beans meanwhile exploded. The skins became overly chewy while the insides completely dissolved into the sauce. The resulting flavor was really awful. Someone suggested that a jar of marinara sauce would be easier and tastier. So true.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious and easy- when we eat it as a vegetarian main dish, we double the beans to add more protein and substance.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: no sugar needed!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Considering the amount of oil/day recommended is 5-7 tsp/day or about 2 Tbsp, with 4 Tbsp in 1/4 cup, 3/4 cup of oil is excessive for 4 people.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made with a couple of modifications but in the spirit of the recipe- no cherry tomatoes on had so used canned plum tomatoes crushed with a potato masher added to bloomed olive oil, garlic and spices in large pan on the stove top.  Had a half pound of Ranch Gordo cassoulet beans previously cooked to use.  I haven’t been a fan of crispy beans (could be my timing or temp) so added them right before tossing in the pasta to warm up!   Served with grated parm - perfect for warming up after the frigid temps and winter storm!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Directions are a little confusing. It says; "cook the pasta until al dente. Reserve 1 cup pasta cooking water then drain pasta.
Transfer the beans and tomatoes to the pot." It next tells you to add the pasta to the pot". I am assuming when you drained the pasta you were supposed to also remove it from the pot?  If so, why remove the pasta only to add it back? Seems like you could add the beans and tomatoes to the pot while the pasta was still in the pot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Because many people drain their pasta in the sink using a strainer. Therefore the pot is empty and the pasta is in the strainer.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: After following the recipe as closely as I could, I had to add half a jar of RAO tomato soup to make it edible. I accept, to a degree, that the cherry tomatoes I found were bland but that doesn't explain the flatness of the flavors before I upped the ante.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
What exactly is a "glug"?
1/4 cup? 1 tbsp?....???

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: In Italy we call a glug a "filo" which means a thread. There is no exact amount, it just means swirl a bit into your pan. Whatever you like.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Easy. Healthy. Good flavor and texture. (Pasta water is a must.)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I cannot fit two pans in my oven and put everything on one. The beans didn't brown of course, but the end result was delicious nonetheless.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Used 1/2 a red onion instead of shallot and baked using parchment paper. May have lost some browning/toasting flavor because of it. Used garlic Chile oil with beans, eeov with tomatoes and added in scallion flavored olive oil at end mix, more like a 1/3 of a cup oil rather than half. Used full one cup of pasta water.
Finished with black garlic sea salt.
Good comfort food for a damp day.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Just made this for dinner following recipe exactly. It’s delicious. Served with crusty bread and butter. I will use more fresh rosemary next time. I could only really taste in one bite and the addition of the rosemary flavor was really nice. The amount of pepper flakes is perfect. There’s no way it takes 30 min, at least not with my level of knife skills m, but the active time is minimal. I’d love to know what vegetable people put on the side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made a riff on this for dinner but didn’t have cherry tomatoes so I used a large can of whole canned toms. Would be better with fresh roasted wee ones but this was still worthy. Used a Dutch oven to soften the shallot and garlic before adding tomato paste and spices to the pan for several minutes to concentrate flavor. Added the tomatoes whole but not the juice for fear it would be too watery. Curious to use chickpeas and roast gnocchi with the tomatoes rather than pasta.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I definitely used too much onion (1 bulb) in substituting for the shallot. Also, the smell of all the onion roasting gave me a headache unfortunately, next time I'll caramelize on the stove top while the tomatoes and beans are roasting in the oven to avoid the pungent onion smell

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Simple and delicious.   I made as directed except I puréed half of roasted tomato and beans.  Didn’t have cheese but imagine that would have made it yummier!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made a few changes that produced a great product. For one, I used gnocchi as my pasta and in my opinion that was the best choice! Also, I put the tomatoes in a pot from the beginning and prepared them as more of a pasta sauce, adding the beans in at the end. Saved me time on dishes. Last, olive oil is added as nearly every step… I just used it as a cooking oil instead of adding so much. Great recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I was intrigued to try this, as I’ve never roasted beans before. They didn’t really get like croutons but they did crisp up. The sauce is delicious. I used coconut oil instead of olive oil because I happen to have a lot of it on hand. Nice, unusual dish, which I will make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Not very saucy as written. Would likely double both the tomatoes (same amount of ‘dressing’) and the beans next time. Also even though the beans were crispy on the sheet pan, they immediately rehydrated in the tomatoes and were not crispy in the final dish for us.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This takes longer than 30 minutes to make (at least for me it did). Most of us are not trained chefs who can slice and dice the ingredients in just a few minutes.  However,  it is absolutely delicious and worth the time and effort to make.  Will definitely make this again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Same exact experience. This was delicious but after making it I decided it's not a weeknight meal for my household -- took nearly an hour with prep time included.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I work from home and made this for lunch on a chilly day. I used one small can of chopped  tomatoes b/c I had them in the pantry (don't drain). They melt down to a thick delightfully smooth sauce. About 20 minutes to roast the tomatoes and beans.  Used less oil -roughly `1/2 cup it to roast and alternated with oil and pasta water at the end when combining to finish. Definitely feel the roasting adds a dimension to the flavor and crispy outside, smooth inside to the beans.Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
Good flavors, but seemed like it should have been two cans of beans.  

I mixed in some spinach that I needed to use up with the pasta and let it wilt a bit before adding the tomatoes and beans.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The flavor in this dish punches way above its weight, in my opinion. Delicious! For the record, I only used one sheet pan, and my beans crisped just fine. I also used parchment paper on the sheet pan, and those brown bits just slid right off into the pot (there surely weren’t as many as if I’d done it without parchment, but I’ll take ease on any weeknight). Will be making this again! I’ll add some kale tossed with olive oil to the sheet pan about halfway through next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: We gilded the lily by topping with 4 oz of crispy pancetta that I needed to use up.  It added extra texture and savory saltiness that we loved.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Added some feta cheese to the baking tomatoes. Took the place of some of the olive oil at the end- I only added a splash. Very tasty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I left out the sugar and added a splash of balsamic to the plate for a hit of sweet. Really really good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I substituted harissa for tomato paste and toasted breadcrumbs for grated cheese.  It tasted great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I think this may be one of the few dishes where canned beans are better than dried! I genuinely did not have any canned beans but did have dried - so I soaked and cooked them in the instant pot and then carried on the recipe as written. The beans dried out too much in the oven and were hard as rock. Despite this, I thought this dish packed a lot of flavour and depth for simple ingredients. I will happily make again though this time with the humble canned bean!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: use large skillet for tomato steps, mix other into that

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: For children who don’t like cooked tomatoes, I subbed a can of tomato sauce for the fresh tomatoes. I cooked the shallot, sliced garlic (reduced to 3 cloves), tomato paste, and dried rosemary in olive oil, then added the tomato sauce and let it simmer for a while. A quick pasta sauce in the spirit of the recipe! Success: Nobody complained about the beans in the sauce!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I don't even have words for how good this was. Took the advice to roast the tomatoes in an ovenproof skillet. Cut back by 1/4 cup on the oil at the end and it was still phenomenal. Served it with broccoli rabe, and it was a great meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Omitted the sugar, doubled the beans, added baby spinach at the end/mix all together step. My family loved!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Halved the oil throughout because it really did seem like a LOT, and I have no regrets.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
The beans came out strange - I used cannellini beans and the skins split when they were roasting so each bean kind of “exploded”. They definitely got browned but I would not describe them as crispy… and once I dumped them in the sauce since the skins split in the oven they totally disintegrated into the sauce. Definitely no crunch. 

However - beans, tomato, pasta is a good combo so I still enjoyed it. But might be more simple and yummy to skip roasting the beans so they don’t disintegrate.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I only had 8oz tomatoes on hand, so I added an extra tbs of tomato paste to the dressing and  added about 8oz chopped spinach to the tomatoes half way through roasting. Spinach got a little crunchy which was a nice twist.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Big hit. Super easy. Not a fan of orichette - elbows?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I thought that 375 was a little low so I roasted everything at 450, also combined the beans and tomatoes into one pot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, easy, and plentiful---a perfect recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: doesnt need 2 pans for a creamy bean saucy thing, cook tomatoes around edge and beans in middle probably could DOUBLE the tomato paste sauce and just toss the beans in that too. make the tomato sauce and cook everything in a deep pan not a sheet pan, then cook/mix in EGG NOODLE

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Brian - it's a 1/2 tsp!!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: The 375 roasting temperature risks burning the sliced garlic. To be on the safe side, drop it to 350 (or 325) and add 5 minutes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is really good! I cut the olive oil to a 1/4 cup in the last step. Also if you are a “sauce” person like me, reduce the pasta by as much as half and have some bread on the side to soak up the sauce. Lastly, I think this would work with any substantial pasta or even thick spaghetti.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was a disappointment. Tomatoes were ok but boring beans=flat flavor. Try Colu Henry's Tomato and White Bean Stew (you can serve over pasta), that really hits the spot and is very easy to prepare.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: You can halve the pasta and double the beans. I added bay scallops at the end when I combined the tomatoes and beans to the pasta, and used fresh basil, which I had, instead of rosemary. Perfect!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was pretty good but I wished I had doubled the beans.  They shrink when roasted and got lost in the pasta.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Love this and will make it again. I also agree with the two sheet pan approach. Would anyone try roasting the garlic with the beans to give them some added flavour? Could full-size roma tomatoes also be used in this dish?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this as written except I used way more cherry tomatoes from an overachieving garden. The recipe can handle it; just needed to cook longer to reduce the moisture. It was delicious. I really like how the beans add crispness & creaminess.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Italy and Mexico get married and have a beautiful child. Don’t forget the pepper flakes. Makes a delicious dinner with a steamed vegetable such as broccoli on the side.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, however, since we are only two I halved the recipe, and it still made enough for four.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made it exactly as suggested and it was wonderful.  The two of us ate all four serves.  Next time I'll try it with half the oil.  This is now on our recurring list of favourites.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: “Laura” suggested adding an anchovy. Do you have a brand you recommend? I’m always a fan of the anchovies at a tapas restaurant, which they call boquerones, but I have yet to find good anchovies at the store. Thanks, in advance.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I'm disappointed when I see a recipe that says it takes 30 minutes but then realize that the ingredients require significant preparation before that timer starts. The roasting time alone is 25 minutes, so this recipe is only 30 minutes if you already have a minced shallot, 5 cloves thinly sliced garlic, and 16 ounces of cherry tomatoes all cut in half.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: If you learn that cooking time does not include prep time (chopping, slicing, shopping), you won't be disappointed. Everyone slices/chops at a different speed; it would be difficult to predict the prep time and have it accurate for all levels of ability.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Nice blend of flavors. The beans make it taste richer.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I roasted some Italian sausage, chopped it up and added that as well.  This is a delicious recipe that I will cook again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Not enough flavor for my family. Though I see the potential of this preparation. Next time will use more tomatoes and seasoning ingredients.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Followed the advice to use a oven safe pot. My Le Creuset braiser was perfect. Used a half sheet tray lined with parchment and did the beans on one side and 8 oz of halloumi torn into bite sized pieces on the other. Everything roasted up at the same rate. It was delicious and absolutely a keeper. With minimal clean up!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
If you have an oven with convection roast setting I think it makes a big difference and it does cook faster 
I checked it at 10 min., stirred and cooked only 5 more - I was able to keep all on one sheet.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: What did I do wrong?  These beans are breaking my teeth off.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Loved this. Added spinach and extra chilli but love how creamy it gets because of the beans...will definitely make it again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as written (even with native tomatoes in season), but thought it was bland and ordinary. And called for way too much pasta in relation to other ingredients.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was good but much better after I added marinated artichokes!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Anchovy is a good idea.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Followed other tips and roasted the beans/tomatoes on one large baking sheet…no sweat. Otherwise, followed as written & came out stellar. Will add to rotation

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. I found the ratio better using 2/3 of a pound of pasta.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: This was pretty good, but not sure I'd make it again. A little bland, and I did not enjoy the dry crisp beans mixed in (although my husband did). I wish it had a little more flavor. I used cherry tomatoes from my garden so they weren't lacking in flavor. Overall we enjoyed it and it was fun to make.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Meh.  Edible but I wouldn't make it again.  I don't feel that the flavors and textures were as prominent as they could be.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Subbed garbanzo beans. Scooped ricotta atop rather than parmesan. It was really good. Kids had seconds.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: So delicious!! Didn't think all that pasta water would be necessary but it soaked in... very flavorful, blended well together, love it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Tasty dish! I omitted the rosemary and used extra tomatoes for a little more sauce and some dried basil per my family's preferences, and it turned out great. Everyone was pleased. Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I found this to be a lot of extra work with little reward.  Roasting tomatoes and cannellini beans to be uncesssary. A simple fresh stir fry of tomatoes in a bit of olive oil then adding the sauce ingredients to the pan and cooking it all abit would have been a more moist and flavourful fresher brighter tasting dish. Overall I thought this turned out bland dry and uninteresting.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Next time I would use less pasta, maybe 3/4 pound instead of 1 pound, and more tomatoes. It was good, but not great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made it as written,but using the suggestion about less pans and cleanup. My only issue was that the little pasta hats clumped together.  My fault, too little water.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: A little too much pasta for the amount of sauce and beans; 3/4 lb. perfect for proportion purposes and still offers 4 generous servings.  Big round of applause for this recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Just made this for the first time and loved it! My sauce was not creamy - anyone have some advice? I may not have added enough pasta water....? Thanks!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: My kids called this dish "mediocre".  I followed the directions exactly, but the beans gave a bit of an unpleasant texture.  If I did it again I'd half the beans, but I don't see us making this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made a lot of different versions of this recipe, but this one is the hardest and doesn’t wow me. Try one that keeps it stovetop

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Roasted White Bean and Tomato Pasta Recipe
commentBody: We loved it!  I used garbanzo beans since I had no white beans.

author: Alexa Weibel
