recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Works beautifully with chicken - I used leftover roasted chicken.  Don't forget a poached egg right on top.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried cooking with less sugar than is asked for in the recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: yes I did, and it actually needs some sweetness to balance the intense umami from the other flavors. So I added later. You won't taste it directly in the end product.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: 99 Ranch sells pre-sliced, frozen ribeye, which cuts prep time to practically nothing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Any good tips on what to do if you can’t find Saki? I have mirin, but don’t want to add too much. Recently moved to Boston and still haven’t figured out where I can buy alcoholic items or not yet haha!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful I’ll definitely make it again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: I usually read all the notes before cooking. This time I didn't. Wish I had! The addition of the poached egg (or raw, stirred-in) would be great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and satisfying meal. Added poached eggs and picked carrots with ginger.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: I just ran out of soy sauce, and due to quarantine I can’t run out and pick up more! Anyone try substituting the soy for coconut aminos or ponzu? How was the flavor?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Aminos are similar

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody:
Next time 1TBLS brown sugar
Tad more Mirin?
No more than one tsp ginger

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Works like pork too, and dumped a can of corn on top for more veggies.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night. Did not have any Sake (never really liked it) so I used Chardonnay instead. For the Mirin I used the Kikkoman variety that's available in grocery stores. I had frozen the ribeye a few days ahead of time and put it in the fridge a couple hours before cooking. I took it out and it was still very frozen. Using an excellent chef knife I was able to shave the steak into paper thin strips at a steep angle against the grain. The final result was slight sweet, incredibly tender.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: I happened to have several packages of Japanese mushrooms on hand so I sliced and added those along with the onions. Made a rather small four-person meal into a much more substantial one. Love this dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: This was easy and delicious. It’s made our family rotation

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: So good and easy to make. Wonderful flavors from a few ingredients. Use instant dashi or make your own but don’t omit it. I added chopped broccoli with the onions. Maybe also mushrooms next time? Sushi rice hot from the rice cooker and a little fresh cilantro sprinkled on top (because we love it). Did not have togarashi so used some chili crisp instead. Togarashi is now on the grocery list. Will make again and maybe try with chicken next time?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: I love Yoshinoya's Gyudon but it certainly is not made with boneless beef rib-eye.  It apparently uses an obscure cut that is not normally sold at retail anywhere so Yoshoniya is able to buy huge quantities at a low cost.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Frozen beef (and chicken) slice easily and to a consistent thickness on a mandolin. (You can adjust the slice thickness on all the mandolins I’ve ever had). This is true even when the meat is frozen in marinade, and whether it is raw or precooked (ie sous vide and then frozen). It’s also good for stir fry.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Use the Hondashi and absolutely use the boneless ribeye. Accept no substitutions.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: We love making this at home. Easy and tasty meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Go to a good Asian market and you can buy pre-sliced ribeye and save yourself the hassle of freezing and slicing the meat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody:
Very quick and easy. SO tasty. I had everything in my pantry except the sake and I was not going to buy a bottle just for this. So in addition to the mirin, I used some dry sherry instead of the sake.

A good source for powdered dashi is Amazon. The dashi really gives this a traditional taste—not fishy, but adds depth of flavor.

I could see keeping some trimmed steak in the freezer to make this any time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: I think I would remove the sugar in this recipe. The addition of ginger at the end makes the recipe sweet enough, in my opinion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Broth reduced too much on high for 8 min. Reduce heat or time. Double onion, but cook longer. Add veg on the side?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: Cooking wine or vermouth

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: This came out perfectly ! I added a bit of extra liquid because I like a lot of broth to pour over my rice. Loved it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: I use frozen sliced beef from my local Asian market.  It comes out better than slicing meat myself.  Also, I let the onions cook longer than what the recipe suggests, more like 15-20 minutes, so that they really soften and absorb the cooking liquid.  I start the broth, add the onions, then get started on the rice in my rice cooker, then wait about 10-15 minutes to add the meat.  The whole thing is done inside of an hour, and we have dinner and a lunch done.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: What is "sliced from stem to stem"? I'm no botanist, but I'm pretty sure onions only have a single stem end...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: This was fantastic, super easy and tasty. I tried the mandoline thing but found it way too fussy, a well sharpened chef's knife will do the trick. Definitely agree with the reviews to double the onions and let them braise for longer. Will definitely be a go to in this house.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody: So easy. Super delicious. Halved the sugar. Had leftovers with hash browns and a cpl chili oil fried eggs. Yum

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Gyudon Recipe
commentBody:
WOW. I *love* gyudon, but I can’t eat gluten anymore, so, like many things it is now tragically off the list of things I can get at restaurants. 

I just made this with gluten-free soy sauce and OMG I MADE GYUDON, it tastes just like it’s supposed to and…. I’m over the moon.

Furthermore I made my own dashi (with the nyt recipe) and… I’m just so happy and I feel like such a boss! THANKS FOR THIS RECIPE!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: My Mom’s albóndigas we’re ethereal. Combined with frijoles a la charra, avocado slices and corn tortillas and you were in heaven. One key is the tomato base sauce—-it needs to simmer at least 30-45 minutes. Then add the albóndigas.  Best to repent now because you won’t get them in any place but heaven.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: In Guadalajara people chop a green zucchini in tiny pieces, and mix it along the mint, one egg, rice, and meat, and add water not chicken stock, the meat makes a great broth.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I'm always looking for a vegetarian spin on delicious recipes. This one works well with gardein meatless meatballs and vegetable stock, or if you can find it, no chicken base by better than bouillon. The veggie meatballs need less time: only 15-20 minutes, instead of 40 like the original recipe suggests. I find it's enough to have the rice cook through as well, so the soup is done quicker.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: In Mexico albóndigas are usually not a soup but a main entree. Usually served with a side of rice. (Beans optional). We use fresh tomatoes to make the sauce and add a little chipotle chile so it’s a bit hot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
Undercooked rice? Cover rice in boiling water and let stand for 45 minutes. Then drain before adding to meat mixture. 
     Also, 40 minutes is a bit on the short side, especially for such large albóndigas. 1:15 or even 1:30 would be ok. 
     Finally, Keep pot covered while simmering. 
For more info see: The Cuisines of Mexico by Diane Kennedy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: In the extraordinary kitchen that Mom ran, albondigas was my favorite meal as a child. Mom’s Mexican rice, chopped onion, chile, and oregano on the side were always served along side the delicious brew. Now, I use ground turkey instead of beef and pork, and I experiment with vegetables and spices. Never the same twice. Salud!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: WOW! I am seriously a novice chef and I followed this recipe to make one of the best recipes I have ever tasted. It was super delicious and even my two young children loved it. I highly recommend this soup, so tasty!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Love the rice in the meatballs, chicken stock kept the broth lighter... a margarita while the sopa is cooking is recommended.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious, even without a night in the refrigerator, which I find always improves the flavor of soups and stews. It was more of the latter....huge, tender, flavorful meatballs with a gravy. Perfect over rice or mashed potatoes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I used cilantro in the meatballs instead of mint and one 16 ounce can fire roasted tomatoes instead of the 8 ounce can tomato sauce. The soup was delicious and I would give it 5 stars

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This absolutely kills every time we make it - deeply emo toddlers included. Would suggest halving if there are only 3-4 of you.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Six degrees (C) in San Miguel de Allende this morning. This recipe was just what the doctor ordered. I did add a dried chili to simmer with the broth. Looks fabulous. Thank you so much.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Loved this soup, I would cut down on the ingredients for the meatballs  and make them bite size. Other than that the flavor were spot on.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I used premade spicy Italian meatballs and it turned out great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Soup is tasty but I wish I had baked the meatballs instead of poaching them in the soup.  The soup ended up heavy and greasy.  Removed this recipe from my box.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I’m glad I baked the meatballs at 400 til they browned, flipping once halfway because they were super greasy. They were done after 30 min simmering in the soup. Really good recipe, tasty and only about an hour total.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Made with Beyond meatballs, so I baked them for about 15-20 minutes before tossing it in the soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: All very tasty I followed the recipe to a tee.  My rice didn’t cook completely after 45 min.  I think next time I would make the rice separately and serve the soup over it. It was delicious thoug!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: After step 3,  I would strain the stock and remove the excess fat, then add the stock back to the soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: We really enjoyed this delicious soup but I agree with another comment that the grease from the meat was a lot. I think partially roasting the meatballs to render some of the fat would be helpful and not affect the flavor too much as they would finish cooking in the broth.  I couldn’t find a large red potato so I used a handful of tiny red potatoes cut in half (unpeeled).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was great! I would make two changes next time. There were a LOT of meatballs to the amount of broth. Next time, I’ll freeze half of the meatballs and save them for another batch of soup. It was time consuming to skim the large amount of fat off the top while it cooked, so next time I’m going to brown the meatballs first to get some of the fat off. I used many of the toppings and I think they’re necessary to get the full picture of just how amazing this soup was!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe. Doubled it and it made 55 2 oz meatballs, just one shy of the recipe estimate! I hooked up Grandpa and the kids, and we have plenty left over. 3 meals for six people when doubled, no problem (3 meatballs per serving). I followed another reviewer's advice and covered the raw rice with boiling water and let it sit for a while. I wanted to make sure the rice cooked all the way. Used the recommended toppings, but only Fresno chiles for the peppers for a sweet and spicy kick.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Very good. Followed recipe. Could use a bit more heat, but added habanero on top. Served on rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I found it a bit bland both the meatballs and broth. I think it needs some heat in the broth and a tad bit more salt.  Hoping the garnishes do the trick.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Ok, hear me out. 1lb ground beef, 1lb ground pork, 1lb ground lamb. With mint, it was spectacular. I also used short grain rice and had no problem getting it cooked in 40 min.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: When do you add the rice?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: The rice is part of the meatball mixture...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Can use premade meatballs, or include chopped zucchini and egg in the meatballs. Some use fresh tomatoes for the soup rather than canned sauce. Some use water, not chicken broth, to get a meaty taste. And some serve the soup over cooked rice or in/with tortillas.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was truly one for the books! Love this recipe. I did leave in fridge overnight and scraped a huge amount of fat off the top. Still delicious. I also made chicken stock from scratch which I felt added to the rich flavor. Love it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: The taste was wonderful and got rave reviews from my (Mexican) friends. But, like some others, I found the meatballs to be denser and tougher than I like. Also, the wild rice took forever to be done. Next time I will try white rice, or maybe even soaked bread (I read this helps meatballs to be soft), and also will take care to mix the meat only enough to blend everything. Don’t skip the mint! It really adds to the complexity of flavors. Cheddar cheese and chipotle sauce for toppers were super.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Loved it, the garnishes are so delicious with the soup. I used chuck and pork shoulder and my meatballs were super tender.  I did, however, need to siphon off some of the grease from the top of the soup.  Highly recommended.  It tastes better after a couple days.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Made this recipe exactly as is and it was wonderful!! You do not need to precook the meatballs or rice. The only thing I did was soak the rice in some warm water before adding to meatball mixture but that was mainly to clean it. I let everything cook much longer than the recipe called for since I wanted it to have a more stew-like consistency. Absolutely delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Followed recipe exactly. It was fantastic and is now in our menu rotation. The ingredients are not exceptional but the balance of flavors is so good. I think cooking the meatballs in the same pot must be what makes it so delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was so good--the broth was fantastic. I ran out of celery so I substituted fennel, and didn't have cotija or queso fresco, so went with sour cream as a garnish. I also only had pork so just made them with the meat I had, and it all was scrumptious. I also have a lot of kale in my garden so added a side of sauteed kale on top, which was really nice, if you want to get some healthy greens in there. I will definitely play around with this dish for awhile.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I added a little rice to the broth and added about 2T of ground rice to the meatballs to both hold them together and to lighten them. No celery, because I had none on hand. Delicious and satisfying. I liked the mint in the meatballs. We topped the soup with a little chopped mint/cilantro and some shredded pepper jack cheese.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
Please share your mothers' albóndigas recipe.  Thank you.
Lynda 

"Gil Narro Garcia - 4 months ago
My Mom’s albóndigas we’re ethereal. Combined with frijoles a la charra, avocado slices and corn tortillas and you were in heaven. One key is the tomato base sauce—-it needs to simmer at least 30-45 minutes. Then add the albóndigas. Best to repent now because you won’t get them in any place but heaven.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Brought into our monthly rotation- we love this soup! I have my girls roll the meatballs while I prep the rest. Make it exactly as is, except we double the garlic (always). Top with crema, avocado, and radishes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Like many I made some adjustments. I used homemade turkey broth and homemade sauce. I added cumin, paprika and garlic seasoning to the meatballs. I also added bell pepper and lion's mane mushrooms. Turned out very well!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Followed this step by step and it worked a dream. I let the tomato and vegetable base sit for a while as we ran errands. Then turned heat on again when we got home and added the meatballs. 40 minutes seemed to do the trick (definitely skim the fat off the top fit a more flavorful broth), though leave in longer to let the rice cook completely inside the meatballs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was the second time I've made this and it came out great. I think not having an audience to perform for (because of the pandemic) makes my cooking better. This was also very easy. Now if this virus ever gets under control, I can whip this up for a spontaneous gathering.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
This was lovely. Great for a person with a low heat tolerance. But the great thing about this soup is you can make it hot to your liking with toppings. I would add zucchini next time for more veggies. The amount of meat is waaaaay more than you think. I made 38 golf ball sized balls. So either cut it or freeze extra meat balls for later use. 

I would also like to  par bake the meat balls and only drop them in the soup for a few few minutes so they stay moist.   Overall a pretty excellent soup

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I grow copious amounts of mint in my garden, specifically to add handfuls to the meatballs for Albondigas Soup.  It absolutely makes the soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was such a flavorful, nuanced soup. Thanks to those who recommended cooking the rice before incorporating it into the meatballs. I didn't have a pound of pork, and so opted for chorizo instead. The result was delicious, if you like a little spice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Definitely skimmed quite a bit of fat from broth as it neared the end of cooking and while it rested, but this produced a lovely, flavorful dish. Also took the suggestion to cook covered (or partially) to aid cooking the rice through, and added +/- 15 mins of simmering, to be sure. Threw in sliced mini sweet peppers 5 mins before taking off heat, and glad I did. Added lots of chopped cilantro and a good squeeze of lime before serving. And crumbled tortilla chips were a welcome garnish. A keeper!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Make this with half as much meat and the same broth.  Used 1/2 cup pace instead of the tomato sauce.   Needs a lot of mint….buy two if not in the garden.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely amazing!  I have found that letting the pot sit for 2 hrs after cooking results in a richer flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Sublime. This is the first time I’ve made this, I did my best to hew as closely to the recipe as possible. My only substantial change was substituting a combo of light and dark meat ground turkey for beef. I used a light hand in forming the meatballs so they would not get too dense and gently poached them as instructed. Fantastic.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: El mexicano crema is a great addition too

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Add egg to meatballs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Why? Did you make it without egg and needed more binder? Do you just always add an egg to meatballs? Is it a superstition, like not using an egg brings bad luck?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: A delicious recipe that I converted into a vegan soup and made it in under 25 minutes.  I baked my frozen Beyond Meatballs in the oven (10 minutes!) using the thermometer insuring a 165 degrees temp.  Heated up the 8 cups of water in a kettle and added a few teaspoons of vegetarian Better than Boullin to make the broth.  Used a 6oz can of tomato paste instead of the paste/sauce combo.  At the last minute, I added a boil-in-bag rice packet because the soup felt a little too thin.  A keeper!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Made a half recipe with what I scavenged from the freezer: a pound of ground beef and a half pound of Impossible Italian sausage. Worked like a charm. Total keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty. Used ground turkey to make it lighter, then halved (or a bit less) the recipe, which made enough soup for 2 people for 2 nights. Otherwise, cooked as written. Delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: My preference is for ground chicken to lighten the dish.  It has the added benefit of not exposing my lack of skill when skimming off the grease. I know it’s not authentic, but this gorgeous soup isn’t harmed by that minor change.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: As a vegetarian, I tried this with Impossible and Soyrizo "meat" balls (with the uncooked rice, per the recipe).  I know that this may seem heretical, but it was absolutely delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This is the third time making this recipe and it’s perfect as is. This is one of my favorite soups. We’re California transplants in the Midwest and we make Mexican food often because we can’t go down the street and pick from multitudes of excellent restaurants anymore. I’m trying to perfect the home wet burrito, but for now at least I have my albóndigas! Now if we could just grow limes in a greenhouse…

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I was looking for a Mexican flavored broth to use up my rotisserie chicken.   So I just made the broth, not the meatballs.   Broth is fantastic.   I did add some leftover refrigerated salsa from Costco, and a bag of roasted corn.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!  Made as directed, rice was perfect.  I did cover the pot and only uncovered after 20 min for a brief stir. I might add a dried chili to the broth next time since we like a little heat, but it was so good and so well-balanced, I might not, after all.  Served with flour tortillas that my Venezuelan partner toasted over the gas burner just before serving.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Was delicious!  I upped the spices and soaked the rice as per below.  I added zucchini as well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I liked the recipe just as it was but would halve the meat for next time. Used lean ground beef and ground pork so excess fat was not a problem at all. Rice came out done. Used all of the toppings but added chopped fresh kale to the bottom of the bowl before adding soup. The recipe is a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious.  Finally found a great albondigas soup recipe.  Followed recipe, except I baked the turkey meatballs.  I used cooled cooked rice with turkey meat, baked meatballs on parchment paper at 350 degrees for 15 min.  Added cooked meatballs, to soup after step where broth is first brought to a boil.  Then simmered soup with cooked meatballs for 20-30 min.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: As written, the recipe is very good, but three pounds of meat, IMHO, is overkill. I had to skim quite a bit of fat off before serving. With that one small caveat I give this five big stars.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I made the recipe on a whim and only had 1lb ground beef and 2 lbs ground turkey. I made everything else as noted. The meatballs were tender and delicious, the broth was not too oily but beautifully flavored. I also threw in two zucchini when I added the meatballs. This will become a regular meal for us! Inexpensive, healthy, and delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I prepared this for the cast and crew of El último sueño de Frida y Diego and they loved it. To make this more of a soup, and less of a meatball dish with some broth, I kept the meatball ingredient proportions the same as the recipe states and made walnut-size meatballs, but doubled the broth proportions. A HUGE hit for a large crowd and now with this cold weather settling in, I am craving this!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I needed to use some ground turkey, so that went into the mix with the pork and beef. I added more Mexican spices to both the meat and the broth, and peas and corn. Most importantly, regardless of authenticity, I baked the meatballs, then simmered them in the soup for about 15 minutes. They did not absorb the soup as they would have, but the leftovers will do a better job.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I used leftover cooked rice, otherwise followed to the letter and it was wonderful.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: The meatballs in this were a hit, but we felt the broth needed more flavoring. I would bump up everything except the tomato factor. I also think leaving about half the grease on top is preferable. It was very greasy, but I took too much off. And a little more potato in case you have someone who doesn’t like the garnishes (I do!). I garnished with avocado, sweet pepper, cilantro, chopped onion and lime. Some corona would be delish too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Yes, the word “albóndigas” means “meatballs!” This sounds good; think I’ll try it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Can this be frozen?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I made this with half of the suggested meat. I also reduced the chicken stock by half and added water to make up the difference. The soup was delicious and still had plenty of meatballs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This soup was delicious. I cut the meat in half and used a 50:50 ratio of pork to beef for a juicier meatball. I kept all the seasoning without any reductions. Even my little picky eaters had a second bowl each.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
This is delightful. I made two adjustments that I think don’t violate the spirit of the recipe: 
1. I used two pounds of meat instead of three to make the meatballs (it was plenty), and
2. I was nervous that the meatballs would disintegrate in the soup if I dropped them in raw, so I par-roasted them at 400 degrees for 15 minutes before adding them to the broth. That firmed them up and they held their shape perfectly. 

I didn’t think the toppings were cheffy, either. Thank you, Alexa and Wesley!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight. Outstanding. My wife loved it, I loved it. It's on our make again list.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: My mother’s recipe is very similar you’d add chopped tomatillos to the meatball mixture ! It add some tang to it !

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I liked the flavor of the soup and it was easy to make. I thought the meatballs were a bit firmer/tougher than baked or fried Italian meatballs. Not sure if it’s that there’s no breadcrumbs, the method of cooking, or that the beef I used was pretty lean. I halved the meat quantity and used a 1:1 ratio of beef to pork and thought that was definitely enough meatballs. I would consider adding rice separately and letting that cook in the soup next time. Would make again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Followed this recipe as written and it was delicious. The toppings and lime added texture and zing. I couldn’t find the suggested cheeses where I live so I used crumbled ricotta salata which while obviously not authentic worked well with the other flavours.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious.  Even better than I have in most Mexican Restaurants.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I followed this recipe to a T.  It was tasty (especially topped with cotija cheese and lime juice), but I found the meatballs to be disappointingly tough.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and hearty, especially with the garnishes. I would half the meat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night and it was amazing!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: not great;  because the temperature on the burners runs so high, it was difficult to cook at right temperature; and didn't have pork, so used JD pork sausage

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Have made this a few times as everyone here loves it. Perhaps not traditional, but an additional topping we enjoy is crumbled up, good quality tortilla chips. A great way to use up those small chips in the bottom of the bag.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Use more meats and vegetables (more than what’s recommended)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was great! I used 1.5 lbs of impossible meat and halved the other meatball ingredients, still made a bunch of meatballs! Great flavors, fun and easy to cook.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I don’t dig on swine so I substituted ground chicken.  Lovely dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious soup on a rainy cold day. I had 52 meatballs, golfball size, not 28. I had used cooked frozen rice instead of uncooked, so I sauteed the extra meatballs in oil and froze them. I followed the recipe otherwise.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Very good, mild, but with sliced jalapeños was delicious. Served on top of rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Wait, where's the green squash? And a tiny bit of red chile!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: The meatballs were delish. But the veggies turned to mush. Next time I’ll reserve veggies for the last 15 minutes of simmer.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Make with half as much meat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Excellent with perfect a amount of spice. Preferred a bit more mint so the flavor comes through,  and served it over a small portion of rice.  If you blitz the veggies and sauté you will end up with an amazing tortilla soup base. Still add the potato cubes for some texture.  I used russet which holds up but cooks very tender.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I was staying in Puerto Vallarta last winter and made this, likely one of the best things I've tasted all year.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: When I was a kid, a local restaurant served Albondigas as a starter and my dad would (begrudgingly) share. This recipe has a richer broth, buoyed by the tomato paste & sauce. I followed what was written, but added 8 oz of Modelo to the vegetables after sautéing and before the broth/tomato sauce. I also threw in a seeded Serrano and 1/2 tsp of liquid smoke. Even without these mods, this one is a winner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: super yummy!  Toasting and grinding the cumin gives it an added depth rather than using just ground cumin.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Made per the recipe, with three exceptions. One: Didn't have white rice so used the brown rice I had on hand. Two: As recommended by others, I soaked the rice. Three: I questioned the amount of kosher salt in the meatballs, so opted to only put in half, but the meatballs definitely needed the amount as per the recipe. Dish got rave reviews from a group of people with a variety of palates.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely love this soup. Made it many times. Does anyone know if you can freeze it (before adding the toppings)? I am unsure because of the meatballs. Thanks !

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was an amazing soup. Healing and flavorful and filling.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was quite delicious. had to upgrade from my usual large soup pot to the biggest pot in my kitchen. The tomato base is delicious and will be used in other recipes too- this was well worth the effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: We really enjoyed this recipe! Next time I make it, I will add more potato, and squirt in the juice of a whole lime at the end. The lime flavor of the wedges as a garnish were so good, we wanted more! We loved all the garnishes… super simple! Not many dishes!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
I made this vegetarian using 2 packages of Impossible, lots more mint, a handful of cilantro and parsley and a tsp each of onion and garlic powder, a few dashes of Worcestershire, 1/4 cup bread crumbs and an egg to better bind everything. 

I also used hot Mexican tomato sauce. It's a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as written. My husband absolutely loved this. I did not taste as I went because I don't eat meat. The smell alone told me this was delicious and it was. I will try a version with meatless meatballs and veg stock (thanks Alisa) and try it for myself.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I’m not sure what I did wrong—The soup was unpleasantly greasy and the meatballs didn’t taste very well seasoned. I could barely pick up on the mint or spices. I followed the recipe to the letter except that I was out of potatoes and subbed a little cooked rice in the broth.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Excellent!  We are vegetarian so we used a ground meatless alternative and vegetable broth. We had no issues with greasiness. Fresh Serrano peppers were a great condiment to spice it up. A keeper in our recipe box.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This tastes a bit bland until you do the garnishes.  No worries it really comes together.  I also added diced white onion to my garnishes

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: My mother would make a couple of hard boiled eggs, cut them into large chunks and place one inside each meatball. Also, she would build her sauce using fresh tomatoes, then add water. The meat adds plenty of flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe to the letter (just halved everything, we're only two at home), and it was absolutely amazing! I'm of Turkish origin, and believe it or not, the soup reminded me of my childhood. My mother used to make a dish like this when we were kids and we all asked for seconds. Her meatballs were smaller but that was the only difference really. All this to say, I recommend this recipe wholeheartedly. It's a complete meal and it's delicious. Have it with a glass of red wine (or two).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: DELICIOUS. Run don't walk to your stove to make this! This soup is really satisfying and magically not as heavy as it might read. Follow the recipe, it is well-written and it works! We had the privilege of enjoying a bowl of this at Wes Avila's Angry Egret Diner before it closed, and we're so glad we have the recipe to carry that happy meal forward. It halves really well too!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: The favorite soup in my household. It feels luscious and heartwarming and healthy at the same time. Long to cook so this is a week end meal for us but the leftovers are also amazing so definitely worth it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: No pork, just with beef and was satisfied. Doubled the broth because we have cold and wanted more hot broth.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! I did make a couple of changes -- I didn't put rice in the meatballs (honestly, I just forgot, but seems like they're controversial in the comments anyway). I also made the meatballs from fake "meat," but I don't really count that as a deviation. And I added the potato and carrots after the rest of the ingredients were already simmering for a while so that they didn't overcook. I'll definitely make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Followed the recipe exactly but made the meatballs a bit smaller so there were more of them. Around 36. Had to skim a lot of fat off the top as 80/20 ground beef was all I could find. I would recommend pre-browning the meatballs to get rid of some the excess fat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
The foundation of this recipe is good but it has a few things you want to tweak. 1. You need 8 cups of stock which is two quarts not one. One will not be enough. 

2. Use cooked rice. If you use raw it takes longer to cook and dries out the meatballs. 

3. The meatball to veggie ratio is a bit off. Use at least two potatoes. I also added in 1 zucchini. There is almost enough meatballs to double the recipe. Which, sometimes there is the opposite problem so not a bad thing as long as you also add more veg 😁 

The flavor of it was great though!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody: So I made MANY edits. 4X the amount of potatoes. Only 1lb of ground lamb. 1.5X the rice. And finally, did not use tomato paste or tomato sauce…. And it was still DELISH.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
DELICIOUS! I swapped out for bison and used a higher-fat pork to compensate, but kept the measurements consistent.  Really hearty and wholesome with some plain yogurt and crunched tortilla chips.

Only complaint is it’s a lot of meat! I think you could probably use 1/3 less meat and still have a delicious soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Sopa de Albóndigas (Mexican Meatball Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
Great recipe with authentic flavors. I prefer a bit more rice in the albondigas and make sure you simmer it really low to ensure that the albondigas are tender and moist. I would also recommend to add carrots later in the cooking process - I felt adding at the beginning made the carrots a little overdone. 

 Also, based on other albondigas I've had at restaurants, I added vegetables like corn, chayote, and Mexican zucchini and it was great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
Really look forward to trying this recipe! 

Just one note: I always leave the noodles on the side and plop them in bowl first and pour soup over. I love leftovers and typically enjoy soup the next day but i hate when the noodles are left in there and breakdown. This method keeps it all still kinda 'fresh'.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: @K Absolutely agree!  Also, starches tend to soak up all of the broth so you are left with a stew rather than soup. Preparing them separately and combining before eating is the key!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: @K This tip truly changed the way I think about all soups!! Thank you for it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Adding  thighs and legs on the bone, sautéing them first, will add much more depth to the broth.  Just take out the chicken after its cooked for 1/2 hour, pull off the meat and add it back in to finish the soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: @Jan I found the ground chicken kinda gross

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: For even more intense flavor,  try poaching boneless chicken breasts in white wine & herbs, and then pull them apart and add chunky shreds to the sautéed veggies & broth. Noticeably richer in both flavor & texture!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Our family loved this. We couldn't find cavatelli so used orchiette instead. Sauteeing the chicken added a really good depth of flavor, as did the coriander and celery seed. We added dill and lemon zest--just the right amount of savory lip-smacking goodness.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: We love turning the ground chicken into small meatballs. Just season as you like and use wet hands to shape, drop in the pot with the heated olive oil for few minutes, the remove and add at the end to heat through in the soup. A typical Dutch recipe and yummy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: @DrPat I love any kind of meatball cooked in simmering broth for the best soft and tender texture. Then strain the broth for your soup base. Thank you for the idea to make small chicken meatballs for chicken noodle soup. I’m making this tonight!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I liked this a lot and even preferred the ground chicken texture. Next time though, I would use at least twice the amount of veggies and half the noodles

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I made this for work day lunches to freeze.  I usually buy a whole chicken and make everything from scratch but was short on time.  LOVED IT.  Full of flavour. I used a fennel bulb and fennel seeds instead of celery just a personal preference. I also always add my parmigiano-reggiano rinds (I keep a bag in the freezer) when making any soup for that extra umami flavour.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Anyone else just keep the chicken in while cooking the noodles?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I left them in (on accident). Probably not the best idea, but the chicken still tasted excellent.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This was unbelievably good. Even my picky teenagers loved it. Don’t skip the lemon juice and dill at the end.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Used chicken sausage and spicy Italian pork sausage instead of chicken and the soup was delicious. Home made broth makes a difference.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Lemon zest a nice touch. I would go a little less on the olive oil though. 4 tablespoons rather than 5 would be fine.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this soup many times and we always devour it. Grind your own chicken = pack of boneless skinless thighs in the food processor. So delicious. But not loving the texture of ground chicken, Just diced it into small pieces. Much better. A dollop of sour cream, squeeze of lemon   lemon zest, bunch of herbs. It’s to die for. Last time added udon noodles. OMG.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Made this per recipe sans the crème fraîche thinking I’d save a few calories (he says on January 3rd). The extra texture and creaminess would have been a nice add, but this was great. It also is a perfect canvas for additions and experimentation. Perhaps some thinly sliced jalapeño rounds added back with the meat if you’re looking for heat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe. I wanted to whip up something relatively quick and easy for the ailing hubby, so this was just the thing. To my annoyance, the store was only selling big bags of celery and I knew I wouldn’t use the rest of it, so I ended up subbing in leeks and it worked quite well, even on the canvas of chicken stock from the carton.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Lemon zest to finish really takes it somewhere extra

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This soup turned out delicious! I used bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs. I seared them skin-side down with a salt and pepper crust, then added them to the soup once it started simmering. After 15 minutes, I removed the thighs to shred the meat, then added it back into the broth. I also used what was available in my nearly-wiped out grocery store, which meant: using chicken bone broth instead of stock, and sour cream instead of creme fraische. Still - the end product was a success!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is perfect. I make it at least once a week and double it to share. Perfect blend of herbs and veggies. I crush my own coriander rather that use ground and if I don’t have homemade chicken stock, I use Swanson’s low sodium organic chicken broth. A tip from my brother who was a chef at Boulevard in SF, it is in my opinion the best flavored stock on the market. Thank you for this gem!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I'll have to try that broth!! (:

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: good to know. i love boulevard in sf and respect their judgement so even though i never use 'canned' stock, i will give that a try as a back up plan for when out of homemade. thnx.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I didn't have ground chicken and used shredded dark meat instead. Sautéed the garlic and spices by themselves until fragrant then added the vegetables and thyme. Used kluski noodles and out of necessity, subbed fennel seeds for the celery seeds and half an onion for the shallot. I prefer to cook the noodles in the broth as it gives it more body. To store, I strain the broth which not only prevents the noodles from soaking up the broth but also keeps the vegetables from getting soggy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Made this today for lunch - cold day, snow flurries, perfect!  Came together in about the 30 min as stated. Notes: If using higher 'fat'/lower lean ground chicken/turkey, 5 T of olive oil made it quite oily, so would recommend cutting the 5T down to 4T or use 93% lean meat. Herb pantry was pretty empty, so substituted 1/2 t of Bell's Seasoning for coriander+celery. Think it was perfect - made the soup taste like it was made w/the Thanksgiving turkey carcass.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I have become obsessed with this soup and have made it 3 or 4 times since I first discovered it about a month ago.  Extremely easy, perfect as is, but also adapts quite well to tweaking. Have added a can of diced tomatoes and some  Italian herbs and thrown in a touch of parmesan.  Works well with a dollop of sour cream as garnish also. Love it love it love it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Simple, yet effective! I made it as is, except without the crème fraiche at the very end. My only notes, echoing others, is to 1.) increase the veggies (I tripled) and 2.) decrease the noodles (I used egg noodles and 8oz overwhelmed the dish, unless you prefer a noodle-heavy soup). Also, the lemon at the end is non-negotiable and really elevates the soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this 20 times, every time with different brand of broth/stock, veggies from different growers, different crème fraiches, etc, etc and every time it has been spectacular. Make it. Memorize it. You won’t regret it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is fantastic. Didn't have celery seeds, but it was still amazing. I'll definitely be making this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I've made this at least a dozen times.  It never fails.  It's the corriander!  I don't use this spice for anything else, but it's critical to this recipe.  I use lots of carrots, celery, onions (in place of leeks), and garlic.  The ground chicken is just a conduit for the spices.  Tonight, I added...wait for it...Ramen noodles!! Bought 3 packs of the cheap noodles and discarded the flavor packets.  The Ramen noodles really changed the flavor..for the better.   Keep tweaking!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I always use fennel seeds…smells good and tastes delish. I also add in broccoli and any other extra vegetable I have available to give it more depth.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Celery is a deeply annoying ingredient. You can only buy it by the bunch and you almost always only need a few stalks. That said, in recipes where the celery will be cooked down you can use frozen celery without a difference in texture or flavor. Buy the whole bunch, use what you need, and cut and freeze the remainder in airtight freezer bags. However, if you are going to use the rest of the celery soon put it in a vase of water on the counter. It can last for up to week this way.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: My boyfriend and I love this one, and I've made it several times -- usually when one of us is sick. We love subbing the ground chicken for spicy chicken sausage. Amps up the flavor. We also use small pasta shells or whatever small pasta is available at our local Publix. Works great and taste great every time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
Pretty outstanding considering it's made with ground chicken, which I'm normally not a fan of. I've left the ground chicken in it and also taken it out; it's worth taking out for those curious as the meat gets 'squeaky'. 

I also had a good laugh at all the comments suggesting folks add chicken thighs (and shred them after!) or poach chicken breasts (and shred them after!) or use chicken legs (and shred them after!). There's a reason this particular recipe is called EASIEST chicken noodle soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Agree completely re: those who missed that this was "easiest" chicken soup, not "best" chicken soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This results in a broth that is quite tasty, but the ground chicken has a horrible texture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
Agree completely 
Wouldn't make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: The pasta really soaked up the liquid, with the result it came out more like a stew than soup - which was actually just fine with us! Crazy simple to prepare, tastes great, and is "basic" enough to allow infinite experimentation. Look forward to making it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe! Have made countless times now. I use herbs de Provence and 1/3 cup orzo for the pasta. Also like to 3x the amount of carrots. The squeeze of lemon before serving is crucial!! This also freezes beautifully.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: The lemon truly makes this soup-- don't leave it out!! I ate it first after forgetting the lemon and thought it was just meh. Then, I realized later that I hadn't added the lemon and squeezed some fresh lemon juice into it before eating it, and it was really, really delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I like to experiment with meat recipes and make them vegetarian. Not always successful, but this recipe was excellent to that purpose. The celery seeds and the coriander gave great flavor to cubed, firm tofu (liquid removed) that I substituted for the chicken. Fast, hearty and easy recipe with great flavors.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Cook the noodles before adding to soup to avoid noodles absorbing the broth.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: You want the noodles to absorb the chicken flavor. To avoid noodles absorbing the broth, stop cooking the soup when the noodles are done.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This is my favorite, go to chicken soup. I add farro instead of pasta right before I serve so it doesn't soak up all the broth. I also top with arugula and sometimes a little red pepper flakes. So good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: My market sells shredded rotisserie chicken. I think I’ll try that instead of ground chicken.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: After years of making hit/miss soups in a search for the "One" best all-round, this is it.  The brief cook time threw me and I couldn't imagine the broth would have any depth but I was wrong.  I've made it twice now; the second batch I doubled, using 1lb  ground chicken 1 lb ground turkey.  I doubled the veg portions,  split the celery 50/50 with fresh Bok Choy and added 8oz of pearl onions. All of it went into a crock pot this time, with half the recommended broth and slow cooked for 4hrs.  WOW

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Very good, easy, followed almost exactly. Didn’t have shallots so I used a leftover half of a Vidalia onion and sautéed it a bit first before adding the other veggies. For pasta I used smaller egg noodles, a tad less than called for; what I had left from another recipe. Oh, also didn’t have dill but plenty of parsley and lemon zest. My husband is watching calories and didn’t want the crème fraîche. He loved it and said, Why bother with whole chickens for chicken noodle soup anymore!?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Holding the cooked noodles aside serves two purposes. First, the noodles don’t get mushy and second, the noodles soak up the broth and you’re left with chicken noodle stew.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
I doubled the vegetables. Fried them for longer so they would be more tender, poured in hot stock and simmered for 10ish minutes(don’t like crunchy carrots celery in my soup), added the fried chicken mince halfway through that. Finished off with a REASONABLY amount of short vermicelli pasta. 
Lemon zest was the fantastic cherry on top. 

Surprisingly enjoyed the different sized pieces of chicken mince. Better than meatballs for me.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This was sooo good!! Used 8 cups of water with better than bouillon and only 6oz of pasta. It could probably suffice with 4-5oz of pasta. Added 8oz of fresh spinach and zest of half a lemon. Topped with Greek yogurt, dill and lemon. Definitely going to be in the soup season rotation!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: These directions are great. My partner and I are vegetarians, so I made this with a bag of Quorn frozen “chicken” chunks and Better than Bouillon veggie stock, and it was delightful. Sautéed the chikn first in oil for 15 min. A few things saved from the summer garden made this so cozy on the coldest day of the year (wind chill of -33): dried rosemary and thyme, and one parsnip! The lemon zest and parsley at the end are rather important. Mahlzeit!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Adding 1-2 tablespoons of duck fat really adds flavor and depth that this soup lacks. Schmaltz! Jarred duck fat can be purchased at Whole Foods. Substitute cooked pieces of chicken to replace ground chicken.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I was dubious about this soup; essentially it's stock with some veg floating around in it BUT, once you re-add the chicken, it changes everything. Crème fraiche and citrus lifted everything beautifully.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Very delicious quick soup! I did not use ground chicken. I had a pound of frozen chicken thighs that needed to be used and chopped them up in small pieces. I used fresh thyme twigs for ease of use (the leaves fall off on their own and the the twigs can be removed with tongs). My garnish was fresh cilantro. The soup was just what my recently hospitalized husband needed on this early fall day.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I don't love the idea of ground chicken in soup.  I have a quick recipe which is essentially making veggie soup with chicken stock.  However, the last step is adding cubes of raw chicken breasts to cook for 10 minutes - adds flavour to the broth and the meat retains its flavour.  Especially good if you want soup and realize you only have chicken breasts in the freezer - I often add the chicken cubes still frozen. I have poached whole chicken breasts in the soup and then shredded them also.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: More meat, less meat; too many vegetables, add more vegetables, more carrots, more celery; too much broth, add 2 more cups of broth; too spicy, add more spice. Everyone knows how to make the perfect/best chicken soup!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was really good for a quick bite for my sick husband. I used a regular yellow onion instead of a shallot for a more traditional flavor, and I definitely needed to add more broth, probably 10-11 cups (the noodles soaked up a lot more than I thought).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: You want a better tasting quick chicken soup? 2 chicken breasts, cut cup bite size pieces. 5 peeled carrots, 1 peeled russet potato, 1 peeled sweet onion, 3 stalks of celery, all chopped to 1/4" dice. A handful of chopped fresh herbs, whatever you have (basil, parsley, oregano, thyme), sea salt, lemon pepper blend. Saute all in 4 tablespoons of EVOO until the veggies are fragrant and softened, about 5 minutes. 2 cartons (32 ounces each) of chicken STOCK (not broth), simmer 45 minutes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
@Lorraine Fina 
Thank you, this sounds tasty and easy. I’m snapshotting it and will try it soon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This is an ideal light soup because the ground chicken (I use white meat) has flavor but is somehow not dry like chicken breasts. The spices make it flavorful and the addition of fresh herbs is refreshing. I didn't have small pasta so I broke up thin spaghettini into thirds, a nice visual contrast. Don't wait, this is easier and better than most chicken soups.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic soup and so easy. I processed boneless thighs in the cuisinart instead of buying ground chicken, which is somewhat like mystery meat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I suspect that ground turkey would also work--easier to find than ground chicken.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I was craving soup for lunch and this seemed like a quick fix. I used ground turkey because that was all I had. I otherwise followed the directions. Not a repeat in my opinion. The coriander was so overwhelming. I appreciate the formula and feel that this recipe is a great one to riff on. Definitely keeping the lemon zest tip. Otherwise, this is one time try. Womp womp.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This soup has a rich, satisfying flavor but is light enough to serve to a person who has just had surgery.  This is a great recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: If you make the soup as directed you will get food poisoning from the undercooked chicken (I did).  Removing the chicken from the pot while cooking the vegetables and the noodles makes no sense - leaving the chicken to cook with the vegetables at least ensures that the chicken gets properly cooked.  Also, another commenter noted that there are too few vegetables and way too much noodles in the recipe as written.  I agree.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe, but I also got food poisoning the second time I made it. Removing the chicken from the pot while cooking the vegetables is risky.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I use a Costco rotisserie chicken instead of ground chicken. Just pull meat off bird, shred with a fork and add to pot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This soup is now one of my all time favorites. Because of this recipe, I always slice my celery in half lengthwise before chopping   incorporate ground chicken into most soups where I’d normally use roti or chicken breast. This is definitely the most quintessential and classic tasting chicken soup. I think this recipe is pretty dang perfect recipe and follow it exactly as much as I can.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
followed recipe, using fresh thyme and setting the chicken aside, with a few additions:
  canned mushrooms we had on hand to the mirepoix
  cavender’s seasoning on the chicken
  used 24 oz of chicken broth and 8oz of mushroom broth
  topped with lemon zest, fresh cracked pepper & grated parmigiano-reggiano
  served with sourdough bread and a homemade garlic butter

it was excellent, going to make it again this week!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Simple and quite delicious. The garnish of yogurt (in my case), dill and parsley really adds a little 'oomph'.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe and have made it exactly as written for many years. Oops, my grocery store didn’t have ground chicken today! No worries, I got ground turkey and turkey stock and it also turned out well (but chicken is better)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: The ground chicken “hack” is no contender to using a rotisserie chicken, in my opinion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
This looks like a fine recipe, but since you knock using rotisserie chick, let me share my own hack:

Strip most of the meat of a rotisserie chicken and save it for another dish, reserving some to put back in the soup after you have boiled the carcass, surplus fat, etc., in a pressure cooker along with a bay leaf, vegetable trimmings from your other dish, celery, carrot, more onion, parsley stems, etc., and water to cover. Bring to pressure, cool, strain and use the stock.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: If you don't love the taste of thyme (as I don't), I'd recommend halving the amount of thyme.  Otherwise an easy and delicious soup!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Lemon is essential and the smaller the pasta the better!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Awesome chicken soup that comes together fairly quickly. I suggest cooking the noodles separately and adding when ready to eat as my noodles absorbed a lot of the broth. I also used sodium free chicken stock and salted to taste along the way.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
This turned out delicious!!! I normally do not enjoy making soups (I know I know), but was so pleased by the results. I will certainly make this again.
I added some ginger and turmeric to the chicken, used 1:1 mushroom to chicken broth for a boost of umami flavor, and of course, a splash (or 4) of fish sauce. Lovely recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: For gluten free I added 2 cups jasmine rice. Mmmm

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I made this with 2 large chicken breasts cut into cubes instead of the ground chicken. Also used gluten free egg noodles. It was delicious!! The fresh dill and lemon zest really added freshness to the soup. This is my new go to chicken soup recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
Used veg stock. 

A little too much pasta

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Excellent!  Cooked pasta separately, added lemon juice to the broth at the end, as well as the zest.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
This is great. I made it with spätzle noodles. Lovely. 
And I agree, lemon is a must!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I like to save all the skin and bones from rotisserie chickens in zip bags in the freezer.  When I have 2 or 3, I throw them in the slow cooker with water, a couple of chunks of celery and carrot, a small clove-studded peeled onion, a few garlic cloves, a few slices of ginger root and some herbs de Provence.  Slow cook overnight, strain, and either use or freeze.  SO much better than canned or boxed stock, easy, and practically free!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Excellent.  The herbs and the crème fraiche take it up a notch, especially the dill

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This soup always delivers. I’ve substituted minced chicken thighs for ground, either way it is amazing! No need to alter!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Happy accident with this recipe… I had ziplocks of spices I had brought back from the Middle East that weren’t well marked. I accidentally used yellow curry thinking it was coriander. I know… I should have known by the smell. HOWEVER, It was AMAZING! I make it with curry every time, with a dose of coriander as well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Easy to make on a weeknight but incredibly bland.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: @AC I thought it is only me who thought that it was very bland unless you squeeze lemon juice at the end

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Good soup!  Be careful adding salt, though.  I didn’t taste as I went, just added salt per recipe, and ended up with salty soup.  Lesson learned.  Ground chicken is a terrific time saver.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: I really like this recipe! I added a spoonful of Better Than Bouillon because the flavor seemed to need something. I love tarragon in chicken soup and am going to try that next time instead of thyme.  Or maybe more thyme. It was just a wee bit flat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
Using ground chicken is a genius idea. 
If you want to stretch the recipe, a can of rinsed cannellini beans does the trick!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: My favourite chicken soup for the past year. I've made it a countless time and am about to do so tonight again. The lemon zest with the creme gives it a great tangy taste which I adore. I'm not using ground chicken but simply just chicken breast and omit the celery seeds as I cannot find them anywhere in Germany but even without this soup excellent. Strongly recommend!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This was great! Chopped up a chicken breast and used a tsp of whole coriander seeds instead of ground. It gives a big pop of flavor every now and then.  Added extra broth and veggies per other reader suggestions and skipped the dairy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: 1/2 amount of pasta

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: No onions? Interesting, I always (always) begin soups like this by sautéing onions, adding other vegetables and spices before the rest. Must be a conscious choice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This has been my go to chicken soup this winter.  I like the ease of using the ground chicken.  I've also been using mini gnocchi (found in the pasta isle) instead of egg noodles.  They give dumpling vibes which I enjoy more than noodles.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This is the 15th week in a row I have made soup, and this is the best one so far! I made it once with the normal recipe and made it once with veggie broth and white beans for my vegetarian wife. Both were great! Highly recommend! 5/5 stars!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
It's delicious and reminds me of backed chicken

Changes
1. Substitute chopped chicken thighs for ground chicken
2. I cooked four pieces of bacon in the same pot after cooking the chicken.  Chopped the bacon and added back to the pot right before serving
3. Cooked the noodles in a separate pot
4. In future attempts i may cut back on thyme slightly

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Subbed fennel for celery and skipped celery seed.  Otherwise made to recipe, using orzo (half the amount called for).  Added chopped parsley & mint at the end since I didn’t have dill.  The fresh lemon zest and crème fraiche (Tr Joes) made it special.  Very easy and tasty weeknight dinner.  Note re food poisoning:  you need to cook the chicken till cooked through and no longer pink.  It might take longer than the listed times, depending on the pan, your stove, and the burner size.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: We love this soup - so flavorful and easy! I’ve made it every week for the past couple of months, always with double the celery and carrots, and some extra chicken stock. The recipe is terrific as is, but is also great with any of the following additions: a can of diced tomatoes, garbanzo beans, fresh spinach.  I cook the noodles separately, then for those who want noodles, I place them in a bowl and ladle hot soup on top. Delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
I cut up a Rotisserie Chicken into bite size pieces for the chicken to be added at end of cooking. 
1 sautéed 1 yellow onion and 3 ribs veins peeled celery stalks diced.  Add to 2 boxes low salt chicken broth with 1 pkg baby carrots, cook til carrots done.  For T-2 D Friendly - 1 can drained Cannoli Beans instead of noodles with chicken.  Heat for 5 minutes.  Squeeze 1-2 fresh lemon juice always end in soups for brightness not bitterness.  Serve hot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: used this as a starting base but changed almost everything lol; did step one as normal (used fennel seed & rosemary in place of celery seed), in step 2 instead of thyme i added: dill, cilantro, parsely, scallions; used better than bullion & added potatoes; cooked pasta on side and added in; added additional herbs. topped with labne, lemon zest, herbs, red pepper flakes, and a squeeze of lemon

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody:
I agree with "K" who stated that they leave the noodles on the side - esp if this is going to be several days of leftovers!
