I made it last night using some left over brown rice instead of noodles which I added just before serving.  We love a little spice so added a tablespoon of roasted green chili to each bowl and finished it with a dollop of sour cream.  So good and even better the next day.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Agree with just about every comment here.  Great as written, great to riff on.  Made a double batch to have a pot to share with a friend recovering from colon problems.  To our half doubled the veggies and added chili flakes.  So fast and easy.  Lots of goodness for little work.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: This is the best chicken noodle soup recipe I’ve ever made I definitely recommend for sick people or children love it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: Literally the best chicken soup!! I use fregola instead of noodles, make it in batches and when I reheat a portion in a pan I add a leafy green, such as spinach or kale. The magic is the lemon and coriander at the end. I leave out the creme fraiche to make it healthier although I am sure it would be a fabulous addition to this absolute menu mainstay.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: my husband is always skeptical about ground meat - so he approached this with low expectations.   Voila' - he loves it.    Easy to make, and different from my other chicken soups.  A change of pace is always good - and this one is tasty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: A hearty soup for a sick husband but I won't make again. Very little flavor. Did not care for the ground chicken.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Easiest Chicken Noodle Soup Recipe
commentBody: my most beloved recipe! perfectly fine with less noodle but otherwise don’t change a thing

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! We didn’t have enoki mushrooms, so we used thinly sliced creminis, and added a squeeze of lemon (we also had neither yuzu nor Meyer lemon at hand ) at the end — which truly added brightness while somehow also deepening the umami from the dashi and the miso. My husband broiled the salmon fillet just briefly to give it a hint of char before adding it to the pot. The broth was silky and incredibly satisfying; and for such a light dish, we all felt quite full. Guests and family loved it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Took much longer to cook the fish, but still pretty fast.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. I used parsnip because I couldn’t find daikon, and used regular lemon juice at the end. Otherwise followed the recipe to the letter. This is at once rich and light, we cleaned our bowls. Tasted like a restaurant would make it, not a home chef. Two thumbs up!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious! The earthy saltiness of miso combined with the tempered sweetness of the mirin and sake, and the irresistible flavor of garlic butter create a powerful flavor bath for the salmon and vegetables.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I couldn't find enoki mushrooms or dashi, but I did substitute with both shiitake and cremini mushrooms and used chicken broth.  I also added some nori while cooking and a dab of wasabi at the end.  This was a delicious recipe!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Just making it now. Even with salmon filets that are medium thickness we're already looking at 11 minutes cooking time. I just hope the veggies are not soggy by the time the fish is done.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: 15 minutes to cook. 2 weeks to get ingredients.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Can I give this 10 stars? This was as good as it gets for home cooking (and for most restaurants I know). I substituted (because I couldn't get) a ramen broth (Ocean's Halo brand) for the dashi and shiitake mushrooms for the enoki and didn't have dill. It cooked quickly and the end result was better than I imagined it would be. Served over jasmine rice in a soup bowl. This dish was a wow, wow.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Need more than 1 1/4 C of dashi for 4 servings to look like the broth in photo. I tested the recipe for one, used almost 3/4 cup. Five minutes veggies/salmon cooked perfectly. Used shiitakes, served w/baby bok choy & 100% buckwheat soba. Yuzu juice at supermarket is $10.29 for small bottle, so used lemon. but will buy when I make again for guests. Don't skip shichimi togarashi & dill. This recipe is a winner!!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was super yummy, event with swaps--soy sauce instead of dashi, baby bella mushrooms instead of enoki--and no dill or shichimi togarashi

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is yummy! I wanted to use what I had in the house as well as ramp up the vegetable quotient. So added some shredded green cabbage below the layer of daikon (used purple daikon), used dried shitake instead of the enoki (and added some of the mushroom soaking liquid as well.)  Scallions on top instead of dill and used some Shichimi togarashi with yuzu in it that I had gotten at Toiro! Really delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Truly outstanding recipe.  I made it as written except I used shiitake mushrooms.  It will go into the regular rotation, I get lots of compliments.  I realize you can modify the vegetables as long as you have the magic umami powerhouse of the dashi, mirin, sake, and miso.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: My family LOVED this.  I accidently used way too much dashi, although I am not sure it made too much difference -- my daughter said, "No, you didn't!  I love the broth!"  The only knowing change I made was to use shimeji mushrooms instead of enoki, because I couldn't find enoki, and that was the closest I could find (I think I like them better anyway, but I can't imagine it made a big difference).  I did invest in a small bottle of yuzu juice and I did use both dill and togarishi.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Don't care for sake, certainly not going to buy a bottle to use 2 Tbsp, mixing is a lot the same, is in our cupboard for years, so that's a pass. I'll experiment and use the miso of course, but dry white, a very small dash of soy sauce, and garnish with what I have.  A delicious sounding recipe, but way too many things the generational Americans won't have way in their larder. But I would up the dish using sockeye salmon, not what appears to be spring or even farm raised. That will elevate mmmmm

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: What color miso is recommended?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I don't know anywhere that sells 1/2 lb daikon. The smallest I could find was 3 lbs. Now what?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I always ask a clerk in Produce Dept. to cut off a piece, as many are practically the size of a baseball bat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Cooked sans carrot and served it over jap chae 10/10

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! Go buy mirin and sake if you don’t have it, you will not regret it. I’m sure whoever is trying this recipe will repeat. I replaced daikon with parsnip, dashi with chicken stock and enoki mushroom with frozen button mushrooms, all because I couldn’t find the original ingredients. Even with all the replacements, this recipe is a winner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: i am cooking it next week for a dinner party. no need to test it as Naoko's recipes are ALWAYS perfect and my husband, our guests and i ALWAYS love them...without exception. i cant wait to taste this one as well. jg

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Made without daikon subbed more carrots and used rice win vinegar and sugar sub for mirin and used shaoxing wine as sub for sake and used furikake/fried garlic/Thai dried pepper flakes subbed for togarishi. So good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Omg this was amazing. Used a rice koji miso as that was in the fridge and wowowow. Served with somen but soba or rice would have been great. 10/10 will trade again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: My toddler’s absolute favourite dinner. It’s easy, fast, light, and healthy. Highly recommend. I like to add fresh ginger to broth. Serve it with a little white rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe. Like others, I have had to sub out ingredients. Have packets of dashi from H-Mart. Shitake instead of enoki mushrooms and halibut in place of salmon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
This was outstanding! Definitely restaurant worthy. I used vermouth instead of the sake, and cooked the salmon a couple minutes longer, as other commenters had suggested, but otherwise followed the recipe. So glad to find a good use for all that daikon we receive from our farm share.
Trader Joe's carries a Yuzu hot sauce that was perfect here.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Substitutios are necessary sometimes but here, with such a flexible recipe, they make welcome, diverse meals! 

Excellent recipe! Made Dashi, per link in recipe for Kombu and Bonito Dashi. Easy! Used half here and froze remainder.  Then, as per Peter in the Dashi reviews, made a Secondary Dashi, re-using the now one 1x used Kombu and Bonito Flakes for 2dary Dashi. Froze that too. 

Per another review added cabbage

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Oops! Meant to add that you should NOT use red cabbage - turns the broth pink! Sauteed, briefly, shrimp (no salmon) for char with garlic slices for taste.

Added cabbage (red-dont! use green for add'l texture) and sliced parsnips vs daikon. Great results! 

Definitely will make again. And again. Recommend!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: For the amount of effort, we felt like this was not that great. Also, we felt it would be better with a white fish like cod over the salmon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Salmon and Daikon in Garlic Butter Miso Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I have made this several times most recently with steelhead trout shimeji and matsukake mushrooms and hikari turnips and daikon. Love this!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Also, in response to the suggestion that only people with “true anaphylactic reactions” to gluten should need to be accommodated—that’s simply not true. I have celiac, and my reaction to gluten isn’t anaphylaxis but brain fog, joint pain, and damage to my intestine that causes malabsorption of vitamins and minerals. Others with celiac or gluten intolerance may suffer severe gi symptoms. “A little” gluten is too much gluten for people like me or those with gluten intolerance. Please believe us.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I haven't cooked this yet, but when I do I will do my usual substitution of broccolini for some of the rabe so that the bitterness is tempered and my child will eat it. Will come back once I do the cooking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: How was it, Deena?  I, too, usually substitute broccolini for broccoli rabe and am curious about how it worked with this recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
For a gluten free version, which I would make myself as I have celiac, I would use Schar’s baguette. It would work perfectly and no one will know it’s not a wheat bread.

Another baguette I love and my non celiac guests love, too, is made by Against the Grain. I heat up the whole baguette for around 8 minutes at 400°F then slice or toast slices before serving. I’m telling you, your wheat-eating family & guests will gobble it up. But I think Schar’s is better for this particular recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Family loves broccoli rab so I make it often.  Not necessary to blanch it.  Why waste a pot which you have to wash? Cut lower tough stem then chop slice whichever way you like ( we live it chopped up into bite size pieces ) and throw into hot sizzling oil have a splatter screen nearby …toss around w salt red pepper flakes, silk chili etc .  sometimes I ad. butter because we live butter.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Roasted in oven because i can't be bothered to char 3 lbs of broccolini in a pan. It was still delicious! Could use some more vinegar in the ajo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious. Recommend cutting the rapini in thirds before blanching. It will make it much easier to eat. It’s very rich.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Used almonds instead of cashews. The lemon was a hit. Mellow garlic flavor.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I think I would just roast the Rabe. Seems like more effort to pan saute.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this tonight as written. It was delicious!  I highly recommend giving it a try, paying attention to acid and salt levels when tasting.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
I am so excited for this—I eat broccoli rabe at least once a week! 

**This would go great alongside a Spanish tortilla.**

I am not GF but second the use of Ezekiel bread (the GF version) as an option—just discovered the brand sold @ TJ’s makes sprouted pasta & must hunt some down!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Cooked as written. The ajo blanco sauce is  overwhelmed by cashews--there's no ajo flavor to speak of. All of the nuts also make the sauce anything but blanco....rather more of an unappetizing barfy-tan. Last, despite using both a high-powered blender and an immersion blender, the sauce's consistency was not smooth, which added to its poor appearance and made for an unappealing mouth feel.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I would love to make this for a dinner party where one of my guests is gluten free -- can anyone suggest the sub there for the baguette.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I've tried many GF recipes/flours, can never truly get to GF bread that is like wheat bread. Some GF bread is delicious, but not a one-to-one for non-GF bread. Ezekiel bread (FoodForLife) at Trader Joes is good, there is a KETO bread at Aldi's (may want try to toast them first). GF Panko Bread Crumbs may work too. You may have to experiment. ...so happy when folks work to balance their menu to include everyone. Most people who are GF don't constantly want to be called out as "the problem child".

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I had neither rabe nor broccolini available; used a blend of regular broccoli florets & cauliflower. The density, especially when emulsified, is more of a hummus, which we served at the side of a meat dish; and it held its own. Without the emulsifier, the oil separates and looses some joy. The cashew is a lovely nutty flavor I would miss if it were almond as described. As she mentioned, a toasted bread is also great with this, and you will have plenty of extra baguette.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: All cashews, as in a cashew cream, could be subbed for the small amount of bread it’s no problem!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Celiac disease isn’t an allergy to wheat or gluten. It is an autoimmune inflammatory disorder in which the body is unable to break down the polypeptides found in gluten-containing grains and their products.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Yes , but sometimes times is is easier to say allergy rather than give a full explanation.

Celiac, 15 years diagnosed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: thank you for the gluten free comments!  i have had a similar dish made w potato. and generally like Udi’s GF bread and find trader joe’s while economical, too sweet.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: For the gluten free version, the 1.5 oz of bread isn’t much. Check with your guest and discern the extent of gluten allergic they are. They could do without the sauce or have a tiny smear for the effect. This would work for anybody except those with a true anaphylactic reaction to gluten.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Instead of interrogating your guest about a personal problem, simply honor requests to avoid gluten. It's not difficult.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: To the person looking for a gf version, how about cooked potatoes? I know some people add these to toum, even though it’s not traditional.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Three pounds of rabe is way too much for 4 servings!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I found the Ajo sauce a bit underwhelming. I might try roasting the garlic instead of boiling

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Am I the only person who ended up with a gluey mess instead of ajo blanco? What did I do wrong 😭

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Charred Broccoli Rabe With Ajo Blanco Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is solid. I didn’t have cashews in the house so I used almonds. I also added some tahini to the purée as it was kind of lacking… tahini made a big difference. I couldn’t really get the texture of the pure right either, but the flavor was on point. I served this as a side with a ribeye steak and cooked the broccoli rabe in the pan with the beef fat while the steak was resting. It was absolutely delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Dashi Recipe
commentBody: Traditionally dashi is made in primary and secondary forms. The primary (subtle and "refined") is made is described here except DO NOT simmer the kelp but remove it as soon as the water comes to a simmer. Then bring water to a boil, remove from heat, and add the bonito. Let sit for 1 minute then strain. Secondary is more robust and is used for thick soups, noodle broths, and a cooking stock. Return bonito and kelp to pan with fresh water and simmer for 15-20 min, then strain. Dashi freezes well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Dashi Recipe
commentBody: Is it OK to freeze Dashi for later us without affecting its flavor?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Dashi Recipe
commentBody: Easy to scale up or down with the gram measurements.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Dashi Recipe
commentBody: yes you can freeze it

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Dashi Recipe
commentBody: I found dashi is best used immediately. It doesn’t keep in the fridge but loses its edge like a Coke that goes flat. That said, finished soup, like with veggies, keeps and reheats well for a few days. Not so for miso soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Dashi Recipe
commentBody: Love this!! So simple and flavorful. Feel like I’m back in Japan

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Dashi Recipe
commentBody:
Made as directed but... Used Peter's rev
iew tips to make both Primary and Secondary Dashi, which I froze for future. 

Easy schmeezy, no need to buy at exorbitant prices! Plus you can alter per your taste. 

I'm freezing what I don't need today for future use. 
Excellent!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Dashi Recipe
commentBody:
No need for scallops. 
Omg. This is perfect. 
I feel like I’m on vacation

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: A food mill is useful to get rid of any lingering seeds and chile skins, something I learned from Susana Trilling / Seasons of My Heart in Oaxaca. This sauce is very similar to hers except she toasts the chiles, onion, and garlic first. But if I were in a restaurant and had to make gallons maybe I wouldn't toast them either. Tasty sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Oops this comment was meant for the red sauce, although it generally holds true here (except for the garlic which isn't in this recipe).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I usually roast and peel my peppers before making enchilada sauce.  After trying this sauce I won't bother with that anymore.  It was quick and delicious.  I followed the recipe exactly other than adding a couple of cloves of peeled garlic.  This will be my go-to green sauce for enchiladas and chilaquiles from now on!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious, very full- and deep flavored. My husband + I worked as a team with me doing mise and him taking over from there; we used veggie stock. Seriously -- YUM.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Um - where are the Hatch green chiles?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Absolute fire. I cooked this down for an extra 45 minutes and the flavors got really deep. Good stuff folks.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is an easy-to-follow recipe. I made it for El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas mentioned above but this is an especially flavorful green sauce that can be used on other dishes. I  followed the recipe as written. I'll make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
substitute the  spinach with  2 bunches of cilantro ( stalks included)
substitute the chicken broth with a heaping tablespoon chicken concentrate broth ( comes in a jar, very rich and delish)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: 3-4c chicken stock instead makes a TON

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Can you freeze this?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Does it freeze well?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Yes, I make both red and green- and freeze in glass Mason (or Ball) brand jars with tight lids. Let sauce cool to lukewarm/room temp and leave a bit of space for expansion of swuce as it freezes, No formal ‘canning’ process needed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I am glad those who think there is only ONE proper way to make enchilada sauce (with hatch chiles e.g.) or marinara or pizza or applesauce or anything do not hold sway. We'd have a boring food world. The world is so full of a number of things (RLS); we should appreciate all the wonderfully different ways to make food.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: It works for a quick enchilada fix, though I think more depth of flavor would be achieved by roasting the tomatillos first.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Agree, Skippie! Lol I am going to use my roasted Hatch Green Chiles in place of the other peppers. It’s going to be delish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Is it possible to use canned tomatillos?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Im from Texas and I found this to be a very inadequate tomatillo sauce. Maybe it’s not Tex-Mex enough for my taste? Not sure , but I was surprised given it’s good rating.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Even picky kids loved it, ate it in spoonfuls. Added a couple garlic cloves.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding! Versatile, too. Love this as a topping for rice or added to salad for an extra kick. Only modifications I made were to (1) roast/broil veg before simmering w spinach, and (2) subbed assorted peppers for poblano because I didn't have one on hand. This is well worth the effort and will go into regular rotation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I used 5-6 roasted hatch chiles and 1 Serrano, plus a handful of cilantro, cooked up in my Instant Pot Blender. Absolutely the best enchilada sauce I’ve ever had. I used it to make the NYT Green Chili Enchiladas and it was just perfect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Green Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: According to the Chile Institute at New Mexico State University in Las Cruces, New Mexico there is actually no chile pepper variety named Hatch. The name refers to the area where the chile peppers are grown. The peppers commonly sold as "Hatch" chile peppers are actually Anaheim chile peppers.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: These are ALL dried chilis.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Thanks, Ajax.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Hi everyone,
Where I live in Europe, I can only get dried chilis. Any thoughts on how to adjust the recipe to accommodate them? Would I need to reduce the number of chilis, for example? And, I can imagine it’d be best to strain the mixture to get out fibrous bits of skin.  Any help would be much appreciated.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Cut off the stem ends. Split the dried pods carefully, keeping the pieces as large as possible. Remove seeds. Steep the pieces in hot water, just enough to cover the pieces. After they reconstitute, carefully scrape the flesh from the skin, using a straight knife or spatula. Discard the skins. Blend the flesh with some or all of the steeping water. Simmer with garlic and some orange zest. Make a roux, not too dark. Now make the sauce. Add a little salt, maybe some veg broth. Freezes nicely.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: To be honest, I worry about the skin. Just cut off the stem end and remove the seeds. Once reconstituted in water, a quick whizz with the food processor should end up with a smooth result (as described above). Not at all fibrous.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Super helpful and apologies that it wasn’t clear to me that the chilis are dried and not fresh. I’m making the recipe today. Can’t wait!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: In Mexico, and in traditional Mexican recipes, Chilis are always dried. Peppers are their fresh cousins.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Yes, these are all dried chiles. A fresh guajillo is a mirasol. Californias are dried Anaheim peppers, and de Arbol is de Arbol, but that one does specify dried. I wonder why they're not toasted; I think almost every recipe I have for chile purees involves toasting first. Maybe not all, though!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: It's a good recipe but according to my tía in Mexico, you need to use chicken stock/broth instead of water and use only two cups to steep the dried chilies instead of four cups (you can add more later, if needed). And the last step, where you "shock" the puree, be sure to have the oil good and hot so that when the puree hits the oil, it produces the needed "shock". Also, I would suggest using three to five arbol chilies.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I made this as written. Like others, I found it to be thin and lacking flavor. So I added 2T tomato paste, chili powder and some oregano and cumin. Then I continued cooking until it reduced to 1.25 cups. The flavor and texture were then spot on.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I had to substitute peppers in that were available in Germany but it turned out beautifully. Thanks you for sharing!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: They are referencing dried chiles. Read the notes above.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This sauce dried chile sauce needs some help. First, make sure you VENT the blender, or you will have the sauce on the ceiling. Boiling onions and garlic is sort of odd, the results are bland. Experiment at Step 3, use bacon grease for the oil, add tomato paste, cumin, paprika, other pepper powder,  a tiny bit of Mexican or other chocolate to make mole, corn starch to thicken. This wants to be good and rewards ad hoc additions. There are similar recipes on NYT for inspiration.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Pretty easy recipe!  I usually puree, strain and then add to a roux, but this was easier and good.  I used all dry chiles as it is hard to find fresh Guajillos. The dried chiles reconstitute during the boiling process.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Straining the puree results in a smooth sauce and is less tedious than trying to de-skin the reconstituted dried chiles. This recipe reflects how red chile sauce is made in Mexico. Unfortunately, I have seen many red sauce recipes on youtube so far from the Mexican traditional sauce that I gagged just watching.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Remember to let the sauce cool before you puree it in your blender

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I have made this sauce a handful of times and always seem to need to make an adjustment. Feedback I would give is make a roux instead of just adding oil. Use whatever stock you are using (e.g. chicken, pork) and some flour let that meld then add the blended Chile mix.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Although it doesn’t say dried chilies in the recipe, that’s what you need.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: "California" chilies are actually just ripe, dried Anaheim chilies.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I forgot to add. Don't use so much water. Two cups are enough, and one cup should be drained off and set aside after the chiles cook. If more liquid is needed, add the drained water to meet your desires. I also use 3 to 5 of each chile type. This recipe doesn't use enough chile for that amount of water. I also don't use any onions in the sauce but used chopped onions to top the enchiladas as desired.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
I have dried Ancho chiles. Before I use them instead of “California chiles” I read the following in a post about peppers, is it true? 

“The Ancho chili, which is a poblano, is also known as a California chili, and the California chili is also known as an Anaheim chili.”

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
The tricky thing about chiles, and especially the dried ones, is that the same chile goes by different names in different regions of Mexico. And what they're called in the US also varies. 

The easy thing about chiles, especially the dried ones, is that as long as you stick to the same general type they're pretty interchangeable. Californias are dark red, smooth skinned, mild, long, and pointy. Anchos are blunter, wider, wrinkled and nearly black. They're both mildly hot and either will work.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: A food mill is useful to remove any remaining chile seeds and skins. This recipe is similar to one I learned from Susana Trilling in Oaxaca, except she toasts the chiles and onion first. But if I were making gallons at a time in a restaurant maybe I wouldn't toast them either.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Agree the sauce was thin and not full of flavor. That being said, it was compelling, and I ended up putting some in a small pitcher on the table to add to the plate as I ate. Kept coming back for more. I used 5 CA chilis and one chili de arbol. Skins and all went into the Vitamix and a smooth sauce was the result.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Dried chili pods, cleaned and seeds/veins removed.  Water, salt, garlic.  

Put in blender and run thru a colander into a skillet that has a teaspoon of oil and tablespoon of flour.  Make your gravy and that's it.

Most people in our family eat a fried egg on top, however I do not.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is an easy-to-follow recipe. I made it for El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas mentioned above but this is a good red sauce that can be used on other dishes. I substituted New Mexico (Hatch) chiles for the California/Anaheim chiles, otherwise followed the recipe as written. I'll make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: For a little more flavor, sub ancho for árbol, and toast the dried chilies to awaken their aroma. Leave the black pepper out.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: How spicy (hot) is this sauce?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: not very. Californias are mildly spicy, and guajillos only a bit more spicy. The arbol is hot, but there's only one. Leave it out if you want less heat, use more if you want more!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This seems so thin and watery. I made the green enchilada sauce with 2.5 cups of liquid and it came out perfect consistency-wise. This red sauce is not something I’d want to use for enchiladas. I’m reducing it way more than it calls for.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Different stoves and different pots will produce different results given the same cooking times. Absolutely cook it down until it matches your needs for sauce. Recipes are never written in stone.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Disappointing. Very (very) thin, not much flavor. To doctor it we a) reduced it, b) used (homemade) chile oil (instead of plain), added ground dried tomato and a pinch of (homemade) chile powder. Better. Will not use this recipe (as written) again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I ended up with a tasteless, watery sauce with some kick to it. No depth of flavors.  Where does it say to make a roux to thicken it?  Where did I go wrong?  Tomorrow using another recipe we are going to try to rescue this one.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Living in France limits accessibility to dried chili's. I used what I had, NM chili's, Arbero's and one other that I put in a jar and didn't mark them. I thought there was too much water in the original batch so I made two more batches using very little water and adding everything together. I have a beautiful textured, super flavourful sauce that I'm very happy with. Enchiladas tonight!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Where does the oil come in?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Hi Scott, the oil is to further cook the puréed chiles, it comes in at the beginning of Step 3: "Heat oil in a medium saucepan..." Hope that helps.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I’m curious. I have the El Cholo cookbook and this recipe differs?  I guess I’ll have to do a cook off to see if I can determine which they actually serve, if either, in the restaurant.  And which I prefer.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I make it as is and make sure to add enough salt. In the rotation. And family finds it delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Red Enchilada Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Had to make some modifications due to what I had on hand. Used 6 Guajillo peppers only. Blended in my Ninja so I had to leave out 1 cup of the cooking water but the resulting product had a nice consistency so I didn’t add it back in. Added 1 1/2 t of salt. Next time I might use slightly less black pepper. Final result was really good especially since it was much simpler than other chili pepper sauce recipes I’ve tried—just one sauce pan.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: This would be so easy to make into a complete vegetarian meal simply by omitting the chicken. With the beans and eggs, it’s still a hearty meal.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: and if you sub the chicken stock from the green sauce with veg stock.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: After years of trying and failing to make rolled enchiladas (the corn tortillas would always break even after dipping in sauce) this method is my new standard. It's faster, easier and makes an impressive presentation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: These are quite similar to the enchiladas served in New Mexico.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: https://www.elcharrocafe.com/history/ Sonoran cuisine is also enshrined and in fact, started in, the Sonoran desert. It ranges from Mexico up thru Tucson. Los Angeles is not in the Sonoran desert El Charro, founded in 1922, is the goto for Sonoran Cuisine. I believe they invented Carne Asada, Carne Seca dried meats on their rooftop. As well as the Chimichanga. The flat tortilla is generally of the Sante Fe and New Mexico cuisine style. My wife has a version, she calls "Tortilla Delight"

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I think dipping the tortilla into oil is a matter of personal preference-it’s sufficient to drag them through the red or green sauce in assembly step.  Love the idea of alternating red- and green-sauces tortillas!  In New Mexico, we order these as ‘Christmas’ style!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I learned to make these, sans chicken, from my mother some 70 years ago. She, in turn, picked up the recipe in New Mexico, where they go under the name "enchiladas montadas", presumably because they're topped with a fried egg. They make an excellent vegetarian meal just by themselves.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: This is an extraordinary preparation. This a time consuming for starters. It is more of a restaurant prep rather than a standard effort for the home cook. Making both sauces enabled me to use a pile of pots and an incredibly difficult clean up. The directions and method were a jumble of directives not easily followed. Finally after hours making this I served it. It was delicious. I’d like to  simplify this maybe with one sauce or at best to find a restaurant that does this style of cooking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody:
@John Golden
My impression too, explaining why I haven’t made it though I WANT to eat it! I’m always dumbfounded by how many pots and utensils I use when cooking Mexican food. For a cuisine that’s often snobbishly dismissed, it’s involved and has much subtlety and variety. Patience is a requirement!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: This was dinner last night—and it was excellent, rich and flavorful. Took about two hours to put together and was worth it. Next time, I’ll reduce the liquid in the filling (mine was a bit soupy) and sub chicken stock for water when I do. I reduced quantities to feed two; left over sauce and filling will become nachos tomorrow for lunch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: My mom always made  "stacked" enchiladas for breakfast - cheese, onion and lots of NM red sauce with a fried egg on top.  The perfect hangover food!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: It's similar in construction to the enchiladas we have here in New Mexico, i.e, flat and smothered with chile. However we don't use a lot of poblanos. I've only seen them used in chiles en nogada. Maybe the very occasional relleno--at least here in the north. I'm going to give them a try!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: inverted the proportions of green and red sauce (my red was too firey), and it came out amazing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: The sauces are amazing. Layered with beans instead of chicken to make it vegetarian.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I make enchildas montadas all the time with home made red sauce and the egg on top.  I always make these vegetarian (with cheese and onion).  I grew up in El Paso and spent lots of time in close by NM. This is a staple at our house.  Adding the green chile sauce is interesting...kind of like huevos divorciados.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: So these are not tortillas wrapped around the filling? You stack sauce and filling. I’m making it with just one of the sauces to save time to use up my leftover roast chicken

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Could you assemble and broil all of it in one large dish as opposed to individual dishes?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: You could do multiple enchiladas together on a baking sheet lined with foil.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I've fried the tortillas and assembled them as shown above but always called it a tostada. Not sure what the difference would be.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: After reading this I decided to go out for dinner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Why not just layer everything in a 9x13 pan lasagna-style?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: About frying tortillas- fried to a crisp is a tostada, yes. When making enchiladas you are really dipping in hot oil. This is done to keep a firm, distinct, corn tortilla. If you skip this step, the tortillas become a bit mushy in the sauce. At least that is my experience. Also: layering in a casserole is a great way to do flat enchiladas…and have lunches for the week.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: These are not similar to anything in New Mexico or the Southwest, at least not anything authentic. This is an atrocious recipe and an offense to real enchiladas.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Given that these aren't Rick Martinez's  Enchiladas, using Safeway canned Red/Green Enchilada Sauces worked just fine for me

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight- sauces lacked depth and flavor, and the enchiladas turned out  SUPER mushy! I make flat-style red-chili enchiladas all the time with and will go back to doing it my way. The green sauce is spinach soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Interesting; my hubby and I thought both sauces tasted authentic ( we're former Angelenos, and we've eaten there many times.)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: The dish was quite good, but it was also a reminder of why I generally don't like restaurant cookbooks or recipes (too many simultaneous pans required). I would make it again, but only if I already had the sauces.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: To make this recipe super easy, I store bought shredded chicken and both enchilada sauces. It’ll be a much easier recipe to do a second time when I already know the assembly steps.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Typically the initial step of frying the tortillas (sometimes messily preceded by a dousing of sauce) is to strengthen them against splitting during the rolling operation of traditional filled enchiladas.  Here, with the simpler stacking operation, you can safely omit that step with little effect on the taste or texture and a big effect on the fat content.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I don’t believe this was worth the time it took for each stage of prep in a home kitchen. However, the filling base was a pleasant surprise and worth replicating in other dishes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: This was undoubtedly one of my favorite NYT Cooking recipes I have ever made.  It took me a lot longer to make than I thought it would so next time I will make the sauces the day before.  Family members said this was FIRE!  I will make this again and again and again......

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe and thought it was a fun change from ordinary enchiladas. It was enough work that I probably won’t make it again. But I look forward to visiting El Cholo on some future trip to LA.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: This is excellent! I used a store-made cooked chicken   pre made enchilada sauce to speed up the prep & it came out great!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: As the recipe notes, there’s a lot going on all at once at the end—tortilla frying, assembling, broiling, egg frying.  It became a team effort with our guests and was fun and only mildly dangerous.  And the presentation and taste were fantastic—every bite a little different.  Great recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I don't know why you would do one stack at a time. On a baking sheet, I put the first layer of all 6 stacks, then the 2nd layer on all, etc. I also used store-bought green and red enchilada sauce - still absolutely delicious! I used one rotisserie chicken.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Mexican CREMA

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody:
Always a hit. Made it vegetarian by omitting chicken. Sometimes I add sautéed veggies if I have it on hand. 

The sauces freeze nicely too.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I have known this dish for decades as a "Mexican Kitchen Casserole" which I first encountered at a Westchester County, NY, roadside restaurant, unfortunately now gone. I more or less recreated it and raised my family on it. Great to know its origins!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Are most kitchen plates (say, Fiestaware) broiler safe??

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Is this recipe very spicy?  What should I change to make a version only slightly spicy?  My family won't eat spicy, but I love the sound of this recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody:
poblanos are mildly spicy but you can switch to a green bell pepper for the filling.
For the green sauce, omit the jalapeños. For the red sauce, leave out the arbol, consider using more california peppers instead of the guajillos.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I’m confused. Where are the instructions for making the sauces?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: The sauce names are underlined in the ingredients, indicating they are links to the recipes, so you can just click on them.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Another alteration to modify heat: I made only the red sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: The links to the sauces are in the opening write-up, NOT linked in the recipe itself. Very confusing.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I have not made this but the El Cholo recipe you need to publish is the one for green corn tamales!! The signature dish of El Cholo for the 40 years I've been going there.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: To use less oil when making enchiladas from a Diana Kennedy recipe, I just spray them with a bit of cooking spray and cook for about 30 seconds each side and dip in sauce. Just using “raw” tortillas does affect the texture. I’m gonna have to try these

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: This is a delicious rendition of enchiladas. I boiled the chicken water down so it was more saucy and less soupy

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: It can be daunting to make both sauces and finish the dish all at once. I'd suggest making the sauces ahead of time. Also, use an immersion blender; it works very well for both sauces.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I made this for dinner tonight and we couldn’t stop eating! Wow. The flavor of the chicken filling came out delicious. I made as written but added 3 tsps of chicken stock paste and omitted the salt. I let the stock cook down to almost half but next time I will only add 2 and a half cups of chicken stock. Frying the corn tortillas was a bit tricky. I was paranoid to over fry them, so I counted twenty five seconds each and the tongs made a hole in one. I only had red salsa so I used that. Yum!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Yum! Clean plates by the entire family. This was a lot more work than I had bargained for, but totally worth it. Next time I’ll make the sauces and chicken ahead of time. The green enchilada sauce was my favorite. Substituted pickled green tomatoes for the tomatillos.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Excellent but it took mw so long to stack I'd like the middle of the stack to be warmer and meltier.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions for substituting the homemade sauces with store bought?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody:
Made this exactly according to the recipe and it was fantastic.  Will definitely be using the sauce recipes on their own.  

Probably my favorite NYT recipe so far.  I am dreaming about eating this again soon.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: The chicken filling part was a bit soupy using the listed amount of water, but it is so delicious that I'm going to use what's leftover as a base for chicken tortilla soup the next day!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: A very laborious recipe, considering the two sauces. I might modify to make one sauce - the red. What California chilis, please?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: An easy, healthy option for leftovers is to dip the tortillas in the broth left over from making the chicken then role them up with a sprinkle of leftover beans and chicken then broil according to the recipe. I have leftover chicken and broth and enjoying it as chicken soup!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Daniel- try using flour tortillas.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Though the fried egg idea is new to me, this is very similar to the stacked enchiladas I've been making for years.  The main difference is that I assemble a single stack of many layers (which gives the opportunity to alternate another different filling or two) and then cut individual wedge-shaped servings like slices of layer cake.  It's fun to see all the layers in the cut slices and really makes the logistics of the final baking and cheese-melting operation on a single stack much easier.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was the closest one to restaurant style enchiladas that I have ever found.  My only changes were to use queso fresco cheese (melts better and is what the restaurants in So. Cal. seem to use) and only the red sauce (just because that is what the family likes).  Absolutely stunning recipe - will definitely make again.  The sauce is supposed to be thin.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Excellent flavor. Putsy to put together, but worth it when you eat it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Really enjoyed this recipe. Did with only green enchilada sauce and skipped the egg - delicious and would make again for sure! Served with black olives, sour cream and red salsa.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Instead of putting each stack onto a plate separately, I placed all the stacks I would need to feed my family on a sheet pan, broiled and then served from there.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I suspect that there may be a typo in the list of ingredients. Adding 1 tablespoon of salt means that each individual serving has one half teaspoon of added salt. I wonder if they meant 1 teaspoon in total.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe.  I put off making it for almost two weeks because it was intimidating.  I'm glad I finally did it though.  Really enjoyed it.  I did not put 1 T salt in the chicken filling.  Only about half that and it was still plenty salty.  I did cook the tortillas in oil as directed, but next time I'll simply lay them on the jelly roll pan, spray them with cooking spray and put them under the broiler for a  minutes or so, turning and spraying the second side too.  Much easier.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: A straight-forward and easy-to-follow recipe, or rather, recipes. A lot of steps, none difficult. I found it fun to do. The sauces alone are good and can be used on other dishes. Yes, you use a lot of pots and implements but the cook times give you a lot of free time to clean up as you go. The results are well worth the effort. This is a delicious and filling dish. We'll make it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: These were very delicious but more time consuming than it appeared. The dipping and assembly turned out to be a chore. Next time, I’ll enjoy these at a restaurant or eliminate some of the steps with dipping in oil, etc.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: These are the only enchiladas I have ever had that come close to the enchiladas a Mexican friend made for me years ago. Absolutely delicious. My new go-to recipe. If you don't feel like making both sauces, making these with either the green or red sauce on its own is a good option for simplifying the recipe a bit.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. I used smoked shredded chicken, which added a nice smoky flavor. I topped it with guacamole rather than sour cream. I used locally prepared flour tortillas instead of corn. I would be tempted to skip the third tortilla next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: El Cholo’s Sonora-Style Enchiladas Recipe
commentBody: I used high-quality red enchilada sauce and high-quality green enchilada sauce rather than making my own. This allowed this dish to be a fairly quick meal preparation. I also reduced the amount of liquid by half when preparing the chicken mixture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Do you think this could be made ahead, al dente... reheated to complete and then topped with the herbs?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody:
FYI One 3 pound butternut squash makes 4-1/2 cups of cut up squash. One cup of cut up squash yields 2/3 pound. 

Just in case you wanted to buy the already cut up squash.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Made this using one of the treasured Blenheim spicy ginger ales we had on hand. It took longer to cook than the recipe suggested, but that can happen due to various sized squash. It took time to carefully dice the squash compared to our usual easy to roast efforts. It never did get to syrupy even after an hour of simmering in a large skillet. The squash did not seem to embody the ginger flavor we wanted, especially after the coughing fit from heating the gingery ale! I would not make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody:
This is a surprising result. 

The delicious Blenheim products are ginger ales.

Ginger ales are made from flavor ingredients and carbonated water.

The recipe calls for ginger beer, a fermented product, though with little or no alcohol. I wonder if that could be the difference.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Ginger beer is similar to ginger ale but with a much more intense ginger flavor. It is hard to find in some areas, but since it is used in some mixed drinks, you might try looking in a liquor store that sells mixers.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Trader Joe's sells a ginger brew. It's good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made ahead of time and reheated?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I found the liquid evaporated before the squash was fully cooked. I would cook covered for the first 15 minutes to infuse the squash with the ginger beer flavor (BTW it is alcoholic in the UK) and uncover for the last 10 minutes to allow the liquid to reduce. Also popped it under the broiler for 5 minutes before serving to crisp up the edges. Delish!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I used an old trick for cutting winter squash:  wash the squash and leave the outside wet.  Put in the microwave for about 3 minutes.  This softens the rind enough to make cutting it into 1" slices much easier and safer.  Then I trimmed the rind off and cut into  diamonds.  It smells good, I'll see how it turns out.  Making the squash today and the gremolata fresh on Thursday.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Costco carries Bundaberg Ginger Beer - don’t worry about having to buy the 12-pack - it is addicting!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: What is a "jewel-shaped" chunk?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Fevertree makes an excellent ginger beer which is a component of making a Moscow or Kentucky Mule drink.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I made this to take to thanksgiving this year. Doubled it, used Fever Tree ginger beer and pre-cut butternut chunks. I cooked the chunks until they were just tender (yes, the liquid reduced to a thick sauce), then transferred to a shallow baking/serving dish, and covered it in foil. Reheated it for 20 min at 350. Topped it with the gremolata before serving. Thought there wasn’t enough gremolata so I doubled it. Also gremolata needed salt. Everyone liked it. Would make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: This was tasty but  my sauce was not syrupy.  I will place it under the broiler at the end next time.  The gremolata was tasty with the fresh ginger and the orange rind.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody:
Yes, this can be made with sweet potatoes. Increased other ingredients by about a third, while keeping the same weight of sweet potato as squash. Used ginger beer.

It is wonderfully delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Delicious flavor however I do feel the need to warn people that simmering ginger beer will result in a coughing fit from anyone in the kitchen during the cooking process.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Ginger beer that is available as a mixer is nonalcoholic, but many make ginger beer by fermenting pineapple skins, for example, that is alcoholic. Both are ginger beer but for this recipe it’s probably the dark and stormy and Moscow mule variety.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I've never heard of fermented pineapple skins making ginger beer! Interesting...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody:
@Shirley P.

The photo above shows that the pieces are elongated, not cubed.

Such a shape can be achieved by cutting batons (which would be shortened into cubes by cutting all at right angles) on a bias.

Cutting in this way increases the surface area, which helps in the development of the sauce. It also makes more interesting shapes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: this was such a big hit at my thanksgiving table, maybe my favorite dish this year! flavorful yet subtle. something different yet it went perfectly with the rest of our meal. can’t wait to make it again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I made it using cut up butternut squash.  Followed the recipe faithfully.  Had mush.  No glaze. Awful.  Thought I would try something different and it sent me running back to my faithful air fryer recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Loved this, but cooked it slightly differently from the recipe as written. Instead of simmering on medium high, uncovered, stirring occasionally, for 25 mins…I simmered the squash on medium, covered, stirring occasionally, for 20 mins. The squash browned up on some of the sides which was lovely, and the sauce was nice and syrupy by the end. Doubled the gremolata because I wanted more garlic heehee

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Too much liquid, squash is mushy, no glazing, looks horrible. Brown cast to the squash.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: This was strange as written. Made w/honeynut squash which cooked down to a puree in 12 minutes. Kind of tasted like candied yams (which is fine). The gremolata did not add anything, and I didn't find it pleasant to eat the ginger/parsley mix alongside squash puree.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody:
I skipped the topping, but thought this was
a great dish. The hint of clove was just right with the honey and butter. I got busy with other things and was pleasantly surprised how wonderful it was with little or no work.  Plan to have this for the holidays and believe all ages will enjoy the dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Definitely yes on the Bundaberg. I’m actually enjoying some right now with a healthy dose of gin and a splash of lime. In my town, I can find it at Kroger and Target in 4 packs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Can someone tell me what "1-inch jewel-shaped chunks" are?  I'm not that familiar with 'jewels'  I thought it might mean the classic shape of the diamond in a cartoon engagement ring, but how would you cut that?  Is it just trapezoidal 'cubes' or more like a D-20 D&D die?  The picture isn't that clear.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Loved this!  Very easy and a great alternative to plain roasted squash.  The glaze came together just as the squash was al dente.  Yum!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: The sauce got syrupy after an hour of simmering post removal of squash. I removed the squash pieces after they got fork tender so that they wouldn't be overcooked. The gremolata is where all of the spice flavor came from, and the butternut squash+raw ginger taste was very nice. Would not recommend recipe as written.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I had the opposite problem that @Joan had. By the time all that liquid cooked down, the squash was mush. I would use half the ginger beer if I made this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I wouldn’t make this recipe again. The “sauce” never became syrupy, even after cooking the squash for longer than directed and removing the squash and boiling the liquid for several extra minutes. The gremolata also needed salt. Overall, not worth the effort of peeling and cutting the squash. If you do make it, I’d try cooking the squash and just broth and reducing a small amount of ginger beer separately.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Followed directions faithfully, but this did not reduce and never got to the glossy glaze. Turned out a bit mushy and bland even with the gremolata. I would not make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: This is a very forgiving recipe to riff on. I first made it while my kitchen was under construction and substituted whatever I could find—a couple clementines, candied ginger, maple syrup for the honey and cinnamon were pretty much what was available. I made some quick vegetable stock from a leek and celery. I followed the preparation on the stove. The 2nd time I used OJ instead of stock and baked it in the oven, basting it every 15 minutes for an hour. The ginger and ginger beer are delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I LOVED this. This is going to be in regular rotation in winter squash season. I made it the day before Thanksgiving and then reheated it and added the Gremolata right before serving.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Substituted 3 tbsp of brown sugar for the honey (not a fan of honey). Avoid evaporation by lowering the heat and adding squash as soon as the beer (used Fever Tree) boils. Used a braiser skillet, kept it covered for 25 minutes. Remove the cover, turn the heat up some for about 5 minutes, letting the liquid turn into a syrup. Added gremolata and set the braiser right on the table next to a Pork Roast rubbed with Garlic, Rosemary & Fennel. Lovely Sunday dinner.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I felt there was too much liquid. Squash got very  tender well before liquid cooked off. Ended up having to  pour some off.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: It takes around 30 minutes to cook and 30 minutes to prep.  Otherwise, it was a Thanksgiving hit.  I made it 2 hours ahead.  Separated the squash from the liquid, covered with foil with holes for steam to release.  Heated the sauce up before serving, poured over then add the gremolata.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Christmas Eve and loved it. Thanks to Annie M for the tip about preparing the squash (this is usually my least favorite part) and to the others below, I cooked the squash until just aldente and then removed it to a warm platter in the oven to finish reducing the ginger beer/stock combo. I used fever tree and included the cloves. I poured the reduced sauce back over the squash when ready to serve and topped with the gremolata. The leftovers were even great the next day!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Did as other cooks did- removed my cubes when they were done and reduced the liquid with no squash in it. Reheated next day tossed in the glaze. Made twice the amount of gremolata and generously sprinkled on top. It was a hit at Easter dinner!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Is this ginger beer alcoholic or not?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: No, ginger beer is a soda that has more intense ginger than a ginger ale. More flavor!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: No, non-alcoholic. Read the label to make sure, but you can usually find it in the soda aisle.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Jey, ginger beer is a non-alcoholic soft drink, although it is often used as a mixer for cocktails (like the Moscow Mule).  It is like a much stronger ginger ale.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody:
Sorry. 
What is ginger beer? The same thing as ginger ale, the soft drink?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Ginger Beer is more intense, a popular brand is Reeds.  You can get it at Trader Joes and Whole Foods or online.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made with sweet potatoes?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Could you substitute sweet potatoes for squash

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: For a smaller group or a couple, this could be made with chunks of carrot.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I’m going to make this for our Thanksgiving dinner today. Planning to add some pomegranate seeds at the end of cooking- more jewels!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Never did get a glaze. Added some corn starch to thicken the sauce before the squash was to mushy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: My squash-loving mother would like this again. I'm not a big fan. Even with the ginger beer and the gremolata it was still...squash in sauce.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: I had to scoop out the squash with a slotted spoon and reduce for an extra 20-30 minutes, but it's pretty tasty once I poured the sauce back over.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Can I make this with carrots instead?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Tasty! I had no issues with the sauce reducing but kept it at a pretty rapid simmer. I don't love cooking squash this way as it was hard to cook evenly and some pieces got mushy. Easy gremolata was a great touch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Shockingly good

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ginger Beer-Glazed Butternut Squash With Gremolata  Recipe
commentBody: Could this be made with carrots instead? Just looking through side dish options for turkey day and thinking could be a version of glazed carrots?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Make ahead plan.  Treat this like a breakfast strata. Prep all vegetable and cheese then top with custard mixture (half way through step 8). Cover with plastic wrap (pressed against the surface of veg mix) and refrigerate overnight.  Let come to room temp (2hours) before proceeding with baking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: @Hut - I tried this, but when baked the top layer of potatoes never cooked through 😕

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: After reading several comments about the color of the beets permeating through the thickness, I soaked the slices in cool water before assembling. The color striations came out perfectly.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: You can make a nice cauliflower crème with boiled, puréed cauliflower, sautéed shallots and garlic, Purée all together, then bring to a simmer with a bay leaf. Add salt and pepper to taste. An optional addition that really rounds out the flavor and body of alt dairy is to whisk in just a little yellow or white miso (yellow is sweeter than red)- start with just a tbsp and taste test. It shouldn’t be enough that you can identify the miso taste.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: For those of us who tend to bite off more than they can easily chew on Turkey Day, do you have cook ahead of time advice for this?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Can I freeze this ahead of time?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Can confirm, purple sweet potatoes work great for the bottom layer!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Can you describe the forming of the rosettes a little more? Are you twisting the phyllo to make a kind of pinwheel shape? The directions are kind of vague.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Short NYT video on Twitter showing how to make the rosettes… https://twitter.com/nytimes/status/1592978366281703424?s=46&t=GIv_Yakw7JjHCJcweH_VVA

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Make ahead: proceed through step 8,baking vegetables for 1  hour,  cool, tightly cover, refrigerate overnight. Bring to room temp, warm at 375, increase oven to 400, add phyllo, and bake as directed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
@kate. Color me grateful for your suggestion. The two hours I put in prepping and taking this through the initial hour of baking saw me saying, “Uncle!” 

Can’t wait to have it *tomorrow.*

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made this to test for Turkey Day and can report back. Using a food processor it took 1 hour to prep everything & get it into the oven.
