My beets produced lots of liquid that bubbled up during baking, so the ombré effect was less pronounced on slicing. I couldn’t find filo, used buttered bread crumbs instead. The flavor   fall colors are sensational & festive. But it’s a lot of work prepping and cleaning up. Next time will skip the egg yolks, whisk in potato starch instead, and reduce the salt.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I feel like I need to issue a PSA for this recipe now having made it twice at Thanksgiving. It's undeniably beautiful. But it's just not that good. Somehow the sum of all those delicious vegetables is less than the parts - and substantially so. It takes an age to make (and I'm game for complicated cooking projects). No one finished their servings so we've ended up throwing out 2/3 of it the past two years. Pleasing to the eyes, but disappointing to the palate. Save yourself!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Agree with this! It's not great, even when made well.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
@CarameLIZation 

Interesting…..  this was my impression from reading the recipe. I couldn’t imagine the flavors melding together very well, especially not with that kind of cheese. Thanks for saving me hours of time and a lot of wasted ingredients.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
@CarameLIZation
I’ve made it at least 4 times now, and I disagree, it’s an amazing thanksgiving main dish for vegetarians. But I do a lot of intense side dishes so this is like more adjacent to turkey in a meat eater thanksgiving, where it is a slightly mild but still tasty main.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Please for the love of everything SOMEONE describe how to do the phyllo rosettes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: For the “rosettes”. Phyllo sheets fresh out of package are very soft and malleable. Take a sheet and scrunch in you hand as if balling a sheet of paper to throw.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
to veganize, part 2:
- I’m an omnivore and would actually skip the cheese for this recipe- the flavors will radiate through if you just sprinkle veg slices with nutmeg and black pepper before using crème to bind. If you really want a vegan cheese sub though, Minimalist Baker’s “parmesan” is perfect, as it would also add a little texture
- phyllo dough is often vegan but you could instead make a savory vegan crumble topping if you prefer (this option easily made gf as well)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
to veganize, part 1:
- make cauliflower crème instead of the bechamel (see my other comment). You can add alt butter to it if you like- I use miyoko's because it “browns” due to nut fat, which adds complexity of flavor. 
- use good quality olive oil instead of butter with the veg (I personally think this is a better taste combo with root veg than vegan alternative)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Thanks!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: For the “rosettes”.  Phyllo sheets fresh out of package are very soft and malleable.  Take a sheet and scrunch in you hand as if balling a sheet of paper to throw.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: You will not want to freeze ahead with the heavy cream/butter content. It will separate and become a gloppy mess. Suggestions about making base layer (like strata mentioned above) and refrigerating  until the next day should work fine with all veg submerged/covered tightly. Note: if jostled too much the beets could color the entire dish hot pink and lose the hombre effect, so handle gently.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Can this be prepared & frozen a few days ahead of event?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night and included parsnips instead of butternut. It is absolutely wonderful..a dish  I will most certainly make over and over again due to it's elegance and flavor. Thanks NYT cooking team. This one's a keeper.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Thanks.  So many of the comments are questions, etc. (not that there is anything wrong with that).  Great to see a report from someone who made it.  This looks like a big challenge with a big payoff!  I'm trying to decide if I have it in me to add this to the Thanksgiving line-up or if this is better as fall dinner with a salad.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I’m definitely going to try this but with purple sweet potato followed by butternut squash then yellow beets then potato. I’m too worried the red beet will stain everything else.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I’m so glad I tried this a second time & followed the make ahead suggestions the night before (up to step 8). It’s REALLY beautiful when you can get the layers right - and very impressive. It’s not hard, just labor-intensive and very particular. I used a mandoline on the thinnest setting for all the veg. Bottom layer I did just one thin layer of beetroot, and 2 layers of purple sweet potato right next to that. It also helped to put the beet slices in icy water and pat it as dry as possible - last time the whole thing was red and you couldn’t see the layers. Rest of the veg layered as suggested. Perfect texture for all of them when cooked for an hour the night before. I didn’t have phyllo, so instead of fussing with the rosettes, I used a random sheet of puff pastry I had in the freezer and honestly - I preferred this texture! It made the final presentation more cohesive than when I tried the rosettes last time. The morning of, I took it to room temp then baked again for about another hour before putting on the puff pastry, then baked til the pastry was golden. Voila. I’m so proud of the final product. Oh! And I halved it - it’s very rich and cut into 9 hefty squares in an 8x8. That was plenty for us. 🙌🏽

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: @Monica This is SO helpful Monica, thank you for all the useful tips!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This dish is incredibly delicious. Even my kids who hate beets, squash and sweet potatoes gobbled it up. Well worth all of the slicing. I covered the pan during the first hour of cooking which ensured the vegetables cooked through as my slicing efforts were imprecise.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Suggestions for making this vegan (or find a different recipe?) favorite vegan subs, including any vegan phyllo recommendations? (I found vegetable-based puff pastry, but ....)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Athen's Fillo dough is vegan, Nature's Balance butter sticks, Crountry Crock heavy cream.  Forgo the gruyere for vegan parm or a bunch of nutrional yeast mixed with some cream for some gooeyness

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: You can make a nice cauliflower crème with boiled, puréed cauliflower, sautéed shallots/leeks, and alt milk. Purée all together, then bring to a simmer with a bay leaf and a couple smashed whole garlic cloves. Add S&P + nutmeg to taste. An optional addition that really rounds out the flavor and body of alt dairy is to whisk in just a little yellow or white miso (yellow is sweeter than red)- not enough that you can identify what it is. Thicken with arrowroot or cornstarch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Cook for an hour without the phyllo a day or two in advance. Then heat through for about 1/2 hour and then turn up the heat and add the phyllo. It's even better than baking it all in one day.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Here’s the short NYT video on Twitter showing how to make the rosettes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I'm having a hard time imaging how to make the rosettes, any tips or video?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: My make-ahead suggestion would be to slice all the vegetables ahead of time and assemble the day of. Thatʼs how I plan to try it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Ultimately this is quite a production. It took me about 90 minutes just to get the main dish ready for the oven. I used a mandoline. Including cooling time, it is two hours from the minute you put it in the oven until you put it on the table. My timing didn't work out the day I did the prep, so I put the oven-ready dish it in the fridge for a day and cooked it the next day which seemed to work fine. (Bring to room temp first.)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Isn't the order of potatoes wrong in the recipe?  Don't the darkest (red beets?) go in the pan first? then sweet potatoes? then butternut squash? then red (*skinned*) potatoes?  The recipe has the red potatoes going in first, then the beets?  

One might add purple Stokes sweet potatoes which are really really purple as the first layer just for fun? then the beets etc and maybe skip orange sweet potatoes since the butternut squash is orangey.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I read the recipe to say the beets go in the bottom and the potatoes are on the top layer. They had been added to the cream earlier.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made this last year for Thanksgiving & it was indeed a labor of love. It was a show stopper - absolutely stunning dish & tasted incredible. Lessons learned: I will never make this dish on the day of Thanksgiving again and will be making 2 days in advance. Make ahead method totally works here (in fact, the leftovers of this dish last year were just as good as the original IMO). Also, use of the mandolin slicer is essential here - I wouldn’t endeavor this dish without one (just be careful!).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: A  solution to the beets staining the dish & ruining the ombre effect: I prepped everything day of rather leisurely.  First thing I did was slice and salt the raw beets, then let them sit. By the time I'd gotten through all of the other prep for the dish (maybe 75min) they had let off a lot of liquid, which I poured off. I then pressed them between some paper towels before placing them on the bottom to assemble- came out perfect after cooking! Resting also seemed essential.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe is a 3-hour proposition, even if you’re a mandoline-wielding pro with the vegetables. 

Like others, I took it through Step 8 and thought I’d reheat/serve this the next day with the phyllo, then said, “Screw it!” And simply reheated for 30 mins without doing that step and it was well-received with a drizzle of the leftover heated sauce. I’m not certain adding more butter and the phyllo would have given it anything it needed. If you’ve shingled the top potato layers, it’s quite attractive without.

The recipe could be better written to describe how the vegetable halves and quarters should be sliced—across in demi-lunes or in plank-like lengths of 1/8”.  I went for planks with the sweet potatoes and butternut squash. But absolutely agree: the consistent 1/8” slices ensure even cooking for all the layers. 

The quantity of vegetable slices also produces far more volume than can be layered in 2 layers each vegetable in a typical lasagne-sized pan. I recommend something a bit broader so you’re not tempted to layer this more thickly and risk uneven cooking of additional layers. There’s plenty of sauce rendered, so a bigger pan won’t be an issue on that count.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made with vegan butter/vegan milk? If so, what do you recommend using?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I’m wondering if purple cabbage could be used in replacement of beets? Or purple carrots, yams, etc as suggested by others. Glad to see I’m not the only non-beet eater.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Salt the beets ahead of time and wipe them down when they start sweating, this will prevent the beets from coloring the entire dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I've made this several times and found it to be delicious, as well as beautiful!  I've preferred it without cheese because I found the cheese lumpy.  I added white wine to the cream and found the cream sauce to be nicely infused; I'm surprised by the comments that say this was bland!  I also added a layer of white potatoes between the beets and the sweet potatoes to help absorb the extra beet juice.  The beets DO have the strongest flavor, so omit if you hate them!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I did a similar recipe (epicurious, I think), and ended up par-cooking all the veg (roasting at 350 F) to control for the varying cook rates. That recipe didn't call for a sauce, which I added for my second attempt. Made a bechamel and used a sprinkling of gruyere. It froze well after baking. I'll try this one, and maybe make individual servings instead of one large one. They should bake faster. And be a big reveal as every serving is dug into. :-)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Took about 7 hours, not 3. The veg cutting & layering & first bake I did the day before phyllo. A very sharp knife worked fine instead of mandolin. To make rosettes, use room temp phyllo. Butter 2 sheets, stick together & let sit for a minute. Pinch one long side with right hand, use left to fold the rest of the sheet around your right hand, while spinning right hand. Don’t be too precious. There was no ombré, beets overpower everything. My family LOVED this and it was delicious & beautiful!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I ended up making this for our Thanksgiving dinner. I didn’t find the prep to be extremely overwhelming or exhausting, though it is time-consuming. I did however find the amount of time that you put into this recipe completely not worth it. The beats ended up coloring most of the gratin pink so you lost a lot of the ombre effect also even though I let it rest for well over half an hour it still came apart upon slicing. It wasn’t a total disaster, but again, definitely wasn’t worth the effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I disagree with the statement that the wow factor justifies the labor since the flavor of the dish does not justify the labor. It is a beautiful photograph, but my effort resulted in a fairly bland, only mildly interesting casserole. I won't be making it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: My daughter and I are both experienced cook and got very excited about this recipe. Unfortunately this was an all around fail. Lots of work, but the veggies cooked at different times and do some were perfectly cooked and others were still firm. The beets bled a lot into the upper area. In what recipe does it seem like a good technique to soak up the juices (mostly beet) from the top of the dish? Seems like an indication that this recipe needs work. Not much bang for the buck.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Re: using yucca or rutabaga - they would be wonderful! 
You might also try fennel, jicama, kohlrabi, celeriac, Jerusalem artichokes (sunchokes), and daikon radish. 

These are all delicious and mild. I’ve used all of these as a substitute for onions, one of my common food allergies.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Is there a vegan version of this? It Iooks amazing!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I really did not love the beets in this recipe. I think that red potatoes as some have said might be better.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made this to bring to my parent's Thanksgiving celebration.  While it was beautiful and tasty, the amount of effort required to pull it off was extraordinary.  Thus it was a once-in-a-lifetime effort for me.  I would happily eat it again.  But only if someone else prepares it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Veganized this for Thanksgiving by leaving out the egg yolks and using soy milk (cornstarch slurry to thicken a tad), plant butter, and quality plant-based cheese. Loved it. The sauce was so flavorful that, if I make this again, I might just skip the cheese and layer sauce instead. Layers of root veg, a fragrant sauce and a crispy, pretty lid: yes!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Christmas dinner to rave reviews from the adults (the kids avoided it entirely). I used my food processor to slice all the veg, which was a huge time saver. Did most of the slicing and dicing one day, assembled and baked the gratin on a second day, and added the phyllo dough and baked again on the third day. Managed to keep the beers from leaking too much by soaking them in water, and then salting them.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
I made this a little over a year ago and was considering it again for Easter dinner this year. While I ultimately didn’t make it, I noticed all the comments about the prep time, etc, and wanted to provide my own experience: this recipe is phenomenal.

I didn’t find that slicing the veg was overly time consuming, and the variation in the colours was perfect and added a little touch of drama to dinner. It was delicious and definitely worth the effort if you want a showstopper side dish that is like scalloped potatoes on steroids.

The phyllo rosettes are a nice little touch and easy to make. Mine looked like crumpled balls of paper, and no one cared. It was very pretty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I am going to make this next week for a vegan potluck.  Looking at the recipe it should be very easy to convert it to vegan.  Vegan butter, Vegan heavy cream  and any egg substitute.  Athen's Fillo dough is vegan and found just about everywhere.  If you have the time and patience for homemade Gruyere cheese, there's that option, otherwise a vegan parm from Trader Joe's will work or even just a bunch of nutritional yeast.  I think you can make ahead up to step 10 and then freeze.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This took me (with help) 3 - 4 hours to get through the point of assembly. I pressed the layers tightly as I layered and had a real problem with sauce not seeping to layers below. I would consider pouring a little sauce on top of each layer during assembly. I refrigerated this over night, let it warm up for about an hour and a half before baking. I did not have the problem with color bleed, other than the slight pinking on the outer edges. This was beautiful, tasty, and quite the project!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: One sheet of puff pastry (slightly rolled out) plopped on top with a few steam holes and fork pricks worked just fine… seems way easier than the fiddly rosettes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Flavorless, IMhO, but looked pretty.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made this for New Year’s Day dinner and it was really tasty. I sliced the veggies in the food processor starting with the lightest first so I didn’t have to wash everything in between. I didn’t do the phyllo topping because it was just the 4 of us so there would be lots of leftovers. Instead I used panko toasted in butter mixed with Parmesan cheese. The leftovers have warmed up beautifully and the topping stayed crisp. I’ll try the phyllo topping when I make it for a crowd.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This truly is a labor of love like the other commenters say. A mandolin is almost a must otherwise you will not eat this until it’s dark outside. Totally worth all the effort and I took some of the advice of rinsing the beets a little so the beet color  doesn’t  overpower the ombré. Used a fontina and a smoked Gouda in addition to the suggested cheese. So delicious and a memorable dish that is its own main course.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Second year I have attempted this and it turned out beautifully. Per many of the comments, I processed the beets quite thoroughly before layering them. I soaked them in cold water and salted them a lot. My final result had gorgeous striations, all the vegetables cooked at about the same rate. Every freaking grocery store was out of phyllo so I just added more cheese on top. My food processor saved me hours as I used it for all the vegetables and the cheese. All in all, when this recipe is done right, the flavors of each vegetable come through along with the seasoned cream and Gruyère. Absolutely delicious second time around. Eating it for breakfast currently.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I too would love tips on how to make this Vegan.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Please help me make this VEGAN!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely loved this dish, such a stunner and delicious! My only suggestion would be to tie up the bay leaves and peppercorns in a bouquet garnie rather than straining out the delicious flavorful garlic and shallot

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving and it turned out fantastic. Probably took about an hour longer than the recipe states and it was tedious. While delicious and it looked spectacular, I don’t think I’d do it again. I also used a lot more sheets of phyllo. I don’t know that I made “roses”- mostly just crumpled up the phyllo into little spiky balls because I couldn’t be bothered by the end of the process as to what was meant by roses. It still looked pretty. Luckily, my beet juice didn’t leak.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Made this THREE times already. Loved all versions, the first being true to instructions, minus the phyllo. It's a hit.

The third time - due to what I had in the pantry + fridge, and to use up some of the leftover veggies - I used onions instead of shallots, white cheddar instead of gruyere, added fresh rosemary w/ the thyme, and poured all the onion/milk mixture on top of the potatoes at the end, w/o straining. I even left the bay leaf on top, centered. Added cheddar after 60 min. EXCELLENT

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I followed "Hut"'s comment and prepped halfway through step 8 the day before. I completely forgot to thaw my phyllo and had to skip that step, but it was still lovely. I would also recommend starting by slicing all of the vegetables, and adding salt and pepper every time you layer. No need for this reusing/wiping out a bowl nonsense that makes the recipe seem more onerous that it is.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. Prep took about an hour. I prebaked it on Christmas Eve and added the phyllo dough for the final 30 minute cook on Christmas. I followed the other comments and soaked the beets to prevent the color from running and it was perfect. Can’t wait to make this again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Cover with aluminum foil during cooking. 
Maybe double sauce/cooking liquid. Great with dis and anything else on plate. 
Really, really give it a 30-60 minute rest.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I used buttered puffed pastry sheets doubled on top so I skipped the phyllo rosettes and saved a bit of time. It came out delicious! My sister-in-law said it was her favorite dish on her plate (she's not a vegetarian), and she called it pure comfort food. I did not use the full amount of potatoes  and squash the recipe called for and it still turned out great.  I'm a vegetarian, and this was a hit with everyone at the table,  regardless of age or food preferences.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Will be my second time making this! It was a winner at Thanksgiving last year and since Santamas day is all vegetarian this seemed like a perfect show stopper! Ms Claus sent an early gift to help cut down the time - a CuisineArt!!!
Happy! Happy! Merry! Merry!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I'm in the middle of making this and I wish I had either a much larger Pyrex or a out a third less root veggies. getting this much into a 13x9 pyrex is literally impossible.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Is there a substitute for the half and half for vegans or those who are lactose intolerant?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This looks amazing! My kids hate beets…I wonder what other red/dark vegetable I could use instead? Purple carrots?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Organic Valley makes lactose free (and organic) half and half. I am lactose intolerant and have had wonderful experiences using it - first time in years.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Definitely going to make this for my vegetarian husband, but what about the beautiful shallots and garlic that get strained out? I think I will add them back to the cream once the yolks have been incorporated. Thoughts?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: If you add the shallots and garlic back to the cream, the cream will likely be lumpy and interfere with the lovely striation.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Doesn’t all Gruyère have animal rennet?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
I hate beets. Would purple sweet potato work as a substitution? Any recommendations of which variety? 

(All beet haters thank you in advance for your service!!) :)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions for gluten-free option v. the Phyllo pastry sheets? Or is it possible to purchase that gf?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I've never been able to find GF Phyllo sheets, but I was thinking of a topping of GF croutons (easily homemade--you can search online) or store-bought GF breadcrumbs. One can also usually find GF crispy shallots at South Asian and Southeast Asian grocery stores if that's an option where you live. Or they too can be made from scratch. Just a few options to consider

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Gruyère is made with rennet. It is NOT vegetarian.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Can leftovers be frozen?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I need help with the rosettes. Can I get more detail on how to form those? A video sure would be helpful

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This video is super helpful https://www.bonappetit.com/video/watch/from-the-home-kitchen-sam-makes-ruffled-milk-raspberry-pie

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I’m perplexed about those rosettes. Can you explain how to form those?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Q about Step 3: Are we reserving the cup of cream before or after the egg yolks have been whisked in?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Anything wrong with using a food processor to slice the veggies?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Georgia, I used a food processor to slice the veggies and shred the Gruyère—it’s a big time saver. Using a knife would take forever.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Sara: the egg goes in the cream before you reserve a cup of it. You reheat it again later for serving on the side. MacNeil: red potatoes start softening in the cream while other items go in the casserole dish. I’m glad that purple potatoes worked because now I might try making this since that appeals more. Regarding the phyllo rosettes: you butter the sheets and scrunch them up to add visual interest and crunch. They won’t look like actual flowers because we regular people aren’t food stylists

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: How does one prevent the lovely pink beet juices from leaching into all the other layers? I understand they are on the bottom of the strata, but wouldn't the cream mixture carry them throughout?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Hi Aine, I poured the cream very slowly and carefully. At the edges pink cream bubbled up, but it didn’t ruin the colors. Each slice showed the nice ombré effect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Can someone explain the phyllo sheets, do I really need SIXTEEN?! they are $7 for a box of two????

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: When I search on the Internet, I see some fairly expensive 2-sheet packages and also some relatively inexpensive packages with many "very thin" sheets. For example, Amazon sells a package containing 44 Kral Phyllo Dough sheets for $14.95.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This was beautiful, delicious and made the house smell so good! I used some Rao’s white sauce from the pantry as a hack. Same aromatics and spices. So Good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Well. This was a lot of work! I don’t mind peeling squash, beets, sweet potatoes etc but when you have to do ALL of them…and remember to stir the cream…etc etc.

Could not use phyllo due to a guest with celiac disease, so used gluten free pastry, and frankly it didn’t add much. Would not bother next time. Colors bled during the cooking; definitely not as spectacular as pictured.

Husband loved it, but then he had no clue how much work it was. It was tasty, but was it worth the effort? Eh.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Made this today. We skipped the phyllo layer and it still tasted great!

Took 2-2.5 hours to setup (with two ppl peeling and cutting) & the 1.5h of baking. Let it cool for 10min and it cut cleanly. Total ~3.5-4h

Ombré effect appeared cleanly everywhere excluding the perimeter, where the beet juice had transferred to.

Next time I’d do the following:
- cut&layer the veggies/cheese the night before (red potatoes day of)
- add another beet (only had 1.5 layers)
- use 5 red potatoes (had leftovers)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: In this video, a cook shows one way to make phyllo rosettes (different recipe but same idea, I believe): https://www.bonappetit.com/video/watch/from-the-home-kitchen-sam-makes-ruffled-milk-raspberry-pie I thought it was helpful to picture in advance how I would do this.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as described and it was absolutely delicious! However, aesthetically, it didn’t look as pictured. The cheese and cream curdled during baking and the red from the beets permeated up the sides. So it looked a bit gory actually…lol. Didn’t stop us from devouring it, but next time I’ll try a different cheese and stabilize the cream with flour/butter after infusing with the aromatics.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I made a substitution of 1/2 the amount of beets as we are not sure we like them and I used Pepperage Farm Puff Pastry instead of the phyllo(I was feeding teens and well why not). Loved the addition of the custard style cream! We will definitely make this again!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Did you attempt rosettes or just lay flat across the top?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: The flavors of this are excellent, however this baked for a full hour on Thanksgiving eve, then for another 30 minutes before adding the fillo rosettes, and the veggies were still crunchy in spots. I think this would benefit from an hour covered, followed by a second hour uncovered. Yes, the beets bleed upward. It was not pretty like the picture, but very tasty (where fully cooked).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: What a fantastic surprise. The texture and flavors were a really nice surprise, everyone at the table loved it. The recipe however is not the easiest to follow and could be greatly simplified. Essentially you make the creme sauce, slice the vegetable, toss them with salt and pepper, then layer the whole thing up starting with beets followed by cheese, sweet potatoes, cheese, squash, cheese, potatoes. The phyllo on top looks great but doesn't add to the flavor, I will probably skip it next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Note 2: I substituted salted butter (throughout entire recipe) and it really made the phyllo rosettes much tastier than other people are mentioning, but next time I might also sprinkle some garlic powder or something for added zing. Also, my ombre effect was definitely there but I think I should have gotten one more beet to make the layer bigger, as it was my smallest layer. I also added more garlic, and pureed the cream (after taking out bay leaves and peppercorns), instead of straining.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: One of the best vegetable dishes ever. I left out the phyllo entirely and it was delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I’m not going to lie, this was a lot of work. Being dense vegetables, cut at different widths, resulted in what felt like slightly undercooked and crisper than I’d anticipated. I almost missed this step, but the red potatoes are supposed to go into the sauce to start to cook them even though they are listed as one of the first veggies to slice. The darkest beets are the bottom layer and result in a beautiful color, but also stain and bubble over in hot pink rivulets.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it was an overwhelming success. It took a while but it was absolutely worth every second. The only change I made was for some reason my store grocery didn’t have enough red beets, so I supplemented them with a layer of golden beets under the red potatoes. Incredibky good and impressive.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it turned out beautifully and delicious. My only change was that for some reason my grocery was low on red beets so I added a layer of golden beets under the red potatoes which added a nice color to the ombré. Time consuming, but worth it for a special occasion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was a nightmare from start to almost finish. To be fair, it is tasty, though the sauce and the gruyere is really all you taste. The rest is textured layers. My husband and I are both experienced cooks. We decided to do the prep the day before, and the finishing on Thanksgiving day. While the mandolin-ing of the veggies took a lot of time, it was the shingling in the pan that was tedious. I have a lot more to say, but have run out of words. What a disappointment.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Very pretty! I don’t eat eggs, so subbed 1 box of silken tofu, puréed and added in with cream. Skip whole peppercorns and just grind pepper into cream. Then, lift out the bay leaves/thyme sprigs before pouring. I used 1 layer beet followed by a layer of purple sweet potatoes at the bottom. In the future, I’d skip the beets and stick with an all-sweet potato/potato gratin to have the color without worrying about color bleeding through. Mandoline is a must.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
This was a smash hit at Thanksgiving. Thanks for the great recipe!

A couple of notes:
- Don’t skip soaking the potatoes in the cream. I did and they didn’t cook through as well as the layers below.
- I read a caution that beet juice can contaminate the colors of the other layers, so used two purple sweet potatoes instead. Worked beautifully!
- Lots of Qs about rosettes: when you stack your buttered sheets, pinch softly at the center of the rectangle and twist clockwise to form a rosette.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Big hit with the fam. Used a food processor, which was a lifesaver. I  tried to salt the beet slices (like you would for eggplant parm) to reduce beet juices, but still bubbled up a bit.  I did not have 3 cups of cream after straining, so had to supplement. I baked through Step 8, refrigerated overnight, and finished next day. It was spectacular and absolutely delicious - all vegetables were perfectly cooked, and the flavors really sang. To my surprise, it was much better reheated the next day!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
NYT! Y'all need threaded replies! This is silly. 

Fantomina, I totally agree. This recipe, which sounds and looks delicious, lost me at "shingle them on top of the cheese-topped beets in 2 to 3 even layers." 

I would totally cook this, but it's rather comical.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: The vegetables have varied cooking times so I needed half or full hour more oven time. Might use convection setting next time. DO cover the pan when cooking, my potato layer dried out. Though I had some bleeding up from the beets (might try purple sweet potatoes?), it was not so bad and my eaters loved the flavors. Make sure your mandolin is VERY sharp, knives VERY sharp...though I will likely use food processor next time! Make 1-2 days ahead then warm up and add the phyllo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: For the phyllo rosettes: The two phyllo layers are flat on the counter. Squeeze them together from top to bottom so that they form a rod with fan-like folds. Twirl the rod into a round shape. No need to be fastidious about it. Give it a carefree pouf, fluff it up, use your imagination. It will work! It's the easiest part of this recipe and an inspiring way to top the gratin. Phyllo, who would have thought? To be eaten right away otherwise, the phyllo tends to get soggy -- that's in its nature.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: There’s always that one completely impractical recipe you bookmark a month before a holiday dinner and then foolishly insist on proceeding with even after you read the comments! “And I won’t back down.” So, I par cooked the vegetables, and roasted a head of garlic. I baked for 30 minutes and I will re-heat it tomorrow. I am going to make the phyllo rosettes using these instructions: https://smittenkitchen.com/2018/05/ruffled-milk-pie/

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
I love the look of this dish, but anything that takes 3 hours and requires 19 (!) ingredients automatically drops to the bottom of my priority list.

Now if I were a restaurant chef with sous-chefs and preppers at my command I would definitely give this time-and labor-intensive recipe a try.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Use yellow beets if you’re afraid red are too strong.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe is delicious, beautiful, and unique… but it also takes an insane amount of time to cook and prepare. In all, it took about six hours of mostly active prep time, rather than the three that were promised.

As far as tips go, I followed the hint in the comments to briefly the beets in cold water to remove excess beet juices and maintain the ombré look and feel. With that, the ombré came out perfectly. Thanks for the hint!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Beautiful way to impress your dinner friends!! 
Amazing flavors!! 
Followed the instructions exactly and wouldn’t change anything next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
I made this for Thanksgiving, due to the uncertainty of who was going to be vegan at the time.

It was an hours long process, but using the food processor really helped. I wouldn’t do it by hand. Others have mentioned that the ombre effect is lost to the beet juices. 

I faked the rosettes, and they turned out fine.  

Finally, to our surprise, the dish actually tasted better reheated a couple of days later so I would recommend that if you have time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Agree with warnings below about the beets. They are difficult to cut even with a mandoline. They create a ton of juice that makes the reveal look like a bloodbath. And everything will taste like beets. Next time I would skip them entirely and go with purple sweet potatoes. Or maybe do one VERY thin layer only, basically cutting the amount of beets called for in the recipe in half. 

Another note. Read this recipe 10x all the way through. Print it out if you can. It’s a long haul.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made this with leftover/frozen beets & squash. Pre-cooked thinly sliced potatoes in cream sauce 30 minutes, don't think they would have baked through otherwise. Everything came together nicely except for phyllo dough at end. Found instructions inadequate to obtain desired effect per the picture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This is extremely high-sodium. Is there a way to cut the salt without losing flavor?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I prepared this for a dinner party last Thanksgiving 2022.  It was A LOT of work.  The ombre effect was not as pronounced because the purple beet juice  bubbled up into the next layer and the potatoes didn't cook as thoroughly as they should have.  The rosettes looked beautiful but a bit undercooked - they were doughy.  Needs more salt or some other seasoning because it was a little bland. BUT it definitely stole the show.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I veganized this by making a miso/flax mixture in place of the egg yolks

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This is stunning at the table and really beautiful. But tastes ok, very bland. The sauce is just fattening and not delicious. I won’t make it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I've been wondering if I could just substitute canned condensed for the half-and-half. This recipe looks as though it is over-the-top rich.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made with a gluten free topping instead of phyllo?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Why would you think that a different topping would interfere with the recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made this last year for Thanksgiving. So delicious and beautiful but extremely time consuming and labor intensive. New favorite side-dish though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: The irony is that the key to making this vegetarian dish with ease is a meat slicer. It makes quick work of the veggies without sacrificing a finger. However you do it, it’s so worth the work.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it was delicious!  I followed the directions exactly and read through all of the notes for tips.  The layering came out perfectly.  It was quite a project and I’m not sure I will make it again but it was well worth it for our feast.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Tasty and looks pretty, but not worth the extra work over regular potatoes gratin.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving, it was delicious! It  takes a good 3 hours to assemble, and that was using cheese that was already grated. I do think the phyllo topping was a bit too much, next time I will use another light layer of cheese.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Perfect!  I blanched the beets and that seemed to deal with the color issue; assembled the day before using the 'strata method' suggested in another tip. 
The phyllo was easy to handle, and 'roses' were easy to make (and i'm not good at decorating). Ended up resting about 45 minutes.  Looked great and tasted even better!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Wondering how this worked.  Did you slice the beets before you blanched?  I pre-roasted my beets this morning, 60 minutes at 400F, then let cool for several hours before the mandoline.  The cream sauce is beautiful, but quite red.  Wondering if there is an alternative.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I followed the suggestions to put the beets in ice water, and other thoughts on how to keep the beet juice and color from bubbling up. It just didn't work for me. I love all these root vegetables but in the end, I didn't like it and I'm not sure why but without the beets, I think it would have been improved.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Steamed each veg in the microwave until just soft enough to comfortably go through the mandoline (3 to 5 min.). Rinsed beets until the water was pale. Used lactose free half & half.  Striations worked. Use a glass dish next time. Assembled the day before thru adding the sauce.  Fridged overnight; brought to room temp (2-3 hrs).  Added phyllo and baked day of.  Not difficult, just fidgety.  Making the sauce, put peppercorns in bag and use the strained shallots for soup.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Any ideas for a gluten free alternative to the Phyllo dough?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Perhaps trying gluten free bread or rolls ground in a food processor to make a topping. Or contact the cookingcare email address listed for assistance.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: 1. Make ahead: Treat like a breakfast strata. Prep all veggies & cheese then top with sauce (half way through step 8). Cover tightly with plastic wrap, refrigerate overnight. Let come to room temp before baking.  2. Sub sauce with pureed cauliflower, saute shallots & garlic, simmer with a bay leaf. Add 1-2T white/yellow miso for depth. 3. Soak beets in cold water before assembly to reduce color bleeding. 4. Reduce salt. 5. Skip egg yolks, use potato starch instead.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: For any Australians, shallots are the tiny onions and not spring onions. I made the mistake and had to run to shops...

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Better the 2nd day.  The flavor is better with time to develop!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Food processors work just as well as mandolines or fancy knives at making super-thin slices, with less likelihood of a trip to the ER.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Quite tasty -- rich and a bit more flavorful than an all-potato gratin. The one tip I have for future cooks is to plan the baking dish in advance. I prepared ingredients, grabbed the nearest pan, and then my prepared veggies didn't make the right number of layers. If I'd had extra, or a different dish, I'd have gotten better visuals.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I really really am not a fan of beets---though I see here the contribution made by their lovely colour. Anyone have an idea for a substitution?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: @Yvonne maybe roasted red peppers?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
I made this and stuck with the recipe. It took me 1 1/2 hours to prep and 1 1/2 hours to bake. 
The tastes were delicious, blending together, yet the taste of each different vegetable came through. Next time I will try to substitute something else for the potato layer, maybe Japanese sweet potatoes. They are light colored. The potato layer was the least favorable in my opinion.

Also, it even tastes better on the next day.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Nope. Followed it to a T. Won’t make it again. The beets dyed everything red.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This would be so gorgeous hasselback style, anyone think that would work or are the veggies too inconsistent?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This looks amazing! I wonder if the potato can be substituted with yucca or rutabaga for those of us with nightshade allergies?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Could you skip the beets?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions for substitutes for the alliums? We have a guest who requires a low FODMAP diet ( no garlic, onions or leeks)

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I would just substitute a good herb blend for flavor - like fresh thyme, parsley, thyme, or whatever you like.  You could also use Bouquet Garni or a seasoning blend that you like that doesn't have garlic, onions or leeks.  I do that when I cook for my FODMAP guest.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Since the onion and garlic are strained out, it should be safe: as I understand it, the sugars are not fat-soluable.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I'm so confused about the salting instructions preceding the recipe. When is "later"?  When to salt the potatoes and the beets? After cooking?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: The recipe says you may slice "before you start cooking", i.e. before Step 1. You would add the salt in Step 4 (potatoes) and Step 5 (beets).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I absolutely, 100% hate beets as does half my family. Would just skipping them ruin the recipe? Any alternates someone could suggest (thinking maybe rutabaga)? Any suggestions would be appreciated.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I did this and used my portable smoker (4 min under the dome...battery operated cold smoke infuser) on the gratin before adding the phyllo. Excellent!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Would it be possible to substitute the "ready to use" phyllo sheets and skip the butter/rosette making part?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This looks great. But, for my beet-averse vegetarian wife, would purple carrots do for the bottom layer? Are there other red/purple veggies I can use instead? This looks too good to pass up, but I do want her to eat it. Any suggestions?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: How do 8 rosettes at 5 1/2 inches diameter fit in a 9 by 13 pan?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I thought the same thing, but squishing maybe? two rows approx 11" squished into 9", then 4 rows approx 16" into 13"?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
I like JT’s idea of using purple carrots, but remember you’d get two colors since they’re orange inside. I think purple potatoes would need some help like the other potatoes do, so you’d have to put them in separate cream to keep all the cream from turning pink, perhaps? That sounds like even more work. 

I actually love beets but they don’t appeal to me in this recipe. 

Maybe hand blender shallots and garlic?

I’m happy to hear that the person who actually made this said it was good!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I’m vegetarian but don’t like beets. Suggestions for replacement that will hold up to the other root veggies?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Substitution for beets? Suggestions?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Someone suggested purple potatoes and said it worked.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: The phyllo dough rosettes instructions seem vague…I’m scared.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Can I leave out the beets?  I hate them.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Dear Jeff,
You must leave them out. I don't like beets, but I thought a little layer of them should not be a problem. It was - every other vegetable smelled and tasted beets. Honestly, I don't think I tasted anything other than beets in this gratin.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This took a lot of effort, but I love root veggies and squash. I would make this again. I had to use frozen pastry dough and made a “quilt” of open shapes they crisped nicely and the dish looked really fun. My major recommendation is to cut by half the salt at each layer of veggies.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I have a guest who is not a fan of squash, could I substitute carrots sliced long ways? Or rutabagas?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: What if you didn't tell them there was squash in it? With all the cheese and cream sauce, it appears to me that the vegetables are more about their color than their flavor. This looks dramatic but I wonder how the individual flavors of the vegetables would hold up and imagine that they kind of blend.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: A lot of people are asking about the rosettes. There's a video on Instagram.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: What's the point of the leftover cream? As a sauce you serve at the table? I don't get that one piece of the recipe

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: See Steps 12 and 13. The leftover cream sauce is reheated and served at the table.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this? Wondering how the left overs hold up? Does it turn to mush or keep its structure? When we make spanakopita we have several days of leftovers and can’t imagine this would go any quicker in our half veg timid household!!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Do the egg yolks in the extra sauce get cooked just by reheating or are they left uncooked? I prefer not to consume raw eggs.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Hey Lauren! I got you. The phyllo dough purchased in the store should list how many sheets are in there. Check for the quantity written on the box. The brands I’ve used have about 20 sheets per roll, and two rolls per box. Hope this helps!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I was thinking purple potatoes for the darkest layer….Can you use other root vegetables like parsnip or turnips for the light layer?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Could this be made ahead, or at least some of the steps done ahead? Which ones? Will be pressed for time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Yes, Jen, you can make absolutely this ahead of time without compromising the flavor. I reheated it the next day for guests - everyone loved it. I skipped the filo rosettes because I wasn't able to find filo at the supermarket - it was visually stunning even without them because of the array of fall colors and no one missed them.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Am here for the ombré but why all the phyllo dough? Has anyone made it and thinks the phyllo really makes it extra tasty? Otherwise am proceeding without all the fuss.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I’ve got some fussy eaters (aka kids) and need to come up with substitutes for the beets and squash. Any suggestions (I know that substitutions may affect the ombré effect)?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Is it possible to make this in a springform pan to highlight the layers?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Only if you have a leak proof springform. How about a glass dish instead? You‘ll see the colors when serving.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Phylo was out of stock at the store but I have puff pastry sheets. Would that work (perhaps not as rosettes)?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: The portions sound like a LOT. Almost 6 pounds of veg + cheese etc - planning to make 1/3 of it. Someone wanted to leave out the eggs; did the eggs curdle, please?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Loved this dish. The beets are barely distinguishable. Our munchkin gobbled everything down.

I only dislike peeling butternut squash. I used a mandolin for slicing and it was done in a bliss.

But my beets produced lots of liquid. Any idea how to avoid it?

The filo dough rosettes looked cool but next time I think I can do with fried bread crumbs.

Prep time 2 about 1,5hrs (all by hand) plus cooking time. Very doable. Thanks so much for this show stopper recipe.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this twice, it’s very delicious. My ombré effect didn’t turn out like the picture either time due to the liquid turning red from the beets and bubbling up during cooking. I didn’t peel the beets, and it was perfectly fine. My mandolin worked like a dream to slice everything. I use the leftover butternut squash to make bacon and butternut squash soup

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I don’t think the level of work needed for this pays off in the end. I think if I did this again I wouldn’t strain the cream - of course would pull out the bay leaves and probably use ground pepper. The color is very cool but it was a PITA to make

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Huge production. Did a test today for turkey day. Taking advice and using purple potatoes instead of beets. Beet juice took over. Lost the ombré because of that. Will add more nutmeg next time. 
Did taste delicious!
Love the idea of adding parsnips as well.
Used a food processor to slice. Need to go a little thinner next time!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I followed the specifics of this and it took less time than advertised. Outstanding flavors and texture. Absolutely delish. I found the advice on waiting before cutting to be spot on. And the next morning it was even easier to slice and portion, like a good lasagna.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: No no no. This recipe does not take 3 hours.  It takes at least 3 hours to prepare.  I am still in phase 2.  Had to go and buy more Gruyère.  1.5 cups three times does not add up to what the recipe called for.  Lots of salt between layers as well.  We shall see.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made mid way through step 8 last night to save some time today. Realized my grocery delivery subbed puff pastry instead of Phyllo. Any thoughts on this as a sub? I’m thinking the rosettes won’t work.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I followed Hut's make ahead plan and prepped all vegetables, cheese, cream sauce and assembled according to instructions, sealed tightly with plastic wrap and put in refrigerator. So far, so good. Except that, overnight, my top layer of potatoes have oxidized. Hoping that the phyllo sheets will camouflage the brown looking spuds.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: If you make ahead be sure to push the plastic wrap against the top layer. I sealed but didn't push down and my potatoes turned brown!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Hi everyone. A very Good Samaritan shared this link but it is a little farther down and I had to scroll awhile to find it — for everyone asking about the phyllo rosettes, here is a video showing how they’re done: https://twitter.com/nytimes/status/1592978366281703424?s

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Prep is crucial! So many components in delicate balance requires you to have everything ready for assembly before you start.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I was nervous to make this from all the comments about the time suck but it wasn’t nearly as bad as expected! Slicing the veggies was the most complicated thing about it! Turned out beautifully, rich and delicious with the soft veggies and crunchy phyllo. Well worth the time to make it!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Fantastic, and great fun to make.  The result is something like a very refined pot pie, but obviously more substantive.  The kids and I loved it (and the kids had a thrill learning to use the mandoline).

My only note is that when making the phyllo rosettes, the twice-layered sheets get squashed together and then, when baking, absorb liquid in a way that makes the dough gummy.  Next time I'll use just one sheet of phyllo to make my rosettes, keep the crunch but lose the doughy part

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: So delicious and truly a vegetarian show stopper. Love that it’s such a celebration of fall veg. Lessons were learned! Will def mandoline all the veg except beets and red potatoes day before as this recipe is a bear to prep and needs 2 hrs to cook &cool. Don’t sweat the rosettes. Just scrunch and spin and they will look pretty. This final dish weighs about 50 lbs and fed a hungry crowd of 8 with plenty of leftovers! Leftovers are a beautiful, yummy mess.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Making the sliced potatoes and squash so thin I had way way too much of them for double layers. 

I think I could have made about 5!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Two things. One, this recipe definitely needs a video to show the technique for stacking the vegetables and layering the Phyllo on top. Two, I thought this was way too much work for a gratin. I will not be repeating this anytime soon or ever.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Back again. I just realized I went about making this the wrong way, so will try again. Also, the leftovers tasted fantastic the next day!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Very time consuming, delicious. won't make again but glad I did.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: It’s ironic that the only reason I took on this vegetarian dish is that I own a meat slicer. It made quick accurate work of slicing the hard potatoes, squash and beets. Had I tried to do this with a knife or mandolin, I would have ended up in the ER. That said, I did make it and it was amazing and so worth it. I’d offer one note on the phyllo dough, because how they’re listed in the ingredient list doesn’t make it clear when you go to purchase: one 16oz package of Athens phyllo dough will work.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I was challenged to make this using a non-cow milk cheese due to a guest with allergies. With input from my local cheese monger, I substituted Ossau-Iraty which is made from sheep milk and has a somewhat mealty quality. The dish produced the hoped for oohs and ahs for its looks and tasted great as well. Definitely a time-consuming project but worth the effort for a special occasion meal. I’d suggest cutting smaller pieces and 12 is an odd number given the 8 rosettes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made this on Thanksgiving day and it’s definitely work.  Luckily, this was the the only dish I was responsible for.  A good solid 90 minutes of prep.  I didn’t have phyllo and use puff instead which worked just fine.  Cooling time was super important.  I’m not a big beet fan so I only did one layer and it was just the right amount.  It came out pretty (not picture worthy pretty but still pretty).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: My 10oz of Gruyère did only yielded 3 cups grated. :( Might want to buy extra. A lot of work but really impressive and delicious!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Visually beautiful but only okay tasting.  I will not be making this again

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Do not miss the 20-30 minute rest of the gratin when it comes out of the oven! It allows the beet juices to settle and the ombré effect is stunning.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I think this recipe is not as difficult as many would make it out to be. While there are many steps they are basically the same thing four times.  Ours looked a lot like the image provided and the taste was good. In my opinion the phyllo dough was an odd addition. I would have added a savory streusel instead.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
As Alexa said, this project requires quite a bit of focus and time. I made the cream the day before which was a help. That step probably took 45 minutes. 
A mandolin is a must. Cutting all those veggies with a knife would be beyond most peoples skill and patience.
Having said that, when complete the dish is beautiful and delicious. I made as the main entree for a vegetarian Thanksgiving and got raves.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This was my favorite Thanksgiving cooking project of all time and my guests all found it spectacular. I hand cut the veggies without peeling, used a thin layer of purple beets at the bottom to avoid too much color bleed, then used yellow beets in place of butternut to gorgeous effect. I subbed cauliflower crème thanks to others’ comments and it was decadent! Can’t wait for leftovers!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Not 1. I made it for Thanksgiving, exactly as it was written.  It took me 2 1/2 hours to go from Step 1 through Step 8.  You get a 20 minutes break while the cream cooks and then once it's in the oven you get 40 minutes while it bakes and before you prep the phyllo.  I scrunched the phyllo like paper, but made it too tight, I made 10 balls and they weren't enough to cover the top and tasted dry and blah.  I would use salted butter and loosely scrunch them next time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Note 2. I did not use all the potatoes or butternut squash, but next time would put in a deeper 13x9 pan so I could use it all.  It is BEAUTIFUL and delicious and pleasant to make if that is all you have to do for 4 hours (which is exactly how long it all took.) We served it room temp and was delish, and imagine it even better when hot.  I did paper towel dry the beets a bit so they wouldn't bleed into all the layers and they didn't, we had the full ombre effect!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Make sure you have a deep dish 9x13 when cooking this recipe! The layers exceed the height of a regular depth dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Agree with most comments --lovely flavor and the phyllo added nice contrasting texture. But A LOT of work and not much of an Ombre effect - only a shadow, so to speak.  I don't see any way for the beets to not color everything, but perhaps I'm missing something.  
I am happy to try a recipe for an impact, but there must be easier ways to show off!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made this as a Thanksgiving side dish and everyone raved about it! Came out so pretty. I substituted rutabaga for potatoes to give it an extra kick. Took me about 4.5 hrs start to finish, but I'm not the fastest cook. The rosettes confused me at first, so my tip would be to gather from the bottom right corner and then scrunch and wind upwards into a swirl for best results.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
I thought this prep would be the death of me, but it was THE HIT of dinner. I made it pretty much exactly as instructed and it was divine. You need the beets!! Yes, on the bottom. No, they don’t taint the whole dish, tho they are juicy. The rosettes aren’t as hard as you’d think—you literally do just bunch it up like you would tissue paper. My 18-yr-old son did them! (And most of the chopping, thank god.)
Oh, I DID completely forget the saved out cream sauce and it was great!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it was a hit! For me though - I found I had too many veggies and not enough cheese. I used a food processor to grate the cheese and slice the veg but it still took me 4 hours to prep everything before I got this in the oven. The phyllo was not a success - the sheets sort of disintegrated into shavings but I buttered them and layered them on top anyway and it sort of worked. I'd make this again but only for a very special occasion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This was a HIT! I improvised on the rosettes: take sheet of phyllo, fold in half, create a cone shape, twist down to form a flower. Very simple. Brush with loads of butter.  I used the cauliflower cream, great idea! Even with the juicy beets I still had beautiful differentiated layers. You could easily make a day or two ahead.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This is a delicious recipe and was a hit at our dinner table. But I didn't have phyllo, so I topped it with a straight sheet of puff pastry from Trader Joe's and some black and white sesame seeds for color. Way less hassle than those inexplicable phyllo rosettes. I served it without the additional cream sauce. The dish has enough richness as it is.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This is my first time leaving a review here after 6? years as a subscriber. This dish is exceptional and beautiful. Prep was a breeze with a mandolin. Most time-consuming part was peeling veggies and shredding all that cheese - this is a great recipe to include an eager helper. I was nervous the cheese would overpower the veg but it all came together perfectly. Will absolutely make again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Frozen puff pastry is not the same as phyllo. Puff usually comes as two sheets only. Phyllo is made from oil, not butter and usually comes as about 16 very thin sheets.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
It’s good but I would say not worth the effort. 
I made it all the way to step 8 as flagged in notes, refrigerated and then did phyllo and cooked the next day. I used a mandolin as flagged in tips, I could not imagine making this without. My sauce was thicker than the sauce in the NYT video, which I think then made it harder for my sweet potatoes to cook all the way. My sauce boiled over, I had to switch pots midway, which I think affected cream texture.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: It was a fun project to make. Too me about 2 hours to do the prep and get the dishes in the oven. I didn’t have a deep dish so I used two regular Pyrex dishes. It looked and tasted good but I’m sure the ombré effect would have been more pronounced in a deep dish that is 9 x 13. I didn’t add salt to the squash, sweet potatoes or beets since I felt that the potatoes and the cream had enough salt. For the phyllo rosettes, I bunched it at the bottom and swirled so it looked like a flower.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This recipe required effort, but tasted fine although I experienced some issues. First, the potatoes on the top layer were not immersed in the cream so they discolored when baked and/or were overcooked. Second, the instructions said to use two sheets of phyllo per rosette, but they were much too bulky. I used one sheet each—my sheets may have been larger than what was in the original recipe. And, third, I had the same issue as many others with the beets coloring everything.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This took about 4 hours in total, and you really need 2 people to make that work. I would suggest mandolining the veggies before anything else so that they are ready to go and the juices drain a bit. I don’t know if i could say that this one was worth the effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I would not bother with this recipe again. I love to buy high-quality ingredients but the cumulative cost of all that fancy cheese, cream, butter, peppercorns, etc. have to make this the most expensive vegetarian dish I've ever prepared--all that, just for vegetable slices simmered in beet juice! Everything looked and tasted like beets, and yes, I did rest it before serving. My guests all said they liked it but they're relatives who *would* say that, and there was no rush on the leftovers.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This was stunning! It was quite the production: I made two batches: one as directed, and a smaller, dairy-free one. We loved the flavors in the cream sauce ( I added a bit more nutmeg) and the colored layer effect. We even liked the phyllo dough on top and wouldn't think of ditching that part.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made this over Thanksgiving weekend - a triumphant veggie main! The beet juice did bubble up during baking, but when we cut into it, the ombre affect was perfect. Maybe it's because we used an oat creamer, but the extra sauce for serving was too watery to use (even with the egg yolks). The final dish didn't need more sauce, so next time I would start with three cups of cream and reduce down to two cups to avoid extras.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Although beautiful and good tasting, the work involved with this recipe far outweighs the reward. Advice from my daughter and I: skip it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: 1) Make in the morning, and let it sit for at least 5-6 hours before serving. 2) Plan for more grated cheese than what is in recipe. 3) Don't forget to throw the potato slices into the cream while it heats up.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Cost Savings: I cut costs by using a gruyere/cheddar blend at krogers at $2.50/ 12oz, also found at trader Joe's. I used ground pepper instead of peppercorns. I used a blend of Half & Half with milk, instead of full H& H. I estimate it was $4 per serving when all was said and done. It came out tasting perfect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Spectacular. We had no phyllo, so we made a decorative crust of leaves and flowers from pie dough. There's no getting around it; this recipe is labor intensive, but perfect for a vegetarian center-stage dish on Thanksgiving.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Lots of prep work but absolutely delicious! Makes a wonderful leftover too! I could not find the rosette tutorial so I buttered two sheets of phyllo together and scrunched them in my hand. 😀

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: The color from the beets mixed with the liquids in the dish during cooking and dyed everything a deep red. No ombré effect whatsoever. Next time I will try the purple sweet potato suggestion. Will also reduce the amount of salt.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
We made this gorgeous recipe for Thanksgiving this year. It was delicious and actually made many of the typical side dishes unnecessary to prepare as it contains winter squash, sweet potatoes, potatoes, and beets

However it took 3 of us to prepare this very complicated recipe. Whew!  It refrigerated and reheated beautifully so over a 3 day period four of us ate the entire enormous dish

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, but I tried to make it for the looks and failed miserably. I need a food stylist, apparently.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Just a note regarding vegetarian dishes and cheese:many cheeses as most dishes are 
Gruyère is , are made with animal rennet. If you take such thing seriously, then you’ll want to read the label of your cheese or ask the cheese monger what type of  rennet is used in making the
cheese.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made this, and yes, it’s beautiful. The ombré was gorgeous. But, for the time it takes I’m still not convinced it’s worth the effort. It’s a substantial vegetarian main dish, pretty to look at. Way too much effort to ever be considered a side dish. Also needs a more pronounced flavor profile, so add some extra fresh herbs and be sure to salt it adequately. Fontina and beets..? Still undecided.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Haven’t cooked this, but got to try it at a family Thanksgiving, and it was underwhelming. The beet flavor was predominant, and the layers hadn’t all cooked to the same texture, so it was a weird mix of crunchy and mushy. The family member who made it was so disappointed. No one went back for seconds.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I wanted to make this complicated dish, and looked to the Notes to other's experiences with the recipe.   I appreciate the useful notes from Deejay and Raymo, who commented on their experiences after preparing the recipe.  It is so much work to have it go wrong in the end!   Based on the comment of watery beets, I salted the beets and squash overnight before assembling (rinsing off the salt before).  No success on rosettes, but it came out very beautiful - thanks for the helpful notes!

author: Alexa Weibel
