recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely magnificent and easier than it sounds.The beets are a wonderful  and such a smart, yer overlooked ingredient! They did not make everything "pink" - just a lot of wonderful flavor. A must for wintry delights and definitely not just for Thanksgiving....

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Amazing flavors. Needed to let the beets salt longer too much juice came to the top while baking.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Could a person evenly chop the veggies instead of slicing?  It won't look as pretty but would it work?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: We made this for Thanksgiving dinner and would simplify it for the future, It is basically scalloped potatoes with veggies replacing most of the potatoes. We used a mandolin but found the vegetables were at various levels of softness because they cook at different time. We laughed at the end as we dabbed paper towels all around - what recipe ends with this as a final step? This was too ambitious to add to an already full menu but would be fun to try again on another occasion.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Read step nine. A rosette is round shaped, with the approximation of the petals of a flower.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: A video would be very helpful.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I like the flavors in this, but next time I’ll cover it for the first part of baking. Some of the veggies were cooked perfectly, others could have used more time. I put the beets on the bottom. I didn’t have room in my casserole for the squash!  Maybe next time I’ll just roast all the veggies together and top with a little cream sauce!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I am Whole Food Plant Based Vegan, as many other people are.  I don't use any dairy products- no milk, cream, or butter.   I also use no animal products, eg. eggs and cheese.  Why don't you make something that beautiful with alternatives?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
It seems to me that the gruyère functions as a barrier between the veggies that prohibits the bleeding of colors. I’d love this as a vegetarian man/starchy side dish for a group that includes vegetarians and carnivores but am a bit skeptical about all the cheese ( barrier functionality non-withstanding). 
Upwards bleeding of the darker colors may also be due to the liquids boiling up. Maybe reduce temperature?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: There is a short reel on their instagram page which shows how to make the phyllo rosettes

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: How did folks plate this? I’m serving a vegan version at a cocktail party and it’s not looking super impressive in the dish. Just slice and plate it, does it stay together relatively well? Thanks and happy holidays!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This was a wonderful dish, and much appreciated by vegetarian family members.  I did have some trouble with the beet juice leeching, but otherwise very pleased.  I made a trial 1/2 recipe first, to see if I could master it.  Despite the intimidating length of the instructions, it wasn’t hard at all - although it did inspire me to get my first mandolin. I’ll try again with purple sweet potatoes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: My family loved this! I forgot to add the egg yolks to the cream sauce- was fine and very tasty. As well, I toasted panko with half mince garlic clove and butter and sprinkled that on top in place of phyllo rosettes(which would have been lovely if I had phyllo! )

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Continued from previous comment: well, I made it again because last time, after hours of hard work, I didn’t even get to try the cursed ombre gratin because it hadn’t cooled by the time the gathering was ENDING. So my hosts saved it for the next night.

I thought I could do it this time. I knew what to expect. But no. In the first two minutes, I mandolined off part of my thumb instead of a red potato. I had to go to urgent care because I was bleeding everywhere. I got a tourniquet and… TK

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Show-stopper of a dish. But I'd do some things different next time:
1. Food processor: easy work, but would use 3rd thinnest setting. Finest setting was too thin and it was so much work to assemble
2. Par-bake veggies: used layer of purple sweet potatoes on the bottom which was my favorite, followed by beets. Everything on top of that wasn't cooked. Place orange sweet potatoes, squash, potatoes between sheet pans, season, low heat to cook through and then assemble
3. Phyllo: was done quickly

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Do I refreigerate before or after baking if I am making this ahead ?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
I cooked this as a side dish for Christmas Eve, and it was a success - delicious, smooth and comforting. I used varieties of purple, orange, sweet and white potatoes - the purple wasn't as clear and beautiful as in your picture, but the ombre effect was visible. Naturally, this dish was even better the next day.
A couple of notes: I cooked the dish for one hour with an aluminium foil cover to ensure all the potatoes were cooked correctly, and I also replaced the gruyére with vintage cheddar.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: PS - it was much more frustrating to keep converting all the different measures to the metric system than to assemble the dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: To make this dairy free, I subbed Earth Balance for the butter, dairy free coconut cream   oat milk for the half and half, and used Whole Foods dairy free shredded mozzarella between the layers (could also leave out the cheese). It turned out amazing! Took at least 3 hours prep time, if not more, so definitely only do this if you’re looking for a project.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This is a major undertaking but well worth it. I needed 5 cups of light cream and used slightly less veggies to fit into a 7.5" x 9.5" baking dish (high sides). I used both single and double phyllo sheets to make different size rosettes. The Phyllo cook time was more like 40 minutes to brown. I was slightly skeptical about the beets but they are a welcome sweet offset to the root veggies and add a festive colorful layer. I made it for Christmas day but it would make a brilliant Thanksgiving meal

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, and will make again with minor changes... use puff pastry instead of phyllo (too fussy and not much flavour). Potatoes could be par-cooked in the cream although there's risk of over cooking.   The egg yolk didn't thicken the cream sauce. Rewriting the instructions to eliminate the repetition would be helpful.... slice all the veg, layer alternately with cheese.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Incredible dish... Took a long time to prepare, but well worth the effort. And, reheated leftovers the next day & was equally delish (just covered the top with foil). Terrific vegetarian main with a nice salad with mustard vinaigrette.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Such good company to a ham roast. Delicious! PSA: locate your mandoline pusher before you begin. If you can’t find it, don’t proceed. You’re not that coordinated and you’ll be slicing a lot of awkward root vegetables - you will get tired. If you have distracting children or pets, send them out of the kitchen for this part of the recipe, you need your full attention to the matter at hand (ha ha). Your dominate index finger will thank you.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
This was good, but I can’t say it was worth the tremendous amount of effort involved. I think of how easy it is to makes Pommes Anna, which I actually prefer. I can’t imagine preheating the oven at the beginning of this process either, as it takes hours. 

I stupidly forgot to thaw the phyllo in time, so I resorted to using panko tossed with olive oil and a little butter instead. Not only was it much easier, but I think I would probably prefer this to the phyllo.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made this.  Beet coloring took over and cheese got a bit lost.  Tasty, though.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Very time consuming, and took at least an hour longer to cook than the recipe advised.  Very dry - consider doubling the bechemel.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Sliced the beets, squash and sweet potatoes the day before and seasoned them.  Put in the fridge. Used Yukon Gold potatoes instead of red. Don't bother separating thyme from stems - just throw in 8-10 hefty sprigs. Recipe needed more than 10 oz. of Gruyere - measure out the 4 cups - don't go by weight.  Bake the gratin for an hour and fifteen minutes and THEN put the phyllo dough rosettes on top.  Mine were way too dark after 30 minutes, but I needed that extra time to get the gratin tender.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This was good. But it wasn't worth the hours of prep that it necessitates, IMO. I am, however, very glad that I bought a mandoline!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! But VERY LABOR INTENSIVE! Made this for an early supper for football halftime with friends. Still working on it when friends arrived! Next time I will:  1. Slice the squash, sweet potato, and beets the night before - store in separate containers.  2. Dice the shallots the night before.  3.  Grate the cheese the night before.  4.  Surprisingly, the beets were hard to slice when slicing pole to pole.  Cut the beets in half along the equator.  Rave reviews - so I'll do again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This recipe isn't one I'd make again.  The flavors of the squash, yams, beets and gruyere didn't meld very well.  The beets wanted to take over, the sweetness of the yams clashed up against the rest of the vegetables.  I think it was one of those conceptual innovations that's likely to make it to the cover of a cooking magazine, but not all that good to eat.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Our main issue with this is that the potatoes on the top were pretty raw even with switching to convection bake halfway through. The rest of the stuff was somewhere between "done" and "very done." We also had leftover potato and sweet potato that didn't fit in the pan, and only one scant layer of beets. So maybe buy an extra beet or two for max visual effect.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Incredibly time consuming and complicated for only an Instagram worthy dish. Flavor is simply cheese and vegetables. Spending precious time working on this rather than with my family was not worthwhile for the outcome.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This was a very tasty recipe, but I don't understand how you could make this without the beets leaking their color through the rest of the dish. I rinsed my sliced beets in water several times to try to get rid of some color, then drying well and salting before laying, and I still ended up with a lot of beet-red liquid at the end. I may try this again with carrots or parsnips instead of beets

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Hi, I made this for a holiday gathering.  It tasted great, but the cream curdled in the oven so it did not look so good.  Any solutions to that??

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: https://www.instagram.com/reel/CoIvHBuMIdG/?igshid=Zjc2ZTc4Nzk= If you hollow the link you can see how the rosette is formed

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions for making this vegan?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Miso/flax mixture. 1tsp light miso. 1 Tbsp ground flax and enough water to make a runny paste. About 3 Tbsp

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Would slicing the veges in a food processor work?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I can't imagine that the slicing method would have any effect on the outcome of the recipe. The slices should be 1/8" thick.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Could I put a sheet of phyllo between beets and the rest to soak up beet juice and prevent it from colouring the entire dish?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I’m confused. Are the potatoes the only vegetable that gets put in sauce before assembly? Will the sauce find it’s way down to the beets?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Yes and yes.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I lost a whole day of my life to this gratin and yes, it’s delicious, but no, I wouldn’t make it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this without the phyllo?  I have several relatives who can't eat gluten.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I'm thinking of making this the day before T-day and, then upon reheating, do the phyllo. Any thoughts on this method–feel free to share.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I'm allergic to beets.  What is a good substitute?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: purple potatoes

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried freezing this? How does it hold up?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with coconut milk?  And nuts rather than dairy  and Gruyère or cauliflower crème?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: A crowd pleaser - spectacular and delicious

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: A great project recipe and fun to make together with a friend when you have lots of time. Delicious and impressive.  I'd make more sauce next time since one cup to serve with 12 servings is skimpy.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This is very similar in content and method to The Geeky Chef’s “Deeper’n’Ever” pie inspired by Redwall. If you are good at pie crust, you might prefer it to this version with phyllo. The sauce in that one does have smoked paprika, which gives a nice flavor, but may affect the purity of the color layers.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This is fabulous, even without the phyllo topping if you run out of time.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I regret the beets. their juices took over the dish! and the phyllo always seems to perplex me. stood in the kitchen all day and now my back hurts. will try a food processor and subbing the base for something new.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I even skipped the butternut squash layer and was delish. Reco leaving in longer with Phyllo layer for crispiness!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: what does shingle mean in this recipe?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Layer the slices as if they were roof shingles, slightly overlapping.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made in two 8x8 dishes and worked well.  The phyllo dough hard to work with (I just layered) -- but it cooks up pretty anyway.  It pained me to toss the onions and herbs -- so I put the peppers in cheesecloth.  Very lovely dish, worth the effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I achieved a fairly good ombrè effect, but there was still too much beet juice and removing it from the top is awkward. If there was a way to remove a lot of liquid from the beets before assembly, this would work a LOT better. I was hoping to find something online that wouldn't overly cook the beets. I will try again but with a traditional gratin sauce instead, because I found the gruyere texture off-putting (gross even) and this cream took a lot of the time though was quite savory.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Do with squash, beets, and parsnips? Carrots if no squash

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: In Step 9 the recipe says you'll Crete eight rosettes in all, using 16 phyllo sheets. So, each time you make a rosette you place another layer of the phyllo sheet, right?  But I didn't see that happen in the video link (thank you) posted above. What am I missing?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: We are vegetarian and GF.  What would you suggest as a swap out for the Phyllo dough?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: @Julie I used puff pastry bc store was out of phyllo. There were both wheat and GF options of puff pastry at my supermarket. I sliced it and did a criss cross design on top. Found it tasty!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This is labor intensive. Layers cooked unevenly. Don’t forget the salt and rosettes should be a thin layer before forming or they’ll be doughy. Good presentation though

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I made this last year and it keeps popping up so now I feel compelled to comment. This recipe is not worth the fuss. It takes forever to assemble, the beets dye everything, and the flavors of the veg all run together. Overall a VERY underwhelming dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: You’ve got waaaaaaaaaay too much time on your hands if you’re doing this to potatoes. Just do the au gratin, I guarantee you it’s better tasting anyway.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Some of us like to fuss for our families for special occasions. And I guarantee you are waaaaaaay wrong about your guarantee!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I’m ready to make this for the second Thanksgiving in a row but I forgot the Gruyère :( I have some interesting sharp cheddars - can they substitute?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: One of my favorite dishes for the holidays.  This is my second time making the dish and I love the feedback from my family: "it's one of my favorite dishes on the table!"  The only suggestion would be to reduce some of the veggie portions, at least the potato, squash, and red potato amounts.  Did anyone else find the amounts listed to be too much?  If so, what amounts did you use?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: @Bethany I did! I ended up using only one thin layer of beets (just one), and 2 thin layers of purple sweet potato right above it. The rest I eyeballed - making the red potato at the top the thickest.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Use half of the cream and don’t strain it. Use pepper instead of peppercorns. Remove the bay leaves and just pour half the liquid in with the shallots. Don’t bother with rosettes, just put a filo top. Delicious.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: What a crowd pleaser!!!! I will add a bit more cheese between layers next time as I will make again very soon!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Made the recipe as directed and received a "colorful" review. Family asked for different root vegetables and a smaller dish. Enjoying the leftovers warmed in the microwave at work this week. Will make again and definitely use a deep pan to allow more room for the cream. My shallow glass dish overflowed.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I found this to be somewhat overly and unnecessarily fussy. I also didn’t care for the phyllo dough topping. It was an overall good dish and I ate it for leftovers but it won’t be making it again.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: The metric conversion for this is 150 grams less than it should be. Currently it asks for less than 300 grams of cheese when it should be asking for 450 grams.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Pretty good, but a lot of work, and produced a lot of liquid draining issues so much more soupy, even after rest, than anticipated

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Sooo, totally agree with other comments—this needs a make-ahead plan for several days.  Totally works to make it through Step 8, possibly further, I will try in my next round.
Feedback from my guests: Unbelievably delicious —even folks who are self-described beet-haters because they grew up with shiny plate rules and the canned ones.  Unless it's the entree, a full rosette is way too much food; even then, it's still way too big.  The suggestion was to make much smaller rosettes and double.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: I found that cutting the beets, and somewhat the sweet potatoes, to be pretty difficult since they are  very dense or hard.  Next time I might partially cook them before slicing them.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: For the amount of time and precision needed to prep all the ingredients, I didn’t think it was worth it. It was bland, so I’m sure an herb or two, possibly something else or maybe more salt? Either way, I’m not going to make it again, alas; I really had high hopes for it.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: If you are making this gluten-free and thus not using the phyllo top, do folks have a recommendation on how to top off the dish?  I know there is GF phyllo, but I would rather not use that. Any thoughts?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: @Rev DJ I haven’t made this. But what about piped mashed potatoes?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
made it with the ingredients listed -- and yum! 

some notes:
- definitely steam the cauliflower for 10-15 minutes before assembling the dish
- use a deep baking dish larger than the recipe calls for (mine was 8x12 in. - helps in boiling off extra liquid from the veg)
- my cook time was 20 min covered, 30 uncovered 
- I think 4 cloves of garlic is a bit much (unless you want your whole house to reek of garlic afterwards).

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: 4 cups of cheese?  Is that right?  Thank you very much

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Am  I misreading the recipe? They seem to be the only one with this question   Are the slices really 1/8 inch thick? My DeBuyer adjustable mandolin doesn’t cut that thick. Are most people slicing these thinner? What am I missing?

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: @Mrmcglone I'm in the middle of the bake and my mandolin doesn't get down to 1/8 either and so far everything looks good.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. I simmered the potatoes in the cream (prior to adding the eggs) because my experience with hasselback potatoes suggests that thinly sliced potatoes never quite finish cooking if they are not pre-softened. This worked great but in the stress of prepping for Thanksgiving, I then forgot to add the egg yolks. it still came out just great. I would definitely make this again. With egg yolks next time!

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
Awww… I wanted this to be the beautiful main dish for tomorrow’s meal. I took advice from people that said to soak the beats in water ahead of time; I even dried individual vegetables layers while assembling. I just took it out of the oven. Not even close to that beautiful image posted on New York Times!. Hoping that it will be delicious. I don’t think I will try this again. Too much effort for not enough TaDa!

I was up for a challenge and followed all the directions and checked the comments. Even with these expert suggestions, I ended up with something that definitely didn’t look as beautiful as the original recipe. Very big bummer since I’m hosting a dinner tomorrow. My solace is that I bet it’s going to be delicious anyway.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Served this tonight for Christmas dinner.  Wanted something festive for my vegetarian daughter.  It was so good!  I made it as directed, forgot to thaw the phyllo so skipped the rosettes but will add them next time for the looks and crunch.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody:
I added the tempered egg yolks to the cream before portioning out the 1 cup for serving. When it came time to reheat it, the eggs scrambled in the cream sauce.  Perhaps I wasn’t gentle enough, but I’d be tempted to portion out the cream on its own before adding the tempered egg yolks for the gratin. 
I too had a lot of beet juice on the bottom once I cut into it after cooling for 30 minutes. 
While it was a lot of work to put together, it was really tasty and looked great.

author: Alexa Weibel
recipeTitle: Ombré Gratin Recipe
commentBody: Everyone loved this dish, and I’ll probably do it again as a vegetarian main dish next time I need one of those. I used also added a layer of sweet potatoes because I was using up root vegetables, and this definitely helped maintain the color gradient (they were a darker orange than the squash, so they provided a barrier from the beets). I didn’t scrunch the phyllo up super tight, so it looked a bit like wrapping tissue at the top of a gift bag. My sister tells me that made it a lot prettier than the ones she has seen online since I made this for Thanksgiving.
